Techniques and Sciences



THE EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES
AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
The place of techniques in economic analysis is and always has been fundamentally ambiguous. All the theories which have arisen since the first Industrial Revolution and which despite their differences, make up the body of economic science, have recognised techniques as having an essential role: to represent the basis of the systems of production, exchange and distribution without which nothing could be explained.
At the same time, the inclusion of technological change in the analysis has shown itself to be very difficult and the hypothesis of a constant technique is frequently made, either openly or tacitly. The study of the short term equilibrium which has largely dominated all the works by the Neoclassical School, and which today remain the most elaborate set of theories thus assumes that the technical level remains constant.
The same hypothesis is widely held by Keynesians. The analyses, meaningful only in the long term, inspite of the fact that theoreticians have not pointed out the real, let alone an approximate, duration, have made very little reference to the eventual change in technical levels.
The Ricardian and even the Marxist dynamics generally run along these lines. One gets the impression that after the ritual bow made to techniques theoreticians strove to get rid of something so cumbersome which hindered the use of their tools for analysis and threatened what appeared to them to be satisfactory conclusions. What would become of Malthus' and Ricardo's theories, if they had simply been interested in what was actually happening in the England of their time instead of anticipating the changes in agriculture and especially in transport which led to the drastic changes which occurred in the 19th Century.
The same could be said about Marx's view and presentation of the English textile industry of the 1840-1850 decade as the perfect and


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
ultimate model of the capitalist system. It is even more striking to see what difficulties were met by those who had wanted, for the past thirty years, to explicitly introduce techniques in growth models which it initially seemed important to them to consider.
It must not be forgotten that the concept of "technique" in economic literature is hardly clear and that it can have different meanings, which need to be clarified. From a static point of view, a technique pertains to factors used in a given activity, primarily of a productive nature, even though the concept can be extended to other things such as sales and organisation and the proportion in which these factors are used.
Technique is also at the base of what is conventionally called "combi nation of production." It is quite possible that at a given time, there exists for the production of a commodity alternative techniques bet ween which one can choose. Thus, an important part of the theory of a firm's behaviour is based on the choice of the optimum combination of production amongst a range of possible techniques be they finite or infinite in number. (Surface or hypersurface indifference curves). It is also possible that at a given time some techniques are better than others. It is from amongst these that the choice will be made; they make up the zone of efficient combinations. This leads to the dynamic notion of technical progress or of technical change if one wishes to use a Jess normative term. The concept of progress however, is strongly anchored in the analysis as one generally assumes that change would only occur if it could bring about progress, and in particular, a reduction in the costs of factors used to obtain a product. There would therefore be at one time and for the same product, a succession of efficient combination zones, each situated at a higher level of efficiency than the previous one.
A correlative concept to that of the combination of production is the production function, which relates the production of a good to the input of factors used. It was initially an empirical concept, globally applied at the national macroeconomic level. The findings by Cobb and Douglas on the American economy between the 1921-1940 period, have especially given prominence to the famous Cobb
Douglas function., The production function however, could also be considered at the micro level: at sector or even firm level. Its link to the combination of production is therefore clear. That is to say a combi nation defined by the proportion of factors that it is made up of.
Assuming that the absolute quantities of these factors varies, without


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
891 altering the proportions, one gets a production function for a given technique. The transfer from one technique to another can conse quently be described as a change in the production function. The total number of techniques represents the total number of the production functions between which the firm can choose. In theoretical analysis, the key element is therefore the relative price of each of the different factors.
When the quantities of factors are increased proportionally that is to say by remaining within the same technique and with the same combinations of production, the economic unit (firm or nation) is developed along the same production function.
The product obtained however, can vary in three different ways:
I - Proportionally to the factor input. In this case the production function is homogenous by degree 1, as follows:
P = f(L,C) §A P = f(AL,AC) Where P = Production, L = Labour and C = Capital.
This appears to be the most normal function and theoretical models usually make the assumption of constant returns of scale; one thus gets an equation (fig. 1) where Pis a straight line rising regularly from the origin.
It is along a similar production function P = T"'C1 -(l' that Cobb and Douglas were able to adjust the growth of the American economy.
II - If P expands more than proportionally to the input in factors, there would be increasing returns of scale or economies of scale, the factors being more efficiently used as the size of the unit grows. This would therefore lead to economic progress without technical change, solely due to a better organisation of production and a reduction in the average cost of production (fig. 2).
III - If P expands less than proportionally, it results in diseconomies of scale and factor wastage with the enlargement of the unit.
Finally, one must note that these three stages can occur simulta neously, generally through increasing, constant and diminishing postulated in economic analysis. The production function then

2P
1 p p
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
T
c Figure 1.
p Figure 2.


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
893 returns, resulting in a U-shaped average cost curve, as is often becomes much more difficult to manipulate as it loses its homogeneous character.
This type of analysis which is carried out at an extreme level of generality and abstraction only makes sense when the assumption is based on homogeneous and undifferentiated factors of production.
Moreover, it is impossible to conceive of technical change other than as a modification in the proportions which hardly affects the quality of inputs. As soon as one abandons this seemingly unrealistic hypothesis, things become more complicated and the concept of technical change widens considerably.
One first of all notices two important types of changes which can be clearly distinguished at the conceptual level, but only with difficulty in practice: an alteration in the product and in the process.
Production modification can affect either the whole of production, if one takes the latter to remain homogeneous, or its composition in the case of diversified production. It is at this point that the concept of
"new product" arises.
The variation in the process does not by itself bring about a change in the product only a quantitative and qualitative change in the compo sition of the factor input and of previously unused factors entering the combination, while others eventually disappear.
In maintaining the hypothesis of homogeneity of the product or if need be that of the unchanged composition, the change in the process may occur in several ways. It can affect all the production immediately, the new process replacing entirely the former, or only a part of it which increases progressively with the replacement of depreciated factors, to which can be added the periodical increases in the capacity of produc tion. Secondly, and not forgetting the essential difference between human (labour) and non-human factors (mostly capital), the change or innovation can be embodied or incorporated in either the one or the other; new qualifications come into being and new types of equipment or raw materials replace those in existence immediately or gradually.
If the distinction between a modification in the product and in the process is very clear theoretically, it is not easy, except in exceptional circumstances, to find concrete examples in reality. Oxygen-steel can be considered as an example, where the quality of the product is not greatly affected by the introduction of a new factor, the jet of oxygen only bringing about a reduction in the cost of production. The same


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
occurs in the production of an electric current using a nuclear process.
On the other hand however, a new process does often lead to a change in the nature of the product or, more frequently, to a new product, whereas one that has been modified calls for a change in the nature of the factors used.
The distinction does however have a certain importance if only as a result of the conditions which give birth to this "novelty". The adjustment in the process is generally conditioned by the search for a reduction in production costs, the profit being sought in the improvement of the conditions of supply. The adjustment in the product on the other hand, is concerned more with demand, whereby profits occur through the creation of a new market.
The combination of production concept in turn makes way for another distinction which concerns the importance of the innovation.
With a combination of production two things need to be empha sised: the factors which make up the combination and in which proportions on the one hand, and the very nature of the combination and the reactions brought about by the introduction of these factors on the other. The first concerns the field of techniques in the strictest sense of the word and the second, that of technology.
In economic literature the two terms are often used indifferently, the second perhaps more recognised than the first. It is more realistic however, to set them apart and to provide technology with a more approximate meaning than its immediate one: the science of tech mques.
A technology is characterised by a certain field of science even if it is empirical in nature, and also by its ability to induce certain types of reactions from the bringing together of factors. A technique is embo died in a technology of which it is a concrete application. Things become clearer if one looks at an example of pure change in the process, the substitution of fuel for coal in thermal power stations results more from technical change even though some new factors are used, whilst the arrival of nuclear reactors on the other hand, repre sents a technological change as it is necessary to control new reactions.
Obviously, it is not easy to mark a distinct boundary between the two as the introduction of a new factor into the combination can lead from a simple technical change to a real technological one; conversely, new technology often results from progressive changes in techniques, without necessarily bringing about a drastic change or a sudden


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
895 introduction of a new sphere of science, which is only slowly revealed.
The importance of an evolution can only be therefore appreciated in the long term.
These comments lead to another question: which is the precise act or moment of technical or technological change? Schumpeter, who was the first to emphasize the importance of technical progress in economic evolution, distinguishes clearly between invention and innovation. On its own, the first does not have a strong economic link. It represents the discovery of a science-enriching principle, but one which remains as such, being of little use for anything else. The second, on the other hand, is an actual economic application whose main feature is to create a new production function by exploiting resources in such a way not previously considered. Innovation is largely independent of invention.
In most cases, they are not even the work of the same individual, and certainly do not originate in the same motivations or mental and social processes: materially, the former is impartial, whereas the latter is particularly interested in and conditioned by, the search for profits.
Some innovations do not even depend upon inventions in the scientific sense of the term. They are rather the fruits of pure empiricism. This is particularly the case in some areas of production as Schumpeter extends the concept of technical progress beyond this sphere to include the organisational changes of the firm as well as its conquering of new markets. When innovation has invention at its origin, the innovator considers the entire knowledge accumulated, making the span sepa rating the birth of a scientific principle and its actual use in production more or less long term and dependent on the arrival of an innovative entrepreneur, whose appearance seems to depend on chance more than anything else. This idea however, is somewhat contradicted by another feature of Schumpeter's analysis which views innovation as the mainspring of the cyclical development of the economies. How such a risky element of innovation can be compatible with the near regularity of the cycle still remains an unexplained mystery. Certainly, in some cases innovation and invention seem to merge but it is the former which prevails in the sense that invention is a sort of sub product of a research essentially empirical in nature. The innovator himself hardly benefits from the scientific results of his discoveries.
Schumpeter's apparently well-founded distinction has nevertheless been criticised. A closer examination of reality shows us that the marking out of such a watertight boundary between the two processes


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
is not possible. Neither the discovery of scientific principles nor the practical application are unforeseen flashes of genius which can be easily spotted and isolated. They often merge and form part of a continuous process. Principles just do not come armed from the brain of a scientific genius. They are not often viewed as being perfect. The first applications have to depend upon this incomplete knowledge helping to clarify its voids and to fill them in. Usher for example, believes that all technical progress is born fromthe perception of a gap in our scientific knowledge and consists in the search for a solution and its critical appraisal. Thus if the first solution appears weak and ill-suited it may lead to a widening of knowledge and science. For
Schumpeter, on the other hand, the two heroes: the inventor and the innovative entrepreneur are quite clearly separate, invention in this case being regarded as a whole comprising basic principles and multi ple applications, and the fruition of a series of efforts and of "small" amounts of progress of which none represents a definite step. Busi nesses have no clear initial scientific goal so that not all science is successful whilst other science only emerges slowly after applied research. In other cases, the needs to perfect lead one into previously unexplored paths, before embarking on the introduction of totally unimagined principles. In the Schumpeterian sense therefore, there is neither a significant advance in progress nor an important distinction between invention and innovation. Furthermore, the distinction made previously between technology and technique cannot support that made between innovation and invention as technology does not have its roots solely in the field of pure science. It also plays a not unimportant part in applied science. Technology is directed towards industrial use. Usher makes a clear distinction here, between three stages of invention: primary, which is scientific in nature, secondary which has a practical use and which creates new jobs either for products or in the field of processes (methods) and is closely linked to the concept of technology, and last but not least, tertiary invention which, as an improvement does not create jobs but is comparable to a new technique. But, if this tripartition is conceptually valid, it is still difficult to pinpoint in concrete terms as the three stages are frequently confused.
The Schumpeterian conception centred on the innovative entre preneur emphasises the first application of technical change hence forth considered as complete and perfect. It represents a real break in


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
897 the development of economic history. The most important Usher-style conception however, reduces the significance of these first applications.
On the other hand, there does occur a series of tentative efforts, in the course of which the change takes shape, becomes refined and finds its important applications so that it eventually has several uses. The break is not so clear and is sometimes difficult to spot. A continuous change takes place in the economy. It is not so much the appearance of a new production function which is important, but the change which takes place in the average function of all the centres of production.
Technical change can strictly be seen as being used exclusively for the development of commodities and their conditions of production; it is for this purpose that it is mostly used. One of the most outstanding features of Schumpeter's work has been the extension of the notion beyond this narrow sphere. For him, technical progress affects equally the conditions of sale and the way in which the firm is managed. It includes the conquering of new markets, the creation of new sales organisations such as the big department stores or chain stores, and the setting up of vast enterprises either through mergers or takeovers.
More than the change in the function one could easily speak of a changed in the "activity function of the firm" which covers all the actions of a firm and is not purely restricted to the material devel opment of the product. Moreover, sales and management are them selves consumers of factors. In addition, one would not know how to reasonably isolate and protect any one of the firm's activities; the firm produces to sell and sells according to what it produces. Technical change in some respect, unbalances the entire range of activities and leads to global change. A method permitting mass production at a lower average cost favours large firms, leads to mergers, creates a need for a new organisation dividing the powers, and implies a mutiplied salesforce and a larger market for sales. The establishment of a network of branches obliges one to rethink the conditions of produc tion and supply. The initial shock can occur at any time, compelling a process of modification intended to re-establish some coherence.
THE BIRTH OF TECHNICAL CHANGE
It has to be admitted that technical progress as explained by economic analysis has hardly been satisfactory. A company's technical level is a


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
part of the data on which the theory is based, but the change is external and acts to modify these data; It comes from the outside and is not built into the system. Marx has undoubtedly gone further than any other economist in integrating technical progress into the functioning of the economy. His attempt however, has not been entirely successful and is open to criticism from within. Technical progress results from an entrepreneur's desire to increase and raise the level of his profits; at the same time however, it tends to undermine its bases by altering the organic composition of capital to favour constant capital which does not lead to surplus-value. Essentially, technical progress results in the reduction of expenditure on labour per unit of output and in an increase in the number of commodities obtained for a given quantity of labour.
Applied to working-class consumer goods, it permits a reduction in the amount of wages in making subsistences less expensive during hours of work (relative surplus-value), whilst applied to other goods, it raises profits if the price is constant. If the savings in labour (wages) is compensated for by increased expenditure on capital (constant) it is not the cost but the creative part of the capital in surplus-value which is reduced. The reduction in the aggregate remuneration per unit must not therefore be balanced by a proportionate increase in fixed capital.
At the same time, the total quantity of labour incorporated in the commodity is reduced and its value accordingly. The increase in profit is therefore only possible if the price of the commodities is no longer based on their actual value but is fixed by a sort of average value, a conclusion at which Marx was forced to arrive. The advantage for the entrepreneur only exists as long as he has a lead over others and if his process bads not yet achieved the dignity of a normal technique fixing the hours of work "socially necessary" in obtaining a product. Marx had clearly seen the two stages of technical progress: introduction and extension well before Schumpeter. There remains nothing more to say other than that it is difficult to explain why working-class consumer goods do not behave strictly in the same way as other goods and especially why the price of labour obeys more readily the law of value than other prices, except for an overabundance of this commodity.
Technical progress, the change in the organic composition of capital and the downward trend in the level of profit which results from it, obliged Marx to send the labour theory of value out of the window as with the ideas of Plato which, as everyone knows were never to be


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
899 realised upon earth. They led him to set up an extremely complicated theory of price which he divided into three: basic price (quantity of labour incorporated) which has never been achieved, apparent price resulting from the realignment of profit levels between sectors and industries with different capital outlays, and the real market price, the result of the play between supply and demand at any one time.
Be that as it may, Marx has shown that in a society of private businesses the motive for profit is the essential, if not the only source of technical progress. Schumpeter who admired Marx a great deal, could only follow him in this, and made a strong connection between technical progress and the capitalist system in its extreme form: where individual firms exist and a man takes upon himself the risks and the benefits of his actions.
That progress occurred in precapitalist or pre-industrial societies is without doubt, but it was not at regular intervals, and based on age-old methods and products. Furthermore, where progress did occur, it was in fields (business and banking) where a real capitalist spirit had developed as for example, in Mediaeval Italy or in the Rome of the first century B.C.
In his last work Capitalism, Socialism and Democracy, Schumpeter doubted whether such a strong bias towards technical progress could outlive the weakening of the capitalist spirit in a world of big business and above all its replacement by another economic system indifferent in its services to humanity, and basically impartial. It did not seem to him to be a strong motivation. For Schumpeter, technical progress is essentially based on the existence of a group of men in special positions and possessing a certain frame of mind: the entrepreneurs.
The entrepreneur only sees himself in a world of free and indepen dent firms, with as many decision-making centres accepting certain authority only. His authority over the firm is total, as well as his responsibility concerning the effects of the decisions he takes. But this is not enough. The entrepreneur must wish to make as much profit as possible and be possessed with a real spirit for adventure.
The capitalist system certainly highlights the drive towards profit but ·it does not necessarily create the determination needed for this to occur. In a world of countless small-size firms forced to compete, profit levels are reduced to the minimum level (opportunity profit) necessary to keep the firm going, but at the same time, the desire for profit is stronger than the rewards received, in the form of income and perhaps


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
more of power, than when it is received by an individual who does not have to share it. It is just such a system which has the ability to draw forth the personality of the entrepreneur-innovator. If the company head wishes to increase his profits substantially, it can only be achieved by substituting a monopoly situation by one of competition. The direct elimination of others not being possible, something else is needed, this being technical progress, in one or other of its forms, products, processes, markets or organisation. Innovation thus places the enterprise in a privileged position which in turn leads to increased profits. On the other hand, Schumpeter praises monopoly, not the achieved, established monopoly which is a contributing factor in stagnation, but rather the desired and researched monopoly. The system can only remain dynamic if this monopoly is temporary and is in turn replaced by the work of another entrepreneur impelled by the same motives. The effect of each innovation is short-lived because it is itself threatened by other innovations on the one hand, and by its own extension on the other.
Not all heads of business however, are qualified to become entrepreneur-innovators. The majority remain trained managers, jobs which in themselves are useful and highly considered, but are unlikely to promote progress, at least not initially, as only those innovations are taken up which have proved their worth. The innovator is therefore led by a special and rare spirit which is the love of the risk or the game.
The Schumpeterian innovator is not an inventor, or a scientific genius or even a technical expert capable of creating a new economic object from certain known principles. He can only guess at what profits could arise from these principles, decides to make use of them, and takes on not only the risks involved but also the profits which might come his way. He steps forth from routine management with the dangers that it entails, which goes to explain his relative rareness.
Schumpeter insists on three basic features of innovation. It represents the results of an individual seeking his own personal gain; it calls for an act of will; and it is a gamble which has enough chances of failure as of success.
In accepting this as true, it is necessary all the same to make a few comments to help clarify it.
As we have noted, innovation is hardly ever perfect at its initial stage. It may be the result of a continuous process in which several entrepreneurs and inventors take part, each one bringing about a


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
901 further improvement or a new direction of use. It is the end product of these advancements of which none are conclusive. This narrows the difference between an innovator and an imitator. The first application or use of an innovation can therefore have little success: the product is not yet perfected, additional commodities are lacking and expenditure appears to be a lot higher than previously expected. It is a second application which often succeeds as it benefits from the experience acquired, helps to improve the product, and adapts better to the demands of the market which by then is beginning to unfold. If the pioneer's quality still remains, then it is the second arrival which introduces progress into the economy and fulfills the real role of a
Schumpeterian innovator.
The second point concerns the individual aspect of innovation.
Without being absolutely certain, Schumpeter feared that the capi talism of large units was less inventive than a system of small businesses where the element of personal interest is more prominent.
There, the ability to innovate is concentrated in the existing bodies and does not provide the same opportunities for embarking on new projects as in a less centralised economy. An individual's role is buried in the administrative structure which is more resistant to the birth of necessarily disruptive innovations.
Nevertheless, the larger enterprises have shown themselves to be far more inventive over the last thirty odd years, but the style of innovation has no doubt changed little. It has become more systematic, requiring less genius and more organisation and based around a group of specialists whose job it is to discover. The maintenance and even the acceleration of technical progress in a centralised capitalist society can be explained in several ways.
a) The amount of funds available for research outweighs those possessed by a small firm.
b) The risk taken is not as huge because the firm invariably has other activities.
c) The survival of the firm, the main aim of its managers, is only possible through its expansion which in turn is conditioned by the need for new products as the market for the existing ones is extendable.
d) The oligopoly markets particularly favour competition through innovation, as competition through prices is dangerous and a firm


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
cannot expect to overtake others on existing markets without risking a never-ending war.
e) The organisation itself favours technical progress to a large extent: once research departments have been set up they have their own dynamic and do not cease producing inventions.
f) Finally, the large firm has greater chances of pushing research to a fundamental level which is hardly possible for the individual entre preneur.
This last point deserves some attention. The role of fundamental research, that is to say, the introduction of scientific principles, has been minimised by Schumpeter in the sense that he establishes a greater distinction between it and the practical aspect of innovation:
the entrepreneur does not have as a goal the development of science, he just takes from it what he finds. Research of a scientific nature is not carried out within the firm as being too expensive and risky, and for not directly leading to profits. Moreover, as it brings laws and not definite processes or products to the fore, it does not lead to patentable inventions but, on the contrary, tends to favour competi tors.
The entrepreneur seeks to create savings for his own firm and not for others. Even the style of this research is not well suited to the firm, because to succeed it must not be enclosed from the outset "in the shackles of utilitarianism" but be free to develop in any direction. It cannot be the research of something concrete defined in advance.
Paradoxically, these same features can make it extremely profitable if certain conditions exist.
a) The applications are saved a lot of time and energy if there is an awareness of principles. The detour through scientific research can therefore prove to be fruitful as it avoids the use of trial and error to solve a given problem. Initial expenditure can be high but the overall cost is reduced.
b) Fundamental research often leads to new economically profitable areas not foreseen at the beginning. Certain innovations only come about in the framework of directed research necessarily based on the known. With such research the aim is to eliminate all that does not contribute to the goal that is sought. Fundamental research on the other hand, remains open to all possible development.


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
903 An enterprise will only embark on such a route if it can meet the necessary cost over a sufficiently long period because it has no guaran tee of a quick or even a small return. This is all the more likely since th directions of research are numerous and liable to multiply, on the off-chance of new discoveries, which in turn increases the initial expenditure. It is equally good that the firm can benefit from eventual discoveries whose effects could not have been initially predicted and that they have no difficulty incorporating them into the firms current activities. A diversified enterprise more than any other, with its large technological base is therefore encouraged to undertake fundamental research as it, more than any other, can reap its rewards.
The decline of the individual entrepreneur-innovator as perceived by Schum peter, does not perhaps signify the weakening of the econo mic system's ability to innovate, but rather a transfer of the centres of technical progress to large firms and the more disinterested gov ernmental and university laboratories. The merging of firms and their increasing diversification is at the same time one of its causes and consequences. One also wonders whether the creation of new techni cal objects does not increasingly rely on scientific knowledge and consequently give less opportunities to the individual discoverer whose abilities are necessarily limited and whose only choice is empiri cal experimentation.
These new features of innovation lead one to question the division between private (enterprises) and public centres (government and universities). The shape of the markets plays an important role here.
Where large firms exist or where oligopoly structures are dominant, businesses can hope to make a substantial and long-lasting profit from new products and processes and to retain for themselves the bulk of the social benefit resulting from innovation. If the process leads to a reduction in the cost price for example, the firm will not pass it all on to its consumers, through price reductions, due mostly to the inflexibility of the price system in a centralised market. The firms would therefore be tempted to develop their research to a fundamental level whose external effects they could internalise.
On the other hand, where markets are competitive, a large part of the profit from innovation is passed on to the public by increasing quantities and reducing the prices. Each firm's actual profit is minute in comparison to the social benefits gained from innovation. It is also difficult to internalise external effects and the incitement to research,


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
especially in its fundamental state is not strong at the level of the firm.
It is at this point therefore, that public institutions and universities take on an important role.
Each innovation responds to its own dynamic. It is dependent on the state of science and the development of demand which is usually favourable, the shape of the markets, and to a lesser extent, the individual decisions of the researchers and the company managers.
One should ask oneself however, whether innovations do not occur simultaneously. Schumpeter put forward the idea of a cluster of innovations to mark periods of change in the economy. The idea, while supported by countless examples, still has no clear conceptual link to the rest of his theory which in comparison underlines the individual and unique aspect of each progress that occurs. The presence of clusters of innovations can however be explained by a series of arguments.
a) Those innovations which are said to be important can have several uses in different industrial sectors. This is often the case with all those concerning the production of energy. Each individual use requires an often substantial adaptation. It is true that in the case of the steam engine the applications were spread over more than a century, whilst for the internal combustion engine things went a lot quicker and the cluster appeared much tighter.
b) Closely related to this are the discoveried which give way to an undefined field of research. It is worth mentioning at this point, the polymerization processes.
c) First innovations are hardly perfect. According to the continual process already described, several innovations are needed. Innovation is itself a cluster rather than a unique and individual act.
d) In the case of products resulting from more than one compon ent, a larger number of innovations are often necessary for the product to function. Striking examples are provided by the car and the aero plane.
e) Many products generally require the co-operation of various technologies and branches of activitiy either successively (the devel opment stage of the commodity) or in one go. They must be suited to each other qualitatively and quantitively. The progress in one sector creates a demand in other sectors and encourages innovation. The


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
905 classic example is provided by the alternate progresses made in spinning and weaving in 18th century England, each invention trigger ing off a phenomenon of excess production in the sector where it occurred and shortages in others. In some cases, an innovation that is technically possible only becomes so economically, if a step forward is taken by another sector (oxygen-steel which relied on the liquefaction of gas).
f) Finally, in a rapidly growing sector the incitement to innovate is great; the necessary financial means can be released because of the major profits that result; the research budgets are often related to the firms' turnover, increasing and decreasing with them. In an expanding market firms often seek to conquer new areas and modify their products to adapt them to these areas.
Innovation is rarely a unique act accomplished in one go which then expands as in the rest of the economy. The new products and processes are constantly modified as they become absorbed into the system.
Birth and absorption cannot be strictly separated, even though the latter has its own particular characteristics which at this point, are worth considering.
THE DIFFUSION OF TECHNICAL CHANGE
Where a business enterprise is first to make use of an innovation it tries hard to hold on to it and to its monopoly for as long as possible. This however, is not always easy.
If the innovation is patentable it can provide some protection but because of the distinct nature of the patents it is not difficult to get round them with similar but still different processes.
In a competitive market where the firms are small, the diffusion cannot be checked on a long term basis, but this does not mean that it is spontaneous and quick.
Finally, in the case of those innovations occurring outside firms, in public research bodies, quick diffusion is often the case but it meets with the reluctance from firms more than anything else. Those busi nesses which are the first to take up an innovation are only experi menters and not inventors.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
The effect of the diffusion is above all to reduce the profits from innovations to levels of "normal" opportunity profit at least for individual firms. The process can however, be slowed down and momentarily stopped by additional innovations. We need to look at some of the suggestions put forward to help clarify this point.
If innovation spreads within a group of firms in an oligopoly situation, they often manage to stabilise their profits despite their rivalry by keeping prices steady as well. They prefer to share the benefits rather than see a reduction in them. Moreover, firms do try to isolate themselves from each other a little, by developing when possible, an aspect or a particular use of the novelty for themselves, thereby setting up a series of monopolistic markets.
Where innovation concerns processes and aims to reduce produc tion costs by becoming widespread, it holds on to this feature and, except when diffusion brings with it an important development in production, or, on the other hand, a shortage in essential factors, profit levels are hardly affected. Decreasing profitability is however, the prerogative of new products for whom diffusion is synonymous with increasing supply. Finally, account must also be taken of the compara tive change in production and demand. A new product often leads to a big increase in demand which can offset and go beyond, the increases in production. The reduction in profitability is only marked when the market reaches saturation point.
The problem of diffusion can be considered from three different angles:
a) Within a given sector. In this case it is the actual rate of expansion which is the main problem.
b) Between sectors:
firstly, there is the transfer of an innovation from one sector to another with the eventual changes needed for its use in another area. Secondly, an innovation in a sector often requires additional ones, a problem which was mentioned when we discussed clusters of innovations, but whose why's and wherefore's need to be clarified.
c) Finally, when an innovation occurs within a country or a region it can extend to other countries and regions. Two problems often arise:
the adaptation of technical progress to different environments and the ability of countries to absorb the innovation.


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
907 The study of particular innovations and their diffusion has led to three main conclusions, of which the third is the least certain.
a) The diffusion of different innovations appears to follow a common outline and can be altered along the sigmoid type function whose logistic function is one of the most well-known models. Diffu sion is slow at first and innovation is taken up by those pioneer firms, for whom it is best suited in its initial stages. It then tends to expand at a faster rate. Diffusion slows down from a point of inflexion and aims for a limit which in the best of cases can be of 100% for the firms in the sector. Obviously this function is very similar to the one which describes the expansion of products among consumers. First of all, the degree of imitation accelerates according to the installations already made which, facilitate diffusion for new firms as shown by the examples they provide.
Secondly, one enters an area where firms are less able to receive innovation and do not gain as much from it; this in turn slows down the process which stops when the number of firms able to take it up grinds to a halt. It may still exist in the untouched areas of a business for whom innovation holds little interest.
b) Despite the common outline, the speed with which the process of diffusion is completed varies according to the nature of the change and from sector to sector.
At this point one can only provide a partial explanation because many of the actual processes of diffusion haved passed unnoticed and consequently, have not been studied.
Diffusion is all the more rapid since the profitability of the inno vation is greater in comparison to the previous one, and because the number of firms which may be affected are smaller but more important. Diffusion is quicker in oligopoly situations rather than in competitive ones which to some extent, reduces the aforesaid advan tages of a large firm.
The speed is also affected by the amount of initial expenditure brought about by innovation. Account must also be taken of the capital which has to be replaced. Many innovations, especially those related to processes are only introduced in a firm when the existing equipment is worn out or at least financially written off. The longer lasting the equipment, the slower the diffusion. Finally, if innovation does not spread solely in existing firms, but also brings about the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
creation of new ones, the diffusion is speeded up because these new firms are already equipped for the new process.
c) The third conclusion is the least certain of all. The penetration time of innovations would shorten on approaching the actual period.
This is an idea which still needs to be studied in greater detail. It may be due to the slight geographical isolation of the firms, the better and faster circulation of technical information or to the more favourable attitude towards technical progress.
The diffusion of technical progress between sectors is still a rela tively unknown phenomenon; until now, inter-sector relations have always been studied from a quantitative than a qualitative view-point.
(The input-output matrix). There is no doubt however, that the complementarity effects brought about by the initial diffusions are also present for the second.
The first and most simple case concerns the use of the same process for different sectors which do not necessarily have main connections with each other. They do however, have similar problems which can be solved in the same way. Innovations in the field of energy are typical of this type of diffusion. The same phenomenon can be found in other less obvious but remarkable cases. The aeronautical and automobile industries have been able to use many processes and components simultaneously. The first, although the more recent of the two, has generally been the more innovative. Paradoxically, this type of diffu sion does not come up against the same obstacles as the one operating within the same sector as the patents do not provide the same protection. On the other hand, innovation can rarely be taken up as it is. It requires large-scale adaptation. In the aeronautical industry the disregard for costs (military machinery) together with the importance of materials, (civil apparatus) justify the use of expensive processes.
The same cannot be said for the automobile industry where the search for reduced costs is essential. Thus an innovation which is technically sound may not necessarily be so economically. This lateral diffusion can give rise to new and different products from the initial innovation, if a sector combines this innovation with its own products. Thus for example, the numerically driven machine-tool resulted from a liaison between the traditional machine-tool and computer processing.
Incitement can also lead to the diffusion of technical progress. It is no longer a case of diffusion in the strictest sense, because the


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
909 innovations are different; they are, however, so closely linked to each other that one can speak of inductive and induced innovations. These liaison are mostly based on the quantitative relations between sectors, technical progress thus following the exchange in commodities. This influence can occur either at the top around the suppliers who must adapt to the new demands of the innovative sector or at the bottom by obliging customers to use new products.
In some cases, the initial innovator even participates in the setting up of additional innovations.
All things being equal, the greater the links an industry has with others, the greater is its ability to incite. Similarly one can believe that the more a nation's economy is "complete," that is to say, the greater the number of sectors represented within it, the stronger will be the overall effects of incitement as will the tendancy towards technical progress. Amongst others this is one of the reasons which makes the developed economies of the West more innovative, headed by the
United States, with perhaps the most complete economy.
This last point helps us to understand some of the problems presen ted by the diffusion of technical progress between countries. We should not dwell upon this too much because it is more a general question of development, and one which cannot be detached from it.
Two key obstacles are placed in the path of diffusion: the economic uselessness of innovation in a given area, which is more a market phenomenon, and human inability to use it which is more a question of training. Some innovations cannot be transferred as they are; they are drawn up when certain given natural conditions exist and are otherwise useless or even harmful. So, for example, the agricultural progress of the temperate zones is dangerous in tropical countries.
The importance of the size of the markets plays a not insignificant role even for countries with similar natural conditions. The importance of the American market, and the existing high incomes have favoured the birth and development of innovations, especially those which lead to mass production and economies of scale. Whilst in Europe, their diffusion has been delayed by wars on the one hand, and by the protectiveness of the n-ational markets on the other, which has made certain products and processes uneconomical.
The human obstacles especially affect the developing countries, even though the protectiveness of markets also plays a not unimpor tant part. Innovations are either not introduced there or they do not


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
succeed because they cannot be assimilated by the population. This may very well lead to a paradoxical situatiton: owing to the lack of a skilled labour force, one makes use of the most sophisticated and automatic processes which remain isolated within the economy, without the ability to diffuse or to incite.
TECHNICAL CHANGE AND THE
COMBINATION OF PRODUCTION
Technical change was described at the beginning as quite an important modification to the combinations of production and the passing from one production function to another. One of the most controversial questions which is still not solved is whether there is a general, if not permanent, pattern to these changes, if, as the economists say, techni cal progress has a "bias". The problem is simply one of deciding if it favours the more capitalist combinations or vice versa.
Marx clearly believed in the former because for him the organic composition of capital moves towards an increased used of constant capital (short-run capital). The same idea has been upheld by a large number of economists since. That this change does not bring with it increased unemployment or the creation of a reserve industrial army, as in the Marxist model, was due to the strength of the growth which offset the "bias" in technical progress in favour of capital.
Certain notions have been put forward to take account of the nature of technical changes at this point. A distinction is usually made between "Labour Saving", "Capital Saving" and "neutral" inno vations. The definitions for these three terms however, are neither clear, nor used by all theoreticians. Two of them, however, have become important but unfortunately they are not quite compatible.
Hicks defined a labour saving innovation as that which raises the marginal productivity of capital more than that of labour for a given combination of production, that is to say, a certain ratio between capital and labour. It is apparent that the marginal productivity of labour does not necessarily remain stable nor does it a fortiori, decrease; it suffices that it increases less than that of capital. A capital saving innovation possesses the opposite properties and a neutral


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
911 innovation raises the two marginal productivities in the same propor tions. The second definition provided by Harrod, is more complex and not as easy to manipulate. A neutral innovation keeps the capital coefficient constant (the capital/output ratio), if the rate of interest remains constant; a labour saving innovation increases this ration whereas a capital saving one reduces it. It has one problem: barring a decrease in the marginal productivity of capital, an innovation brings about an increase in the capital coefficient with difficulty.
Unfortunately, the effects of an innovation on the use of factors are not easy to ascertain as is clearly seen if we look at Hicks' definition. A given innovation without doubt, alters the ratio of the marginal productivities of labour and capital (their marginal rate of substitution) but it can also have an effect on their price ratio and even upon the price ratio of those products made with different combi nations of factors. If the factor price relations remain constant, a labour saving innovation would without doubt push the more capitalis tic combinations to re-establish equality between the marginal produc tivities counterbalanced by prices. But if the factor prices are flexible and the combinations of production cannot be immediately altered, the price of labour would lower in comparison to that of capital
(according to the theory of competition where factor prices are aligned with their marginal productivities). It would on the other hand, lead to an increased use of labour. Secondly, if it brings about a reduction in the relative price of labour, the labour saving innovation favours those commodities highly dependent on labour in their combination of production; if it brings about a change in the combination in a more capitalistic sense, the technical improvement is all the more felt since the commodities use a lot more labour in their development and the effect is the same. The relative price of labour intensive commodities would therefore be reduced which in turn increases demand. The overall effect on the economy would depend on the comparative elasticities of demand and price for the different types of commodities.
It is often considered that this is more likely for capital intensive products; there is no doubt about this and at present the demand elasticity of services which in particular use labour is high. Paradox ically, a labour saving innovation applied in this area would lead to a significant rise in demand and increased unemployment.
Finally, there is the double uncertainty of the overall effect of a given innovation on the whole of the economy on the one hand, and


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
more importantly, the comparative importance of the different types of innovations in the course of economic development on the other.
The labour saving bias has for a long time been considered as the most normal and this was confirmed by Hicks after Marx, although he did not provide convincing examples.
There are some good arguments however, to support this. The main one has been the relative rarity of labour especially in comparison to the natural resources at least in areas like North America. Since its cost is high, one is obliged to use it sparingly. A cumulative movement has developed from this. The accumulation of capital often raises a worker's average productivity and provides higher wages. The rising cost of labour continues to favour the labour saving bias.
Secondly, and more generally, men have always endeavoured since the pebbles of "Pebble cultures," to use artificial means to help them overcome their difficulties. The "turn about in production" is a sign of human genius and its extension a sign of progress. So it is that the labour saving bias is strongly embedded in men's minds.
Some doubts have, however, appeared and several theoreticians have asked themselves whether capital saving innovations have not in their turn played an important role. They have argued that such innovations are not as recognisable as labour saving ones taking the form of progress in organisation and savings in the use of capital, than in the creation of industrial units. Moreover, there are compensatory effects. Each "bias" is corrected, in creating new scarcities to which the firms must adapt by using innovations of the opposite nature, which brings a result closer to neutrality.
The main doubts, however, have been determined by an exami nation of the capital coefficient. It appears that this has remained relatively stable and has even shown some tendancy to reduce since the
1920's. This would show technical progress to be neutral or even capital saving in Harrod's sense of the term. Nevertheless, one needs to be very cautious about such a remark.
The capital coefficient is not well known and the appraisals that have been made (Domar, Goldsmith, Kuznets) vary highly. The extent of the movement is not as great as the differences between them.
Furthermore, an overall assessment of capital coefficient has little meaning. It is a means between sector coefficients which vary con siderably. Yet, with the passing of time the relative importance of the sectors has changed, largely because of the effect of the autonomous


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
913 evolution of demand. A simple theory allows us to see all the uncertainties of a conclusion resulting from the capital coefficient.
Where demand is mainly directed towards commodities whose mode of production is not very capitalistic (services, for example), the innovations in this and in other sectors are labour saving whilst the overall capital coefficient is reduced and the average combination of production is marked by a relative increase in the use of labour.
TECHNICAL CHANGE AND
ECONOMIC EVOLUTION
Technical change is essentially a dynamic notion. An economy in which it occurs cannot remain stationary. It is therefore tempting to use this means to explain the quantitative and qualitative variations in the system, and to make technical progress one if not the main factor.
In ther evolution of economic activity, it can work on two levels. On the one hand, as an element of instability, to take into account the succession of controversial imbalances or cycles which are presnt in the economy, and as a factor of long term evolution on the other. These two roles are not readily separable. The same progress which momen tarily unbalances the economy also provides food for its growth.
Technical progress, therefore, is a factor of instability in the short term. This was particularly noted by Schumpeter who believed it to be the key cause of fluctuations in economic activity. His is an extremely simple and ingenious explanation. Innovation brings with it several phenomena: for the firm using the innovation, an increased expecta tion of profit drives it to develop its activities. For this it needs capital which it does not possess. The firm therefore appeals to the credit system, thereby anticipating profits. The development of credit in turn, leads to a phenomenon of forced savings. In fact, the increase in the means of payments, which precedes the increase in production, brings about higher prices. The demand from centres (private as well as enterprises) other than the innovating centre falls whilst the factors head for the latter thus allowing it to carry out its investments. The increase in prices again raises the prospects for profits and new investments. As long as innovation is limited to the innovating firm or


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
to a small number of firms, the profits that result respond to the expectations, which has as an effect the continuation of the expansion movement. But inevitably, there does come a time when the inno vation expands and is brought into general use. The firms' profits would then begin to decrease. In itself, this evolution is not harmful and a new stage of activity could be maintained at a higher technical level. However, there are secondary phenomena as well. The expected profits may not occur. In which case, the entrepreneurs become disheartened, their creditors (banks or shareholders) receive lower payments than before and confidence is low (there may possibly be a stock exchange crisis if the prospects for earlier profits lead to speculation); the repayment ofloans becomes more difficult. The crisis which takes place is for the most part, psychological in origin ( unfulfil led expectations). The Schumpeterian cycle can be described as a technico-psychological cycle. The technical progress thus comprising expansion and the despondency from the crisis and the depression.
Such an appealing hypothesis does, however, have some weaknes ses. Any innovation, barring the need for considerable investment, as was the case for the railways, does not on its own appear capable of giving rise to such an extensive macro-economic movement. That is why Schumpeter put forward the idea of clusters of innovations. But there is something more serious:the economic cycle presents a certain regularity (about ten years for the Juglar cycle). This regularity is not in the least evident in the flow of innovations where the individuality is essential. The explanation for the cycle is simply dismissed: why is the movement of innovations cyclical?
Even more than the Juglar cycle, whose theory is today a little out of date, technical progress appears to explain the longer and more extensive movements which affect the economy and which are usually known as the Kondratieff cycles. These are fifty year movements
(twenty-five years of expansion and twenty-five years of depression) affecting quantities and prices at the same time. However, as far as quantities are concerned, the evolution shows an alternation of rapid expansions and slow developments (there is not a long term fall), whilst for prices, the increases and decreases follow one another. The approximate dates of the changes in the Kondratieff cycle are roughly as follows: 17? (trough) - 1815 (peak) expansion, 1815 (peak) -
1848-50 (trough) depression, 1848-50 (trough) - 1878 (peak) expan sion, 1879 (peak) - 1896 (trough) depression. (There have rarely been


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
915 abrupt turnabouts except perhaps in 1873 when there was a large-scale Juglar crisis.) From 1914 onwards the movements have been largely wiped out by inflation and monetary problems. Schumpeter did, however, include two dates within the period, 1920-21 (peak) and
1939 (trough). Each period of expansion will have been marked by some innovations of considerable importance requiring heavy invest ments and above all will have considerably changed the functioning of the economic systems:
17?-1815, steam engine, textiles;
1850-78, railways, steamship navigation, iron and steel;
1896-1920, internal combustion engine, electricity, chemistry.
If this explanation appears to fit quite well with Schumpeter's beliefs, it does differ from it on one key point; innovation, as the first application of progress, loses in importance because for each of the above-mentioned techniques it is placed well before the beginning of the expansion period where its role is important. This is closer to an
Usher type notion. After some trial and error, the novelty only begins to affect the economy when, through a series of inconclusive inno vations, it can be considered as almost perfect.
The importance for the swings is at that time somewhat pale and emphasis is placed on the phenomenon of long term development of the economy, and on growth. Here again, Schumpeter's pioneer role must be underlined, as he strongly emphasises the long term expansion behind the cycles. They are the form taken by growth, progressing irregularly in between a series of successive imbalances. The capitalist system is developed in leaps and bounds. But this instability is in the system and does not affect its basic functioning. Importance is placed on this last point with the easing of the strength of these cycles (final or not?). The role of technical progress which at first glance is certain, is not, however, easy to bring to the fore and to explain. Work has been carried out in two directions: on the one hand the research into the respective importance of the different factors in growth to try and assess the role of technical progress, and on the other, the construction of growth models in which technical progress is incorporated to try and take into account the way in which it takes place.
Econometric research intended to separate the different components of growth has multiplied over the last few years. It is impossible at this point, to go into the specifics of the quite complex


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
methodologies of these studies. Only the conclusions will be taken into consideration. On the whole, it can be considered that growth, expressed by the increase in output, has two main origins: the quanti tative increase in the factors of production (inputs) on the one hand, and their increasing productivity on the other. If this last factor cannot be completely compared to technical progress, it certainly depends on it a great deal. The increase in inputs is not enough to take into account the overall increase in production. In linking output to the use of factors, research based on the classical functions of production, allows for their appearance in an unexplained quantity of factors, ironically and unintentionally called the "Residue" when it is integral in a phenomenon which needs to be explained. Some, like Solow, for example, believe that the "Residue" is responsible for at least 80% of growth in Western Economies, especially the American economy. It appears that for a century the increase in inputs of labour has been low and if the quantity of labour per head is considered, it actually seems to have declined, under the double contradictory effect of a generally moderate increase in the active population and a marked decrease in the daily hours of work. The increase in capital, important in absolute terms, does not itself explain the total increase of output per capita. If factor productivities had remained constant, it would only have increased by less than 15% whilst it had quadrupled (in the USA) between 1870 and 1970.
The problem therefore, is one of knowing just how much of the progress in productivity results from techniques. There is the great difficulty of knowing where technical progress starts and where it ends, and exactly what comes under this rubric. The labour force has benefited from a better education, from the process of learning by doing, through which one becomes accustomed to an activity, as it becomes widespread in the economy, on account of the later age of entry into active life, with adults being more productive than the younger population and finally from medical progress and a better diet which are just one of the consequences of technical evolution.
The transfers of the labour force from one activity to another have in turn affected its productivity, without changing the nature of these activities.
As for capital, it is very difficult to separate its pure accumulation from its qualitative changes, each element of additional capital neces sarily including technical progress and not being strictly similar to


EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
917 those units previously accumulated. Technical progress is even present within the resources created and is not placed on the outside as an additional and easily isolable factor.
The theoretical models of growth have followed the same line of evolution. They can be separated as follows:
I - Those which are based on a single production function, but which, instead of only including as terms the quantities of inputs, also accommodate an additional term to take account of technical progress.
The latter therefore, is conceived as functioning outside the factors themselves and adding to them, which is a very basic and not so real way of describing concrete economic evolution.
II - Those in which technical progress is not an added element but intervenes directly by affecting the production function itself. One can no longer literally speak of a single production function but a series of functions for different technical levels. Each function only strictly comprises the contributions of factors and technical progress is the force enabling one function to pass to another. There are synchronic production functions applied in one moment of time and at a single technical level which describes how the production would evolve if there was only a quantitative change in the contribution where q=f
(C, L) .
There is also the diachronic function which incorporates technical progress as a factor of transfer of one function to another so that Q =A
(l) f (C, L) where A (l) is a cumulative factor measuring the effect of successive and added transfers of the function.
But with such a notion it is the whole of economic activity which is affected by technical progress. To increase the model's realism one can concentrate on two areas: to separate the economy into different sectors which in their own way are subjected to the effects of technical progress (for example, production and consumption goods); or to incorporate into the technical progress additional contributions of factors, especially capital.
It is thus that models called "generations of capital," are born where the capital created at different times corresponds to the different technical conditions. Total capital stops being homogeneous, and is instead composed of successive layers of which the oldest are gradually eliminated. The Anglo-saxons use the eloquent term, vintage. The


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
capital is so to speak a "vintage" like quality wines. Unfortunately these models soon become complicated without for all that, possessing any great realism.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
Any piece of land inhabited by people bears the hallmark of their techniques: the houses, villages, towns, communication routes, and even the most modest agricultural soils, for they too are modelled by human instruments. These lands "pose questions" (P. Gourou). In his very attractive work, Pour une geographic humaine, Gourou reveals the enormous scope and fascination afforded by this object to our curiosity. Because land represents merely a point of departure. If technical objects have their place on the surface of this land, it is in order to respond to people's basic needs to feed and house them selves, to move around, and to surround themselves with useful objects. In any research into civilization, there is a place for geographical analysis.
It has to be admitted, however, that a true geographical science of techniques - geotechnics? - which is clearly defined in terms of its objective and method, is far from being constituted. This is not due to any lack of reflection on the subject, or research. Take just some of the
French geographers, for example, from the great classical scholars to their younger descendents, like P. Vidal de la Blache, M. Sorre,
A. Demangeon, J. Gottman. P. Gourou, P. George, J. Labasse, etc.
All these have in their turn enriched a field of study which nevertheless persists in its vagueness. They were all plagued, as we too are plagued by an obstacle quite independent of research, and that is technical progress, which itself is shaking up geography: "The entire world is engaged in a succession of revolutions" (P. George). It is worthwhile considering this difficulty before going any further.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
THE GEOGRAPHY OF TRADITIONS ,
AND THE GEOGRAPHY OF
TECHNICAL PROGRESS
At the end of the 19th century, classical geography seems to have found a model of analysis, that of "the lifestyle." The position of someone like Vidal de La Blache should be recalled here, in that it was rooted in a context as yet largely untouched by industrialisation. In the quality of life, techniques, the societies which apply them, and the geographical environment to which they are applied form a coherent ensemble. "With the help of materials and elements taken from the natural surroundings, man has succeeded in creating for himself something methodical which both assures his existence and provides him with an environment for his use" (Vidal de La Blache).
The "natural surroundings" was a straightforward enough notion for traditional man, even if it is no longer so today in the towns. In
Pelissier's description of the lifestyle of the villagers of Dahomey, on the banks of the river Oueme, he informs us that all the techniques used there depend on the annual flooding of the river. Manioc is planted in the fields of the alluvial folds of the river banks. Further down, in the areas where the land is flooded a longer time, they plant maize and beans. Fishing is practised according to the simple method of "fishing holes" in the backwaters, where it is easy to work after water has gone down. It would be tempting to use the phrase "adapting to nature" if it were not so vague. "The essential mechanism, that of the flood, remains completely unchecked by the riverside dwellers"
(Pelissier).
The "natural surroundings" supplied the materials for habitation.
The African hut is made of palm, bamboo and raffia, which are all found in the neighbouring forests. By the great red plains, clay is also used. The granite which makes up the rocks of Brittany and Auvergne is also used by the peasants to build their houses. It is true that the centres of the older cities in Europe reflect the local geology: take the white limestone of Assisi, the red sandstone of Edinburgh, or the black volcanic stone of Clermont-Ferrand for example. But what should we conclude from this? Is this an adaptation, or limitation of techniques?
The old Breton houses are solid but dark; and as Gourou noted, the


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
921 lightweight Japanese house, although in harmony with the natural surroundings, is unsuitable for the icy Japanese winters. Nature is that which is accessible to the techniques currently in use. In the large collection of irrigation systems in the Sahara, the rallying points for traditional techniques are obvious: spring water, occasional water from the oueds, water from the shallow shafts and galleries are all local and superficial sources. Great dams and engines will increase the horizons of accessible water sources. For the person preoccupied with ways of life, the question is not yet relevant.
This point highlights one more characteristic of techniques: they are local. The men who build African huts work according to a model familiar to all of them. The finished product simply resembles all the other houses of the village. Elsewhere, the methods are different, and dependent on different customs. In Afghanistan in 1974, Baland noted that the geographical zones of techniques virtually still coincided with the different ethnic groups. The Tadjiks have their own particular style of house, their own village layout, and their particular system of cultivation, all of which makes it easy to characterise "Tadjik coun try." Next to them are the Pachtuns who use different techniques, on different soils. An example of Vidal de la Blache comes to mind here, who typified ethnographical techniques by the practices of the Kirghiz.
However, history is rapidly altering this traditional pattern.
Some local and regional traits and pecularities still remain, however, and peasant France is still clearly marked with these. We hardly need remind ourselves here that until not long ago, there were still at least a hundred different types of regional cattle being reared, each one located within its own specified geographical area. In each case, the feeding of the cattle, for example, or the calendars regulating the wintering and summering of the cattle on the mountains, or the ways of trading all kept their particular characteristics. The mountain regions, where cheese is still made on the farms are the last vestiges of these
"ways of life." It is tempting to agree with Vidal here . . . even if his analysis are on the verge of complete disappearance alongside their object. Industrial techniques kept their regional character for a con siderably long time. Take the shipbuilding techniques used in the
French Basque country, in Saint-Jean-de-Luz, for example. Until the beginning of the 20th century, original style fishing-boats were still being made in these regions: like the trainiere, a kind of slender whaling boat, or sardine boats which although equipped with engines,


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
nonetheless were made in the traditional style and with traditional materials, namely acacia. But all this disappeared when it became necessary to build large tunny boats for the high seas, since this was the privilege of the large shipyards in the main towns. A more rapid disappearance from the industrial scene was that of the Catalan and
Champagne forges, and the iron works of the Franche-Comte and Allevard in the Alps, each of which had their own particular character istics. These were all swept away with the general techniques of the blast furnaces.
Ways of life were characterised by one final trait: their relative stability. The techniques changed little from one generation to another, and this is the true sense of the word "tradition." In many societies in Asia or Africa, this permanence had a solid base in rites, which went beyond the simple material analysis of techniques. Take for example the traditional Kabyle village. The agriculture is far from archaic, with fields of wheat and barley, fig-trees, sheep and goats.
Craftsmen work in these villages, and the women weave wool. Servier reminds us however, that the ironsmith who makes ploughs and weaving looms, is living a sacred life as well. The weekly market is active, but the location of the transactions is a sanctuary as well. Any major innovation breaks with the moral code. This does not prohibit great change, but it does give it a significance which Europeans do not fully understand.
No society can do without innovations, and the peasants of Europe have seen many of these. No doubt the phases of stability were more frequent than those of renewal, however. The geographer Faucher has shown us how "routine and innovation in peasant life" were articu lated. When the peasantry of Aquitaine began to grow maize, it was no doubt a veritable revolution. But this "Spanish corn," or "Turkish corn" has been so well integrated into the way of life, that after several human generations, it now appears to be native. Crop rotation, food and animal fodder are all organised around the growing of maize. The intruder has been welcomed into this way of life, and a privileged place made for it. It has been made a traditional, almost routine element.
It would not be possible to understand the old rural landscapes without this long succession of repeated actions. Because the instru ments used are extremely modest. In the Mafa countries of Cameroon, less than ten types of tool are used: "the adze for clearing, the digging stick for planting, the short-handled hoe for harrowing, and a kind of


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
923 mallet for beating." Cows, sheep and goats keep the land fertile. And the gardening of the slopes in particular multiplies the patches of millet and vegetables on the terraces to be cultivated. This is an homogenous, original landscape, and one which permits - but for how long? - a density of two hundred inhabitants per square kilometre. The list of such agrarian landscapes formed from long perseverence would be a lengthy one: the mediterranean gardens, the paddy-fields of South
East Asia, the copses of the European West.
The "lifestyles" model performs less easily in the face of urban societies, even in the past. The geographical division of labour takes place between the maritime city and its distant colonies. Athens possessed real navigation routes, and Rome had an enormously extensive road network. Venice, Florence and Antwerp were unable to find any place in natural, local and permanent schema of lifestyles.
These are exceptions in a general framework which is disappearing only very slowly.
