En j al be rt gives us a timely reminder that the Mexico of the Aztecs could never have been a very large city; the limitations of such techniques as cultivation by hand, or human portage could not be compensated for even by a strong political framework. The decisive break came with large-scale industry.
Natural surroundings gave way to the machine and industrial arti fice. Natural resources are distributed too irregularly, and the rhyth mns of nature are too slow and too irregular to satisfy the industrial system. Nature can still be accomodated, for a while. The great
Swedish timber industry, for example, still floats the trunks down the rivers. The trees are cut down in winter, and then they are floated down river in summer to the factories at a very cheap cost. But the yield of such practices is judged to be too small; and too much time is wasted. The great forest roads and enormous lorries are a more familiar sight today because they enable work to continue without interruption. The machine and the factory - a collection of machines - are the images of this virtually incessant activity. Of course different activities all lend themselves differently to the mechanician's dream of a production free from natural constraints. But this may not be just a pipe-dream - in the countryside of industrial nations, for example, it is possible to see tractors being used at night.
Local or regional techniques are being phased out in favour of universal ones. We shall return later to the meaning of this word.
However, industrial progress is inventing new materials, new


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
machines, and new objects whose material superiority and effec tiveness cannot be disputed. In this sense, the steam engine was the first universal machine in the true sense of the word, along with the railway and electricity. The myriads of different shipbuilding tech niques were to gradually disappear in the face of the new efficiency of the steamship, then the motor-powered ship, and then the enormous specialised ship. Hydro-electric factories and steel plants are virtually all the same, be they in Canada or India. Blocks of flats look the same in Chicago, Paris and Tokyo. If it were not for the crowd in the street which has still maintained some of its original styles of dress, or for the language of the advertisment posters, each landscape would be indistinguishable. This is evidence of the diffusion of a "universalisa ble" technology. Quite rightly, the first iron and steel working on an industrial scale was called the "English iron and steel industry". But what should we call this technique today, now that it has been revised and corrected in the U.S.A. , in Sweden, and in Austria? Some have spoken of a world "continuum" of industrial technology: it connects all regions, all nations, all civilisations in a continuous movement.
(Karie!) Finally, the industrial system accelerates change. It was an illusion to believe that there was merely one industrial revolution which introduced one new division of geographical space. In the United
States, for example, there are a number of belts which appear to have found their equilibrium after a large upheaval: industrial belt, cotton belt, wheat belt . . . The American geographers are forced to admit today that the cotton belt no longer survives on cotton, but on the oil and chemical industries, and that the industrial North-East is letting the most modern factories slip away towards California and the South.
From the end of the First World War, it became apparent in the United Kingdom that the technical and economical bases of the regions modelled by the "first industrial revolution" had to be reconsidered, only a century after they had first been built. The new techniques:
aviation, the car, oil, electricity, all required geographical redistri butions. Today, change is so rapid that the maps of industrial activity become out of date after only a few years. The computer science industry, born in the United States around 1950, is currently [in the mid-1970's] going through its third "technological revolution" - with vacuum tubes, transistors, and micromodules. New factories are being installed in unusual sites, in France in towns with no industrial


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
925 tradition, like Nice and Montpellier for example. In Formosa, South Korea and Singapore, workshops for the manufacturing of spare parts are being opened, where skilled technicians work for large American firms. Future perspectives, even in the short term, are far from obvious: in ten years time, will we be making large computers, or small apparatuses designed for a larger market? Any forecasting with regard to future localisation therefore seems doomed to failure.
GEOGRAPHY AND THE
INDUSTRIAL SYSTEM
The Expansion of the World of Factories Is it impossible to formulate a theory which can satisfactorily explain the localisation of existing industries? Since the emergence of the large industrial centres during the last century, it has become clear that this is a matter of a complex "system" which includes coal mines, factories of heavy industry and others devoted to producing consumer goods, and then transport networks, railways, and canals, etc. We are obliged to fragment the analysis simply for reasons of clarity: from the outset we are facing a new kind of symbiosis which is infinitely more complex than that of the "ways of life" !
The massive use of fuels and minerals is a constant of the new system, which seems to lend new weight to the notion of natural resources. A mining deposit has a certain volume, a certain depth, and a certain grade of useful materials. Some "natural" advantages are striking, like the open coal faces of Newcastle, in the Tyne area, which constitute a kind of permanent "annuity." Geographical and geologi cal inequalities are all the more apparent because a mine, by defi nition, is exhausted of its wealth as it is exploited. This accounts for the principle of constant geographical extension towards new mines or promising deposits. It was noted that from the 1950's, an "average" oil-well in the United States was providing about ten barrels per day, whereas in the Middle East the figure was an astounding 5000.
However, the "global" value of a mine particularly depends on the techniques used to exploit it, and on the entire industrial system


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
(communications ensuring the existence of outlets, factories for trans forming the raw product). The "black countries" of England, Belgium,
France and Germany have been sources of extraordinary wealth because they were developed principally with the steam engine, coking works, and coke siderurgy. Today, this technology is outmoded or ageing. Most of the English basins and the Franco-Belgian basin were hit with a serious reconversion crisis. The Ruhr was not affected to the same extent; this area underwent major industrial development only later, and a large chemical industry based on coal was organised there which encouraged the development of more modern techniques. And the easy exploitation of immense deposits by enormous machines has meant that certain basins in the United States, the Soviet Union and
South Africa have not suffered at all from the "coal crisis." The changing geography of iron mines clearly illustrates the link between mines and "downstream" industrial techniques. The extraordinary dispersion of small deposits exploited in France at the end of the 18th century is well known; this geographical scattering corresponds to that of the forges and forests. Blast furnaces and steel plants were to make the larger deposits worthwhile. The Thomas and
Gilchrist iron and steel process, which enables phosphorous irons to be treated, meant a sudden development of the immense ferriferous basin of Lorraine; in this way a serious natural fault was first attenuated, then wiped out. The major iron and steel region of France was emerging. Today, new technical developments are having the opposite effect; the upsurge in transporting minerals by sea favours the distant, but rich deposits of metals (Mauritania, Brazil, etc.). An old problem with the Lorraine's minette (name of the local ore), that is, its poor
(30%) iron density, is becoming an unnacceptable one. Will the new techniques of concentrating minerals on site open up new possibilities for Lorraine? For the moment, the future seems bright for the sea-links; mineral tankers have travelled 20,000 kilometres from
Brazil to Japan!
It is industrial innovation which continues to make possible the new development of otherwise redundant natural wealth. Central and
Northern Sweden opened up its immense coniferous forest for industrialisation in order to meet the demands of the paper industry in
Great Britain; the conditions of mass production were quickly organised by large companies possessing tens of thousands of hectares of forest, transport means, and eventually the pulp factories on the


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
927 shore of the Baltic. The development of aluminium brought about the brusque inclusion of Guyana and Jamaica into the industrial fold. The industry of electrical equipment invested copper with new importance:
only the Congo-Katanga (now Zaire) and Chili were able to respond to the new needs on a big enough scale.
There is no need to dwell here on the case of oil and natural gas.
There has been a prodigious increase in size of the zones of production.
And this can only be understood in terms of the continual improvement of the means of transport, like methane tankers, petrol tankers, and standardised pipelines. In particular, the new geography of oil and gas is the business of a completely new technology, that of scientific pros pection which deploys a whole arsenal of costly means - electric boring and off-shore drilling. The proportion of oil drilled off-shore already exceeds 20% . New discoveries can also favour the poorer countries like
Gabon for example, or Northern Canada, or Siberia.
The geography of the production of electricity is a fertile subject for reflection on the changing lines of nature and techniques. As soon as a product is completely homogenised by industry - here it is a form of energy - and as soon as it can be produced from various sources, the resulting map changes constantly according to the new innovations.
The alpine mountains were soon furnished with hydro-electric dams, and for several years it meant that these regions enjoyed the profit reaped from a veritable "annuity" - once more - since it was not yet known how to transport electric currents. A process of industriali sation resulted from this, bringing to the mountains the large, energy hungry aluminium factories. But the hydraulic "sites" decreased in number: dams were being built ever higher on the mountains, but this was not accompanied by the building of factories, since the trans portation of electricity over long distances was now possible. A power network was installed, but this time it was the large power stations on large rivers and a long way from the mountains which were able to produce more economically. Thermal plants powered by coal, then fuel, then natural gas were used for the same purpose. These are situated on the main communication routes, and in their turn, they attract new industries. The large modern port, the coal basin and the mountain valley are all forced into competition because of the network of electricity (Curran).
There are several examples of this type. The technology of rubber has evolved a great deal since the beginnings of the tyre industry.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Natural latex was supplied by the Amazon at the beginning of the 20th century, and this was followed by the cycle of hevea plantations and the rise of Malaysia and Indonesia. After 1950, oil became the source of synthetic rubber, and new factories conglomerated around already powerful refineries. The glyceride industry draws simultaneously or successively from an incredible number of sources, of an extremely diverse nature, in order to make "standard" materials. Progress of one of the sources displaces the markets. The extensive cultivation of rape-seed and sunflowers gave the advantage to the plains of the
U.S.A. and the U.S.S.R. Simultaneously, the tropical oil producers, coprah from the Philippines, and peanuts from Senegal both underwent a crisis.
The links of light industry and consumer industries with raw materi als are even more tenuous. Very early on the textile industry was fairly
"free" with regards to the materials being worked, whereas it was more affected by the workforce, and by the possibilities of installing materi als which are frequently renewed. One can only understand, for example, the migration of the textile industry from New England to the south of the United States in the light of the search for cheaper labour. But this migration goes further. Today, the cheapest cloths in the world are made in Puerto Rico, Macao, Hong Kong and Singa pore. As soon as the acts are standardised and simplified, the modern factory can take root in poor countries. We saw above that other very modern industries (mechanics, parts for electronic equipment) are also attracted in this way.
The usual tendency of consumer industries, however, is to stay close to their markets. The old industrial regions, heavily urbanised, were also attractive consumer markets. Today, the newest industries take on the market internationally. The industrial space of a large car firm, or a chemical or electronic firm, for example, overrides all the political boundaries . . . or just about. When a company like Kodak (Rochester,
United States) develops its manufacturing in Europe, it seeks to situate itself at the centre of its european market. Is that Stuttgart? Or
Milan? In the increased territory of these large companies, a division of labour involving all their factories becomes possible. An I.B.M.
computer is conceived of in San Jose in California; some of the "circuits" are manufactured in Paris-Corbeil; the "memories" come from Mainz; the assembling is done in a suburb of Milan. A network of international proportions emerges without requiring a specific locali-


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
929 sation of factories. The example of the Coca-Cola company is striking in this respect; this company owes its extension to the perfect stand ardisation of the product, and to the extraordinarily strong basis of publicity (Huetz de Lemps). These are extreme examples, but they are useful in that they show the new technology as a standardised network of communication.
The Expansion of Communications Networks Infrastructures, like transport vehicles, are factory-products. Trains and railways, ships and ports, cars and motorways, planes and airports all serve industry. Lines develop which form a network, and the group of networks join to make a general system of transport (Wolkowitsch).
The stakes are not only the movement of products, but also that of people and information.
The world railway network is much less dense than that of the roads some put the figure at ten times less dense - but it was nevertheless the first network to be entirely industrialised. The railway was used straightaway for a heavy volume of traffic, of mining products, and heavy goods. It can transport large amounts for a correspondingly small amount of energy, and this has meant vast savings for heavy industry. It was calculated that the cost of transporting one tonne's worth of goods over one mile rose by only 3 cents during the 1850's in America. Fifty years later, progress had reduced this rise to only one cent! Railways open up vast spaces for regular and safe transport. The Transsiberian railway, completed in 1916, stretches over 9300 kilometres of railway from Moscow to Vladivostok and it is indispensable for conveying wood and coal. A new Transiberian railway is currently being completed in the north. The large American transcontinental railways still survive, and new lines are being built in areas where mines are being developed away from rivers, like Australia, Brazil and Gabon.
Almost simultaneously, other functions emerge; the railways open up empty regions for habitation, transport people, and link up towns.
This represents the constitution of a network with tighter links. The population of the large "new" states was brought about by the railways; railway stations are now both centres of commerce and the heart of the human community. In Australia from 1860 to 1920 - during which time the railways underwent their greatest expansion - it was not only the economic framework of wool, wheat and mines which


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
was built up around the railways, but also new societies, which now had access to the distant ports. The older countries of Europe have gone even further than this. In France at the end of the 19th century, the "Freycinet Plan" was instituted, the objective of which was to provide the smallest village and canton with a railway station. This frenetic building of small regional lines ceased only with the coming of the First World War and the competition with the motor car, which the railways were eventually to lose. Although of debatable economic value, the railway network with such tight links was an outstanding agent in transforming rural societies.
In all industrial countries today, these networks are contracting, whilst cars and planes have largely taken over their clientele. Earlier, when they were managed by private interests, and then later when they became part of public services, the railways had to dispense with the less "profitable" lines. 17 ,000 kilometres of railway had already been closed off in France by 1970. In the United States over a period of a decade, half of the network disappeared, while the old stations were auctioned off, destined to join the ranks of historical monuments.
Electrification, however, and the possibility of reaching much higher sppeds of 200-300 kmh. with new materials, are both signs of renewal.
This is particularly significant for a few major routes, such as that of Paris-Lyon-Marseille in France, or Boston to Washington in the
United States, or the famous Tokkaido line in Japan, which is even beginning to compete with the airways.
Maritime transport has the oldest technical history, and it has seen the most daring of renewals. According to Douglas North, the tarrifs on maritime transport were reduced by half from 1850 to 1890 thanks to the introduction of the steam boiler. New, modernised ports and railways both participated in the establishment of new markets and new societies. The average distance over which agricultural goods were taken to London suddenly increased, and by the beginning of the
20th century that figure was 6000 miles. Heavier, cheaper materials were sent to factories on the opposite side of the world. Maurette's excellent little book on "the great raw materials markets," written between the two world wars, makes curious reading. It is noted, for example, that the iron ore markets hardly (Sweden and Algeria) strayed from western Europe, and Maurette saw this as long-lasting obstacle. But this was without allowing for the formidable upsurge in industrial activity and the new transformations in sea-transport.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
931 Some people have spoken of the "second youth" of mantime transport. The passenger liner tends now to disappear, made redun dant by air travel, and reserved only for tourist purposes, but new types of merchant ships have emerged suddenly since 1960. They are enormous, specialised, and automatised. What used to be called a super oil tanker is now merely a 50,000 tonne ancestor. For the tanker used in 1975 is enormous: it carries 300,000 tonnes more than the former, and is 400 metres long and with a draught of 25 metres. With each voyage, the new methane tankers transport 100,000 cubic metres of liquified gas, from Alaska to California, from Algeria to France, and from Brunei to Japan. The transport of general merchandise is also being revolutionised. The use of giant containers made of steel or plastic materials, mea quick transport from lorries or railway carriages. The traditional picture of maritime lines with many ports of call and detours, has now been replaced with a few major routes, which are connected with the land routes. A container service runs from
London to Japan with the compliments of the Transsiberian railway.
These costly routes have been organised from Leningrad to the Rhine and as far as Munich, and from Los Angeles to Birmingham, and ships hold a privileged place within them. The large ships which carry loaded barges mean that there are now direct links between the river ports of the Mississippi and those of the Rhine!
Vehicle traffic is one of the most characteristic signs of our technical epoch. The astonishing development of the car is best explained by the freedom of movement which it provides. Roads are accessible from just about any point, they lead to many other, there are no restrictions of timetables and delays, all of which are the lot of railway stations, ports and airports. The car has enabled us to realise a completely new way of covering space, quickly and freely. In the United States,
Europe and Japan, however, it soon became apparent that this freedom had its price: the car uses a great deal of energy and takes up a lot of space in order to transport only one or two people, seldom more.
The roads first built, even after being improved and enlarged, rapidly proved to be unable to support the growing volume of traffic. This gave rise to a large, specialised route, specifically designed to deal with larger amounts of traffic going at greater speeds: this was of course the motorway. Networks of motorways soon developed. France was a relative latecomer in this respect, but there is now a growing network from Paris to the Seine basin, from Paris to Belgium, and from Paris to


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Nice. New tentacles are added each year, but the less populated and poorer regions are still not catered for. The network in the United
States is already considerable. The motorway interchanges, like the railway stations before them, are becoming new centres of population and industrialisation. ( C. Barthe)
The network of air transport, was, ipso facto, something entirely new and without precedent. The scale and complex technology of aeroplanes meant that airlines automatically became major economic powers. The success of the plane is undisputed over long distances.
Between 1950 and 1970, the number of passengers travelling by plane increased ten-fold, and the distanced covered by air traffic by fifteen times. This also means that the average journey was longer. The reinforcement of the main international lines is the most characteristic aspect of this new network: it is possible to travel from every major city in the world, to any other major city in the space of a day. This has also meant a prodigious increase in size of "business space". When the large industrial firms are installing factories abroad, their main con cern is to locatethem as near as possible to an international airport. It is clear why l.B.M. established a factory in Nice, for example, and
Control-Data had similar priorities when they chose Ferney-Voltaire, which is near to Geneva airport. (Mazataud) But at the same time, the air network is consolidating itself by opening up national links. This is especially so in the vast states like the U.S.A, the U.S.S.R, Brazil and
Australia. Spain, whose railway network was lacking, also organised rapid air links between its main towns. In France, the Air Inter network has already linked some medium-sized towns. As with the motorway network, there is an economic obstacle which precludes very close links; planes are still much too expensive.
The networks for transmitting information are the latest addition to the global industrial framework. For a very long time, information was conveyed at the same pace as human transport. In 1799, for example, the citizens of New York only learnt about the death of George
Washington in Virginia a week after it happened. And in England, the news in the papers was three weeks old, while in China the gap was five months. Telegraphy, the telephone, telex, radio broadcasting, televi sion: all these have brought about almost instantaneous communi cation throughout the entire world. Communication now takes place not through cables crossing the sea, linking distant islands, but directly through the atmosphere. This new freedom in communications


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
933 inevitably favours the increasing homogeneity of the techniques and landscapes of the market. It is no doubt too soon to appreciate the full effects of this; we shall return to this subject later on when we examine the role of towns in this new network of information interchange.
Transport and communication have drawn new pioneering thresh olds, outposts of the world of techniques on the fringes of natural space. What does isolation mean today? Take for example this curious region in Australian Queensland, which is still unpopulated, but already extremely wealthy, with enormous ranches extending over
200,000 hectares! One can be fifty, or a hundred kilometres from the nearest tiny village, where one will find shops, post offices, a bank and a hotel. In these conditions, life depends on a good communication network: a telephone, a particular type of plane, powerful cars equal to the roadways, and television. Is Queensland isolated? In the technical sense, the answer must be no. But is it possible to conceive of any kind of social life - and in particular any kind of education - in these conditions?
In older countries, any introduction of a new network of transport inevitably changes patterns of life. Today, in the villages of Chad, a modern commercial function is emerging. Sautter has shown how, at
2000 kilometres from the nearest port, and without proper roads, commerce was literally impossible before the arrival of the lorry and the railway. Even though cotton was ginned and peanuts were shelled in the villages, there was no market for them. On the other hand, bricks and cement were brought in at prohibitive prices: "all the wealth was lost in transport costs" (Sautter).
Another example is the roads which have been built near Tromso in artic Norway since 1950. These opened up sudden new horizons for the small villages in the depths of the fjord. Until this time, the inhabitants of these villages accepted the tough life of fisher-peasants: they spent the winter on the sea at Lofoten, and the summer harvesting the hay in the village. The new situation dealt a fatal blow to the cod-fishing regions. A new economy of specialised dairy-farming emerged to meet the needs of the urban market in Tromso (Allesfrede ) .
This clearly represents the formation of a new type of geographical space. In the mountains of Peru near Cuzco, roads are being built and lorries are emerging. This is transforming the village - shops are being opened, and the old activities, such as milling and weaving are in decline. What is most striking is the new organisation of space which


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
ultimately favours Cuzco. This town is at the centre of an enlarged road network, and is monopolizing the major activities: wholesale commerce, hotels, and new offices of regional administration
(Brisseau-Loaiza). Was it not the same process, or almost, which changed the hierarchy of markets in western Europe? The progress in communications is "conjuring away" the smaller rural markets in favour of the less numerous, but more powerful central regions.
The Geographical Concentration of Equipment and Urbanisation The industrial revolution and the system which emerged from it have been defined in various ways, all of them fairly vague. One of the most interesting ideas was formulated by Hicks who gave priority to "the appearance on a massive scale of fixed capital." This is the real
"equipment" of the industrial system, the factories and the communi cations routes. It has to conform to a rule which is becoming more and more imperative, that of the "economy of scale" or the economy of dimension. The larger the factory, and the greater the capacity of the communication route, the cheaper the costs. This rule, which seemed only to govern a limited number of industrial activities, is now impos ing itself on more and more areas of techniques. Let us leave the exceptions aside for the time being. And let us note that the great industrial powers, be they American, Russian, or Japanese, are on the whole committed to large units of equipment.
Economies of scale, advanced and costly techniques, and the geo graphical concentration of equipment go hand in hand. This is a rule which seems to be in direct opposition to that of the geographical expansion of technical progress. One example will suffice to furnish this debate. Some of the French navigable routes, particularly the canals linking the Seine, the Loire and the Saone, are the products of an old technical epoch: they were designed to take barges of 180-300 tonnes or more, and drawing 2 metres of water. Today, the new techniques used in canal transport, self-propelled motors, the
"pushing" of convoys, all demand main routes. The European gauge is fixed for the moment, at 1350 tonnes, and it is this which is remodelling the river courses of the routes on the Rhine, the Seine and the Oise.
But the small canals in Burgundy and Nivernais are difficult to modernise; there is very little traffic on them because of their technical obsolescence, and equally, market studies have discouraged further


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
935 investment. A dividing line has therefore developed: if traffic falls below a million tonnes per annum, then the old canals are eventually given over to the craft barges, and for tourism. Progress has moved on to the bigger routes.
We know the "economy of scale" attained by the"large oil tankers.
Their sheer size constrains any harbour infrastructure. The notion of "super-port" imposes itself on an organism like Le Havre-Antifer, which is engaged on a never-ending course of investment: a sea wall of
3.5 kilometres, a channel of 25 metres, and this may go as deep as 30 metres if petrol tankers are made any bigger than 500,000 tonnes.
Modern ports are obliged to continually improve their transhipping equipment and the communication channels with the inland. If Le
Havre, Antwerp, Hamburg, Rotterdam and New York are able to survive in such fierce competition, it is because they have introduced three-fold modernisation: greater depths, better equipment, and larger communications routes with the hinterland. It is perhaps clearer now why the maritime life of France rests on three giant ports: the
Lower Seine, Marseille-Fos, and Dunkirk. And why more than two thirds of world traffic operates in some 65 ports: 45 of which are in
Europe, 12 in North America, and 8 in Japan! The poorer countries are not exempt from this rule, to the contrary: in Morocco, three quarters of the traffic and most of the large investment for the future is concentrated in the Casablanca-Fedala complex. As for Rotterdam, the busiest port in the world, it is not far from monopolising a tenth of the total world tonnage - with only 15 ,000 dockers working there, but thousands of millions of florins worth of facilities.
Airports are probably too recent a development, and their commer cial function not yet sufficiently developed for us to make a clear assessment of their concentration. However the same tendencies seem to be at work, with the gigantic size of apparatuses, the enormous routes, and the exorbitant costs of control equipment. The new airport in Paris-Roissy cost 1,700 million francs, it covers 30 square kilome tres, and required the collaboration of five hundred different industrial and service enterprises for its realisation. The recently-built airport at
Dallas-Fort Worth is twice as big, and also cost twice as much to build:
here the interior network of communications and computers is more refined. Only a few dozen of the large airports, in the most dense and richest areas of the world, could envisage such works. In western
Europe:
London, Paris, Frankfurt, Zurich, Rome, perhaps


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Amsterdam and Copenhagen, or one large airport per state! Labasse's formula is easy to appreciate: "international airports makes urban success complete."
The size of the factories of heavy industry are increasing constantly.
In some twenty years, energy plants have crossed "three technological levels" and their capacity has increased by 400% (125, 250, then 500 megawatts). The chimneys of the most recently-built thermal power plants in France, near Le Havre and Paris, are 300 metres high. The
Alpine aluminium factory of the 1910's was designed to deal with a few tens of thousands of tons per annum. These have now become obsolete in such impractical surroundings. The old iron and steel plant, fash ioned by the Thomas and Gilchrist processes, are now outmoded. The new techniques of oxidising steel and the continuous laminating of steel require enormous investment. They lead automatically to the integrated factory, and these are only considered profitable if they are producing more than five million tonnes of steel per annum. These new units are taking over new sites on the main routes of communication and in the ports: in France, the Moselle valley which has been canalized on a large scale, Dunkirk and Fos-Marseille. The iron and steel complex in Gary-Chicago used to be the most powerful in the world - 9 million tonnes of steel today - but it must now take second place to the Russian achievments at Krivoi-Rog and Magnitogorsk, and that of the Japanese in Fukuyama.
Wickham has spoken of the "increasing scarcity of large factories" in a large industrial area. The large chemical factories making ethylene and plastic materials produce millions of tonnes per annum. This has meant that the parcelling of labour in small units has been replaced with one giant, continuous, and automatised operation. The rule of the economy of scale has produced its effects in every sphere. Car manu facturers, in Detroit first, then throughout the world, soon adopted the principle of highly standardised production lines: they can no longer operate on a small scale. In France, it is clear that factories like Flins in the Parisian suburbs, or Sandouville in that of Le Havre, Sochaux
Montbeliard etc., can no longer be built anywhere but in fairly dense agglomerations situated near large communication routes.
The large city is indissolubly linked to technical progress. It generates progress, and puts the main innovations into practice. At the same time, it is constantly being remodelled by this progress. The town represents the essence of artifice: asphalt, concrete and steel. Even the


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
937 grassy areas and the canalised river between the embankments no longer have anything natural about them. A "showcase" of progress - as Burgel remarked of contemporary Athens - the town attracts first and foremost innovation.
A large city like Paris demonstrates this accumulative process. The newest branches of activity and society which are generated by this are constantly increasing. Paris contains less than 20% of the entire French population, but it nonetheless houses a third of the large industrial and commercial concerns, it has more than half of the country's computers, and almost two-thirds of the country's research is conducted there.
This proportion is even greater if one considers the social centres of the large enterprises. The people who are attracted to Paris with prospects of work are the best clientele for the modern technology. It is the richest part of society - it has been estimated that the average income of Parisians in comparison to the French average is 140 to 100.
Parisians consume more industrial products, they use more services, more means of transport, and more telephonic communication than any other groups. The very difficulty of life in large cities maintains this level of consumption.
With a few slight variations, the attraction afforded by large cities is a worldwide phenomenon. Allan Gilbert has shown in his studies of
Colombia that industrialists and businessmen prefer to establish new factories and offices in Bogota, rather than in the smaller regional centres like Medellin, Cali and Baranquilla, or especially those small towns with no infrastructure. The economic base offered by Bogota is already richer and more diverse than elsewhere. And the population which is coming to swell the ranks of the new districts in the capital finds, or hopes to find, a bigger and more diverse labour market.
The "congestion" in towns dates back a long way. It is true that technical progress is adding a new dimension to the problems of congestion, and the saturation of available space. The first paradox:
communications, which was at the basis of urban development, is too powerful today to be integrated harmoniously.
We find ourselves back with the large ports at this point. Their process of development, as formulated by Bird, clearly illustrates a progressive dissociation of the port from the city-centre. Originally, the two were one and the same. Then the quays began to be built laterally, or on the opposite bank of an estuary. Then came the modern basins, linked to the railway stations in the suburbs. More recently,


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
outer harbours have been built in deeper waters with specialised docks and new industrial zones. Marseille, Genoa, London, Liverpool, and many other large ports, are experiencing great difficulty today in liaising between the original nucleus of activity, and the port instal lations which are several kilometres away. The most extraordinary maritime extension of the old central cities is no doubt that on the
Japanese coast between Kobe and Osaka, which is a form of "industrial polder" gaining on the sea.
The same paradox is true of the airports. They need to stay as close as possible to the city-centres, but in order to maintain their function, they have to move away from them. This is because the enormous runways can only be built in the surrounding areas, but also because the noise and the danger of these large apparatuses. Roissy has replaced Orly, and Heathrow airport, which is now saturated, is calling out for another large airport which can only be built some hundred kilometres away from London. Greater New York is a constellation of airports. And everywhere the main problem consists of getting across cities and suburbs to catch the plane.
Clearly, new types of transport are forcing cities to lose their old identity, and in this respect the role of the car is a decisive one. Even after the railways were built, the old cities centres remained quite distinct, and they often assumed the shape of a star, each new branch forming along the railways or tramlines. The car meant that all the interstices were now accessible and could be filled. A kind of "sheet" urbanisation is taking place, making access to the centre more and more difficult each day. The phenomenon is very advanced in the
United States where enormous suburbs contrast with curious "city centres": the towering office blocks and striking architecture are mixed up with the poorest and most degrading of dwellings.
All these processes result in spatial extension and a redistribution of activities in cities, which have become "urban agglomeration."
Industry too, is seeking refuge further and further away from the saturated centres. New zones specially created for industry and known as "industrial parks," are springing up in the distant suburbs. The so-called "route 128" phenomenon in Boston, where some fifteen miles from the centre, new factories, laboratories, and research insti tutes are grouped around the peripheral motorway, is not an isolated case. The same tendencies are apparent in Chicago, Paris, Tokyo and
Moscow. This represents the "loosening" of the urban organism.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
939 But on the larger national scale, the strong zones - we can no longer call them strong points - are highly concentrated. In the United States, space is being organised around a hundred or so urban regions, which can be termed "daily urban systems." Their centres are cities and their suburbs, or several hundred thousands of inhabitants - which ensures the diversified industrial base, the modern services, airports, local television, etc. Around the centre, the modern means of transport make it possible to define a urbanised zone, the theatre of daily migration to and from work, service and leisure. The urbanised zone is usually of a radius of about a hundred kilometres or more. Is this not a fairly representative model in the world as a whole? It clearly expresses the saturation of space in the traditional town, but it does not imply
"de-urbanisation" - on the contrary.
This is the ultimate paradox of urban regions in the face of technical progress. It was hoped that the new technologies - cars, electricity
-ould disperse human activity towards the smaller towns, and even into the country. And today the same is expected of the most advanced communication techniques. But any such developments are slow in forthcoming. The American geographer Bryan Berry's forecast on
"the United States in the year 2000" is a tempting one: long-distance information will be so refined, with the telephone-television and holograms, that there will be a definitive movement of activity away from the large cities. A rapid transformation is doubtful, however
(Berry).
If cities continue to exercise such an attraction, it is no doubt because they are the most effective type of control post, one which can ensure the liaison between all types of techniques: management, transactions and adminstration. Technical progress is moving so fast that there has never been such a need as now for concrete meetings and personal contacts. Factories and dwellings may be freer in terms of their localisation than previously, but the most important "tertiary" functions are still performed by cities. Labasse reminds us of this in his latest work. Financial areas are still very concentrated. Doubly so, because on the world market, London, New York, Sao Paulo, Tokyo,
Paris and Frankfurt are still the leading centres. Two-thirds of the financial power of Canada is concentrated in Toronto and Montreal.
Paris, which holds the record, holds nine-tenths of the finances of France. On the smaller scale of the main cities, the banking regions and those of big business are still in the old city-centres, like Manhat-


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
tan, and the City of London, for example. "The directors need to be in constant touch with other directors." The eldest and the least visible of the techniques of the industrial system, the bank, is still attached to a few prestigious areas.
THE GEOGRAPHICAL INEQUALITY
OF TECHNICAL PROGRESS
Despite the indisputable expansion of the industrial system, geogra phy always restores the variety, the mixtures, the contrasts. Las Vegas is an integral product of technology today, and at the other end of the spectrum there is the mountain village of Cameroon, for example, which is just beginning to use a few industrial tools. Most often the geographical landscape is hybrid. Before landing at the large tourist airport of Palma in Majorca, one flies over beautiful, but antiquated windmills from the last century. The market held on the water at
Bangkok, and the souk at Fez still exist, and both are attempting to function as something other than simply a relic destined to excite tourists' curiosity. Industrial energy is available everywhere, but an
American "consumes" 8000 "coal-equivalent" kilogrammes each year, while a Nigerian, whose is by no means the most deprived, consumes 200 of such kilos. The road network in Belgium is three hundred times denser - and of a better quality - than those of Chad or
Ethiopia.
Geographical Environments and Innovation The problem of the exact role played by the geographical environment is a difficult one to solve, and is one which is relevant to every period, and to every region. The following paragraph is taken from Endrei, a historian of the techniques used in the textile industry:
How do we explain the development of techniques in some regions, and their stagnation in others? What are the reasons for the advances and delays? ( . . . ) Going beyond the techniques which were becoming fixed in the East, Europe, during the following centuries, remained constantly at the spearhead of progress.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
941 A number of works which are not exampt from a certain "ethno centrism" in glorifying the inventive mind - particularly that of con temporary Europe - emphasise the cases of free innovation. The relevance of the geographical context extends only so far as the birthplace of the entrepreneur. The new technique is incorporated quite simply in the same place where the idea originated. We should not reject such a perspective, but allow that it is insufficient. It is true enough for example, that Riisselheim, between Mainz and Frankfurt, might still be a small town, if Adam Opel had not been born there, and, by the second half of the 19th century, had not capitalised on the progress made in mechanical industry (sewing machines, then bicy cles, then cars). Where would Clermont-Ferrand be without the
"windfall" which brought to the town the Michelin brothers, the pioneers of the early tyre industry? The small town of Mazamet, in
Tarn, still survives on the idea of an entrepreneur called Houles. This was in 1860, and the idea consisted of buying sheepskins "in wool," which only certain Argentinians and Australians knew how to do. An extremely specialised industry grew up from this, the fellmongering of skins brought prosperity to Mazamet. As for the United States, some people have said that Henry Ford made Detroit, and that the landing there of the Du Pont de Nemours family explains the great chemical industry at Wilmington, etc.
The issue gets slightly more complicated when the innovation is brought in by new arrivals. Then the origin of the latter is emphasised.
The name for New England was a fitting one, at least originally.
Samuel Slater who worked with Arkwright in Nottingham, established the first cotton factories in America, using identical models.
Sometimes this line of enquiry seems to be a somewhat difficult one.
There are very few industrial towns in Uruguay today, with one exception: Paysandu. This town is bustling with factories producing leather, wool and agricultural canned food; it owes its livelihood to a few immigrant families from industrial regions in Italy and France
( Collin-Delavaud).
Progress cannot be explained simply in terms of individual entre preneurs. It is more a matter of encounters. Take Glasgow for example, a major shipbuilding region. At the end of the 18th century, sailing ships were being built all along the Clyde estuary, but this was not the port's main role, which was essentially trading with America.
Under exactly what conditions did the shipwrights (like the Wood


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
enterprise) meet the manufacturers of boilers and "fire engines" (like Napier)? The first steam ships were built in Glasgow, and a modest link was forged with Belfast in 1818. A modern centre of activity was thus created which stimulated new enterprises. How did a second encoun ter take place sixty years later between those in the shipyards and the steel manufacturers? In 1879, the first ship made of steel left the Clyde shipyards. Glasgow then began to attract powerful iron and steel manufacturers - particularly from Sheffield - which reinforced the centre of innovation. (Miller)
Gabert has recognised the role of one enterprising man, Giovanni Agnelli, in the rise of the Turin motor industry. He was certainly an original character: "an officer with no taste for the army", and it was he who created the F.l.A.T. But at the same time, he cannot be isolated from some other young men of Turin, the product of the young bourgeoisie who were "passionate about racing cars". Neither can he be separated from Turin's industrial tradition. The railway workshops and arsenals of the Piedmontese capital had maintained the art of metal bodywork, and these skilled workers offered their services to
F.l.A.T. To sum up then, the geographical environment of Turin in the 1900's and its urban history both evidently participated in inno vation.
The accumulation and the encounter of techniques is only one part of innovation. In the space of one human generation, it seems that
Grenoble, in the northern French Alps, was able to gather together the main entrepreneurs and technicians whose efforts were to culminate in the hydro-electric plant: there was Berge who adapted the turbine for use with high chutes to be used in paper mills, then Desprez who succeeded in transporting the current along short lines, and Heroult who constructed the first electric furnaces, etc. But Labasse reminds us that the close-knit network of regional banks played a decisive role in pumping capital towards the first enterprises. Still in the same region, the modern town of Annecy owes its first large-scale plants to the encounter between an electrical engineer, Barut, and a banker, Lay dernier, both of whom are no longer remembered today, but who
"launched" Annecy on the way to becoming the town it is today: a city of tourism and factories manufacturing high quality goods.
A centre of innovation can only remain so if it prepared to accept profound changes. Manchester, a real prototype of the "first industrial revolution", succeeded in taking the lead in the world cotton industry.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
943 All the elements of a long prosperity seemed to be there: coal deposits near the factories, a dense railway network, and a rational division of labour between spinning, weaving and trade. Today, the last mining shafts are closing, cotton is difficult to sell, and the mechanical and electrical industries have taken over. This necessary renewal of activity had been prepared for, perhaps vaguely. When the gigantic canal was built to make a seaport of Manchester in 1894, was this only to stop
Liverpool supervising its imports? Was it the apogee of a long period of prosperity, or the first awareness of the problems of reconversion? The town and the chamber of commerce still support this costly industrial plant. The new port rapidly became coupled with a new industrial zone:
Trafford Park. New industries installed themselves before the real crisis in the cotton industry, such as the electrical firm, Westinghouse, who by
1900 had become the brand image of the new Manchester.
It is clear that only social and economic history in association with geography, can enable us to shed some light on the mystery of technical renewals in a given context. Dalmasso's fine study of Milan presents us with the image of a large city with international influence, which is constantly evolving. Milan's geographical situation is no doubt a useful one, but being at the centre of a crossroads is no good without the industrial base. In 1882 and 1906, despite enormous difficulty, the alpine passes of Saint-Gothard and Simplon were both provided with railways. Milan also managed to place itself at the centre of a large intersection of motorways. The airports at Linate and
Malpense, and the modernised canals leading to the river Po, have added to a work which however, is unlikely to ever be completed: "the
Milanese reply to each technical evolution with the adaptation of the old transport system, and the formation of a new network." Milan had the same capacity for renewal in industrial terms. The medieval town's fortune had been made by the silk trade, but when this declined, relief was provided by the most "modern" textiles, like cotton, and espe cially artificial ones. And today, the old tradition of arms trading is embedded in a constellation of mechanics factories. From one branch to another, the enterprises "divert" in order to progress. One firm went from producing cotton to making weaving looms, and then from general mechanics to turbines. It is the privilege of these large centres of power to be able to effect such transformations without much difficulty: identical examples to those of Manchester would be found in
Lyon, for example.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
The Milanese centre thrives on its own enterprise and innovation, and a number of the big companies are typically Milanase, such as
Pirelli and Alfa-Romeo. Added to this is a capacity for remaining open and receptive to new initiatives. The Gothard rail company is the fruit of Swiss combining with German interests. The Edison company has been in Milan for so long (since 1884), that it is now an integral part of the early history of Lombard electricity. Today, the welcoming of large
American companies seems to be the natural course for a geographical centre which has deliberately sought international status (Dalmasso).
Collective enterprise does not always lead towns and regions to the same heights. L. Papy was inspired by Bordeaux to formulate the idea of "lost chances." "Bordeaux launched itself into industrial enterprise only with caution." Was this because it very quickly became a town of the landed bourgoisie, which was mainly concerned with its wine growing regions? During the 18th century, however, the apogee of ports and towns, Bordeaux introduced a vast number of innovations in colonial trade; sugar and coffee trading was undertaken with the West
Indies, for example. But then what? Bordeaux let the industrial revolution drift by without reaping any profit from it. This undoub tedly reflects a kind of social behaviour which still exists in that beautiful town of the Gironde (Papy). Certain societies seem to lend themselves to some activities while closing themselves to others.
A more dramatic aspect are the cases of decline. Paimpol (Britanny) was an innovatory town during the time of the great traditional fishing industry in Iceland, but then it proved unequal to equipping trawlers, a necessary step for modernisation; there was probably a lack in both initiative and capital. Beaucaire, on the lower Rhone, was one of the great commercial centres of southern France until the beginning of the
19th century. Was it the new conditions of maritime and continental transport which brought about its decline? Or was it also the continued reliance on annual fairs, an already outmoded trading technique?
There seemed to be no possible means of reconversion for that small town of Languedoc. The list of such disappearances from the map of
Europe would be a long one.
All we have discussed above indicates the importances of regional environments in the process of technical innovation. But is this not already out of date? Those parts of Europe which have provided us with so many examples from the 19th century are in fact small, disparate regions. Today, as we have already seen, the industrial


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
945 system has other requirements and dimensions. The development of the Saint-Laurent is out of all proportion with the Manchester canal. It is the product of multiple perspectives on an international scale: the enormous demand for electricity in Canada and the United States, the exploitation of the mining wealth of Labrador. . . Montreal and Chi cago, two large cities both interested in the project, were only able to overcome the resistance from New York when all the international conditions were fulfilled. The scale of things is no longer the same as it once was.
Technical innovation is mainly the deed of powerful companies and states. The geographical apportioning of patents of invention reveal that the creative countries - as far as industry is concerned - represent only a small number. Around 100,000 patents are filed each year by the
U.S.S.R and Japan. A smaller amount are filed by the United States.
In Europe, West Germany is by far the most active. The United Kingdom and France (around 35,000 patents per year each) seem to have lost some of their capacity for innovation. Some countries' place in the list is out of all proportion to their geographical status; like the
Netherlands and Switzerland, for example, who both file about 25 ,000 patents each. It is those countries equipped with a scientific and technical apparatus who are able to systemically innovate. Research and development in the United States is provided each year with about
30,000 million dollars, half of which is provided by the state, half coming from private sources.
It is no doubt the international character of innovations which is changing most quickly. All industrial countries file patents in other countries. A veritable competition has grown up to export industrial ideas, obtain manufacturing licences and create subsidiary firms abroad. American technology is in this sense the great colonising power today, moving into Canada, Europe and the poorer countries.
Some countries seem to have fallen by the wayside as far as this race is concerned. Argentine, which used to be considered as an industrial country, is one example of this. Today, the car industry, and "leading technologies" are making Argentina more and more dependent on
American and European engineers.
The example of Japan, often invoked to demonstrate that it is possible to progress from poverty to high technology, is an exceptional one. It has taken more than a hundred years to buy the industrial techniques, and absorb and improve them - and not, as some people


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
have rashly judged, to simply copy them. Recently, this effort has borne all its fruit and Japan is now the vendor of advanced techniques:
fish-rearing, medecines, protein manufacture, fast electric trains, and not to mention the "antipollution industries" so important in Japan itself. All this rests on the modern research conducted by the major companies, by the universities, and by the state. And furthermore on the capacity of the consumers to absorb a new product more quickly than anywhere else in the world; it takes eight to ten years to arrive at a certain level of household equipment in refrigerators, or television sets in Europe, for example, while the same process takes just four to five years in Japan. Today, it is quite clearly national behaviour as a whole which governs the landscapes of technical progress.
THE RESISTANCE OF "CRAFT"
INDUSTRIES - AGRICULTURE
The geographical expansion of high technology, of the main communi cations networks, and of the industrial system is incontestable.
Surrounding this, however, is a world of ancient activities and ancient establishments, which does not conform easily to the requirements of the system. For want of a better appellation, we have called this the craft world.
Large-scale industry cannot perform all the functions of production, and it is not in its interests to do so. This may explain the persistance of craft-type industries on the fringes of the car, electric, and electronic industries, etc. These are small workshops which produce accessories and special parts. Traditional trades, the product of "ways of life," still get orders from the most powerful companies today. "Industrial joinery," "industrial locksmithing," "metal-cutting" are all industries which can function in a position of subcontracting. The repair industry also provides refuge for a resurgent cottage industry.
Some small regions of Europe still have the facies of dispersed industries which need very little capital. The Arve valley in Haute
Savoie is characteristic of this: the small cutting workshops, thriving on the activities of minor patrons and craftsmen, still reign in the area around Cluses and also in the smallest towns. They supply orders from


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
947 the large car industry in Paris. They are the product of a successful evolution of the cottage industries which has its roots in domestic dock-making. The work was done primarily for Geneva, then for oneself. Now it is done for Paris. Somewhat more surprising is the success of the German Black Forest region in developing even further the industrialisation of old craft industries. This is a rugged envir onment, at 700 metres altitude and far from any main towns- Stuttgart is one hundred kilometres away. It is the small villages, like Villingen, which accomodate the companies renowned in the television and record industries. Originally, the main craft practised was watch making, and it is still the same families as in the 18th century who do most of the manufacturing, although now they have become small, high quality factories. The decision-making is still done largely on the local level, although the inevitable links with the main international industrial system are already emerging. This is a remarkable example of geographical persistance and advanced technological evolution; it is fairly exceptional.
The "workforce" industries still persist in many sectors. As soon as the share of salaries in final production costs reach 60% - or even 80% in the clothing or luxury industries like cristalware or jewellry - the old
"economies of scale" of the industrial system hardly come into play, and the workshop can persist in its traditional localisations. The
Sentier district of Paris still houses many artisans, and the constant changes in fashion ultimately favour the craftsman's "savoir-faire". In the same way, London and New York both have their districts where tailors and workers work at home, as in the past. The impersonal network of large industry is ill-suited here. Not only does the worker count for a great deal in production, but the client also demands close personnel contact with the production process. Despite a rapid evolu tion towards massive industrialisation, printing, for example, still has the flavour of a craft industry.
Artistic crafts are far from disappearing, and they thrive in particu lar on the most modern tourist forms. From the piazza della Signoria to the Ponte Vecchio in Florence, most of the embroidered cloths, or the objects inlaid with wood or leather displayed in the shop windows, are the work of veritable artisans. The districts where they work, like San
Fediano and Santa Croce, are less well known, and it is usually forgotten that most villages of Tuscany are still part of the old cottage industry system. All these objects are produced for the most modern


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
and wealthiest of customers, from London, and the large American cities (Charrier).
For a number of industrial branches, it would be worthwhile distinguishing those elements which rely principally on massive pro duction, and those which depend on a specialist knowledge. On the whole, the glass industries, for example, tend to comply with the geographical system of large-scale, concentrated industry, in places like Liege, Essen and Pittsburgh. But this has not wiped out the more traditional areas specialising in high quality glass, like Jena in Ger many, or Baccarat in France. There are other citadels of traditional artisans in France - in Thiers, (cutlery), and Saint-Junien and Millau,
(glove-making). They are not easily included in the industrial catego ries of our times, but they still persist nonetheless. In Tuscany, the old town of Prato has been practising a somewhat curious textile trade for centuries, and still does so today: wool is spun and woven from old rags and clothes taken from all over the world. These non-conformist techniques, evolving from a long tradition of specialist artisan prac tices, retain something of the past.
For how long, we may ask. The large-scale chemical industry is gradually insinuating itself into the old craft industry of perfumery. In
France, Grasse was the main centre for perfumers who knew how to treat mediterranean flowers in order to extract their natural essences
(rose, jasmin, lavander, carnation, etc). The inevitable appearance of chemical products in the perfume industry brought about a new framework: the large factories in the suburbs of Lyons and Paris took their place in a circuit which led to the most exclusive shops in the centre of Paris but also to the self-service shops in large shopping centres (Di Meo). The future of the craft of perfumery depends ultimately on the consumers' tastes, and on their ability to buy
"special" and costly products.
Is fishing, an age-old activity, still a type of artisan practice? Or is it already an industry? The various aspects of both are clearly visible on the shores of all the world.
The role of the fishing industry is constantly increasing, and its success comes from its technical effectiveness and its scientific basis.
The scale of production is beyond comparison: a factory-trawler can treat 5000 tonnes of fish per year. In Japan and the U .S.S.R, enormous funds are pumped in scientific and technical research, and as a result they are constantly finding new ways of increasing the yield. A large


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
949 English company like Associated Fisheries has about a hundred modern ships used in connection with a network of ports (Hull,
Grimsby, Fleetwood), each of which are connected by fast and effi cient trade routes to the consumer regions. This is the very image of the industrial network, forming a long chain of modern technology:
radars, trawlers, cold chains, and trains coinciding with the tides, etc.
The yield of the system depends especially on its ability to ensure prospection in new zones like Greenland, Peru, and the African coasts, all of which have been colonised by the fishing industry. In a few years, Callao will have become one of the great fishing ports of the world, because it is the integral product of the new sea industries; this is a phenomenon comparable with mining "exploitation," with the same successes and the same excesses.
The more traditional fishing industry still subsists on most of the tropical shores of Asia and Africa, however. But it is limited by the effectiveness of the processes and instruments used: fishhooks, traps, and nets change only very slowly. In some places, nylon nets are now being used, in others, the first engines. The boats used are still the small sailing boats, and they rarely venture out onto the open sea.
Traditional fishing in Europe is still in evidence, witness the spectacle of the small Norwegian, Breton and Portugese ports in particular.
Statistics would class as traditional all those areas where small sailing boats are used; The dividing line is somewhat arbitrary, fixed for example at 50 tonnes. Fishing practices are more obviously traditional where the fishermen still cures the fish; this is true in northern Norway and in the Lofoten islands, where the old techniques of drying the cod are still unchanged. As for industry, there are still some refuges for traditional fishing, like lobster catching for example, which is not so easily industrialised as catching fish. This tends to sustain a traditional colour in some of the Breton ports, for example, like Douarnenez and
Camaret. The slow process of oyster rearing, culminating in a luxury product, is even more a preservator of old traditional landscapes
(Oleron, Arcachon).
The encounter of industrial with traditional fishing practises is therefore a phenomenon of great interest for the geographer. Has the unexpected eruption of large boats chartered by the Japanese in the ports of Madagascar suppressed the small local fishing industry? This is not very likely because industrial fishing is interested only in certain types of fish, like prawns for example, and the new system is accepted


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
in certain ports only if it is likely to encourage the local industry, like in Majunga for example. Mediterranean France has been experiencing this contact between local fishing and the larger industry since 1960:
the innovators came from Algeria, bringing with them new techniques just at the time when there was a general need for modernisation. The results of this varied, however. Port-Vendres, Sete, and Marseille evolved quite quickly, but the smaller Provern;al ports, and especially those in Corsica, remained unchanged. Could it be that this is for very particular reasons, such as the special requirements of the tourists? (J.
Besanc;on).
Agriculture provides a livelihood for hundreds of millions of people on the fringes, or even outside of the industrial system. It is one outstanding exception to the principle of rapid industrialisation, and in that sense is profoundly original.
Agricultural techniques are concerned with the earth and the living environment; it is this which separates them from all other techniques.
Not only do they occupy soils and locations, but they also make use of the earth which for a long time remained the major means of produc tion, and which represents much more than this after the fruits of labour and development. The products of agriculture are not common objects, but living entities. Plants and animals are all subjected, in different ways, to the risks of growth, illness and death. They cannot escape the hazards of weather: cold, heat, dryness and humidity are all strong factors in agricultural production, and in both its quantitative and qualitative irregularities. Finally, the "quality" of food products remains difficult to define: unlike other industries, it is more a question of taste and custom... If this could be summarised in two words, perhaps it would be "the land"?
When one evokes the "technical backwardness" of agriculture - which is very real-it should be borne in mind just what is specific to the techniques of the land. All farmers have learnt to create a certain equilibrium of production which is autonomous, and realised using only the means existing on the land itself: labour, the animals and plants, water, and the earth itself. It is by an original combination of means that each piece of land can be recompensed for the inevitable loss of fertility after each harvest. And this equilibrium can manage without the industrial system completely.
The most "extensive" traditional techniques produce very small yields, but use up vast areas of land. The land has reserves of fertility to


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
951 where the animals and the fields themselves can move. This is an elementary and sometimes primitive form of equilibrium, which cha racterises well the land of tropical Africa and America. Often, a small part of the land is well cultivated, and this contrasts with a surrounding area which is almost "natural." Geographers have described this arrangement in Senegal and the Sudan, which is centred around the
"hut fields," and where most of the work and manuring is done. These fields continue into an area of temporary land, or "bush fields," which are occasionaly cleared when the need arises. The same distinction is made in India, in Uttar Pradesh, where there are manured fields
(goind) and virgin fields (barha) . In Spanish New-Castille the same schema exists which leads us from the large village surrounded by fields of wheat to the olive groves on the hills, then to the enormous monte on which the sheep graze before being taken down to the centre of the land to fertilise it. Pastoral life in the Alps, which combines the valley meadows with the high mountain pastures, represents in many forms the same search for an equilibrium with the same small yield. And although the enormous ranches of Latin America were born of quite different conditions, being the product of commercial speculation, they nonetheless remind us that agriculture can produce without anything being bought from the outside.
Traditional "intensive" techniques offer another pathway, but with many branches. The agricultural practices of the peasantry of the Far
East are in fact more like gardening than anything else. The small parcels of land carefully arranged in paddy-fields retain their fertility thanks to the meticulous control of water and silt. In the same way, traditional farming in Italy (in Umbria, Tuscany, Venice and on the slopes of Vesuvius) is based on the extraordinary accumulation of human labour: the development of parcels of land in terraces, the construction of ditches and canals to drain or irrigate the soil, the planting of fruit trees and vine-bearing trees, etc. The label of tradi tional agriculture seems pefectly suitable, here. But beyond this, the aesthetic value of these Tuscan landscapes is unquestionable: it is the long-established bet paessaggio, which today is being gradually destroyed by machines. (Sereni).
Traditional farming in western Europe - particularly in Germany, Switzerland and France - retains a strong peasant character: land is still divided in to small developments and small parcels, and most of the work is still done manually. There is certainly a less precarious


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
equilibrium between mixed farming and rearing, and subsistance and trade. But we should keep in mind the relative significance of words, like the "agricultural revolution" of the 18th and 19th centuries, for example, which often represented nothing more than an improvement to traditional crop rotation; fodder plants were increased, fallowing land was eliminated or reduced, and most of the space was given to livestock. All this can be described as intensified small-scale farming. Those regions and nations where the peasantry have completely disappeared are very rare. "High farming" is characterised ultimately by Britain and its colonial extensions into new lands. Peasant techniques on the land remain characteristic of a large part of Europe.
It is in this context that we should place the real, very recent revolution in agriculture, that of industrialisation. Machines and engines are taking over the land, and forcing farmers to rethink the old work norms. The parcels worked by peasants (oeuvre, journal, acre, etc.) much smaller than a hectare, lose all significance. The result of this is a selection of new land which is designed for machines, divided into vast, geometric and flat parcels. Furthermore, mechanical equip ment is the result of investment: the tractor "revolution" would have no sense if other machines did not follow. Combine-harvesters, fodder presses, milking machines, electrified folds, and all the interior fittings of a modern farm, like prefabricated buildings, water and energy networks, are all now supplied by industry.
The chemical industries and those of human and plant biology appear to have even more revolutionary consequences for agricultural lands. The extensive use of fertilisers, insecticides and pesticides is completely transforming the old local balances discussed above. Pro duction can now be increased with massive purchases from industry.
New species of plants and breeds of animals are no longer the fruit of local initiative, but the result of scientific research conducted by large government bodies, and major industrial companies.
It is obvious that an industrial agriculture is emerging, which works with the methods and means of the industrial system. The large-scale, completely mechanised cultivation of cereals, requiring only one person for hundred hectares or more, is actual practice on the large plains of the U.S.A. and Canada. True industrial farming, in a controlled environment, is no longer confined simply to ftoriculture.
By using glass, plastic materials and oil, and at the price of a very heavy


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
953 investment per square metre, it is now possible to produce vegetables and fruit which ignore the seasonal rhythms and which grow in vast quantities, even if they are not of such a good quality. Rearing, itself, which seemed to exclude such practices, is also being industrialised.
There is a process now which began in the United States and is now moving to the larger European towns, of installing somewhat bizarre shelters where rearing is completely controlled by the industrial system: the appropriate genetic breed, a rigorous timetable of food and products prepared by industry, and a climatised environment.
Here, the land has completly disappeared.
It is also apparent that the development of such forms is no longer dependent in fact on the farmer. It is the industrial and commercial firms which find themselves both "upstream" and "downstream" of the principal factors in this transformation of agriculture. On the vertical plane: tractors, irrigation apparatuses, various types of machinery, fertilisers, livestock fodder. On the horizontal plane: the large commercial areas and food industries which need a regular and homogenous group of products to transform, mix, prepare and present to their consumers. The industrial system of the United States and that of the large urban regions of Europe thus generate the progress of an industrialised agriculture, which is held in a network of contracts, effective and sure of its modernity.
We need only analyse the concrete life of the world's agricultural regions, however, (even that of the United States), to find that traditional artisan structures still resist such change. They are still clearly in evidence in the giant kolkhozes of the U.S.S.R, where the familial "enclosure" is still the theatre of a whole host of small types of production - milk, fruit, pork - and these enclosures produce about a fifth of the entire agriculture produce of the Soviet Union! In western
Europe, the peasantry is incorporating only with caution into the new system; they have adopted the industrial henhouse as an extra system, for example, but they still adhere in the main to the system of classical mixed-farming. This is true in Brittany, Holland and Denmark. Even though the consumers themselves are engaged in a system of mass consumerism, they still favour the persistance of the traditional artisan system, setting great store by its products, like fine wines, "free-range" poultry, and milk "straight from the cow". A general theory of technical progress in quite clearly more difficult to establish in the rural environment than in any other.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
TECHNICAL PROGRESS
AND THE POORER COUNTRIES
Technical progress is all-pervasive, even in the poorest of the "devel oping" countries. There is a wealth of literature devoted to this diffusion, and on the obstacles it encounters. The best starting point for any consideration of this subject are the monographs written about established vilages by ethnologists, sociologists, and geographers.
Only these can enable us to understand at which point the industrial system is first perceived, then accepted as foreign.
In many villages in Africa, for example, the commercial farming of coffee, cocoa, cotton and peanuts was imposed by the colonial adminstrations. In the hinterland of Abidjan, it took more than one generation before the production of coffee, which was initially synony mous with imports, was completely adopted and appreciated by the villagers. In the Bassa countries of Cameroon, one village retained its gatherer economy based on palm oil, whilst it was being strongly urged to develop cocoa plantations, furthermore, it actually had a railway station on the Yaounde-Douala line: the most obvious reason for this is probably a passive resistance to colonisation.
Some ethnic groups are more prepared than others for the opening of their countries. South-east Asia has thousands of examples of the
