Chinese' astonishing adoption of modernised agriculture - hevea, and commercial gardens - but also of the withdrawal of the Malays.
Pelissier has shown how in Senegal, the Serers are undoubtedly the better farmers, with strong attachments to their lands, but because of this they are less receptive to innovation than the Ouolofs, who are more mobile and assimilating.
Similarly, some village societies resist innovation more than others.
Ottino recounts the social process which eventually condemned to failure an apparently reasonable project, on the banks of lake Alaotra in Madagascar. The agricultural technician proposed drying up the marshes, extending the paddy-fields, and creating gardens. To the surprise of the administration, this project was rejected on the grounds that there was not enough rice, or labour. It sooned turned out that the opposition was coming from a few notables and rich landowners:
modernisation often brings with it a loss of traditional social control.
The diffusion of technical progress requires mediators: as a result of


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
955 this there are curious differences from one village to another. In the same district, Mossi in the upper Volta, some people have described closed villages, withdrawn into their food-producing activities, where the old people judge "progress" very severly (like the village of
Yaoghin). In contrast to this, some time ago the nearby village of Zaongho developed irrigated gardens to sell fruit and vegetables.
Minute analysis has shown the importance of having a progressive chief in the village. Progress is attractive to some, but not to all. In
Chad, the traditional round straw huts are now rarely built in the villages; these used to sustain the well known local pottery and basketry crafts. The new houses are quadrangular, bigger, and made with bricks, which require specialist masons. They are expensive, but are the image of all the others being built along the main roads and in the towns, from Fort-Archambault to Bangui. Building a straw hut today would reveal its poverty and conservatism. In the same way, in the Indian province of Haiderabad, the village irrigation reservoir, the tank, is a symbol of the community, of rice-growing, and of the past;
the individual well equipped with a motor signifies social emanci pation, the new commercial farming practices, and an opening to the towns.
In the same way, industrial objects which are relatively easy to handle are incorporated at varying speeds into the poorer villages. The following description of the Upper Volta could easily be a general statement: "the iron spade made in Birmingham, the bicycle from
Saint-Etienne, the enamel basins from Ghana, the European clothes made of terylene, and the electric torches are an assorted mixture which can be found everywhere." But as soon as the question arises of radically changing the system of production, numerous brakes are applied. Rouveyran's point about farmers in Madagascar has more general worth. The farmer lacks money, certainly, and in particular his attitude towards the risks of innovation differ fundamentally from that of the European or American technician. Black rural worker do not seek maximum profit, they have the logic of "petty gamblers". This is what gives rise to the return to food-producing cultures, and older traditions (Rouveyran).
The choice of a strategy of modernisation in the country is a delicate problem. Should the process be allowed to happen naturally in the villages? Should there be a massive movement towards technical progress in the "perimeters" of development?


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
In India, Pakistan and the Philippines, it is possible to observe the effects of what American technicians call the "green revolution" - yet another one! This encompasses the spontaneous, or discretely organised diffusion of agronomic progress. The Rockefeller foun dation distributes high-yield varieties of rice and wheat which are specially cultured for tropical farming. The increase in performance has been undeniable: the yield has risen from ten to twenty-five quintals per hectare since these seeds were first adopted. But this new practice also implies others, like new irrigation, and the use of fertilisers and insecticides. In India, the shortage of roads, silos, fertiliser factories, and local organisms of credit has left the popula risers of progress in an a very awkward position. In the Punjab in
Pakistan, a profound transformation can be witnessed: this is the emergence of a class of "modernised" farmers in numerous villages, which only emphasises the poverty of the small peasant communities and farm workers who are not touched by this progress.
Expansive, irrigated areas, equipped with machinery, clearly con ceived and presented as a project of technical and economic devel opment, can be found just about anywhere in the world. They are sometimes very successful, like the vast plan to irrigate the Sudan from the Nile (the Gezira Scheme): 8000 square kilometres of newly developed land where cotton, sorghum, and fodder are grown alternately. They can also be spectacular failures. In Tanganyika, the
"peanut plan," undertaken in 1946 on a much wider scale, was completely abandoned five years later: 36 million pounds sterling had been spent only to leave the land fallow. The Niger Office did not live up to expectations. They probably counted too much on the efficiency of the machinery, and on the mobility and the rapid integration of the workforce. Agronomic experts, be they American, Israelian or French are not sufficient to ensure the diffusion of progress; it is the indigineous popularisers, themselves well-trained, who assume the roles of mediators.
The less ambitious programmes of "small-scale hydraulics," or "the development of the peasantry" are full of interest. Within the Ivory
Coast, the Mossi region is involved in a project which is proceeding in small stages: the practice of irrigated rice-growing is first made wide spread, then the rural workers are introduced to cattle-rearing. All this is hardly very spectacular; it is indispensable for the villagers, tortuou sly slow for the state.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
957 Poor nations are often pressed into adopting the highest forms of industrial technolgy. Take Afghanistan today, for example. How can they hope for a rapid development of traditional techniques? the villages supply dried fruits for export, products from rearing, leather and carpets; these are meagre resources. The means of communication have barely been modernised: there is less than thirty kilometres of road for an area of 1000 square kilometres! The average annual income per capita is less than one hundred dollars per year: and it is impossible to expect a rapid accumulation of savings. The first plans for instal lations are being put into operation, along with the building of modern roads and airports - twenty five establishments in a few years. Natural gas is being exploited, thanks to soviet technicians. Then there are the first great factories in the capital, Kabul, where the large German firm,
Hoechst sits enthroned in the new indsutrial zone. Two technological zones are juxtaposed, the most modern, and that of the oldest Asiatic tradition.
The poorer countries are scattered with "enclaves" of heavy industry and high technology. In New-Caledonia, the industrial system is represented by the powerful nickel complex at Noumea-Doniambo; a few kilometres away, the bush appears, and the world of small planters. In Mauritania, the new iron economy - deposits at Fort
Gourault, the mining railway, and the mineral tanker port at Port-Etienne - cuts incisively into old traditional nomadic country. In the Congo, one oil company pays the Republic what amounts to three-quarters of the country's budget. In a more general way, the mining and oil "enclaves" are tending to be transformed into zones of chemical industry of the highest technical level, thanks to bilateral agreements which are becoming more and more frequent. Algeria has gone very far very quickly in this sense: Hassi Messaoud, which is a desert enclave, is one part of the general network of the chemical gas industry; on the coast. the powerful complexes at Skkida
Philippeville and Arzew are directed towards Europe, and are not transforming the surrounding ways of life. The enclave of large-scale tourism in the tropica world is the result of a similar process. The industrial system is able to exploit the sunshine of Abidjan, Paris is only six hours away by plane, and now that large luxury hotels are in place to welcome the clientele. The Ivory Coast has recognised this as a fast means of development, and is now planning this world class
"tourist paradise."


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
More than in richer countries, the large towns are monopolising the new progress. Kenya is developing in Nairobi, and Nigeria in Lagos; it is here that the links with the rest of the world are being forged. Three separate poles of development have been emerging in Chile for a long time now: Santiago, the capital of diverse industries, Valparaiso, with its large port and refineries, and Concepcion, with its steel plants and chemical factories. It has been said that these are three "islands" in a badly unified geographical area. In Brazil, between the wealthiest region of Sao Paulo and the poor North-East, income falls from four to one. Are the efforts currently being deployed to solve this "dualism" sufficient? The poorest hinterland usually responds to new sollici tations by looking beyond their own frontiers. And in this way, the
"ways of life" are disclocated. The oasis at Tibesti, which has been closely studied by Capot-Rey, have already neglected its old irrigation practices, and the yields of wheat and dates have been declining through lack of care. Since 1962, the coup de grace has been the opening of oil fields in nearby Libya. In a more general sense, Africa is tending to divide into vast traditional areas from where people emi grate to go to the few attractive urban regions: people from Senegal and Chad move away to work in Dakar, Abidjan, Accra, Lagos, and the towns of Europe. Nearly all the poor states deplore both the decline of the traditional values of the village and the "explosive" urbanisation of a few large cities.
The search for harmonious "integrated" development, is a difficult art, and one which requires the recognition of certain priorities, at least for a time, like the needs of the massive rural world for fertilisers, lorries, coaches, cheap construction materials, and ever day household supplies. This is to accord a certain amount of trust to the small factory using a workforce which is mainly local. The
"Chinese Way" is interesting in this respect because it holds an important place in intermediary technologies (between the artisans and large-scale industry). This leads us back to the consideration that the most modern norms of the industrial system are not yet applicable everywhere. When the country remains an agricultural one, and the railway network incomplete, the smallest waterfall, the tiniest cement works, and the smallest rural factory constitute useful technological stages (Sigurdson). But the "Chinese Way" seems difficult to adapt to other states. Furthermore, its future development is unpredictable.


GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES
CONCLUSION : THE PARADOX OF
INDUSTRIAL EXP ANSI ON
959 Ardently hoped for in those countries which are still least likely to attain it, the industrial system is beginning to become suspect in those countries where it first struck root: that is the great paradox of our time.
The reaction against large, crowded and over-mechanised towns began first in Great Britain, it then developed in the United States, and is now invading Europe. A nostalgia for the countryside has opened the way for a new social technique - urbanism. The English and
Swedish "new towns" have very interesting prospects. However, the facts prove that "new towns," designed to house some thirty thousand people in verdant surroundings, are getting bigger as they have to accomodate more cars and more factories if they are to avoid the incessant to-ing and fro-ing to the large central city.
A second tendency manifested itself in the U .S.A during the 1930's.
This was against waste of all kinds, of agricultural soil, in the mines, in the oil wells. This idea of conserving natural resources has assumed a singular power today because of the extraordinary tapping of energy sources and mineral substances. Meadows and Forrester have formu lated the theory of the "collapse" of the industrial system through the exhaustion of its supply sources. But the response to this had already been found, in the recycling of raw materials, atomic factories, and over-industrialisation.
Today, over-industrialisation has become the number one enemy. It is no longer simply a question of air, water and earth pollution. The
"quality" of a natural and silent environment is extolled by an increas ing number of Americans, Europeans and Japanese. In France, the new installations of the industrial system - oil refineries, motorways, nuclear plants - are all plagued by local resistance. But in the United
States, where the anti-pollution laws are already very strict, it is especially noteworthy that this new stage has given rise to new and very prosperous branches of industry, like measuring instruments, and the chemical treatment of polluted water, etc.
Does the industrial system have the answer to each new problem posed by its formidable power? It is doubtful. If the position of the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
"friends of the earth" is an extreme one, founded as it is on an outdated myth - that of natural balance -that of the large industrial companies is no less so. Extreme specialisation in a technical branch, and the resultant power, alienates the industrial system more and more each day from the simple aspirations of the "man-inhabitant". (Le Lannou)
. It is in this sense that one can evoke with Labasse the "technica illusion,'' and the "narrow logic of technicians." The idea that every technique which is more powerful and bigger and faster represents progress, is uncontestable on the general level of material effec tiveness. It is not necessarily so however in a social and local perspec tive.
We have seen that the local and regional societies, which were originally suitable for the "man-inhabitant,'' now have less and less influence on the development of techniques. We must count of the wisdom of nations, the richest ones and the poorest ones, in order that
"the Earth will remain a living planet, habitable by mankind" (George).


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUE
The distinction readily made between the history of science and the history of technique is usually broadly justified by the fact that their connection in the past has been most often highlighted in two types of situation: either they have each developed separately or else exchanges have taken place between them, but without their being intermixed.
However, this second type of situation calls for the introduction of a history of these exchanges alongside the histories of science and technique conceived as two separate histories. Furthermore, although constituting a "negative" fact, the absence of a relationship offered us by the first type of situation also calls for a history, because the separate development of science and technique often originates from attitudes of non-acceptance which are worth retracing.
Moreover, besides these two types of situations, certainly predomi nant in the histories of science and technique, a closer examination reveals situations where science and technique are mixed together to such a degree that their separation can only be artificial. The precise understanding of this situation calls for a close analysis of the histories of science and technique.
The outcome of this situation is that the simple juxtaposition of the histories of science and technique does not revive their past in a completely satisfactory way. Moreover, it has been established that histories of each of them experience a certain embarrassment and hesitancy in a number of areas as to the determination of topics for agreement, and that references which are frequently made from one history to the other do not show up their connection in a particularly favourable light.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
It is not a question here of retracing the history of these connections in detail. But we would like firstly to outline the problem areas and typologies and then show the most fundamental and characteristic aspects of each of the major fields of the phenomena under which these connections have been presented in the past.
The problem areas and typologies which we shall outline first are meant to distinguish as precisely as possible the main types of relationship between science and technique. From these some signifi cant features will undoubtedly emerge characterising the whole evolution of the connection between science and technique. But this general discussion is not without its problems. Indeed in various fields the history of the relationship between science and technique undoub tedly provides common features, notably a growing development of exchanges between science and technique which is essentially highlighted by the multiplicity of topics for consideration by technique and science and by the gradual passage from a rule of thumb technology to one stimulated by the spirit of science and founded more and more on its data. Nevertheless, this evolution varies significantly from one field to the other and presents particular features in each field.
GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS
The Duality of Knowledge and Effective Action The distinction between science and technique orginates fundamen tally from the fact that the first aims at knowledge, the second at effective action. But they often meet in a common concern for knowledge, technique always involving to some degree an understand ing of the points and phenomena to which it refers in order to achieve its objective.
But even when its scientific character is more pronounced and becomes more an application of science, technique remains disinter ested in science per se because its prime aim is not knowledge at all but efficiency. Seeing it merely as an expedient, it only has its sights on knowledge which can be useful to it. You could say, rather coarsely,


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
963 that it serves as a working device; it does not need to know how it works. That is to say quite often, even today in a number of areas, technique can be satisfied with fairly concise knowledge.
Right through the history of technique this difference in attitude with regard to knowledge has been the source of tension, opposition and mutual and active misunderstanding between men of science and men of technique. With the former there dominates a need for purity of knowledge and a need for "thought" which leads it to give little regard for those who are preoccupied above all else with effective action, and to believe that, in subordinating itself to the pursuit of practical ends, science does not view its horizons as being restricted, and that its accuracy is not impaired. With the latter we often see an insufficient regard for the value of knowledge per se and an insuffi cient recognition of the help that science can bring to technique.
We also observe that history is far from verifying the quite current conception according to which technique will eventually only be an application of science. Without doubt techniques of today, and sometimes of the past, corresponds more and more to these views. But quite often this "pattern" does not take account of the evolution of techniques. Indeed many a time we find ourselves in the presence of a
"technique" which does not originate in any way from knowledge, which has the characteristics of scientific knowledge. Frequently, however, it is true knowledge but principally knowledge in achieve ments. And it even happens that knowledge recorded in techniques is in advance of scientific knowledge. Thus, not only have technical achievement brought to science ample material for thought leading it to ask questions it would not normally ask itself, but, in more than one case, the knowledge recorded in techniques seems a greater prize than science which has emerged from it, being in fact only a clarification, a
"formalisation. " But, on the other hand, there are aspects of technique which are quite different from science: these concern instances where what makes it essential, original and a direct source of its useful effect does not originate from new knowledge, if it were only the intimate knowledge of a "technique" but the result of a simple combination, a simple arrangement of principles and factors copied from the posses sion of existing data. Thus a very large number of mechanical devices are available and, more generally, many other appliances which serve thousands of uses in our everyday life.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Another source of divergence between science and technique: their difference in "style. " Quite often science is very abstract and is expressed in a language which presupposes the command of a mathe matical system only available to a small number of people. On the other hand, techniques have been developed in the depth of hard realities and have presented in the past, and still presents today, a "text book" characteristics which are hardly orientated towards "speciali sation" and generalities.
Finally, even when it is based upon a matured science, technique differs from science in the fact that the stages of science are strictly divided between the different categories of phenomena - heat, light, electricity, etc. - whereas technique is often induced to call upon several sciences at one and the same time.
Scientific Knowledge and Technical Knowledge The distinction between science and technique from the point of view of the knowledge which they use, where the concise and rule of thumb knowledge of technique contrasts with the general, rational and systematic knowledge of science, constitutes as we have just said an outstanding feature of past science and techniques. However, in many cases there is not such a clear distinction: technique often presents scientific traits and, conversely, science does not appear as a very scientific knowledge.
If we consider science in the first place as a behaviour, like an attitude of mind independently from its subject matter, then past technique frequently seems to posess a scientific trait. A careful study of the facts, an acceptance of the lessons of experience and an intimate familiarity with reality which are fundamental features of the scientific mind, occur in many a technique, even the most skilled, when we can often see in the same period scientific theories formulated like imagi native designs unconnected with reality which the physics or chemistry theorie˄ of the 17th and 18th Centuries so frequently offer us.
Moreover, that other fundamental feature of the scientific attitude - namely concern for the assemby of specific facts and rules in a coherent doctrinal body covering an extensive field - is soon come across in technique. Contrary to the picture d'Alembert presents us of past techniques in his Discours preliminaire a l'Encyclopedie, "unable to express themself clearly about the instruments they use," it was


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
965 already assembled well before the 18th century and understood in many fields for its rather grandiose and dogmatic views. It appeared similarly in numerous 16th and 17th century dissertations such as
Agricola's De re Metallica (1556), Besson's Le Theatre des instruments mathematiques et mecaniques (1578), Jousse's "Le Theatre de l'art du charpentier (1627) and Kunckel's L'Ars Vitraria Experimentalis
(1679).
The spread of scientific culture into the technical sphere beyond the role it has played in the spread of the application of science and technique, which is too obvious to dwell on, has been one of the major reasons for the passage of technique from a skilled style into modern
"Technology." This term should be understood as a knowledge which, by prolonging in a much more scientific and systematic way the trend which has led since the 16th century to the publication of such technical dissertations as we have just mentioned, is distinguished from science by its subject matter and technical reality but is nevertheless a science by its psychology and by the methodic way in which it poses problems; by the importance of expressing in a
"dissertation" the "operation" of technique, the precision of its steps, the generality of the concepts to which it gives freedom and the use it makes of mathematics through the precision of its observations and measurements. It has thereby arisen from the history of science as well as from the history of technique. It is indeed such a technology that we saw develop in the 18th century with works such as Architecture hydraulique by Belidor (1737-1739), republished many times for more than a century, the Elements d'Architecture navale by Duhamel du
Monceau (1752), the Traite des horloges marines by Berthoud (1771), the two dissertations by Bouguer on the construction and handling of ships (1746 and 1757).
But often the improvement of a technique also requires, as has been said before, an investigation of a scientific nature not only in its method but in the aim of knowledge which underlines it. A history of science which does not concern itself with making room for scientific knowl edge of natural phenomena which technique has developed, would be seriously incomplete and would be unable to grasp the continuity in the development of knowledge of certain facts and phenomena which, having originated in the heart of a technique which already gives it a certain scientific character, has become progressively more general and more explicit thus truly acquiring the character of a science in a


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
way which is understood today. To tell the truth, the standard of quality has never clearly been agreed to which knowledge can be qualified as scientific. The trend would be to set this standard a little too high, at least when it concerns science developed by "technicians," because it seems that one is more accommodating where scientists are concerned: tentative efforts and attempts are sometimes revered which are on the whole dependent on a craftsman of truly poor quality.
It is true, however, that their steps most often develop in a fuller and richer intellectual context than that in which the technician develops his inventions. Yet we should be mindful of certain wholly technical creations which are developed in a remarkably open and intelligent manner.
To sum up, considered in the context of knowledge which develops from them, science and technique often appear far less clear than is still generally thought. Undoubtedly the knowledge which technique uses is perfected more and more by science outside technique. But in the past we meet many situations where outstanding scientific knowledge is present right at the centre of technique. From this fact such knowledge must be taken into account by the history of science, but at the same time it is dependent upon the history of technique. Because of that, these two "histories" are closely associated.
Technique - Instrument of Science The service that technique brings to science by providing it with instruments for its knowledge is particularly worthy of attention for two reasons firstly, the instruments most frequently originate from an unusual invention where knowledge and skill of high quality have been required for their development; secondly, they have played a decisive role in a large number of great scientific discoveries. At one time it concerned instruments for measurement and observation which have not changed at all or at least have changed only very slightly the phenomena to which they belong; at another time it concerned instruments which have played a more active role in a way we shall describe in the following paragraph that enabled results to be achieved hitherto unrecognised.
Depending at one and the same time on the spheres of the history of science and technique, the development of these instruments takes place in the one as well as in the other. It is then a matter of simple


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
967 correlation. Yet having introduced it in one of these histories we should not forget that it also belongs in the other.
But the history of these instruments should not only be considered in the light of the service that they have given to science. They interest the histories of science and technique in a more general way. Indeed it is not only science that needs scientific and measuring instruments, but also technique and the practical way of life. But if some instruments have had a unique scientific use as, for example, the astronomic telescope, others in as precise a form or of a lesser precision but of the same fundamental design, have been used for practical purposes. For example, linear measuring instruments, scales, or, furthermore, watches which, at first made with the scientific aim of determining longitude, were soon used in everyday life for navigation and then for measuring time.
Then again numerous instruments could only have been invented or at least perfected thanks to the progress of science; for example, optical instruments and instruments for measuring electricity. From this viewpoint they are like applications of science, of a science that often differs from one for which they provide a means of observation or of measurement.
Finally, it should be noted that with their growing precision, scienti fic instruments seem to be both the cause and effect of the progress in the exactness of the scientific approach. From these two points of view they have played a role which extends beyond the immediate objective for which they were created.
Closely connected with the question of measuring instruments is quite naturally that of units of measure. The choice of these units and their arrangement into a coherent system consequently represents an enterprise which serves science and technique at one and the same time, where they appear to narrowly overlap. Here technique should be considered in its widest sense including numerous applications from everyday life which depend upon very elementary techniques.
But the problem of units of measure presents two basic and quite distinct aspects: the first is purely a practical one concerning the management of performance and deals with the arrangement and standardisation of units and of unit systems. It concerns a technique which, although not "materialistic" or "mechanical," nonetheless constitute one of the major aspects of techniques. Too restrained by the material aspect of technique we did not give sufficient attention


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
until the end of the 18th century to the obstacle which the incomprehensibility of the units of measure had constituted for the progress of science as well as of technique and, reciprocally, to the full significance of establishing an understandable system of units of measure at the time of the French Revolution.
The second aspect of the problem of units is of a much more scientific nature: it concerns the choice of standards of measure and of all the very difficult questions posed by their conservation. These problems were only tackled seriously at the end of the 18th century.
Now they have been, and remain, of vital importance for the progress of technique as well as of science.
Achievements and Inventiveness in Science and Technique The relationship between science and technique which develops from the role of instruments in scientific investigations is only one aspect of a more general "state of affairs" to investigate the intermixture of science and technique resulting from the fact that, if science is contem plation in terms of its investigations, it is action in terms of its procedure. In other words, in its historic development as much as in its present state, science has not been passive as far as nature is concer ned. In order to be able to understand its laws, it questions it, subjects it to many changes, combines its elements in thousands of ways and compels it to reveal hidden aspects. In all these ways it draws closer to science. However, in the overlapping of science and technique a view is put forward which embodies that of the history of the active stages of man with regard to nature. This is a view which highlights all the more the fact that, in this "achievement," science and technique have been mixed to such a degree that if they are split into two "histories" you would risk not seeing the integration of the creative dynamism within which they are recorded.
Thus the founding of a history of man's active attitude vis a vis nature seems justified alongside the separate histories of science and of technique, this history being broken down into major categories of mathematics, mechanics, electricity, optics and chemistry. In such a history the foremost viewpoint is that of the type of action pursued; the objective of that action - knowledge, usefulness or even pleasure - must undoubtedly be taken into account but they only represent a secondary point of view.


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
969 Indeed the history of science and the history of technique have not neglected this active and creative approach but they have not studied it closely enough and have not explained its nature and scope sufficiently. That is why we can still see today, qualified under that imprecise heading of "discoveries," stages relating to highlighted achievements as different as the first isolation of a chemical element, demonstration of the action of an electric current on a magnet, the
"discovery" of x-rays, the production of the laser and the synthesis of a chemical compound. Furthermore, by separating scientific from technical achievements you cannot obtain a satisfactory account of the many situations where these two types of achievements are closely linked.
More broadly speaking, such a history of achievements by man with regard to nature, so far unrecorded, would enable progress to be clearly defined of man's mastership and recognition of the "objectives" and events as well as of the enrichment of nature with important inventive matters. It therefore seems that, symbolised by the progress of science and of technique, a world of ever increasing items better identified and better "verified," has slowly but surely been constituted.
In its entirety it constitutes a sort of "pool" which is a source of knowledge and of service at both one and the same time. It grows unceasingly richer with new first principles with the double stimulation of pursuit of knowledge and of attainment of efficiency.
These events and these objectives subscribe to an appropriate way of life quite independent of sciences and techniques which depend upon them and which overlap their domains, thriving principally on the nuturing and voicing of philosophical thought, of literary and artistic creation and, more broadly, of ways of feeling and thinking.
The various stages of these events can be distinguished as follows:
(A) "detection" of a phenomenon or of an objective, for example discovery of very small living organisms, thanks to the microscope;
(B) measurement of various "sizes" of a phenomenon: length, mass, time, temperature; (C) control of a phenomenon, for example of a waterfall by a mill; (D) separation of substances which are mixed in a crude form, authenticated for example by analytical chemistry;
(E) combination of the phenomena of "objectives" with either a scien tific aim of proving a law with an assumed characteristic, or with a tech nical aim, for example, the production of optical systems by the com bination of lenses; (F) creation of so called "inventions" of paper,


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
chemical, synthesis of bodies non-existent in nature, and production of penetrable light by the laser.
Men, Mentalities and Institutions lf we wish to fully understand the relationship between science and technique during the course of history, we must add to the abstract views retained for us up to the present time, those more concrete ones of men, intellects and practices. Science and technique have developed in surroundings where a certain idea of their nature and role has predominated and where scientific and technical measures have been characterised by well defined varieties of intellectual groups, profes sions and vocations.
In the ancient mediterranean world, especially in Greece, we saw the birth and development of science conceived as idealistic learning; it soon became separated from the "tekne," that is to say from manual skill, from practices passed on by tradition, especially in the form of manual work. Science for Plato is the phenomenon of free men, of citizens. It does not need to practise mechanical professions since these are carried out by slaves. Undoubtedly science can derive many ideas, suggestions and "patterns" from technique. But, we do not perceive any reciprocal flow from science to technique apart from a few exceptions. Science had little need to turn to fundamentals and material actions before any speculation or theoretical ideas.
Indeed the learned Greek philosophers such as Empedocles, Anax imenes and Thales were familiar with the art of the potter, of medicine and of music and they found ample material in them for reflection; and it is well known that Aristotle had extensive information on the techniques of his period. Moreover, some of them were technicians:
Thales produced works of art, Anaximenes constructed sundials. But these concerned achievements having no connection with science. The concern to progress technique with the aid of knowledge which goes beyond the guesswork of skilled practices was virtually entirely non existent among the Greeks. Thus there was no appreciable progress during the period of Pericles. It was only later and particularly in the confines of Ionia that this approach was modified, notably by Archi medes (circa 3rd century), the scientist and engineer to whom both science and technique gave assistance.


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
971 In ancient Chinese times we can see a similar situation; technique reached a high level there but practically no scientific theories were accepted.
In the Middle Ages, although contrary to popular belief there was no partition between them, we see little contact between the circle of philosophers and scientists especially concerning speculation and the sphere of craftsmen (blacksmiths, goldsmiths, weavers and builders) devoid of scientific knowledge. Besides, this would have been of little use because of its abstract nature. However, the systems of learning propounded to us in the 12th century by Hugues de Saint-Victor, and by Vincent de Beauvais and Raymond Lulle in the 13th century, unite technique and science structually. At the beginning of the 15th century a notable change began, not indeed in every area but in several important sectors - chiefly architecture, the art of war, the art of mining and the construction of machines, in particular mills. A new professional person emerged, namely the engineer. Distinctly different from the craftsman and entrenched in a very narrow and corporate system where technical practices were often jealously guarded secrets, the engineer strives to be a creator and inventor and aims at making technique benefit from the resources of science, particularly mathematics, no longer within a narrow speciality but in diverse areas, certain of which have already been pointed out above.
Leonardo da Vinci is undoubtedly the most famous of these inven tors, although there were a number of predecessors in the 15th century.
However, the science which these inventors used was quite limited;
very little would contribute to the progress of science. Nevertheless, certain appreciably scientific contributions should be noted, amongst others by Tartaglia and especially by Stevin, at once a great inventor of the time and one of the most distinguished scientists of his period.
In the 17th century, the isolation between the scientific world and the world of technique was beginning to lessen; being more experi mental, science was moving towards technique to discover instruments which it needed and scientists often had to become technicians them selves. Thus they would be in close harmony with craftsmen, mainly the makers of instruments and opticians. However, interest in tech nique was fairly limited and "the spirit of the times" as well as the slow development of sciences still did not lead to a systematic enquiry into the progress of technique through the application of science.


1ECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Another important factor in bringing science and technique closer together was the creation in the second half of the century of the first great scientific organisations, mainly the Royal Society in England and the Academie Royale des Sciences in France. These bodies very soon became interested in various techniques, especially in machines, and many great technical ventures were accredited to certain of their members such as the first accurate geographical surveys by the Abbe
Picard.
Furthermore, engineers and architects of broad scientific background, such as Claude Perrault or Vauban in France, gave a significantly more scientific look to technique. However, in many areas
- construction of machines and instruments, metallurgy, textile fac tories - it was still the craftsman, the expert, who dominated.
In the 18th century techniques still remained to a large extent guesswork, but they benefited in intellectual spheres and particularly in scientific spheres by an ever increasing knowledge. The distinguished inventories of Arts and Professions contributed signifi cantly, which the Academie des Sciences first undertook and which was published between 1735 and 1777 in the seven volumes compendium of the Machineset inventions approuvees par l'Academie (The Machines and Inventions approved by the Academy), the Descriptions des arts et metiers approuvees par Messieurs de l'Academie royale des Sciences, avec figures (Descriptions of the Arts and Professions carried out and ratified by the Gentlemen of the Royal Academy of Sciences, with designs) (76 volumes from 1762 to 1789); then the writers of the
Encyclopedia which, by its title Encyclopedie ou dictionnaire raisonne des sciences, arts et metiers (Encyclopeadia or Dictionary of the
Sciences, Arts and Professions), accurately records the importance of associating techniques with sciences. The first edition started in 1751 was not completed until 1780. The term "arts" should be understood as both the liberal arts (letters, painting, music) and the "mechanical" arts which really constitute techniques in the modern sense of the word. Of the twenty-eight volumes of that edition, seven are engraved volumes devoted specifically to the description of different profes sions. But these descriptions are based particularly on traditional techniques. The new techniques, notably steam engines, have only a small mention.
The increasing intermixture of sciences and techniques in the 18th century took place due to the multiciplity of engineers and to the


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
973 eminence of their scientific culture. Engineers played a growing role in the art of war, especially in fortifications; in public works (construction of roads and bridges), as for example Perronet in France and M ylne in
Great Britain; and also in the working of mines. But the manufacture of machines was not in fact the achievement of engineers but of high quality technicians. These replaced the "millwright," the traditional type of machine manufacturer. These engineers and technicians played a major role in Great Britain in the development of new industrial techniques, Rumford for example. They enjoyed a high esteem in scientific circles. In the middle of the 18th century great technicians like John Smeaton who played a prime role in the progress of the steam engine, and John Dollond the maker of scientific instru ments, were welcomed at the Royal Society. In France on the other hand a certain reticence was shown by "scientists" towards those who dealt with "mechanical arts." Vaucanson complained bitterly of the little attention he received from the Academie des Sciences, and it was only at the end of the 18th century that an expert of great talent, Joseph
Lenoir, joined a scientific body for the first time - the Bureau des Longitudes. " In Great Britain in the 18th century, the education of engineer˅ and superior technicians was still done "on the job." But in France we saw the creation of a higher technical education, comprising basic scientific instruction: Ecole des Pants et Chaussees (The School of Roads and
Bridges) (1740), Ecole du Genie at Mezieres (School of Civil Engineering) (1748), Ecole des Constructeurs de Navires (School of
Ship Builders) (1765), Ecole des Mines (School of Mines) (1783), Ecole des Arts et Metiers (School of Arts and Professions) (1794) and a number of other schools of design, cartography, hydrography, etc.
From the French Revolution onwards a new style of relationship between science and technique was founded, particularly in France. In more and more extensive areas, traditional experimental techniques gave way to technology. At the same time the cause and effect of this evolution, the Ecole Polytechnique founded in 1794 came to play a major role in this connection in France. Mainly in the areas of public works such as civil construction, mechanics, hydraulics, the building of ships and the working of mines, the poly-technicians contributed to the development of a much more scientific approach to the treatment of technical problems, particularly by giving major attention to careful, preliminary studies and to systematic trials. Mathematics which


1ECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
underwent a remarkable development with effect from the 19th century was a suitable instrument for dealing with these problems.
Techniques also benefited from the development of mechanics, espe cially the mechanics of fluids. At the same time a large number of these engineers became scientists of the highest status, such as Hachette,
Poncelet, Coriolis and Lame.
But the fairly abstract education given to engineers, not only at the Ecole Polytechnique but also to a lesser degree in the other great schools which had been born in the 18th century, or which were then created, in particular the Ecole Centrale des Arts et Manufactures
(Central School for Arts and Crafts) (1829), resulted in a number of inventions of the 19th century, mainly in the sphere of mechanics, being due not to engineers but to experts especially as far as textile machines were concerned. However, the important role played by scientists like Chevreul, Berzelius and Liebig in the 19th century in the development of the chemical industry should be noted.
In Great Britain also in the 19th century, engineers were often self-taught, even those to whom we owe the most notable discoveries such as the great builders of bridges and ships, Mark Isambard Brunel and his son Isambard, and the inventors and builders of machines,
Henry Maudslay, Richard Roberts, John Nasmyth and Joseph Whitworth.
At the end of the 19th century and in the 20th century the great inventions were due more and more to engineers and even to sci entists. Nevertheless, empirical experts such as Gramme and Edison in the sphere of electricity played a first class role in the progress of technique.
RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN SCIENCE
AND TECHNIQUE IN NOTABLE FIELDS
OF DEVELOPMENT
Arithmetics The history of arithmetic is usually dealt with entirely within the framework of the history of sciences, and the history of techniques has


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
975 willingly surrendered to it the "abstract," non-materialistic tech niques. Because of this, the practical role of arithmetic is not always sufficiently explicit in its specific characteristics. But is is for practical purposes - everyday life, commerce - that the systems of numbering were set up and the rules of calculation established in ancient times.
The calculations encountered in ancient and medieval astronomy led also to a significant number of practical necessities: the invention of the calendar which led to the art of computation, the referencing of positions both on the land surface and on the sea; and also astrology which, particularly in the Middle Ages and in the Renaissance, greatly occupied even the most eminent astronomers like Kepler.
Practical arithmetic of ancient pre-Hellenic times is not based upon an explicit science yet it demonstrates in several areas quite a remark able understanding of the correctness of numbers, especially in the civilisation of Mesopotamia.
An impartial theory about numbers, closely associated with philo sophical and "mystical" views, originated with the Greeks, mainly in the School of Pythagoras. It had little in common with the techniques of arithmetic. Because of their practical character they paid little attention to the "contemplation" of numbers and were usually satisfied with approximate calculations. However, the methods of approxi mation gave way in the Middle Ages, especially with the Arabs, to works which were often in themselves of significant scientific value and which contributed in a major way to the progress of pure mathematics, mainly in the establishment of minute calculations. Commercial arith metic which saw such a great development from the 13th century especially in Italy, was of a particularly practical nature. But this application was often marked by quite deep-seated views including those which led to the creation of double entry book-keeping in the
15th century, and the invention of logarithms by Napier in 1614.
Geometry Like practical arithmetic, practical geometry, was not sufficiently considered by historians because, traditionally, it derives from the history of sciences. It is seen most often as only a source of pure geometry.
Because of its abstract nature geometry occurs in a variety of practical fields. Firstly that of area measurement and then that of


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
volumes. Methods of calculation derive from this exactness which, purely experimental in early civilisations, led slowly but surely to unprejudiced speculation and which only became really established in ancient Greek times. Having become a science, geometry therefore had a thorough knowledge of the remarkable developments with which we are familiar, firstly by the early Greek philosophers and then by Euclid, Apollonius and Archimedes. The standard area techniques were to benefit from this "scientific" progress. However, they still retained a certain independence even into the Middle Ages, and a number of formula for this calculation still remained experimental because the approximation which was accepted enabled it to be unaffected by the strictness of pure science.
We can also see a geometrical technique in angular measurements.
They played a fundamental role, theoretical as well as practical, in astronomy and in the processes for land measurement (geodesy and topography). Termed "mathematical instruments," the instruments which made these measurements were until modern times due to the co-operation between craftsmen and scientists. The most widespread must be the astrolabe. The skill of craftsmen led to the achievement of more and more precise graduation whilst the very method of making these measurements made great progress, notably with the microm eter invented in the 17th century by a scientist, Auzout, and later with the development of the theory of errors and of their correction to which great scientists like Laplace and Gauss made a remarkable contribution; a theory moreover which also applied to the meas urement of length.
The arrival of optics into angular measurements, especially from the beginning of the 18th century, led to new types of instruments of more expedient utilisation and of greater precision through the introduction of sighting devices in the astronomic telescope and, in navigation, with reflexion instruments of which the sextant is the most developed. Here again, we see this progress guaranteed by close collaboration between scientists and craftsmen.
Closely associated with topography and geodesy, cartography is a geometric technique, which only became truly scientific from the 17th century. Cartography is the study of the flat representation of the earth and has been practised from ancient times; it poses problems which geometry did not manage to resolve satisfactory for a long time.
However, Hipparchus introduced stereographic projection and


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
977 Ptolemy conical projection. But the representation of increasing latitudes conceived by Gerard Mercator in the 16th century was only experimental at first, and it was not until the beginning of the 17th century that a proper geometric theory was established.
The representation of three-dimensional shapes in flat forms is another area of great importance in practical geometry. Until the beginning of the 17th century it was quite separate from pure geometry.
For a long time the cutting of stones or stereotomy required only very basic geometry. It certainly made use of flat projections which had already been used in the construction of the Egyptian pyramids, but their application was very experimental.
The increasing interest of inters in perspectives in the Renaissance period often led to quite remarkable research, in particular by Albert
Diirer. But this did not lead to true scientific theory and geometricians had little interest in it. It was only with Desargues, originally a prac titioner, and then Pascal, that the scientific importance of the problems posed by perspectives was recognised. The geometry of projection was thus born. But we know that it only really gained full recognition in mathematical science at the end of the 18th century and particularly with Poncelet and Chasles at the beginning of the 19th century.
As for descriptive geometry - the general method of presenting three-dimensional shapes by two projections - this only dates from the second half of the 18th century. Its founder, Monge, played a major role in this regard and contributed in particular by giving a scientifc character to industrial design, specifically the design of machines. But this scientific method was only applied to the construction of really important machines in the first half of the 19th century.
Optics In the field of optics the relationship between science and technique first manifested itself in the 17th century in the advent, and the development of, optical instruments. This relationship was in three areas which should be carefully distinguished as follows:
I - The services rendered to science by optical technique:
A) An increase in the precision of measuring lengths and angles, already seen in the use of the telescope as an instrument of sighting


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
both in astronomy and in the measurement of the earth's surface (geodesy and topography).
B) An increase in the field of recognised phenomena; the stars with the astronomical telescope, small living organisms with the micro scope.
II - The benefit brought by scientists to the manufacturing tech niques of optical instruments. Thus Galileo and Scheiner were closely connected with the craftsmen who made the first astronomical tele scopes and had, it seems, their own manufacturing workshops.
Descartes, Huygens and Hooke were involved with the improvement and cutting of optical glass.
III - The progressive application of the progress in geometric optics to the conception of optical instruments. For this the doctrine of geometric optics had to be sufficiently developed. Undoubtedly the laws of reflection of light were known ever since ancient times and
Ptolemy had already observed the phenomena of refraction. lbn al Haitham, an astromomer of the School of Cairo, tried to explain the magnifying power of spherical lenses, experimented with spherical and parabolic mirrors and demonstrated spherical aberration; at the beginning of the 17th century Kepler, Snellius and Cavalieri determined the focal points of lenses and Descartes, in the Dioptra
(1637), stated the law of refraction. But the optical characteristics of glass were as yet unproven and its cutting was too imprecise to be able to be applied to geometric optics, even to the most basic instruments.
This application only began with Huygens in the second half of the 17th century. The first composite eye-piece was due to him. It was not until the 18th century with Clairault, Euler and d'Alembert, that a true calculation of optical combinations was realised. So between 1720 adn
1780 the manufacture of astronomical instruments developed signifi cantly and made remarkable progress in not only optical systems but also in the arrangement of tubes of telescopes, and the sighting and measurement of angles. However, craftsmen at that time were slow in putting these theories into practice. The first simple microscopes which saw great success in the last quarter of the 17th century owed nothing to geometric optics. However, the first compound microscopes were made from them a little later.
The utilisation of optical instruments, however, came up against the problem of achromatism. This problem was manifest from the end of


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
979 the 17th century but in this field science was not equal to its task. It was overcome by the "technique" of the expert who even had to contradict it: Newton, in his Dissertation on Optics (1704), had declared that the manufacture of achromatic eyepieces by the combination of two lenses of different indices of refraction was unachievable. Newton's authority was such that for nearly half a century that opinion prevailed. The first achromatic eyepieces made in 1733 passed unnoticed. The precise scientific solution to the problem was only found by Euler. The first complete theory of focal systems was established at the beginning of the 19th century by Malus (1808) and then by Gauss (1838-1841).
The relationship between science and technique in the 19th century in the field of optics was manifested in several new forms. Firstly, by a remarkable increase in the field of "optical" phenomena: infra-red and ultra-violet optics joined visible optics; also the development of spectroscopy led to the recognition that rays of a particular wavelength were linked to various elements. The "control" of these new phe nomena was beneficial to industry as well as to science. In particular it led to an upsurge in techniques in the analysis of matter.
Photography is another interesting case of the relationship between science and technique. From the optical viewpoint its principle, that of the camera obscura, was already known in the Middle Ages and photographic lenses could have been made from the 18th century. In this regard, therefore, the type of science required for the creation of photography was available well before its invention. But that inven tion assumed a fixed image; here too, science was far from contributing the necessary components at the start of the 19th century. But it was a matter which was dependent upon photochemistry which we shall discuss further in the paragraph on chemistry.
Mechanics Considered from the modern point of view, mechanics is clearly divided into distinct areas, in particular the mechanics of solids and the
.mechanics of fluids; but it also constitutes a unified philosopy of basic principles and concepts. In the past, mechanics was presented under much more clearly defined features, especially from the point of view of the relationship between science and technique which we are discussing here. It is not a general view, however, but by drawing our attention to the sub-areas in which it was in fact divided in the past, we


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
can understand the relationship that science and technique have with mechanics.
Statics It is too obvious a fact to dwell on that it is from the heart of technique that the theories of force emerged, followed by those of moments and of work which are closely associated with it. They form the basis of statics which is the science of the arrangement and equilibrium of forces. In the civilisations of ancient times, we saw numerous types of devices for multiplying and changing the direction of forces; firstly the lever, then in Greece in about the 4th century the pulley, winch, and the glock and tackle. War machinery and various types of balances should be added to these devices. We owe to
Archimedes in about the 2nd Century the first general theory in the use of these devices. It is not certain, however, that it contributed very much to the progress in these areas which, moreover, was very limited until modern times although we should mention the noteworthy contribution in the 2nd century by Heron of Alexandria and by Philo of
Byzantium. The progress of statics as a science had only manifested itself in the calculation of centres of gravity, a problem prompted to a great extent by technique and which, as we know, has been a valuable stimulus to the development of infinitesimal calculus. The devel opment of statics and its application to technique, in construction in particular, implied the theory of vectors. But this was only developed after the 18th century. The tricky problems of statics posed in the construction of cathedrals in the Middle Ages were resolved in a purely empirical manner. It was only in the 19th century that statics was systematically and scientifically used for the calculation of frameworks and stone constructions.
Hydrostatics presents us with a similar situation. Since early ancient times the pumping and channeling of water often led to ingenious practices. But it was only in ancient Greek times, at an imprecise date, that the siphon was invented. As for the water pump it seems to date only from the Romans. Moreover, the limits of its lifting power was only recognised in the 17th century leading to the discovery of atmo spheric pressure. However, a scientific hydrostatics was founded with the Dissertation on Floating Bodies by Archimedes which provided guidelines that had a significant influence on the devedlopment of hydraulic techniques. In the 17th century appreciable progress was made in hydrostatics, especially by Pascal. However, even in the 18th


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
981 century, harbour works, the construction of dykes and canals, irri gation and drainage were undoubtedly based upon rules which were the result of numerous systematic observations, but which owed little to so called science.
Machines By the term machine we do not mean simple contraptions such as the lever, pulley, etc. mentioned above, but the more complex devices involving combinations of movements which made them essentially original. In ancient times these complex machines were quite rare apart from certain war engines and those devices that relied less on a practical objective than on the effects of surprise and impressiveness. They multiplied in the Middle Ages with the devel opment of mills then, in modern times, with the automation of threading, weaving, working of material etc. But as we stated in the first part of this chapter, these machines did not use science directly as their principle; the arrangements on which they are based constitute a particular aspect of technique which clearly distinguishes them from science. These contrivances only assumed a scientific character in modern times, and then only partially with the clarification of the theories of information and feedback and of the amplifying relay.
The machines, however, had a significant influence on the devel opment of sciences, especially from the 17th Century with Descartes in particular, because we saw in them patterns of astronomical, physical and biological phenomena. However, we know that these patterns were most often quite remote from reality. Also the conceptions of mechanics which they advanced tended to delay science and did not contribute overall towards its progress.
Dynamics Dynamics, founded in the 17th century by Galileo, Descartes, Leibniz and Newton, will not detain us long, at least as far as the general doctrine of force and movement is concerned. This is for two reasons.
Firstly, it owes very little to technique. Indeed we could not truly consider as a component part of technique the processess and devices as basic as the falling of bodies, the inclined plane device and the throwing of a projectile, the study of which played such a big part in its constitution.
Secondly, the application of new mechanics to technique was quite limited in the 17th century, apart from Huygens' application of the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
pendulum to the measurement of time in 1656. Even in the 18th century the techniques where it should have intervened - the first steam engines, ballistics, hydraulics, the movement of ships, the building of machines - had in fact made little call upon it. The dissertations by Belidor mentioned already that were so widely published, undoubtedly contained a number of general ideas and referred to a great extent to mathematics, although they relied on a very empirical mechanics. In spite of the progress it made, notably in the area of mechanics of fluids which we shall study in more detail, the science of mechanics was very "learned" and the problems of mechani cal technique were too complex for the application of science to technique to be able to genuinely take place in this field.
The mechanics of fluids It was only during the 18th century, particu larly with Jean and above all Daniel Bernoulli, Euler, d' Alembert and
Lagrange, that the mechanics of fluids began to materialise. It utilised a mathematical system that was only developed in that period, in particular the theory of equations of partial derivatives. The tech niques which used the dynamics of fluids were purely experimental until that time. However, certain aspects of the behaviour of fluids had been recognised by an advance that already showed a scientific char acteristic, especially with the development in the Renaissance, of the jets of water, fountains, etc. which saw great success at that time. But most of the technical creations in this area - water wheels, steering and design of the shape of vessels - owed nothing to science even in the
18th century when noteworthy theories that were then developed "idealised" reality too much in order to be able to influence practices.
It was similar even in the 19th century: the mechanics of fluids played no part either in the invention of the propellor or in the design of ships. However, from the middle of the 18th century the mechanics of fluids was applied to the utilisation of water wheels, particularly by
Euler in 1750, and in the 19th century, the remarkable progress in hydraulic turbines to which the French engineer Fourney especially contributed around 1850 originates to a great extent from scientific studies.
An even more difficult development - that of aerodynamics, the mechanics of air - did not really become a science until the 20th century. The scientists who were interested in it therefore delayed their involvement in its application into useful results. The <level-


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
983 opment of aviation at the end of the 19th century and beginning of the 20th, owed nothing to science. Moreover, the first attempts at heavier than air flight were considered by a major part of the scientific world as being doomed to failure.
Heat The somewhat impressive field of physical behaviour of gases, of their compression and expansion, of the vacuum and of heat, presents us with historical situations where the relationship between science and technique reveals such diverse aspects that a closer study is necessary in order to fully understand it.
The beginning of an understanding and true mastery of these phenomena is considered to date from the demonstration of the existence of atmospheric pressure and the vacuum. But in this remarkable stage of development, science and technique were closely associated. It was a technical achievement that raised the problem of atmospheric pressure and it was declared as impossible in 1640 by the fountain makers of Florence to raise water beyond 18 feet; on con sidering this impossibility, Pascal was led in 1647 to carry out an experiment at the Puy de Dome that established definitely the exist ence of the vacuum. It opened a new horizon that explains the rise in the number of pneumatic pumps constructed in order to achieve a vacuum in an enclosed space, with experiments by Otto von Guericke from 1650 to 1654 followed several years later by those of Boyle,
Hooke and Huygens, when scientific study and techniques in the construction of machines became closely associated. The vacuum experiments were to remain for a century that was in the current foreground of achievement.
This new field of knowledge, however, was to have no significant importance for some time either for the progress of science or of technique. As far as the latter is concerned, this originated with Denis
Papin, Newcomen and Savery, and the steam engine which was the most remarkable technical achievement in this area, progressed more from the ingenuity and skill of experts than from a rational and technological progression and scientific application. However, we have not sufficiently stressed the fact that the theories on atmospheric pressure and the vacuum were derived from science and that they formed the basis of the conception of the steam engine.


1ECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
The remarkable improvements brought to the steam engine in the second half of the 18th century, especially by Watt, were still quite experimental in particular concerning the processes for the conden sation of water vapour. Science was nevertheless present in it since observations of an unquestionably scientific character developed on the phenomena involved in the steam engine. Thus Watt is not a pure expert; he had a close association with science having being, as we know, a repairer of instruments of physics at the University of Glas gow, and he was familiar with the experiments by Black in 1765 on latent heat. Furthermore, in Watt's work we see the theory of work gradually develop although this would only be fully explained in 1821 in a posthumous work by Coulomb. Again, as early as 1782, in his
Essai sur /es machines en geeneral (Essay on Engines in General) Lazare Carnot had already posed questions of a distinctly scientific nature on the efficiency of engines. But the problem of efficiency was only fully explained by thermodynamics. The well known thesis by
Sadi Carnot on the subject, La Puissance motrice du feu (The Driving Power of Heat) which dates from 1824 remained unknown for ten years. Clapeyron understood its full significance and began to spread these new ideas, but it was only at the end of the 19th century that they became established as the basis for the realisation of devices for the production of mechanical energy from heat. The increase in the efficiency of steam engines through the construction of high pressure engines at the beginning of the 19th century, followed by the creation of the first internal combustion engines - Lenoir's gas engine (1860),
Otto's internal combustion engine (1876) - owed nothing to thermo dynamics. It was not until Diesel that thermodynamics began to clearly intercede in the design of internal combustion engines.
Electricity and Magnetism The field of electrical and magnetic phenomena is one where science and technique seem to be most interdependent and where conse quently a history separating science and technique would be particu larly inadequate.
The first machines for the continuous generation of static electricity created in 1672 by Otto von Guericke did not originate from a knowledge of electrical phenomena that could be considered truly scientific. The same applies to the invention by Van Musschenbroek in


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
985 1745 of the Leyden jar, a capacitor for the storage of electric charge.
We know that the general public at the time became enthusiastic about "inventions" but this was based more on a feeling of astonishment and admiration than on scientific knowledge and practical use. However, in the second half of the 18th century, these achievements and
"experiments" by Franklin in particular began to assume a scientific character. A mastery and verification of electrical phenomena began with Coulomb in particular who, with the torsion balance, measured the first electrical force. But we still owe the electric battery to speculation and experimentation although this was guided somewhat by scientific ideas, and the first realisation was by Volta in 1799. It played a fundamental role in the progress of electrical science in the
19th century since it produced a current for the first time. The works of Oersted, Ampere and Faraday between 1820 and 1835 depended to a large extent on these creations - these "artificial" phenomena - and they came out with theories and laws which contributed to some degree to the great progress made in electrical technology during the
19th century. As early as 1828 Faraday described the principle of the electric motor which led to the production of a continuous current by a turning device. But we had to wait for nearly half a century (1871) before a really usable electric motor was produced, and it was to a technician, Zenobe Gramme, that we owe this achievement. It was undoubtedly based upon the general laws of electricity formulated at the beginning of the century, but the methods that made his discovery
"operational" were experimental. The scientific explanation of Gramme's machine was not provided until several years later. It is reported that Gramme fell asleep at the conference where this was expounded for the first time.
As for the long distance transmission of energy achieved in 1882, this was undoubtedly due to Deprez, an engineer of high scientific culture.
But it was developed more by empiricism than by the application of the laws of electricity. In particular, the capability of reducing the loss of joules by raising tension was only clearly recognised following this achievement. Until then it was considered that these losses constituted an insurmountable obstacle to the long distance transmission of energy.
The invention of the electric telegraph seems to be more directly an application of science. In 1833 it was the well known scientists Gauss and Weber who achieved the first long distance telegraphic transmis sion - one kilometre - based on the laws of electricity recently


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
discovered. But it took several years for the laws of magnetism and electromagnetism to be accurately applied to the construction of electro-magnets which were to play a fundamental role in the really practical development of the electric telegraph. It was not to a scientist but to a teacher of painting, Samuel Morse, that we owe the invention in 1832 of the code system of long and short signals which soon developed rapidly. The very idea of this code, considered to be one of the foundations of the "invention" of information processing, origi nated with Morse not from science but from practical activity.
It was also practical activity that formed the basis of the invention of the microphone in 1874, made up of two electrodes between which a carbon rod was placed. Thus "amplification" was achieved for the first time, since variation of the resistance of carbon "modulates" the variations of a current. But the "concept" of amplification would only be fully explained much later.
In the invention and early development of radioelectricity, the fusion of science and technique, of empiricism and rational procedure is even more pronounced. No doubt there were scientific experiments by Hertz at the basis of radioelectricity. However, in these experi ments a certain "do it yourself" was mixed with theoretical ideas, principally with Maxwell's electromagnetic theory. It was also undoubtedly due to a scientist, Branly, that in 1898 we owed the invention of the first detector, the coheror of filings. But this invention, which was based on the cohesion of metallic powder under the action of sparking followed by electromagnetic waves was also the result of a large degree of stumbling, of empirical manipulation. It was only much later that a scientific explanation would be brought to the functioning of the coheror. The invention of the aerial, also by Branly, was even more empirical; however, the scientific knowledge available at the time on the emission and propagation of electromagnetic waves should have facilitated its conception. In 1902 the invention by John Fleming of the diode detector originated directly from science. Fleming moreover was a scientist as well as an engineer. But it was based to a large extent on empirical creations, as was the invention of the incandescent lamp and the improvements made to it by Edison, who was more of a technical man than a scientist.
Chemistry At first sight the field of chemistry might appear to be one in which science and technique have had little connection for some time. We


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
987 usually consider that chemistry as a science only dates from Lavoisier, that is to say from the end of the 18th century and that even in the 19th century technical chemistry was still quite independent from scientific chemistry.
But if, in the general remarks which have been put forward, we understand science in a less restrictive way than we understand it today, it seems that there were scientific steps in chemistry well before
Lavoisier and also to a large degree in the very heart of technical chemistry. Undoubtedly until the end of the 18th century, chemistry lacked the sufficiently general and reliable principles and concepts which science postulates. The principle of alchemy, and then of phlogiston, enabled unified views to become evident; but they were particularly imaginative and failed to connect the facts satisfactorily.
In other respects a number of practical chemists presented simple remedies without any illuminating or guiding ideas. However, throughout the history of chemistry and especially from the Renais sance, we encounter an endeavour to identify and classify phenomena and natural or artificial creations which, although often coarse and unskilful, nevertheless present the characteristics of a scientific proce dure.
But closer ties between science and technique become evident in past chemistry if, in the light of actions and creations described in the general considerations presented above, we devote our attention in chemistry not only to the aim of knowledge and research of a utility but also to the progressive precept of "powers" and "new substances" which instigated the pursuit of these objectives. An even more justified point of view is that in the field of chemistry more than in other fields,
"art" has a larger place there than science. Chemists - not alchemists and technicians but also "scientists" - devoted the greatest part of their efforts to preparations and analyses which constituted more active and creative steps than the observation and proving of laws of nature. In this regard, chemistry before Lavoisier's time was by no means in a state of stagnation, contrary to the view still in circulation.
