The history of that creative chemistry merits a significant place beside the history of chemical philosophies which, moreover, had little influence on technical chemistry before the 18th century. Let us highlight amongst these "inventions" those of metals, discovered in their natural state or prepared: since Antiquity silver, gold, iron, bronze, copper and in the
18th century, cobalt, nickel and platinum; acids that have played a fundamental role in a multiplicity of preparations: nitric acid or aqua


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
fortis and sulphuric acid or vitriol in the Middle Ages; ethyl alcohol or elixir of long life in the Middle Ages; phosphorus in the second half of the 17th century and chlorine by Scheele in 1774. The knowledge and use of fermentation and putrefaction processes since ancient times should also be mentioned which, although only explained fully in the
19th century, were nevertheless already quite well advanced.
As we know, chemical products mutiplied significantly from the beginning of the 19th century. They were due as much to science as to industry and led to the synthesis of chemical bodies from elements in the second half of the 19th century. It should be noted that the progress of chemical science is so recent that it relies to a large extent on the considerable wealth of experiments and development accumulated in previous centuries.
The chemical industry utilised the new chemical science only after a long delay and only accepted Lavoisier's influence half a century later.
It was in the field of organic chemistry that it rapidly became scientific.
Metallurgy on the other hand remained to a large extent empirical throughout the 19th century not only because it was not sufficiently manifest in science, but also because scientists paid little attention to it.
In 1848 the mining engineer and social reformer Le Play stated: "It is an indisputable fact that metallurgical phenomena are to this day hardly recognised by scientists." Already in the 18th century metal lurgy had undoubtedly been the subject of scientific observations with studies on steel by Reaumur (1712), then by Berthollet, Monge and
Vandermonde. But it was experimentation that was the basis of the method for the production of smelting by coke in 1735 and much later in 1855 by the Bessemer process. Even today, industrial chemistry calls for a number of "knacks," mainly in the field of catalysis. Moreover, the fact that scientific chemistry today cannot be completely deduced from basic laws of physics which quantum physics provides us, shows that it is not entirely a science in the fullest sense of the word.
The slowness of science compared to technical creation is also particularly interesting to note as far as the invention of photography is concerned. Undoubtedly the first attempts by Nicephore Niepce in
1816 to make an image on a plate were based on the use of silver chloride whose properties had been proved in the 18th century by a scientific process; but Niepce progressed in quite an experimental manner. Moreover, scientists at that time were not really interested in this problem. When consulted on this subject by Niepce, the great


SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
989 chemist Humphry Davy replied that the fixing of an image by chemical means had very little chance of success. We know that Niepce was not discouraged. In 1826 he exploited the bitumen of Judea - and that was still quite an empirical procedure. Then, in association with Louis
Daguerre in 1829, he managed in 1833 to fix an image by mercury vapour by using silver iodide and eliminate the remains of the iodide with hyposulphite, thus achieving the first true photograph.
The picture of the relationship in the various fields between science and technique that has just been presented is far from giving all the complex and diverse aspects of these relationships. We have confined ourselves in this general view to those events which seemed to us as being the most important and the most significant. An example of the general views presented, rather than a really exhaustive and balanced description of these relationships, can be seen in the first part of this chapter. However, we hope that, just as it is, it will have enabled the relationship between science and technique in the course of history to be understood and show that they cannot be reduced to an oversimpli fied sketch that is generally presented.


TECHNICAL PROGRESS
AND SOCIETY
This subject deserves to have a whole book dedicated to it. There is an abundance of sociological literature, yet there are gaps. We shall not attempt to cover the whole issue - or rather, all the issues involved - in great detail. We shall therefore fill in the picture with light brush strokes, often through allusion, and sometimes by deliberate omis sion.
First of all, what do we understand by the title, Technical progress and society? In fact, it is a whole spectrum of interlinked questions, none of the answers to which is by itself determinate. We are all well aware, though often in a vague and confused way, that the technical and social systems are always very closely connected, though not necessarily inevitably so. Nonetheless, there are incompatibilities that are sometimes constraining in one way or another. Both the impact of technical changes on societies and that of the rigidity of a society on technical progress are clearly evident.
Marx was undoubtedly the first to establish a link between tech niques and social organisation. The last few decades have seen much research in this area without exhausting a subject that deserves further development. The core of his reflections on technology is to be found in chapter XV of the first volume of Das Kapital.
The German thinker adopts a historical approach. The development of history, which brings about the development of society, is basically linked with the development of the means of production and of technology. Marx upholds two idees forces of the technical society of his time: the division of labour as set forth by Adam Smith, and the machine -a product of the English Industrial Revolution. "It was the machine-tool (not using the term in the narrow sense) that initiated the


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
991 Industrial Revolution in the 18th century." To quote Costas Axelos's interpretation· The use and making of tools and the correlated development of productive forces and instruments of production are the real clue to the historic future of humanity; they engender - and are engendered by - an infinite dialectic. For this is the second historic condition: "secondly, once satisfied, the first need itself leads both the act of satisfaction and the already acquired instrument of satisfaction to new needs." Natural needs and instruments of satisfaction, new needs and new instruments, have a reciprocal effect on each other although they cannot all be drawn together either into an initial dialectic proceeding from these needs, or to a primordial dialectic of the evolution of productive techniques. Need determines the instrument leading to its satisfaction and the available means of production engender new needs. The action of these two realities is indeed reciprocal, but does it not rest on the dualism of natural need on one hand, and technique on the other? The common foundation of these two is elusive, since Marx continues to be less concerned with seeking the ultimate historico-anthropological foundation than with the process of the development of technology; his point of departure is the initial relationship that man has with nature. The natural and social relationship between man and historicised nature is a relationship of struggle, and the natural history of man is a product of this struggle. Man makes tools, and his history is made through the development of the weapons of struggle, and throughout its devel opment this history itself also makes the weapons that determine its development ( . . . )
As long as men used instruments of production which were primarily natural, for instance, water, they remained subject to nature, while the means of production created by, and which create a technical civilization helped them them to confront nature more effectively; but while exploiting nature, men let themselves be exploited by other men and remained subject to things that they themselves had made. The development of the instruments of production led ineluctably to the creation and development of the machine, for the work that a machine involved showed itself to be the most susceptible to development. The machine is the ultimate expression to date of the constant and gradual development and perfection of the instrument of production. The long road of the historic development of humanity is, in a sense, the synthesis of all instruments: it contains them and does synthetically what they did analytically. Meanwhile, man has not stopped gradually alienating himself in and through his work; the era of the machine completed this alienation, and man, having himself produced the machine, finds that he has become no more than a cog in the vast machine and machinery of capitalism. Marxist positivism, so admiring of the evolution of the forces of production, waxes passionately romantic before the alienating and inhumane machine. While the machine is necessary to the development of human societies, it nonetheless crushes people; it is not so much that the machine itself crushes them, but it does so through the relationships that workers have with it.
Marx was literally dazzled by the machine. For him, it was the division of labour which led to its widespread use: the machine was introduced into the textile and not the shoe-making industry. But the effects of the machine had a profound impact on society. It transfor med the mode of labour and the social nature of the collective worker in a way that shattered any any obstacle in the way of its development.
Workers who have (often violently) fought it know this only too well.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
This new machine eliminated the skill of workers and, by bringing in women and children, turned owners into slave traders. It cut jobs:
according to Marx, the steam-driven loom put 800,000 weavers out of work without transferring them to new industries, in particular, that of manufacturing the machines themselves. "Manufacturing them created fewer jobs than their use eliminated." Not to mention the practice of working at home eliminated by the factory.
Marx's thinking is a little vague. On one hand, he formally admits the usefulness of the machine. What he condemns is "its social mode of exploitation." Once more he writes, "One must learn to distinguish between the machine and its capitalist use." To some extent this affirms that technical progress and social structure are independent of each other, or at the very least, implies that it is possible for a technical system to operate with different forms of social organisation.
Moving from Marx to our day, which is technically and socially different from his, it is evident that viewpoints vary with writers and their interests. General studies of societies usually pay little attention to technology. In his Le<;ons sur la societe industrielle, Raymond Aron clearly champions the preeminence of ideologies over techniques in the formation of social antagonisms and structures. He returns to the very debatable division of history into three great technical revolu tions.
Anthropologists suggest that there have been three great technological revolutions. The first took place in the earliest days of the human race, when man learned to use fire and simple tools; that was several hundred thousand years ago. A second period began around ten thousand years ago when man learned to cultivate plants and domesticate animals. This was the beginning of neolithic societies, and then of civilizations. And we are presently living in the third technological revolution. The irregularity of technical progress is one of the major facts of history. The differences of technical possibilities between Antiquity and yesterday's world were negligible. Thus, Caesar took roughly the same time to travel from Rome to Paris as Napoleon did. There were many technical inventions, but they did not bring about any fundamental changes in human societies.
The respective proportions of people working on the land and living in towns did not change significantly between Antiquity and the 17th or 18th centuries. The resources of a
Roman bourgeois were not much inferior to those of a bourgeois of the century of Louis XIV. On the other hand, there is a vast gap between the lifestyle of the latter and that of a present-day bourgeois.
This is, in our opinion, an oversimplication of the facts. And such oversimplifications lead directly to the conclusion that social organi sations are totally autonomous. Though one may accept that the technical system of the Neolithic period remained unchanged up to our


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
993 time, one must perforce note that social systems have evolved con siderably. Based on such assumptions the definition of technical progress is equally vague.
One may speak of technical progress (in the positive sense of the term) when a quantitative measure is available, or when the objective of technical activity may be unambiguously defined. If one says that the objective of technical activity is to command the maximum amount of energy or to control natural forces in the most efficient way possible, one has defined the sole aim of said activity. On the other hand, and this is precisely where economy differs essentially from technology, it is not possible to define a single objective or sole meaning of economic activity.
Of course, Aron does not deny the importance of technology. But "the strictly technological perspective is inadequate because, the same technology may create different modalities of the ownership of the instruments of production and class relations." And further on: "On the other hand, the degree of diversity of societies possessing the same technical equipment belongs both to the sphere of positivist investi gation and sociological analysis."
One cannot relate every transformation that has occurred during the development of historical societies to a given technological change. What technology allows are vast, vague assessments. Let us suppose, for instance, that 7% of the active population of the
United States is employed in agriculture, 45% in industry, and the rest in the tertiary sector, such a division requires a productive force (to use a Marxist term) that did not exist before the modern period. A certain amount of available energy fixes a certain margin of variation for societies, but it does not determine the details of organisation. Modern societies seem to be of a new and original type, precisely because of their energy potential.
It would seem that there is a contradiction between the last two sentences. Moreover, a distinction must be made between the econo mic, the technical and the social spheres: the relationships between them are determined in various ways, but no one of them is totally different from the others.
But because Friedmann studied the sociology of work, he has always, from his earliest research and throughout his work, focused on technical factors. "Technology is not neutral. Not only does it modify the natural environment, but it profoundly transforms society. And to cite numerous examples of this action: the effect of the mechanisation of agriculture on rural society, of means of transport on provincial life, and of the development of industrial techniques on the concentration of populations. From the Machine et humanisme series to Travail en


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
miettes, the French sociologist creates a concept of the evolution of industrial world and its social and psychological consequences - a theme to which he returns in several articles in Le Monde with tremendous intellectual honesty.
Touraine was a disciple of Friedmann. In fact, his first works deal with the modifications effected in society, and primarily working class society, as a result of technical changes. And no sector was more significant that that of the motor car. An assertion clearly proven by the study of the evolution of the working class group (in this case from Renault). In two generations, everything from the distribution of professions to the quality of technical knowledge has completely changed. The collective inquiry into the installation of a new rolling mill in a metallurgical factory focuses on an immediate change.
However, it would seem that the connection between technology and social evolution remains relatively loose. There is not a word in his
Societe post-industrielle, about technical development.
Since 1950, numerous studies, usually initiated by national or international organisations, have been trying to draw the technical and social systems together. Between the extremes lies a whole range of intermediary opinions. F. Hetman raises the issue very directly. In fact, in his opinion, it is not simply a question of knowing whether or not there is a link between the technical and social systems, but
"between technological determinism and social progress." According to the majority of prevailing opinions, he notes, "technology is the essential factor of social change." This idea may be based on a number of converging theses.
The first thesis sees "technical progress as an autonomous process.
The sequence of scientific discoveries and technical inventions seems continous and ineluctable."
Another thesis "takes the principle of the optimization of costs and advantages as is point of departure. According to this thesis the social advantages of a new technique by far outweigh the corresponding costs, making technological development inexorable. The criteria selected are historic comparisons of GNP's, productivity, working hours, urbanisation, capital intensity, etc. According to another point of view, the imperative nature of technical progress proceeds from the organizable foreseeability of technical phenomena.
"All these points of view agree that technology is the dominant force of social development." With technology, all things are possible "as


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
995 long as sufficient quantities of human and natural resources are mobilized and organized. On the other hand, technological determi nism is dialectically opposed to the evident instability and indetermi nation of social phenomena. " Hence an ever increasing gap between the two systems.
In fact it would seem that a number of issues have been badly presented. All "technological change" is not necessarily "technical progress" (nuclear destruction, ecological cataclysm, the exhaustion of raw materials, etc.). Sometimes, there are gains, but within narrow functional areas. Similarly, a conception of the social assessment of technology should be devised. Accepting the fact that technology is not independent as the prime mover of political and social action shows how the social role of technology remains ambiguous and distorted. Hence this refusal of an absolute technological determi nism: technology must be an instrument of social objectives. Clearly, it is not technology, but people who create society. But on what basis?
It is the whole methodology that is at issue here. It is important- and awareness of its importance is increasingly widespread - that a techni cal decision be subjected to an examination of its effects on society.
Indeed, a reading of reports and work published on this issue by international organisations reveals that research should deal with a whole body of consequences of a new technique or even the appli cation of an existing technique in a given place: social, as well as economic, financial and environmental dimensions must be taken into account. And withing this group, the social evaluation of technology remains very vague. "The social evaluation of technology may be defined as a process of analysis, anticipation and evaluation of techno logical factors and their impact on society, leading to the formulation of options for the decision-makers" (naturally the responsibility for his opinions rests with the author). Here the term "society" is very vague.
These investigations seem to deal with working conditions, the level of employment, repercussions on neighbouring populations (and more widely, on the environment), the geographical distribution of popu lation (the problem of towns). These documents give the distinct impression that there is a multiplicity of links between the technical and social systems, but that it is difficult to demonstrate this in a precise fashion. Unquestionably, research in this area is still relatively scarce.
Once a problem arises, it is both good and useful to tackle it with the help of historical perspective. History is a valuable source of experi-


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
ence in the social sciences and humanities. We should therefore become aware of this historical perspective which is so productive for in-depth analyses. Henceforth, it is possible to envisage a number of great themes that each person must learn about when they open their daily paper. We have deliberately reduced them to a few. Of course, there are many more, but they seem to lay less emphasis on the relationships between technology and society. After this historical introduction, we shall therefore attempt to deal with the following sectors: (a) socio-professional distribution; (b) the organisation of work; (c) technical transfers and traditional societies; (d) the hopes of those who have the heavy responsibility of managing society in the face of a technique overflowing with vitality and change, and the nature of the new powers.
And we stress the fact that this is not a second-rate sociological discourse. That does not fall within our sphere of study. Basically, it is vital that problems, from the most general to the most individual be pinpointed, counterbalanced by the solutions - at least provisional ones - that have been proposed, and that an effort be made to determine the reciprocal constraints existing between technical and social realities.
HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE
Social history and economic history have been closely linked right from the start. The history of techniques has come too late to share her sister's territory. And today, the demarcation lines are still extremely sharp. On one hand, there is a technical history of techniques; on the other, only social, polical oir economic ideologies are taken into account. Thus, though there is an immense bibliography of social history, there is a singular lack of precise studies from our own particualr perspective. On the other hand, there is not shortage of peremptory declarations. But this being said, we must try to find our bearings.
For a long time, prehistorians were the only ones to follow this line, since material remains, and in particular, tools, were their only source of information for reconstructing societies. Let us listen to them. Says


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
997 A. Leroi-Gourhan, "The statement that social institutions are closely bound up with the techno-economic apparatus is an assertion that is constantly verified by the facts. Moral problems do not change essentially, but society shapes its behaviour with the instruments that the material world offers it." The technology available to a society dictates a certain lifestyle, a certain social organisation, and these are equally affected by the natural environment. Hunters, pastoral people and farmers are distinguished by their lifestyle and by their technical system. "Primitive" man at once organized his society, his territory and his technical system which linked it all. The move to agriculture inevitably presupposed a social change comparable with the present urbanisation of the majority of the population. Once more Leroi
Gourhan writes: "With man, the relationship between the individual and society varies as a direct function of the evolution of techno economic structures, and in order to understand certain characteristics of the social body in the various stages of evolution, these techno economic structures must be defined. The most direct impact of the level of technology on the social group is related to the very density of this group. From the moment that intellectual evolution created values characteristic of homo sapiens, the relationship between technical level and social density became the principal factor of progress.
The same author makes an observation that should be developed:
"Social security is no more conceivable in a mammoth-hunting society than is the patriarchal family in an industrial society." And perhaps this is the greatest lesson of history: it is less in terms of compatibility than in terms of incompatibility that the relationships between the technical and social systems must be understood. Whether one is dealing with so-called primitive societies or with so-called developed societies, the technical system may be said to be not dominant, but it excludes certain possibilities And one might even doubt the importance of certain facts: thus we have the system of property which, to a certain extent, also conditions social relations which are independent of technique - though to a limited extent. It is obvious that the hydraulic turbine, which was a considerable advance on the old water mill, did not at first bring about any social revolution. But successive adaptations and improvements of it brought about tre mendous social transformations in a number of American industrial regions, changes comparable with those generated by the steam engine in England.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
No doubt, the same was true of the techniques of "primitive" societies. They could prove compatible with very different, or at least, apparently very different societies. As techniques become more complicated and develops, one encounters phenomena that crop up in all civilizations. Added to the specialization of work between men and women, is the specialization between men. The development of tools and the growing complexity of technical tasks lead inevitably to the division of labour. This division of labour inevitably brings with it a social organisation and economic system. And the more techniques develop, the more the group must grow, so that all work may be carried out. After herdsmen come farmers, potters and metalworkers.
One is then dealing with highly structured societies. And furthermore, the first political systems may emerge, and the town concretizes this whole transformation.
The logic of the social and technical systems must be coherent. A society of farmers cannot be organized like a society of nomads, because the lifestyle of each is closely tied to material activities, and therefore to their respective techniques. But it does not necessarily follow (and there history can enlighten us) that any one of the great variety of technical systems should necessarily dictate a social system, given the infinitely greater variety of the latter.
We must be wary of over-categorical explanations and overly rigid linkages. Lynn White, dealing with a more recent period - though in fact, only the early Middle Ages, places the invention of the stirrup at the beginnings of chivalry But to do this is to confuse everything. In ancient societies there did exist a "chivalry" that was not essentially different from that of the Middle Ages, at a time when the stirrup was unknown. The fact is that the stirrup is not an essential technique of chivalry: it was only to become indispensable later when the weight of armour became capable of unbalancing the rider.
Let us go back in time. The study of ancient societies (Egypt, the Near East, then Greece and Rome, and the same may by said of the so-called non-Classical civilizations of China, India and America) has paid little attention to techniques and their influence, As far as their organization, evolution and indeed their technical systems were con cerned, they certainly were very advanced societies. The evolution of societies is not exactly parallel to the evolution of techniques, and it is precisely this fact that creates blockages. What is most interesting in these processes is that these blockages have been attributed to only


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
999 one factor: social organisation, or at least, one of its elements, halted technical progress: the civilizations of South America, China and
Classical Antiquity are perfect examples of this.
In Classical Antiquity techniques created clearly defined social groups - those connected with pottery, metals, wood and stone.
Everything else was made at home. However, it may be that there were artisans working in textiles, probably making rich fabrics. In the first two groups, the presence of kilns and furnaces meant that, to avoid fires, their activities had to be concentrated in certain areas. It is possible that this physical separation had social repercussions. There is a clear distinction between this linking of technical progress, the division of labour and the composition of particular social groups, from the clashes that may occur between them, and from the hier archies that have less to do with individuals than with the techniques they practise.
A number of problems have been posed regarding the relationships between technology and society. They have alrady been alluded to.
The first brought together the blockage of techniques and the existence of a social reality - slavery. The same would have been true of Ancient
Greece. This view is still widely held, and we have tried earlier on to give it the place it deserved. And here once more we see clearly the relative independence of the technical and social systems. Many will agree that slavery was not in any way the cause of the blockage of ancient technique but rather that the blockage was the result of factors within the technical system itself. There is no evidence of any remarka ble technical progress when slavery began to disappear. Moreover, there have been blocked technical systems in certain civilizations that did not know slavery: South America and China, to name but two.
Today Commander Lefebvre des Noettes's thesis has been completely abandoned. At the time when the important technical innovation of which he writes was widespread, slavery had long since disappeared in
Western Europe.
Since this ancient era, it may equally be said that some techniques have given birth to particular social organizations. We have stressed this point in connection with certain artisanal techniques, and no doubt the system of corporations is much older than has been thought. There are analagous examples in the organisation of rural areas as a function of certain technical constrains. J. Chesneaux's explanation of the antiquity of Chinese communal villages and their incorporation into


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
rather strong states is that they needed to struggle together against catastrophic drought or the threat of floods. Wittfogel arrives at the same conclusion: the rice civilization required a system of irrigation that presupposed a highly organized society. The same could be said of the Spanish huertas, a society as much evidenced in the 13th century by the regulation of water as in the 19th century by Blasco Ibanez's novel.
However, F. Braudel insisted on moderating this explanation: "This explanation - which is debatable on some points, as attested by a number of contradictions - is clearly much too simplistic. If there is
(and clearly there is indeed) a determinism of controlled water, of the water needed for rice (or any other) cultivation, of the rice itself - like any other basic food, these constraints constitute no more than one of the elements of a more complex edifice. This is a truth that should not be lost sight of. But the constraints of the rice civilization should not be lost sight of either. They matter. They always do.
One could mention other constraints imposed by nature and by the technical needs to respond to nature, constraints which produce very rigid collective social "formulae." From a certain time, probably from very ancient times- and doubtless up to the 12th or 13th Centuries, the production of iron was organized communally. It is possible that during the agricultural dead season there were permanent groups of mine operators who extracted iron ore together, as well as the making of charcoal and the reduction of iron ore in blooming furnaces.
Collective operations of this kind, which must have functioned from the First to the 4th Centuries supplying the Roman Empire with iron, have been found in Kieke, Poland. Some were also found in Bohemia in later times. Texts from the 8th to the 12th Centuries reveal that the same was true of France - in Normandy, the Alps, the Pyrenees, and
Champagne. They were also to be found in Italy (the Scalve Valley), in Germany (the Fulda region), and in England (Forest of Dean). From the day that the hammer furnace appeared in the first half of the 12th century, this continuous-running device was to overturn the previous organisation. The production of iron them passed to the monasteries, the nobility, and then to wealthy burghers: the old communites disintegrated more or less slowly.
As we have noted, the technical system of the Middle Ages was different from that of Antiquity. And we would agree that the two societies differed. Certainly, there were similarities. Probably the most striking are to be found in the artisans organisations. To a certain


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1001 extent, the revival and strength of the Medieval corporations hindered technical progress. The rigidity of the corporate society carried with it a relative fixity of techniques. From the technical point of view, the rules of corporation regulations mainly consisted of prohibitions: the use of certain materials and processes and machines. This was true of textiles, for instance, and affected the use of dyes, greases, carding and the spinning wheel. Since technical progress more or less threatened to overthrow the artisans, and consequently, social structures, it was rejected, and this time, as a conscience-salver, reference was made to product quality.
In other cases, the differences are more notable. On one hand, the development of a certain number of agricultural techniques - the clearing of land as much as the three-field system - tended to bring about the elimination of serfdom, or at least its replacement by various forms of rental. On the other hand, they had a tremendous impact on village organisation, and the whole life of rural populations. The texts that we have today cannot tell us of the consequences of the move from the swing plough to the plough, and then to the heavy plough, which required ever more powerful yokes of draught animals, and therefore a different type of peasantry. The same is true of the northward spread of the vine. In the realm of agriculture, where natural and technical factors are both particularly important, social structures are undenia bly shaped by them to some extent.
Certainly, there are other points of view. Some may think - and with reason - that feudalism, which is indeed a social organisation, is in no way linked to the development of techniques, the spread of the water-mill, or even the appearance of the stirrup. But the Chateau did appear; it did not exist in ancient civilizations that centred round towns, and the art of war also underwent some changes.
Doubtless the transformations engendered by the period known as the Renaissance have not been stressed enough. We shall not go over the transformations of the technical system; they have been mentioned earlier. Moreover, everyone is familiar with the social and political revolutions that accompanied them. A whole world was transformed, a whole new world was being created. Parallel to the setting up of a new technical system was its seizure by centralized powers, and through these centralized powers, a whole new society was created.
The cannon and modern armies did away with the final vestiges of the feudal system. The corporations and all their technical organisation


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
came under royal control. With mercantilism, which was as much an economic as a political system, society was organised differently. If technology became state business, the technician - in the widest sense of the word - acquired another dimensiosn. And this was true of every sphere.
Social reactions and tensions are in evidence here. The printers' strike of the 16th century; the inventer of a loom for making stockings thrown into the port of Dantzig at the end of the century, an event pointed out by Marx; The dismantling of the chfiteaux under Riche lieu; the development of a certain type of capitalism linked to the possession of perfected techniques (for instance, mining techniques).
All the technical works, from Agricola to Olivier de Serres and Bernard Palissy, from Alberti to Biringuccio, deal with social change.
The old social system had become totally incompatible: technical revolution and social revolution must take place at the same time, while preserving the possibility of options.
Social structures assumed other dimensions, and the evolutionary process was to continue to the end of the 18th century, with its
Industrial Revolution evidencing the repercussions of the Renaissance revolution. Then several elements gradually appear. Firstly, a working class - in the modern sense of the term - that was to some extent distanced from the corporate guild system. In the 17th Century, although techniques were not very advanced, the establishment of large privileged works was to reassure this class. The Saint-Gobain works, the Van Robais works in Abbeville, and Dijonval's in Sedan were practically factories in the present sense of the word: with a similar concentration of labour and a permanent labour force. The connection between the development of manufacturing and that of the division of labour is no doubt less clear than Marx would have us believe. To tell the truth, we are poorly informed vis a vis the or ganisation of labour in these great works of the Ancien Regime: it has always existed in all technically advanced societies, and the example of Adam Smith proves clearly that it could exist, at quite a high level, in small workshops which could in no way be defined as manufacturers.
The second element, almost as important as the first, was the formation of a class of technicians. Of course, there have always been technicians: the architect is as old as construction, and artillerists as old as projectiles. But let us consider the type of man produced by the
Renaissance: artists, military engineers, architects and hydraulic engi-


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1003 neers. Military engineers and all the other technicians required by centralized states were to take over. Consider Vauban in France,
Christopher Polhem in Sweden, and many others, who, at the end of the 17th century took charge of the technical progress of the day.
Consider the extraordinary management of the dykes in the United Provinces of the Netherlands of which Simon Stevin is a remarkable example. The management of technology depended on these men.
They were not yet technocrats: they carried out what was asked of them because only they knew how to do it. The creation of this new social type (in fact it may be unwise to speak of a social class) coincides very precisely with the establishment of a new technical system and preparation for the one to follow. It also coincides with the application of the rules of economic policy known as mercantilism.
There is no better example of this than France. Systematization in every sphere became a state policy in Colbert's day, and was to spread up the end of the 18th century. It's basic pillars were the armed forces
(including the navy), the inspection of works, public works (especially bridges and roads), and even the administration of justice. A world of technicians applying diverse techniques, yes, but all harmonious, all coherent. And all of it institutionalized, whether it involved great principles (the Academie des Sciences), or their application (the great public bodies), and very soon, training schools. From the day that qualifications (i.e. training) and employment (i.e. state control of techniques coincided, it was inevitably quite another type of society, one that rejected birth and land ownership.
Interpenetration developed quickly. Technical know-how and capi tal, whether associated or disassociated, were connected. The nobility invested industry which was managed by technicians. But because their own property, land, and practically their raison d'etre was involved, the nobility did not want to fall behind in other areas.
Agricultural societies, model farms, and rationalized mining multi plied throughout the county. When the new technical revolution unveiled future changes, there was a whole world ready to welcome it.
It is very difficult to determine the figures, and it is a pity that no attempts have been made to do something in this area. Even if the number of technicians (and boundaries must yet be defined) was relatively low, they exerted considerable influence. It was artillery officers (admittedly, one of them was the son of an ironmaster) who founded Le Creusot; it was Monge, an engineering officer, who


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
participated in the development of science, and was soon to reorganize all this higher technical education. It was works inspectors who urged
French industry to adopt English innovations. All of them were helped by governments, the vast majority of which were perfectly aware of the changes ahead, and by magistrates, themselves also aware of the appropriateness of systems, and who trained new judicial officers favourable to the new techniques.
From top to bottom, it was a society on the move. though not without reservations or conservatism. We shall give but one example of this, one that is as typical of Spain as it is of France. Towards the mid-18th century there was an attempt to introduce certain pre industrial techniques into the countryside so as to boost peasant income. This was the case with the spinning wheel, unfamiliarto many, despite its long history, and destined to supply yam to a burgeoning textile industry. A national campaign was mounted. Spinning wheels were sent just about everywhere, and "training centres" for spinners were organized. Then suddenly, the project came up against violent opposition. The authorities were accused of attempting to attract young women away from the countryside, of somehow creating uphea val in a rural society that did not want these things even though it was not satisfied with its level of income. Certain priests even went as far as to denounce the "white slave trade," as we would call it today.
According to them, this collection of young women was destined to provide the American colonies with women. Nowadays we encounter analogous situations in certain Third World countries, where some people wish to introduce (if not impose) techniques which perforce bring about changes in traditional societies. And traditional and technical systems overlap each other very closely. Opposition took different forms in Spain. The spinning wheel confines the woman to the house, whereas the distaff and spindle allowed her to go out and have a chat with the neighbour or mind the cows. If Joan of Arc had spun at a wheel, she might never have heard her voices.
This whole aspect of social evolution has been studied very little.
The situation on the Continent differed from that in England. But analyses of the technical conditions of the industrial revolution by the historians of the various countries vary greatly. And yet it would seem that there is a basic problem, precisely in the relations between the technical system and the the social system. In fact, the vast body of literature on the Industrial Revolution of the end of the 18th century


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1005 evades the question. In most cases, when they discuss society, it is in an attempt to define the social position of the inventers and innovaters.
Unlike that of the Continent, English society towards the end of the 18th century was not rigid. It was no doubt partly this factor that made it possible for a new technical system to be implanted there. One system certainly did not influence the other, but they were not incompatible. England's Industrial Revolution did not have social foundations, but it benefitted from a favourable social environment.
The situation in France was vastly different. Note first of all - and this is not stressed enough - that the new technical system was not truly established until the 1780's. Commerce and industry in France were not as dynamic as in England. French rationalism effectively created a technical class: civil, shipbuilding and mining engineers, veterinary medicine, works inspectors, as well as officers of the army's learned bodies all belonged to the state, not to industry, commerce or agricul ture. Given a more rigid society, technology was institutionalized, thanks to a social group, though the group remained isolated despite its efforts, and, admittedly, despite its success. Part of this technical elite was to take part in the political (and therefore social) revolution that the country needed. The monarchy was aware of this devel opment. It was indeed in the 18th century that they proposed to add a mercantile nobility (one that was both mercantile and industrial) to the ancient nobilities of the army and 'noblesse de robe [Translator's note:
people who owed their patents of nobility to administrative or legal posts that they or their ancestors had bought]. But, in a sense, this meant strengthening divisions that were not very favourable to techni cal changes.
On the other hand, the 19th century demonstrates the impact of the technical system on the social system. Social evolution was perhaps slow, but it asserted itself in the second third of the century. At that time, more or less throughout those countries in the process of industrialization, a so-called egalitarian society and a real working class developed. Says Engels in his The condition of the working class in England, "The history of the working class began in the second half of the the last century with the invention of the steam engine and machines made for working with cotton. We know that these inven tions set off an industrial revolution which at the same time transfor med the whole of bourgeois society, a revolution whose significance in the history of the world is only just beginning to be understood.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
The multiplicity of solutions led to an incomplete imposition of the constraints of the new techniques on society. In principle, it was an egalitarian society, but in reality, the picture was very mixed. It is therefore hardly surprising that there is a lack of serious studies on the suject - and that is true of all the technically advanced countries.
Except for a few rare exceptions, no one has studied the employers or their relationship with the political class. The emergence of the working class is practically unkown. Not to mention the middle classes
- commerce, the civil service, that whole tertiary sector which assumed a growing importance throughout the century.
This development is certainly difficult to calculate. It varies according to the country, and even the region. Moreover, it is very difficult to establish boundaries between social groups. Certain enquiries, in short, certain figures derived from official statistics, must be taken with a pinch of salt. Some of them should only be taken as rough indications of magnitude.
However, it does seem that 1860 may be taken as a cut-off point, at least for most continental countries. It is only from this time that the truly industrial society acquires its identity, the 1848 revolution (or revolutions) having created a sort of break in a significant number of countries.
There is no overall study of the composition of the working class.
But a few monographs contribute to a general appreciation. Indeed, there are two sides to the question: on one hand, the make-up of the working class, and, on the other, its evolution.
The recruitment of the working class was certainly much slower on the Continent than in England. The first example - a chintz factory, established in 1752 in a village bordering the Lake of Neuchatel. Right from the start, the structure of the group of workers was clearly established. At the top were the designers and engravers, very skilled workers, organized into four years of apprenticeship. Next came the printers who applied the first block and the warpers who applied the rest. All these workers went through a three-year apprenticeship.
Most of the other workers were generally not very skilled: hand painters who painted some colours on with a brush, various unskilled labourers who washed, satined or wrapped the fabric, and finally, tireurs - young children individually assigned to a skilled worker as assistants. The division of work is thus relatively advanced. The first recruits for the factory came from the immediate area. Village popu-


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1007 lation was already losing momentum and the vineyard provided a very under-employed labour force. Skilled labourers were more mobile, and they came from a much wider geographic area - even from beyond
Switzerland. Note that the partial decentralization of hand-painting that the problems of recruitment were considerably eased.
Technical progress was to modify these structures. For technical reasons, the roller machine was only gradually introduced. Thus the disappearance of block printers was relatively slow. "The disappea rance of hand-painters was much more sudden. The discovery of new application colours between 1810 and 1820 made their work useless.
Child tireurs disappeared in a similar fashion in the 1830's, following the invention of the mechanical tireur, a device which allowed the printers and warpers to dispense with their services." Thus Technical progress (in this instance the development of mechanisation) consider ably increased the proportion of skilled labourers, and in particular, it reduced the role of female and child labour. The following statistics clearly demonstrate this development.

Switzerland, apart from the brushers, who could work at home, these workers were concentrated in one spot. The vast majority of workers were local and rural. But note that here, as in other industries, there was a high proportion of foreigners among the more qualified workers
- in this instance mainly Swiss. Moreover, they were recruited by an employer who was himself Swiss.
We have the list and origins of the initial core workers at Le Creusot;
this too is from the final years of the 18th century. The unskilled labour came from Franche-Comte, Alsatia, and above all, Lorraine, where they were recruited by Ignace de Wendel, one of the originators of the enterprise.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
In the first half of the 19th century, that is to say, precisely at the time that English techniques began to penetrate the Continent, a number of phenomena may be observed.
The first concerns highly skilled workers. And it is an old one.
Thanks to tax exemptions and special status, and on the condition that they taught their craft to local people or settled down permanently in the country through marriage, Colbert had arranged for the recruit ment of foreign workers to bring into the country manufacturing processes hitherto unkown in France. Thanks to English entre preneurs like Holken or Waddington who travelled with workers from their own country, English-type spinning mills were set up in the second half of the 18th century. The same movement recurred follow ing the revolutionary and imperial interval. It has been claimed that between 1820 and 1830 there were some ten thousand English workers in France. An investigation of the iron industry shows that in the majority of Fench iron and steel works, the rollers and puddlers - in other words, the most skilled workers - were still English. The same is true of Le Creusot, Decazeville, Liege, and Vitkovice (Moravia).
These trainer-workers to not appear to have been permanent immi grants. They taught a craft to French workers who were probably already engaged in parallel or similar industries. For this class of workers, this did not mean a total change, but rather a change of techniques in an industry where they had already worked. It would seem that after 1830, apart from a few exceptions, these jobs were generally taken over by local people.
There is a corollary to this situation. Highly skilled workers were rare and were literally snapped up. There is evidence of in the 18th century, involving designers, engravers and printers of chintz as well as blacksmiths. This phenomenon was to continue to the first half of the
19th century, both with English and Continental workers. This was equally true at a time when techniques were steadily improving and becoming more complex, creating veritable worker aristocracies which have been very rarely studied. Puddlers, rollers and glass blowers passed on their craft within the family, and thus practised a
Malthusian policy whose aim was to maintain high wage levels.
Professional and technical heredity was added to social heredity, because the practice of a technique was still passed on by word and gesture, and could not be learned from text books or in schools. In
France, as in just about all other countries, large-scale professional


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1009 training- usually carried out by charitable institutions - dealt only with drawing and arithmentic. Employers tried to break these monopolies by setting up their own schools practically in the factory: Montgolfier did this in the first Anno nay paper mill at the end of the 18th century, and this change spread in the first half of the 19th century.
A look at the labour force as a whole, of which unskilled workers usually constituted the majority, leads to two observations. The first relates to the difficulty of recruiting labour around 1860. At least three times, between 1830 and 1860, the mining industry of Liege experi enced a serious shortage of workers. A similar situation arose in the mining region of Northern France, as well as the iron and steel mills of
Ales and Decazeville. So envoys were sent to regions with an analo gous industrial tradition, in an attempt to entice workers. From the moment a factory found itself in difficulties, there would be a great rush to recruit its workers. This happened in Le Creusot between
1832-1833, and Ales, 1834-1835.
In most cases, recruitment was carried out within a limited geogra phical area. This was the case with the Cockerill factories at Liege, with a widening of the radius of recruitment (as a percentage of the work force):
0-5 km 1826 46. 1 1830 40 1836 18.9 1840 30 1846 28.4 1848 5.3 5-12.5 km 38.4 40 54 30 27.3
24.4 12.5-25 km 15.3 20 26. 1 22.5 32.4 17.7 more than 25 km 0.9 17.5 6.1 4.4 Note that after 1840 the origins of all the workers is not known. The company probably sought a considerable proportion of its workers outside of the region. At Decazeville, competition with other industries was less intense than at Liege, and workers were recruited from the countryside within a relatively narrow radius.
Two things must be said. Firstly, in many cases, the level of technology did not demand a high proportion of skilled labourers, but on the other hand, a lack of means of mechanized transmissions meant that unskilled labour had to be employed in considerable numbers.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Hence the discrepancy between the scarcity of certain professions and the difficulty of recruiting large numbers of workers. Moreover, the workers recruited from the neighbouring countryside are still not quite detached from agriculture. At Decazeville, one notes that a number of workers abandoned their jobs during the vintage season, and at Ales, during the silkworm harvest.
The problem of training the industrial proletariat was closely linked to the social and economic situation of the surrounding countryside, which at the time, engulfed factories in a rural environment. The recruitment of workers for the metallurgical factory at Vitkovice in
Moravia covered an area of 2100 square kilometres. In this area, about 17% of rural property provided enough for their owners and their families to live on. Consequently, the remaining 83% of the population must have been wage-earners. Worker numbers rose from 45 in 1833 to
1300 in 1842, and 2596 in 1850. During this period, 32% of workers came from within a radius of ten kilometres. In this grouping, one notes the distribution indicated below by percentages, a rather rough one, due to the the fact that certain categories were not defined clearly enough. The artisans in particular were mixed with urban people; and some of them came form small towns and others from the countryside:
farmers . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
58. 1 city dwellers,artisans . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
17. 1 workers . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
15.7 liberal professions. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
