5.8 Among the farmers, small landowners still represented 41 .88% ;
persons referred to as peasants, 23.91% ; sub-tenants, 17. 11% ; and "gardeners," 14.53% .
The second problem has been more fully covered. It concerns the entry of women and children into this semi-modem industry. And here again, the rudimentary level of technology opened factory gates wide for them, at least the factories of a certain type of industry. It is rather curious that the percentage of women and children continued to rise during the whole of the first half of the 19th century. In certain industries, this proportion reached as high as 70% . We shall see that later on, the pressure of mechanisation was to generate a reflux; today we are witnessing a resurgence of female labour due to new, partly automated industries.


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1011 Even in what would today be called factories, in the modern sense of the word, the organisation of work in the first half of the
19th century seems to have been quite peculiar. As has been justly said:
Very often, behind the large concern's external appearance of modernity lay a thor oughly traditional organisation of work. As a rule, rates in the collieries (of the St.
Etienne region) were arrived at through a process of bidding and bargaining between work gangs, with tenders based on tonnage or the length of veins. And even in the factories of Givors and Saint-Chamond, it was based on the casting of the blast furnace.
In glass works, the factory (several hundreds of workers, as at Rive-de-Gier) was no more than an administrative shell where the real sphere of production was the "place" in front of the furnace. The glassblower, who was the one who held the contract, paid his assistants himself: first assistant, foa and mixers, who, in their turn, paid the unskilled maintenance workers and women and children who packed the bottles. One could cite many example which show that the factory was not the articulated organism of the 20th century, but rather an aggregate of individual trades in which daily work could preserve the appearance of a crafts industry.
It was an exact translation - the area of work organisation - of a technical system in which the various stages of production were broken up into groups, and in which each element of the old technical system was transferred to the manufacturing process. Lequin is right to refer to this as an aggregation of crafts with each one retaining its own peculiar organisation. Furthermore, as mechanisation increased the concentration of worker populations as well as the dangers that some people attributed to it , and because techniques made it possible, activities were dispersed. Some (as was the case with a number of
Mulhouse industrialists) were able to keep their hand-loomers in the countryside. Following the 1832 and 1834 Lyonese revolts, others
(such as Lyonese silk Manufacturers) were to move their workshops out to the departments of Ain or Isere where they could employ a female work force that was both cheaper and more docile. But such a choice can only be made under certain technical conditions. This field is full of material for research: on the organisation of industrial enterprises, attempts to establish factories in selected geographical areas, the problem of supervisors and discipline, all of which are functions of a particular technical system.
As far as the available documentation would seem to indicate, the period corresponding to the Second Empire was a time of transition.
On one hand, there was a tremendous increase of industrial produc tion and a considerable development of certain activities such as


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
transport (railways, steamships). On the other hand it was a turning point of two technical systems, of which the more recent would not be completed until the last two decades of the 19th century.
The changes then taking place in France were revealing. The overall population of workers remained more or less stable. On one hand female and child labour was clearly declining. This certainly led to an obvious increase in mechanisation in labour-intensive industries. It is equally certain that the proportion of skilled labour also increased, though admittedly, more slowly. All of this was more or less linked to the first part of the "second" industrial revolution which came in the
1860s. The introduction of the pneumatic drill led to a dramatic rise in the output of miners, and there is evidence of increasing productivity in the iron and steel industry.
Two other important phenomena must be pointed out. Indeed, they are very closely linked. The concentration of manufacturing in fac tories was to eliminate most of the work done at home (mostly in rural areas) that had come about in the textile industries. While spinning had quickly entered the factories since, weaving had remained rural for a long time. The spread of automatic looms was to bring about the same transformation rather suddenly. Working at home was a very important source of extra income for farmers with very little land. Its elimination brought with it such a financial imbalance that these rural workers were forced to leave the land. A certain number of them continued with their crafts in the factory which, except in the North, was often established in a semi-rural setting. Others swelled the numbers of the industrial proletariat.
The second phenomena is exactly parallel to the first. From 1850, even without the determining factor of industry, the rural economy gave rise to emigration. The mechanisation of agriculture was indeed only beginning. In France it took one hour to harvest 100 square meters of corn with a sickle in 1800, fifteen minutes with a scythe in
1850 and two minutes with a reaper ter in 1900. There was also Whitney's cotton gin (from 1793). Here the problem was slightly different. Cotton depended on slavery, and clearly it was as much this ever more advanced mechanisation as ideas that led to the American
Civil War, and finally brought about the abolition of slavery. Besides, mechanisation gave value to the great wheat fields in the centre of the country. It is equally true that after 1865, industrialization made great advances in the United States. It was then that a truly industrial society


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1013 - hitherto only roughly outlined - was born. Taking 100 as a base in 1929, a work hour gave 27.3 in 1869, and 66. 1 in 1914.
As we have seen, the second part of this "second" industrial revolu tion takes place between 1880 and 1900, a period which saw an accler ation of all these changes. It has been pointed out that in Alsatia, the advent of the bicycle made it possible for small farmers to work in the factory, leaving their wives and children to manage the farm. This expansion of the industrial class towards the end of the 19th century is still little known. The great depression of the Eighties, which came early in the Lyonese factory (from 1876), later in other industries
(1890-1900 in the glove factory at Grenoble, or the trimmings factory in Saint-Etienne that we have just studied) is seen as initiator of another industrial and working world; a world in which we still live today to a certain extent. It may be that the final development of the new techni cal system played an important role. And let us not forget that it was at this very time that phylloxera completed the ruin of the vineyards.
It was not technical factors that brought about the introduction of foreign or colonial labour (for instance the Chinese in France during the war, and later the Italians and Poles) during the First World War.
This labour force was generally unskilled - except in the mines - and destined to take the place of local people who were at the time more educated. But, and we repeat, the technical changes which were then only the development of the previous technical system, were no longer a driving force.
The long half century stretching from 1850 to 1914 was to see the steady rise of another development - the tertiary sector. Certainly, technology, in the narrow sense of the world was not responsible. Both public and private administrative tasks continued to increase. In fact, the management of industrial development demanded an ever greater
"logistic" support.
Take for example the public civil service. The following figures show the number of inhabitants per civil servant:
1839 1870 1900 1914 France 261 165 85 United States 128 128 68 54


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
A review of the figures for the general distribution of the active population between the various sectors is equally revealing. This concerns the proportions of this active population in the primary, secondary and tertiary sectors.
1850 1880 1900 1910 1930 1940 United States 65 38 18 primary Germanay 43 35 27 sector France
53 42 37 Great Britain 22 9 6 United States 17 27 34 secondary Germany 37 40 41 sector France
25 31 30 Great Britain 48 47 46 United States 18 35 48 tertiary Germany 20 24 32 sector France
22 27 33 Great Britain 30 44 48 This table makes it possible to measure the general professional distribution in some highly industrialized countries. Obviously,the dates of industrial take-off vary from country to country. The advance of Great Britain in this area is undeniable. At the end of the period, that country remained at the head of the most modern professional distribution.
The other professions, many of which are closely related to techno logy are almost totally unknown to us. The problem of executives, which is studied so much today, does not seem to have seriously attracted historians. However, some French historians have stressed the importance of certain state bodies, particularly civil engineering and mining, to industrial development and technical innovation. The replacement of the old capitalist classes by technicians, the interchangeability of members of these technical state bodies and the management of private enterprise, should also be analyzed. Still, replacement of inherited employer status by what has been referred to as "assumed employer status" was only just beginning.
As for the liberal professions, they were equally ignored. But here also, technical evolution was not a major factor of the evolution of


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1015 these social categories. But extensive research would show that it was not totally absent.
A new factor was to come into play in the closing years of the 19th century - the organisation of labour. Two studies were carried out more or less simultaneously. The older of the two - in conception at least - is that of the Frenchman Henri Fayol who gave a general overview in Administration industrielle et generate - today a largely forgotten work. It deals with management (in the broad sense of the word) of companies where everything from techniques to book keeping, from the training of employees to the hierarchy and social and humanitarian considerations. Unified management, coordination and control are in fact the product of industrial dictatorship, however softened this might be by social and humanitarian considerations. He arrives at a general theory of society based on his outline of industrial organisation, in which skill is the only entitlement to a given power. He draws up an extraordinary table of concordance which places the worker, soldier, ordinary churchgoer and citizen side by side; on the next level, the foreman, non-commissioned officer, assistant priest, and town coucillor; higher up, the engineer, leading citizen, senior partner, commander, priest, and mayor; and higher still, the general manager, general, bishop, prefect, and so on.
The scientific organisation of labour interests us more directly.
Surely, it is hardly necessary to say that Frederick Winslow Taylor was its disseminator, if not its inventor. More or less self-taught, Taylor somehow trained himself on the job. He became a good engineer, and even inventor, or semi-inventor. At the same time, he took an interest in the organisation of labour within a given technical system in which, on one hand, machine tools were almost perfected, and on the other hand, a large enough proportion of manufactured products could be adapted to the new methods: mass production, the rationalization of components, and relatively simple assembly formulaes.
Friedmann notes that Taylor's principles are a magma of very diverse ideas of varying value. He introduced the experimental method to the field of human industry, which until then had been left up to empiricism. The experiments on machine tools began in the autumn of 1880, and then gradually spread to all industrial work.
Task analysis became a universally applicable method, thanks to a precise definition of timing that could be applied to any kind of operation, and rendered it possible both to make a strict selection of movements, and to determine the "normal" length of time


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
necessary for all tasks done in workshops and offices. Thus, a definition of an exemplary method, "the one best way" 'r"as arrived at, and it was to spread by instructions in the factory and reach all establishments that had been won over to scientific organization.
The whole process would begin with a purely technical study, a physiccft analysis of the operation to be carried out. A second stage consisted of studying the work being carried in the production workshops. From the start, this naturally presupposed a certain stabili zation of techniques, with any change requiring new research and new definitions. Technical progress must be immediately transferred to the everyday life of the workshops, thanks to the scientific analysis of jobs.
In fact, movements were resolved into a certain number of basic elements, and this was the foundation of the fragmentation of tasks.
The most important part of the analysis of movements was timing.
Frank B. Gilbreth's cinematographic technique was translated into chronologically distributed figurative diagrams; thus the division of operations was well established. The conditions for applying such methods almost went without saying: teams of workers carrying out various successive operations, a spirit of discipline, and, to a certain extent, of abnegation. Ultimately, Taylor's aim was to bring about a lessening of human misery. Departing from purely technical postu lates, he ended up with a highly moral result. He did not raise any of the questions that his method would direct at the working world, if not at the directors of business concerns.
There was a danger that technology might be rigidified to a certain extent. No longer was initiative, preference or training asked of employees, regardless of their level. Everything was imposed from the outside. Strictly speaking, this was a drill, or in other words, a question of reflex and not of imagination. Moreover, there was a tendency to replace the skilled worker with a mass of specialized, unskilled work ers. To quote Friedmann once more:
Did not [Taylor] himself anticipate the essential issues when he said to his workers at Midvale - the firsts subjects of his experiments - "You are not asked to think; there are other people here paid to do that."? And can there be any further doubt regarding his views when one sees him at the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, priding himself on a drill that makes the worker produce more, not through his own initiative, but by carrying out orders promptly and punctiliously? Such precise order, such strict surveillance, that the worker, even if he is hostile, or warned by his union against any experiments of this kind, is automatically induced to produce more.
This is the opposite of the view discussed earlier, of the factory as an aggregate of artisanal workshops.


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1017 There is surely no need to expand at length on Taylor's system. It is by nature at once technical, psychological and social, as well as political and moral. Fragment jobs, individualize and isolate the worker, teach him a minimum of movements and knowledge necessary for the partial operation that he is responsible for, inflict on him the fastest pace possible, and all this thanks to a system of transmitting the parts to be assembled, or materials to be worked, that corresponds most exactly with work standards. And above this was a framework of regulators, and supervisors of very kind. And capping it all was the assembly line which first appeared in car factories and developed under the influence of needs engendered by the first World War. In fact, the assembly line was first used at Ford for the Model T 14 in 1913, and systematized in 1924. All this was only possible through an advanced mechanisation both of the machine tools themselves and of the channels of transimission. Moreover, it was essential to conceive a mass production whose elements must be totally standardized. Taylo rism and the assembly line were wholly based on a well defined technical system: (a) The breaking down of a complex, skilled oper ation into basic, non-skilled components; (b) the synchronization of the flow of raw materials or semi-finished products with the movements of the operator; (c) the interchangeability of components.
The other advantage of this method was the rapid training of workers who shortly after were to be given the absurd label of
"specialized" or "semi-skilled" workers. With a doctor, specialization denotes the most advanced skill; a worker's specialization is the most basic, the most lowly. The following statistics giving the training of
Ford workers in 1926 as a percentage of the working population, are very revealing:
no more than one day. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 43% one to eight days . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 36% one to two weeks . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
6o/o one month to one year. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 14 % up to six years . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1°/o Almost 80% of the workers received no more than eight days training at most. This tallies well with Friedmann's report on an
English car manufacturer: "We are trying to reduce skill as much as possible. " This was leading to a totally downgraded apprenticeship, or even the total disappearance of apprenticeship. Within half a century, there was a move from a workers' world to one that was quite different.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Car manufacturing, which was certainly the ideal sphere for the assembly line - at least initially - has been mentioned often. But clock and watch-making provides equally good examples. And even the textile industry, and in particular, the clothing industry, could provide equally good examples.
The making of waistcoats at a factory in the English Midlands was carried out by 65 operation stations. In 1932, jacket making in a Dutch factory involved 40 operations stations. Some years later, this rose to
54, with a reduction of overall production time.
No more need be said about the aȩsembly line. Its economic and technical advantages have been magnified, particularly in so far as the fragmentation of tasks eliminated qualifications and, correlatively, lowered the level of salaries, especially in labour-intensive industries, thus reducing worker resistance. On the other hand, sociologists and psychotechnicians denounced the negative effects of repeating the same movements, the monotony of the work, and particularly of work that is never completed. Volumes have been written on the subject.
Friedmann stressed the difference between this system and the old methods of work organisation. The main difference was the mobility of the component supply which passed before the worker, whereas, in all the forms of work known up to that time, these elements were stationary, and it was the worker who moved around his work. One must also note that there were different kinds of assembly line. The technical advantages were certainly considerable, especially the pos sibility of maintainaing order in the workshop. There were those who wished to see this as a major stage in the division of work. The only problem was the parallel erosion of qualifications.
Let us turn once more to Friedmann: "Assembly line work therefore deserves neither excessive honour nor vilification, neither the naive or crude apologias of certain "technicists," nor the stirring maledictions of certain novelists. It was an essential stage of the contemporary history of industry in which we still find ourselves (1963), and whose passing is barely perceptible. It is difficult - but not impossible - to humanize it from now on. "
In fact, it is very difficult to get a precise idea of the technical society of the years 1900 to 1940. It may be approached in a number of ways, none of them truly satisfying. Yet, someone speaking of our day does feel that they are unquestionably far removed from those times.


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1019 Certain situations and relations between social groups have survived and seem to perpetuate themselves.
Historians have not sufficiently considered the relationships bet ween the social system and the technical system, although the extent to which they are independent of each other is yet to be measured. When a sociologist writes: to a large extent the steam engine imposed its logistics on industry, and through industry, on society at large, one needs to know the reasons for his stand. It would seem that there is a gap between social realities and social theories. And from this point of view, one could say that techniques are thrown out by intellectualism, both on the right and left of the political spectrum. Meanwhile, it is indeed true that the material world as a whole influences a person's concept of the social category that he belongs to. It all seems to be based on a certain number of technical objects whose possission falls into a hierarchy of values: car, pleasure boat, central heating, elevator, bathroom, running water. The taxman even uses them as criteria at times. If technology suddenly makes considerable advances and per mits access to these possessions, the hierarchy must be quickly reconsidered. Today the car is no more a symbol of social class than running water. So this opens the way for all sorts of confusions. Should the 0 and A levels or high school diploma - or what remains of it - still be accepted as an external sign of wealth? Certainly all societies that are invaded by technology seek refuges to organize themselves within the blurred outlines of a certain number of non-material values. And this has always been the case: remember the ideal societies of Aristotle and Plato.
TODA Y'S TECHNICAL SOCIETIES
Research has made considerable progress and studies have multiplied.
But we still need an overview that would link the technical and social systems, with each preserving its own specificity, its relative indepen dance, the importance of the connections between the social system and other systems, especially ideological and political systems. If lifestyle has been profoundly changed on many levels by technical development, it is no less true that social organisation depends on a large number of factors more or less unconnected to technology.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
However, it would seem that the development of techniques brings about social changes that are equally apparent in socialist and so-called capitalist countries. The differences exist only on the level of techni cal developments and industrialization. The societies of developed countries are ultimately similar, whatever the ideology they profess.
Third World societies are very different from each other.
We shall be focusing only on certain aspects of this social organi sation.
Socio-Professional Distribution This is certainly the most spectacular aspect of the effects of technical progress. Certain trends were already apparent before the Second
World War. They accelerated remarkably in the post-war period. On the whole, it would seem that there was a more or less rapid weakening of the primary sector, a stabilization of the secondary sector, and an explosion of the tertiary sector which is clearly taking first place.
The indices of productivity are certainly a good indicator of the various recorded movements. In France, the index of per capita output
(taking 100 as a base for 1935-1939) stood at 100 after the war in 1949, and at 180 for the period 1958-1961. By 1960, American manufacturing industries had already moved up from a base of 100 in 1929 to 235 per work hour. Thus it was technical phenomena that brought about changes in the structures of the active population, and all this at a steadily accelerating rate, at least in periods of prosperity.
PERCENTAGE OF THE ACTIVE POPULATION IN THE U.S.

secondary sector . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
tertiary sector . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1940 14 31 55 1960-1964 8 32 60 Still in the United States, the numbers of mine workers fell by 25.6% between 1953 and 1963; numbers of agricultural workers fell by
24.7% , and those in the processing industries by 1 .4% . Bearing in mind that the level of production rose considerably, these simple figures show the tremendous influence of technical changes.


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1021 Obviously, all this was not achieved without creating certain tensions which are more apparent today than in the 19th century when social changes were just as sudden as they are today, but did not provoke the widespread reactions witnessed today. And people are much more aware of social changes that affect them directly, than of technical changes of which they have a vague, and, furthermore, often mistaken idea. And we know equally well how much what we call orientation - whether it be professional, scholastic, or academic - proves totally incapabable in most cases, of mastering situations where there is a clash of old social reflexes. The more rigid a society is, the more difficult it is to resolve problems. France is an obvious example of this. When the French Minister of Universities proposed an institution whose aim was to coordinate higher technical education, it was only designated as a sub-directorate. Technology is shamefully underesti mated.
The explosion of the tertiary sector is therefore the most recent and significant phenomenon; to such an extent that the question has been asked whether the rapid increase in the overall cost of services was not responsible for price rises (and, as a result, widespread inflation) evident in the highly industrialized countries. The emergence of new activities (leasing and software, to mention just two that are partly linked to technical progress), the expansion of traditional branches, and the movement of the tertiary sectors towards unexplored areas no doubt explain this development. Note that modern technology requires a much greater "logistical support," and that (possibly temporary) technical inadequacies of the tertiary sector demand an increasing number of personnel, as well as, incidentally, an ever rising cost of services. In France, this rise in the cost of services is about 50% higher than the overall cost of living. According to certain statistics, service industries are responsible for 30 to 50% of retail price rises. n the case of the national product, the service industries account for 55 to
75%. Thus, services tend to accelerate inflation to a considerable extent.
According to 1969 statistics, the following table may be drawn up, showing the percentage of the tertiary sector in relation to the active population, and in relation to the national product.
These figures do pose some problems to the above analysis. Inflation in Italy is very high, although only a relively small part of the population is employed in the tertiary sector. As a rule, these figures


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
do not tally well with the rates of inflation observed in the various countries concerned.

48.5 45.6 43.3 49.9 Two more considerable problems confront anyone who proposes to analyse modern technical society. The first is of a statistical nature, and requires a review of the various social groups. The second is of a dynamic nature, and concerns social mobility.
Very little research has been devoted to contemporary rural life.
Strictly speaking, situations in different countries - and even regions - vary tremendously. However, one thing seems worthy of note, at least in highly productive areas: there is no longer anything between the tiny country farm, and the large mechanized farm. The United States testifies to this as clearly as does the plateau between Marve and Oise, east of Paris. But this evolution has been a slow one. The mechani sation of agriculture is certainly not a recent phenomenon, but since the Second World War, it has accelerated perceptively in certain countries. On the eve of the last World War (1939) there were 35,000 tractors in France; in 1966 there were 1,110,000. The farm is tending to become a real factory. And both the society and countryside have had to change.
On the other hand, the working class has attracted the attention of many sociologists. And particularly so because it has been transformed in the most technically advanced countries. The titles of certain recent studies prove this: La fin des O. S. (The end of the specialised worker),
La nouvelle classe ouvriere (The new working class), etc. Naturally, each sector has seen different developments. The development of the machine-tool, the (admittely slower) progress of automatic devices and automation have gradually transformed professional structures.
However, certain sociologists have resisted the temptation "to see


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1023 technical development as the ultimate explanation and meaning of all things, and to reduce the social history of industry to the natural history of machines." In our opinion, it would be equally extreme to adopt an explanation that would make technical progess a complete abstraction. Touraine believes, and rightly so, that the real problem is knowing "to what extent technical realities determine professional realities at each stage of development." And the sociologists from
Touraine to Mottez (who takes up the former's ideas) draw up an outline of this evolution of work and society.
I - Phase A , or the professional system of work. It corresponds to the general purpose machine. According to Marx, although it is to do with machines, this phase is rather evocatitve of the beginnings of manufac turing. It is based on the autonomy of the worker. It corresponds exactly with the state of the workshops that Taylor encountered before he went on to rationalize them. It was very difficult to predict produc tion conditions. The workman, assisted by his helpers, chose his tools and movements himself. Here one can hardly speak of a firm in the sense in which the term is presently understood. It is rather a coexist tence of two worlds: the world of labour and production, and that of money and management.
II - Phase B. This corresponds to specialized machines. Because they specialized in a limited number of operations, or even one only, it was possible to avoid lengthy adjustments and could be run non-stop.
This was large-scale manufacture or mass production. This image may be evoked by a single image - the assembly line.
III - Phase C, or the technical system of work. It corresponds to special machines, or in other words, complexes of machines carrying out a long series of operations, of which transfer machines might have seemed to be the perfect form. The technical equipment was indepen dent of its operators. Workers were no longer involved in production itself, but in supervision, control and maintenance jobs.
Thus each stage corresponds to a particular structure of the working world. Phase A demanded a large proportion of skilled workers (or rather workmen) trained through an apprenticeship period that could run into several years. Phase B was characterized by the considerable increase of what are known as "semi-skilled" workers, workers who could be trained quickly for a clearly defined job. Phase C began after the last World War: many writers have stressed "the end of the semi-skilled worker." And the number of technicians - persons whose


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
work is precisely to control and maintain machines - is growing.
Naturally, this development varies according to the technical sector.
Some figures for France (1964) show the variations between sectors (in percentages of workers employed in each sector).
semiless than skilled skilled unskilled 18 y.o.
oil 74.5 18.5 6.9 0.1 mechanical industry 40.3 46.5 7.9 5.3 leather and fur 22. 1 33.4 35.9
8.6 lumber 21 36.7 28.5 13.8 Touraine's figures for the making of differential housing in Renault factories may help us to establish the cut-off point. These figures are also given as a percentage of the workers employed in making this product.
1925 1955 professional 46.3 32.4 unskilled and "specialised" workers 53.7 67.6 It was therefore in the 1950s, with the development of automation, that the trend was reverse, or began to be reversed. Is it not symptoma tic that in 1958 a journal opened a debate on "the new working class?"
Several consequences of this development must be remembered.
The first goes without saying. Automation tends to create labour savings, and by this we mean manual labour. Thus various types of transfer, between degrees of qualification and between industrial sectors, become necessary. Then there is also evidence of a trend that had been very marked in the 19th century - the feminization of semi-skilled labour. According to the latest French statistics, 52% of semi-skilled workers are women.
The second consequence is linked to the first. And here we quote a U.N. report:
In the face of the demands of modern technology, there is a general tendency to require a wider educational base of workers. They are expected to have better qualifications


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1025 related to certain jobs, and also to move from one job to another if new techniques make their qualifications obsolete. Attaching less importance to specialized training facilitates versatile training that allows the same worker to fulfil jobs that had previously been done by two or more specialists.
The danger of excessive specialization is that it tends to eliminate certain qualifications, and to render knowledge quickly obsolete in a period of rapid technical progess.
In fact, it is hard to make even short-term predictions. Technology plays an important part in the evolution of the working world, but it is not its only component. Opinions on this evolution vary: the value of the figures produced is often questionable. Recently an author showed that in 1954 the proportion of skilled workers to workers of all categories in France was as much as, or more than 50% in seven branches of industry:
clothing. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
84.8 printing and related trades . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
75.9 leather and furs . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
72.8 general mechanics . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
60.5 construction . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
55.2 paper-cardboard . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
54.4 lumber-furniture . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
50.3 In 1968, skilled workers constituted more than 50% of the labour force in only two branches: clothing ( 67%) and printing and related trades
(60.5%).
Obviously, partial examples say very little. Moreover, note that the statistics show "specialized skilled workers." They are estimated to be
10% in mechanics, 25% in ironworks and welding workshops, 48% in food, 87% for industrial painters, and up to 97% in the fabric industry.
Sociologists have a hard time finding their way around in this world of statistics and jargon. Besides, the head of the big German firm
Siemens declared that in his business, he had only "associates." Today there is no denying the fact that the working class (did not
Proudhon speak of "working classes") is in the throes of change. Not only the technical revolution, but also the organisation of labour, are essential elements of this evolution. The emergence of the transfer machine, to name by one example, and the disappearance of the assembly line, are equally important. Working-class society was


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
formed in the 19th century, with the Industrial Revolution of the end of the 18th century. It experienced its first transformation during the
Second Industrial Revolution of the end of the 19th century. It will be transformed once more by the industrial revolution that we are presently experiencing. Touraine's sketch seems perfectly valid.
The tertiary sector covers very diverse professional categories. there is no uniformity of skill, income or lifestyle. A member of the management staff of a steel plant (which belongs to the primary sector) is not unlike the lecturer in higher education (which belongs to the the tertiary sector). Similarly, the typist is, in certain ways, not unlike the skilled worker. Moreover, there is less and less talk of the middle classes which were so famous between the wars that a minister was assigned to them in Belgium. We must speak in terms of categories.
Once more, we shall cite only a few examples, derived from more or less recent works.
Crozier has pored over the "bureaucratic society" and the world of office workers, all those who people that enormous administrative outgrowth, both of the public and private sectors (and neither of them has any reason to envy the other). The expansion of this manpower is certainly a result of the expansion of jobs, but it also a result of the slow evolution of admimistrative techniques. Everyone can confirm daily the unwieldliness of administrative machinery, the number of forms that must be completed, the volume of mail, and all these uncontrolled attacks from a technology that is barely moving. It even seems that the dimensions of the world of administration only grow: data processing is a typical example of this. C. W. Mills notes that in 1870 there were
750,000 middle class office workers in the United States. In 1940 there were more than 12 million. This author further points out the fact that this development has described a more or less regular curve.
The office workers who make up the new middle class do not constitute a compact and homogeneous layer. They have not appeared on a single horizontal level, but are simultaneously distributed among the different levels of society. In short, today, rather than a horizontal layer, they form a new pyramid within the old social pyramid. The vast majority of the new middle class is to be found in the lower income bracket, but whatever the method used to evaluate social rank, white collar workers are to be found on all rungs of the ladder except at the very top and the very bottom.
One final figure from the United States: between 1870 and 1940, the proportion of white collar workers in the middle-income bracket rose from 15 to 56%, whereas that of the old middle class fell from85 to 44% .


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1027 This shift is not just a set of statistics. It is "the movement from property to non-property. From a positive point of view, it is the movement from a social structure based on property to a structure centred round employment". When the great grand father of the man who penned these lines died in 1918, he had lived on the income from his lands all his life without working personally. Today, all, or nearly all of his descendants are "office workers," and none of them owns more than a small plot of land. It would certainly be unwise to claim that technical changes are solely responsible for this development.
Many other elements have come into play, and, since this ancestral land has not vanished, its successive transmissions should be analysed.
Who are the new land owners? The subject deserves investigation.
Balzac only partly glimpsed this development.
The link between property and employment should have been the subject of serious studies for which we are still waiting. A not inconsiderable portion of landed property still belongs to non farmers. And furthermore, it has been pointed out that it has often been such people who have introduced technical progress into agriculture. This was an obvious fact in the 18th century, and is surely no less true today. But such property, especially in so far as it is not profitable, or when it is a luxury, can only be maintained over the generations through permanent employment. If it is an inheritance that is transmsitted without too many difficulties, under reasonable demographic conditions inherited property may be secure, even at the price of certain sacrifices.
But what is not sufficiently appreciated is the fact that a job -at least at a certain level - whether in the public or private sector, is private income, and as such, more or less transmittable. A justly famous study of French magistrates from the first half of the 19th century, stressed the widespread belief that heredity was a bonus in this case, in that, when a person lives in a certain environment, he becomes permeated with its mission, and, more importantly, with its techniques, with the whole body of knowledge necessary for a profession.
We would need statistics on the inheritance of certain offices, whatver the modalities. By modality we mean simply the transmission of a job that one owns, or a slow preparation sanctioned by a certain selection (i.e. exams and competitions). We could thus cite a number of liberal professions, such as medicine, law, and a number of public offices such as the military and teaching, to mention but a few


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
examples. The Ancien Regime did indeed practise a sort of insti tutionalized venality of offices.
Apart from special cases such as revolutions which throw a large number of persons out of their offices - usually not all, so as to avoid a break in continuity - there are gaps nonetheless. In fact, these gaps coincide precisely with changes in technical systems. It may be one of two things. On the one hand it may be a question of completely new techniques, like the car in the 1890's, or atomic energy in a more recent period. In this case these is a change of the previous ruling classes, or and this is becoming increasingly rarer - an adaptation that involves only a few individuals. Or else, it is a transformation of old techniques, and in this case, the adaptation of the old classes is favoured to some extent.
In short, stable, slowly evolving techniques ten to maintain a clearly defined social structure. For a long time, this was true of agriculture.
And today it is wtill true of a number of very special industries. Once a technique is modified, sooner or later, social structures also evolve.
And one might say that they tend to change more profoundly in what are generally referred to as upper strata. And this is even more so in countries where the conditions for access to jobs are a function of a certain number of conditions that have nothing to do with inheritance.
It is very difficult to get hold of the clew linking technology and society. Apart from material wealth which is increasingly fragile, positions attained may always be challenged by the evolution of technique. In certain industrial sectors, the obsolescence of knowledge is such that for certain social categories, nothing is certain any more.
All one hears about in certain circles is retraining, and constant training - two institutions that are more relevant to persons of "a certain rank." In other words, social position and technical skill are very closely linked.
There is not much more to be said. Social resistance is extremely strong, and any technical change seems to pose some threat. The liberal professions are not giver! to this reaction, because they are perhaps further away from technique proper. They commonly reject it even though they benefit from certain aspects of technical progress. A member of this group will easily go by car to a demonstration against atomic energy, and be proud that his child has been accepted at a polytechnic or school of engineering. It is in these circles that the generation gap is most perceptible. The older generation belongs to


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1029 the technical system of the turn of the century, somewhat perfected up to the eve of the Second World War. The younger generation is already involved in the Third Technical Revolution that we are experiencing. A social and educational world separates the two. And it is across these differences that a new society will be built.
Nothing embodies better the problems of sociologists than the concept of the executive. It corresponds more to a certain income level
- and consequently lifestyle - than to one or another professional category. And here, once more, the transitions are almost impercepti ble. It seems that there are those who have tried, for many and various reasons, to create a socio-professional category based on questionable criteria, whose conception varies according to social habits, educa tional backgrounds, salaries, actual functions: legal, administrative, and social functions ... The range is virtually infinite. A review of job offers in a paper aimed at an average, educated readership is symp tomatic of this. There are categories, but there is also overlapping.
Does not the executive in a legal or commercial department need technical knowledge? Does not a person with technical responsibilities in a factory need some social and even legal training?
To quote a recent study that applies to France only:
Although the executive must be a salaried employee, not all salaried employees are executives. To determine whether a person belongs to the category of executive, two criteria must be taken into account: 1. Professional training, ability and technical skill.2.
The exercise of a real function of initiative or responsibility derived from a delegation of authority by employers (or management) . Professional training, ability or technical skill may be attested either by a diploma, or usually, rather lengthy "work experience". But the actual work carried out remains the main criterion.
The above defines reasonably well the concept of executive, which may involve giving orders, supervision, research, commerce or administration. It is very difficult to distinguish the differences and gradations between the foreman, or even what used to be called the overseer, the technician, higher technician, engineer and administra tive executives. Salary scales provide the only clues. There have been some shifts: when Napoleon decided to develop the ecoles des arts et me tiers (schools of engineering), his intention was to train the
"N.C.O.'s" of industry. But very soon, what they produced were engineers. And this particularly as the rapid development of techno logy in certain sectors quickly redefined all frontiers. At times, rank and on-the-job training may replace "scientific" training. Though the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
same categories appear in nearly all advanced countries, the channels of training often vary. In some places, there is an extreme reverence for the diploma, and this influences social recruitment; in others, slow on-the-job training is preferred.
It is therefore very difficult to give distributions. The following figures were supplied by France in 1970 - as percentages of industrial executives:
Research . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 10°/o
Departments of technical studies and manufacturing . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 38%
Techno-commercial and commercial . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 15% Administration and others . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
37% Altogether, and once more referring to France, "executives" repre sented about 15% of the active population. But, and we repeat, there is evidence of changes. Recently, there has been a rapid growth of administrative, legal and commercial executives. In certain sectors, the technicity of production and development of automation have perceptibly augmented the proportion of executives among active personnel as a whole. In short, every new technique must, perforce, create its executives: as is the case with data processing. We may anticipate that the stages of preparation, technical organisation, con struction designs, and research and development will tend to involve more and more people, at the expense of executive posts. It has been estimated that by the end of this century, 20% of workers will be involved in the sciences and research and development, and these workers will essentially be executives.
The Organisation of Labour By forcing American industry to change suddenly under the pressure for armaments, and move towards new productions, often with an inexperienced work force, the Second World War certainly played an almost revolutionary role in the organization of work. As we have seen, the technical system of the end of the 19th century led to
Taylorism and assembly line work. Piece work was its most spectacular result, and numerous studies have been devoted to it, in particular, G.
Friedmann in France. It would seem that this trend is being reversed.
The road will no doubt be long, but today, famous examples are taking


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1031 a fascinating turn. "Yes indeed, after Kalmar, everything seems possible,'' announced a widely read weekly.
No doubt the phenomenon is still too recent to have acquired its own history, and one must often confine oneself to more or less isolated cases. In the long term, what is still rare will become increasingly common. There are certainly technical sectors that will fall behind: this is a development that has been evident throughout this whole history.
Meanwhile, let us consider the large Oise farm that functioned before the last world war with about ten horses and 30 day labourers. There are no more horses, and at the height of the agricultural working season it employs a maximum of six workers.
Like Taylorism, this new movement was born in the Unites States.
And it has given its name to the system - job enlargement. It may have first appeared around 1943 at l.B.M. 's Endicott factory in New York
State. Assembly line work which was generally piece work of a kind very common in all assembly industries, was replaced by team work in which each member saw his work broaden. The continuous assembly line was therefore replaced by a number of groups that accomplished production together. Each group organized itself and distributed jobs, which could vary from week to week, or even from day to day. One of its first consequences was to eliminate supervision and regulation grad ually. At Endicott there was one regulator per 11 workers in 1943, one per 48 workers in 1946, and in 1950 they were eliminated. And the pace of the assembly line, in fact imposed by the slowest workers, dis appeared by itself. Had the assembly line been retained, the inexper ience of workers hastily recruited to cope with considerable production programs would have tended to slow down assembly. The formation of teams that divided work among themselves, and organized job rotation became the pillars of the new organisation of work.
This example was soon followed. We make special reference to the case of Sears Roebuck and Company, a huge American distribution and delivery firm that is both commercial and industrial. Administra tive decentralization and the regrouping of tasks encouraged responsi bility and initiative. Employees changed from being highly specialized to being more or less versatile. M. Worthy, one of the gurus of these new formulas attacked Taylor directly in 1951:
Our task largely consists of developing a more adequate science of organisation. One gets the feeling that the division of work has got out of hand and that it has gone far beyond what is necessary for efficient production.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Cadillac followed the same path in Detroit. A 1943 study recalls an experiment carried out in two factories producing tanks. The first, an old one, was run according to the strictest rules of the assembly line.
The second, which had been quicly built, had not had the time to set up a strict organisation, and had distributed production between large groups. Output in the latter proved higher, and the rate of accidents lower.
This new organisation of work gained ground after the war. Natu rally, it spilled over into Europe, with examples appearing in Belgium in 1952. At the same time, it was being clarified, completed, and perfected. Notes G. Friedmann:
While broadening jobs, particular care has been taken to enrich them, through new requirements of responsibility and skill. Included were sets of bench tools for regulating the machines for each new series of blueprints, a knowledge of the principles of sizing, of its effects on the sequence of operations and - doubtless the most important measure - completP- quality control of the finished product, which meant the use of gauges, calipers and comparators.
It was under such conditions that certain professionals, regulators and supervisors, tended to disappear. Similarly, even before the arrival of the most modern machines hastened his disappearance, the old semi-skilled labourer no longer had a place in manufacturing. It was at Kalmar in Sweden, at the Volvo car factory that this movement attained its true dimensions and its most spectacular success: it then reached a wide audience.
In fact, those who ran Volvo believed, and rightly so, that the development of the organisation of work required suitable factories, because the old factories had been designed for assembly line work.
The model was created by architects, production engineers and trade union representatives. According to a French weekly:
From both a human and technical point of view, it is a tremendous success. The structure is built on two levels and made up of interlocking hexagons set out in the shape of a crown. Each hexagon has large bay windows that open onto a green landscape, and is an autonomous workshop allocated to a team of fifteen to twenty-five workers. The workers have a changing room equipped with a sauna and a rest area that is a veritable little cafe.
Each workshop was responsible for one stage of assembly: electrical equipment, safety equipment, the fitting of windows, testing, etc. The members of the team could divide the work up into a number of


Figure 1. - Plan of the new Kalmar factory. The numbers indicate the route followed by trucks. The car bodies are taken on at A. The finished cars leave the factory at F. The checking of materials is done at B.
The control centre is at C, on the first floor. At D, hoists carry the vehicles from one floor to another and then, when they are free, they return to E.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
sections, or on the other hand, they could form large groups to do all of the operations together. They could alternate jobs. It was up to them to decide. No rhythm was imposed on them, and no timetable for fittings, If they finished earlier than expected, they could leave. They were collectively responsible for the quality of their work.
Logically, this organisation was accompanied by new techniques for supplying the various workshops. The traditional assembly line was replaced by automatic guided vehicles with a whole traffic system even including storage areas, and the vehicle itself could be turned into a work area. Obviously, such machinery called for the computer.
Apart from the social and psychological problems of workers, there are also problems of an economic nature. This factory is more expen sive than the more old-fashioned type, although the extra cost is relatively little - 10% in this instance. It is also a fact that this factory specializes in manufacturing the most expensive car of the Volvo range. They have tried to cope with certain phenomena, especially absenteeism, which has admittedly led to a reduction of labour. Many questions may be asked, and have been asked by qualified visitors. The
Kalmar factory only makes luxury cars; it limits its activities to assembly; it requires only skilled labour.
This is the beginning of an experiment that needed to be attempted.
Certain aspects of this experiment present difficulties: the transfer of workers and work stations several times a day, a broadening of jobs that leads some workmen to follow a car body for an hour, the organisation of work in responsible groups, 130 hours of training before employment as an assembler, and a conscientious struggle against noise, all demand a special climate. It is here that one becomes aware of the need to make society and the organisation of work compatible. The president of Italy's Fiat was one of those who visited
Kalmar. "In Mr. Agnelli's opinion, the Kalmar experiment could not be tranplanted to Italy. Because, more than technical initiatives, it presupposes a social consensus, a veritable collaboration between employers and employees. The Swedish have been working under these conditions for nearly half a century."
And no doubt this is the real problem that crops up on other levels and in different ways in developing countries. Any new technical invention that has social repercussions is problematic. A gradual development such as that which occurred in the United States at the end of the Second World War definitely proved less complicated than a


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1035 creation ex nihilo like the one at Kalmar. It is not easy to move from one organisation of work to another that presupposes more thorough training, team work, and greater responsibility. In order to change the organisation of work, a whole society and its taboos must be changed, and this has its difficulties. Mr. Agnelli understood the problem perfectly.
The above example also demonstrates that techniques were not everything. From a technical point of view Kalmar was not very inno vative; the only novelty was the method of transmitting components and units. It was the sociological venture that was quite different. Here the imagination and initiative of the worker recover their full meaning.
the fitting of the dashboard was done without moving from the A.G. V. 's to the unit to be built. The details of the method are left up to the workers, who may change them as they please.
It was natural that other experiments should be carried out in certain sectors, especially those where, like the car, assembly is very important. l.B.M. has just built a factory for making type-writers in
West Berlin. There they too wanted to broaden jobs, replacing the single assembly line with six separate mini-assembly lines where the
3500 parts that make up a type-writer are assembled by only 25 workers. Of course, each one is a specialized worker, but his job is not reduced to a simple monotonous repetition. There is a certain variety, and job times are rather long - never less than twelves minutes - compared with six minutes for the classic assembly line. But this strictly physical aspect of the new breakdown of work is not the most important thing. Above all, the mini-assembly line has the advantage of making it possible for a small number of people - in this case, 25 people who know each other - to assemble a complete machine. This creates group consciousness, a feeling that is clearly be reinforced by the responsibility of the group, which is total, since it includes even the final quality check of the product or work.
After a number of experiments, the nature of the problem has become clearer. If the factory is new, one is aware of the influence of colour, lighting, temperature, humidity, space, etc. One is less sure of the effects of the new organisation of work. The examples available are only specific cases deriving not only from types of production, but from certain populations. It has been reiterated that Italians would not take to this development of working conditions. And finally, there are all the economic repercussions, especially on prices.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
France seems to have been late in joining this movement, and has often done so only partially. This is the case in the car axle assembly workshops at Renault's Le Mans factory. It consists of a rather vast arrangement of spaces within which the worker carries out all assembly operations seated at a simple table. The factory for reconditioned engines at Choisy has adopted a similar system in which each person assembles the whole engine himself. At the Douai factory, twelve sections of assembly line separated by pull-through stocks each employ groups of ten to twenty workers at semi-fixed stations.
The theory of this new organisation has not yet been formulated.
When the plans for the l.B.B. factory in Berlin were drawn up, psychologists and sociologists conducted an inquiry on the key factors that determine the satisfaction and motivation of workers. At first glance it would seem that this trend points to a reconciliation of worker and work.
It would seem that this development is taking place in several stages that are separated less chronologically than sectorially, or in other words, according to the development of technology in the various types of manufacture.
A first and more primitive stage would consist of the breaking of rhythms. There has clearly been a desire to fight the growth of rhythms that appear to be undeniably one of the primary causes of fatigue and dissatisfaction among semi-skilled workers. For instance, it has been thought at Fiat that it was less the division of work into small sections that was at fault than the collective rhythms that tie workers to the assembly line. It has been said that there was a need to act on the "level of saturation that produced the ratio of the period of effective work to the paid period." Let us consider once more the exampale given by
J.-P. Dumont: "For the salary for one minute of attendance at the factory, the worker must carry out one, two, or three operations that in fact required work lasting less than a minute; depending on the case,
'engaged time' accounted for 89 to 90% of a minute." The level of saturation was thus reduced to a bracket of 80 to 84 % .
This could be taken further by limiting the obligation of workers to follow the same rhythm together. In order to do this, the assembly line was to a certain extent divided up by placing pull-through stocks between each semi-skilled worker. The 1971 example relates to a knitting factory where the assembly line system was strictly observed:
"Now, instead of doing his work unit by unit as before, the worker does


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1037 it in packets of ten components. That has made it possible for him to adopt a relatively free work rhythm." The only technical problem was supplying each work station. At the Kiev shoe factory, "The distri bution conveyer made it possible to supply each work station with baskets of components to be processed as soon as as they were finished, thanks to an automatic dispatcher." Similar experiments were tried in France at Telemecanique, and at Bel's in Lons-le
Saunier. In the last-mentioned case, after some hesitation, output rose by 10% and waste fell perceptibly. In the Netherlands, the Philips company log book shows similar results. Previously, the assembly line for televisions comprised 104 workers who produced a television set every 107 seconds, with operations that had been broken down into very small sections. Five groups with pull-through stocks were created.
The most difficult part of it all is the place that shop floor supervision should play in this development.
It has been said that the rotation of stations is "an old story." However, it constitutes a second phase. As in the case just mentioned, there is no appreciable modification of the organisation of work based on the division of work into sections. There is simply a rotation of jobs.
In this way, time is divided between periods of stress and periods of calm, breaking the monotony of the work. Sometimes-as in the case of the Rochas perfumery, or the front axle assembly workshop at the
Renault factory in Le Mans - the rotation of jobs is implemented spontaneously by the workers, either by shifting work stations or by broadening rotation. this system of alternating work has also been introduced into Soviet factories. Some workers at a Bulgarian factory attended training courses so as to be able to work on four or five improved machine tools. In the case of skilled workers, the more versatile each one becomes, the greater the impact of rotation. Note that this type oforganisation had already been used at Bata before 1940.
It also has the advantage of reducing the effects of absenteeism and fewer supply workers. But we shall see that there are other difficulties.
Job enlargement would therefore be the final stage. It couldregroup several fragmented operations; it may also regroup all the jobs in a workshop. It may apply to a single worker or to a group of workers. We have seen examples of this above. In its most elementary stage, the worker who runs a machine may also be given the job of maintaining and regulating it. From this it is possible to move on to the near total elimination of the assembly line.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Obviously, the changes that have taken place in the organisation of work are not exclusively due to technical developments, but they occur within a technical context that is different from the one that gave birth to the assembly line. The development of machine tools, and the automation of conveyors has been essential to these changes. In some cases, this is equally true of checking and measuring.
Naturally, parallel changes in the technical training of workers come next. Rapid supplementary training facilitates rotation. If a semi skilled worker can be trained in a few hours, a few hours will suffice for him to change stations. With the broadening of jobs, which involves skilled more than semi-skilled workers, clearly a longer time is needed
(in some cases mentioned, between about 50 and 150 hours). It is no longer professional ability that is involved, but a level of knowledge.
But many difficulties remain. According to the authors of the Traite de sociologie du travail, "technical development transforms the rela tionships between the various categories or workers, their hierarchy, cohesion and situation in the company." This produces certain tensions which are sometimes very apparent.
The future is relatively vague. Some people who once expressed doubts about certain experiments have finally accepted them. Take for instance Fiat's factory at Cassino. One of the directors of this firm made some telling remarks to J.-P. Dumont:
On the technical and economic level, the only feasible solution in the metallurgical industry is the fragmentation of tasks. Today we are under attack for making people do fragmented tasks. Can job enlargement remedy the malaise of the semi-skilled worker?
Such an approach does not seem feasible to me. Indeed, we are carrying out research in this direction, and we are trying experiments in assembling products in teams, in groups, but we are also pursuing research in another direction - that of a greater mechanisation that will lead to automation. (And he continues] Look at what is happening in other countries. Experiments concerned with improving workers' jobs remain very limited.
Despite some attempts at broadening jobs, the American Motorola company envisages more advanced mechanisation, the much-publicized formulas have not been extended to the car industry. In fact, General Motors recently installed new assembly lines that have intensified the fragmentation of tasks. As for us, we have just put robots on an assembly line.
This robot had been installed at the end of 1971 on a body-work line.
Naturally it occupied a fixed station, and therefore disturbed the rest of the assembly line. Other robots have appeared just about everywhere, mainly in car factories. There is an automatized gear-making machine


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1039 at Peugot, Mulhouse. Five or six skilled workers tend the running of the machine, whereas the old system required 30 to 35 mainly semi skilled employees, Richta believes that there will be a professionali zation of semi-skilled workers, and their proportions will drop by 10 to
70% , depending on the industry. The category of regulators, mainte nance workers and repairers should increase by 6% to 50%. The same would be true for engineers.
This development, whose latest phase has been barely realized in history, has been met with enthusiasm, reservation and fear. Shortly after the opening of the Kalmar factory, the E.E.C. advocated the absolute elimination of assembly line work. Fragmented work and the assembly line will perhaps lose some ground, but they will never disappear completely. If the organisation of work is experiencing profound changes, it is because people have a new concept of work.
But it is obvious that without technical progress, these changes could never have taken place. Automation has clearly played a significant role in this development.
Transfers of Technology and Traditional Societies This is a problem, or rather, a set of problems that have become rather important over the past few decades. And it is very difficult to separate the facts, whether they be of a technical, economic, social, or even political nature. Historians should have been able to shed some light on the matter, despite the considerable differences that distinguish our time from the past centuries. Still, there are some common points.
France was actually an importer of techniques: take for instance Colbert, who introduced the technique of mirror making or Dutch linen; and the big financiers who set up the coke foundry at Le Creusot.
Unfortunately, it would seem that those who have taken an interest - and they most certainly have - in these phenomena have not adopted the approach that we are using for today.
First of all, it would be interesting to establish a typology of cases from the the purely technical sphere: either entirely new techniques or techniques that replace old ones, to provide a similar product.
Colbert's introduction of mirror making belongs to the former cate gory, and Le Creusot's foundation belongs to the latter. There is also the technical lag, or "gap," between the supplying and receiving country, a concept that may be added to those that we have just


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
mentioned. A new manufacturing process is more difficult to import than a process replacing an old technique. the only thing that was imported at Le Creusot 1782 was the coke foundry. What was new was the manufacture of coke, although old techniques such as the manu facture of wood charcoal in stacks were used. For the rest, the stoking of the blast furnace was not essentially different from the old wood burning installations. On the other hand, when the French introduced puddling and forging into rolling mills, these techniques were completely different from those that had been used to date. In the first case, nationals coped with the situation; in the second, English nation als had to be brought in.
The second point is no less important. Any technique that enters a country entails social demands: education of the people concerned, the removal or concentration of the working masses, the transfor mation of life styles and working habits. It is no doubt in this area that resistance has been strongest and most enduring. It is understandable that a population of nomads should find it difficult to become an industrial population. Moreover, it is usually from this point of view that the problem has been presented to the relevant authorities. The most obvious problem has not been a purely technical one, but rather, what has generally been referred to as industrialization; and underly ing it is an awareness of the difficulties posed by the transformation of a traditional society into an industrial society.
In fact, since the Middle Ages, endogenous or exogenous tech niques have been acquired gradually while societies changed. There was a clear parallelism between technical progress and social progress.
But at the time of the Industrial Revolution of the late 18th century, there was a break. During that period, the demands of technology were extremely strong, and societies had to adapt - often in a way that repels us today - with the work of women and children. In our day, the shift from techniques of the preceding system to so-called advanced techniques in the more developed countries is no longer a social a problem. It is essentially a problem of practical means, and principally of financial means, or in other words, the scale of the economies concerned. We hope that what we have said in the preceding pages proves that social adjustments are necessary, and that they may be achieved without a total upheaval of society, even if there are tensions
- no doubt temporary ones. Some lack the imagination to conceive of what is popularly called today the "post-industrial society." One must


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1041 be able to assume "the end of the semi-skilled worker," in just the same way as "the end of the peasant."
For many years there have been attempts to introduce modern technology into Third World countries. Here the rift is more recent. In fact, the colonial period was mainly concerned with the problems of agriculture, food, as well as those relating to the exploitation of natural resources. It was after the Second World War and after decolonization that a wave of industrialization burst upon these countries. As might be expected, the speed of this process, which involved social changes, has been the cause of serious difficulties.
It was a question of bringing about the adoption of a technical system that was totally different from that which had prevailed for centuries.
And here one may calculate the necessity of compatibility between the technical and social systems. We have not shown the importance of a change in agricultural techniques: the 18th century has provided us with a perfectexample. This revolution may have passed without too much hindrance in the developing, or already developed countries of
Wes tern Europe, but the same cannot be said of the Third World where the technical gap was infinitely greater, or societies more rigid.
There are various kinds of oppositions, reservations and difficulties.
Admittedly, some of them were, and continue to be, of a technical nature. The introduction of a new technology unquestionably requires a "logistic back-up." According to a study that dates back to 1953, there was an attempt to introduce tractors to Burma. It failed. Not because the people were hostile to the machines. On the contrary .It was because the tractors kept getting stuck in the soft earth, fuel and spare parts were too expensive, and there was a lack of technical skill to drive them.
To quote a UNESCO enquiry, In agriculture, technical changes - whether they affect resources, methods of exploi tation, or the general organization of production, are closely interlinked, and basically depend on the human factor; whether it is a question of organizing hydraulic power, improving land, selecting seeds or breeds of animals, consideration must first be given to the conditions of human effort involved, the way in which people distribute tasks, group themselves, traditionally organize their work, understand their relationships with the land that feeds them and often provides them with reasons for living. Man is the agent of all change, and the least modification of a technique or tool affects his life style and his relationships with his fellow-men.
And here, we should like to confine ourselves to a few examples.
In certain societies, the owner of a field that is greener than the others risks being accused of robbed his neighbours' fields of their


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
fertility. People would therefore hesitate to use fertilizer. In other societies, the farmer is yet to be convinced that fertilizer can effectively improve productivity. There is no better example than the problem of water, around which a whole society may be organized.
When it was proposed that a pump be set up in a village of fellahs to relieve the women of some of their work, the fellahs repied: "but what will our women do all day? " It is not just a matter of occupying the women; drawing water at the well is one of the duties of the woman ( . . . ) For the Tivs and Burmese, the pounding mortar is an inseparable attritbute of women, and the Tiv Akiga sees his wife spinning and weaving for him. What happens when the woman no longer fits his concept of her as a woman and wife? What happens when one wants to save labour and labour is neither a moral obligation nor a necessity, but a way of life, or else one of the highest virtues, as with some American
Indians?
To return to water, let us point out another case. "The peasants of the Ganges valley ( ... ) are reluctant to use the water brought to them by an irrigation project, because they can see clearly that the project will be useful mainly to the great landowners, and will make them dependent on agricultural technicians and officials; finally, the canal strikes them as a government ruse to get more money and work out of them." We shall return to this explanation. It was perhaps not the only one. In fact, to crown it all, this irrigation required the dispersal of dwellings to facilitate twenty-four hour monitoring. But in this region, houses are grouped in village fortresses, and the inhabitants refuse to go out in the evening or night when evil spirits overrun the countryside.
And what can one say of the refusal to spread manure on the land, because it is used as fuel, or the refusal to grow a new type of wheat, because the cows refuse to eat the straw. Similar reactions are to be found in Olivier de Serres's Theatre d'Agriculture. In fact, he is trying less to transform the agriculture of his day than to rationalize the traditional methods of his time. Hence the advice not to modify any equipment, especially that used for ploughing.
In countries where only traditional activities are known, the acqui sition of industrial techniques has equally important repercussions.
Industrialization affects the social group in various ways. The mere fact that it introduces new tools, means that it modifies the very structure of the group, overturning the traditional division of labour.
