In Burma, the British administration congratulated itself on the the increased importation of cotton articles and pottery, seeing this as an indication of a raised standard of living; in fact, it was the result of a change in family life, and more precisely, the abandonment of the loom on which wives and daughters used to make rich silks, and the head of the family would create fabrics of a particularly complex design.


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1043 Thus it became apparent that as well as an imported technique, the entrance of the object related to it brings about similar results.
It should be possible to study the reasons for, and consequences of the introduction of railways into Africa. For some people, these railways were built so as to avoid the use of porters and thus allow the elimination of slavery. And it is stressed that "before they came into contact with the West, the Africans had no need of transport, and for them slavery was simply a form of agricultural or domestic service." It is indeed true that apart from certain methods used, forced labour in particular, the railroad introduced much greater upheavals in African societies than those observed in Western countries.
The arrival of an industry that forces men to work away from home to earn a living, or, more seriously, that removes the family from its traditional environment, completely disrupts social organisation. In
Western Europe, the long familiar phenomenon of temporary emig ration, whether for agricultural work or construction (the Limousin masons are well known), has attained continental dimensions with what are known as immigrant workers. In colonized countries, the army and emigration have been major causes of the break-up of traditional societies on one hand, and on the other hand, of the preparation of certain populations for the adoption of more modern techniques.
In Africa, the villages had not been prepared for the prolonged absence of the men when industrialization came along, bringing with it a great demand for labour, and a multitude of temptations. Family life and agricultural life, which were based on the division of work, were overturned. The standard of living fell, because lower production was not compensated for by wages which the men usually spent on their own upkeep during their absence, or to buy various presents for their return home. With the men gone, the home ceased to be an educational centre; there was no longer anyone there to instil the sense of traditional values into the adolescents.
No doubt we shall be accused of confusing industrialization and the transfer of technology, but in fact, the two concepts overlap perfectly.
Technical Society and Power Every society organizes its powers, the sources of which may be be very diverse. Man's earliest history has amply demonstrated this fact.
Today, the problem of power, which is both political and social, presents itself in a particular way.and therefore is resented, more or


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
less confusedly. Technical progress has been such that technical power may now belong to the person with knowledge, or better still, with technological knowledge. This pressure is felt on several levels, and, naturally, in different ways.
Technical progress appears to be used by the directors of the economy to exert pressure on both the volume of employment and working conditions. As far as the volume of work is concerned, history shows that workers were always aware of the threat oftechnological unemployment: the troubles with the Flemish population in the 14th century, the printers' strikes, the phenomenon of Luddism (such things have been evident since that same century, and right up to periods very near our time), the abuse of inventers and smashing of machines figure in all the text books. Here we shall mention only one example from our time - the strike at Coventry early in 1955, when
Standard Motors laid off 3500 workers after the factory was adapted to automation. And suddenly, the tension disappeared.
Writes F. Pollock: "If it were possible to have it so that the appearance of a new phenomenon known as automation would not mark the beginning of a new industrial revolution, but would be no more than a normal continuation of technical progress, a powerful ideological weapon would be snatched from of trade union leaders."
Helmut Schelsky adopts a similar position:
To date, there has been no evidence whatsoever in this automation-influenced devel opment of new trends or structures - however minute - which alone could justify the use of the word "revolution" in the social sense of the term. All the situations allegedly show social consequences of automation, have existed for sosme considerable time, as devel opment trends have been more or less accelerated and strengthened by automation.
And it would seem that trade unionists more or less everywhere have adopted this conception of technical progress. In 1955, after the
Coventry strike, an English union publication expressed the same opinion, or nearly:
For the moment, British unions cannot believe that automation is more than a simple accleration of the normal development of technology and science. What worries them most is the abundant publicity given to automation; for if their members become too strongly influenced by the fantastic and fanciful speculations that are being published, that would only make it harder for trade unions to resolve the problems posed by automation.
The enquiry carried out by the U.S. Congress in the same year echoes this view. According to this enquiry - and efforts were made to


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1045 prove it - automation had reached only a few rare products, and it would take a long time to spread.
That was twenty years ago. Certainly, automation as a whole moves slowly. The studies carried out by the United Nations are perhaps not as optimistic. Note all the consequences of automation: reduced manpower in automated industries, as well as reduced manpower in competing industries, no new recruitment, etc. All this leads one to believe that whatever the economic or social systems, automation exercises a power that will only increase both in the area of employment and professional distribution. And this powr cannot be separated from the fact that tany progress in a given technique must perforce be imposed on all. We have only partial statistics which no doubt are not representative. According to one set of statistics, running from chocolate makers to factories making railway material, reductions of manpower range from 13 to 92% , that is to say, an average reduction of 63 .4 % . Anxieties about the ageing of the popu lations of advanced countries and the problem of retraining for ever more skilled jobs, are not unjustified.
There are two levels to be considered. the first is to be found in those businesses where techniques have changed little, and where the old organizaion of work has largely been preserved There is a large number of semi-skilled workers, and, with the help of progressive technical improvements, operations are becoming more and more simple. So there are two situations. If the work is tiring, the semi skilled worker is more likely to be an immigrant. 72% of immigrant workers are semi-skilled, as opposed to 57% for French workers. In one factory it was contrived that immigrant workers of different origins and languages be put side by side, so as to keep them isolated from each other. The least technical change brings about a very rapid adaptation of employees. The immigrant, who only comes for a few years - just long enough to ammass a nest egg for his return home, has no reason to revolt against anything. to quote the C.N.P.F. (French employers' association):
Foreign workers play a vital role in industrial development. Their anxiety to save a part of their wages to provide for the needs of their familites often leads them to accept poor living conditions for themselves ( . . . ) For the foreign worker, the important thing is to earn as much as possible, whatever the working hours. a reduction of working hours can lead to a worker leaving for another company offering more working hours.
The second situation concerns those industries where work is less arduous. this is the case with textiles, food, and a number of recent


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
industries such as electronics and household appliances. Here, women are by far the largest group of semi-skilled workers. They too are less concerned with wielding power. Says the C.N.P.F. :
The natural adaptation of women to simple, repetetive tasks would seem to indicate that as far as they are concerned, the problem of unskilled workers is much less acute ( . . . ) It has been observed that women seem to adjust better than men to the monotony of certain work stations. We must therefore ask if it is not advisable to encourage their employment.
The reduced skill content of jobs, which is unquestionably due to technology and its progress, has therefore led to a multiplication of semi-skilled workers, a lowering of necessary qualifications, and the double movement in the direction of immigrant and female labour.
On the other hand, what must be our conclusions about the evolu tion of the organisation of work, even though in this instance, techno logy is not directly involved, and even if this evolution must eliminate the category of semi-skilled worker? There has been no lack of misgivings, especially on the part of foremen who have thus lost a part of their functions, and their power. Misgivings about breaks in rhythm are less clear, but seem more pronounced concerning the other new forms of work organisation. In fact, rotation has been seen as a means of increasing profits. At Olida, near Paris, rotation was condemned by the workers themselves.
We have been forced to rotate jobs. The most intelligent, resourceful workers are shifted about from one work station to another. They are put in here or there whenever someone is missing, because the supervisors know that they will adjust easily and get into the rhythm. So they end up with the toughest, dirtiest jobs, but without any change in wages.
Or, as we have said, the workers see rotation as an oppressive system that leaves them more at the mercy of the hierarchy.
Sometimes the worker feels that he is put to do all kinds of jobs although he has shown aptitude in a stable station, and could even acquire a certain skill. Rotation is resented as a kind of contempt for ability. It was said at Fiat that rotation lowered the quality of products:
when rotation is used in almost every area, there is a big rise in the ratio of retouching. The same has been true in the U.S,S.R.
After six months of experimenting with job enlargement, a Swedish car factory (S.A.A.B.) had to return to fragmented tasks on the demand of workers. The new Fiat factory at Cassino has effectively


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1047 split the assembly line into four. But the factory has been designed so that it may revert to the old system and reconstruct the traditional assembly line. Job enlargement unquestionably requires more advanced training on one hand, and, on the other hand, (this is less well accepted) greater responsibility. The competetive spirit that some expect this type of organisation to generate, may lead to an acceleration of production, and the inspection of their own work by groups may generate greater nervous tension.
At all events, it seems that new techniques and the new organisation of work are leading to a more evident heterogeneity of the working class. On one hand, the employers are accused of encouraging this movement in order to strengthen their power; workers are accused of rejecting it. One therefore gets a vague impression that in the area of production, technology imposes certain constraints on society, or at least, a certain number of constraints in various different sectos. H.
Mendras observes in La Fin des paysans, "Today, the second agricul tural revolution is overturning all structures, and has broken the stable equilibrium." Agricultural societies have long remained pre mechanical, and pre-capitalist. In the 18th century modified rotation cropping and the elimination of fallow land caused a profound uphea val in rural societies. The same is true today of machines, pedology and chemistry.
P. Hetman notes:
The acceleration of technical change is the most striking characteristic of modern society. Its effects are protean and omnipresent. They affect every aspect of people's daily lives, as well as the direction and survival of economic, social and political institutions. If it is difficult to appreciate the exact nature of these effects, it is because technological change is essentially assymmetrical: certain technological developments lead towards complex results, while others bring about results that are quite the opposite of what had been expected. This is evidenced for instance, by the concentration of firms, the disappearance of loss-making firms, the decline of regions that specialize in traditional industries, the accelerated replacement of techniques, the obsolescence of knowledge, the rapid downgrading of professional qualifications, the devaluation of acquired experience: all these various aspects of assymmetrical change inevitably create instability and discontinuities in social evolution. We can no longer maintain the traditional view that establishes a simple relationship between technology and its long-term effects; in fact, the very acceleration of the rhythm of change becomes a vital variable of technological evolution so that it is difficult to say what is cause and what is effect.
Beyond that, it would be difficult to bring together technical pro gress and overall social change. It is obvious that the latter may derive


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
from diverse ideologies, and in particular, more from one's concept of the right to own property than from technology. However, these different concepts of society do not totally obscure certain similarities which depend on the power held by technical skill in our modern societies, regardless of their type. In fact, the real powers of decision stand above social and political powers. The wealth of times past, which is still clearly dominant, the influence derived from property, social rank, or articulateness, are slowly giving way to technical knowledge. These two kinds of power may be linked by educational systems that favour acquired positions; but the number of cases where technical knowledge comes before all other legacies is increasing, and the pace is bound to quicken. can only
Because of his specialized knowledge (for we must pass through him, whether we like it or not), is the "technocrat" the one who definitely holds the final decision in his hands? Is he, or will he be the master of society? Galbraith stresses that "technology" lies at the foundation of the New industrial state. For years, we have known that technology has pushed towards the division of tasks. The more compli cated it becomes, the more precisely the task must be defined in order for it to be broken kown into a multiplicity of tasks, all of which leads inevitably to ever greater specialization. For Galbraith, the count erpart of this specialization is organization. "Even more than equip ment, the massive and complex organisation of companies is the tangible evidence of advanced technology." Everything must be coor dinated. "Company organisation has been defined as a hierarchy of committees." The same is true of the state, as we have seen with technology and politics. Hence the veritable brain ofa company is the unit that provides these groups with specialized knowledge. "There is no name, either for those who participate in the decision-making of the group, or for the organisation that they constitute. I suggest that this organisation be called the 'technostructure'."
In a sense, it would be a society in which technical competence would wield the power. And it is not surprising that studies, research and essays on the subject abound. From Burnham with The managerial revolution (published in the United States,1941) to Mill's White collar:
the American middle class (1951), the Western world is depicted as being increasingly dominated by technicians. No doubt the same is true of the Eastern bloc, as Djilas's La nouvelle classe testifies. It is in this area that, over and above ideological disagreements, there is


TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
1049 evidence of rather striking similarities under the emblem of technicity.
Any imbalance in a society must be corrected. In fact, compensatory powers are emerging parallel with the rise of the technocratic class.
First of all, it is a phenomenon that should be observed a little more closely. In certain rather more rigid societies (France, for instance) it is possible that this new class of technocrats may be produced by the old so-called ruling classes. They would have discovered this new form of power in time to help to maintain traditional social structures. After all, this trend is relatively old, as is clearly demonstrated by a study of enrolment at the Ecole Polytechnique in the 19th century.
Michel Crozier asserts that the power of the expert as such dimi nishes as progress acclerates, and this is due to the rapid obsolescence of knowledge. Moreover, in other countries - the United States for instance, a whole complex of organisations aimed at limiting the power of technology in one way or another, is emerging. However, the play should not be reduced to a power struggle between two or more groups of technocrats. Certainly, in centralized states, the system of grandes ecoles largely favours the ascendancy of technicians.
It is absolutely impossible to deal with all the problems raised by the binomial of technology and society. It may seem that we have tried to conceal some of them. One would need to undertake a whole work to arrive at valid conclusions. Moreover, it is obvious that a social system does not depend totally on the technical system: it was not our intention to cover all the aspects of a society in this chapter. Certainly, today's society is a society of transition, a transition that is more or less marked depending on political ideologies, customs or social traditions.
In any case, technical progress will surely play an important part in future developments.


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
It may seem unusual to reconcile law with technical progress. The same holds true to some extent for economics. Technology, as such, as distinct from its multiple consequences, is difficult to integrate with law. For these reasons, jurists choose to speak in terms of the "ageing of the law in relation to the evolution of economics," probably using this phrase to embrace technical evolution as well.
Law is essentially the regulation of relationships between individ uals, and deals with material things only through these human rela tionships. In relation to property, it is not the property itself which is likely to be the object of aggression but the owner of the property. The law has little effect upon material things in themselves and, if it does, it is only to the extent that the material thing can be the object of a dispute or one of the elements which can resolve the dispute. The same is true, for instance, with legal measures and, now-adays, with the norms which are used mainly to define the object of a dispute.
It is quite obvious that a newly developed technique often generates new relationships among the people associated with it. The situation is the same both in economic and social life. The evolution of techniques forces the law to change to precisely the same degree that these new relationships emerge. As soon as the motor vehicle replaced the stage-coach or, more broadly-speaking, the horse-drawn carriage, the entire body of traffic laws had to be modified. Two situations could have resulted from this: a fixed law hindering technical progress, as happened in the case of many developing countries, or the technique imposing itself in a more or less anarchical fashion in the absence of a law adapted to its needs. It should in fact be noted that law sometimes slowly keeps pace with the evolution of techniques: take, for instance,


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1051 the insistence upon the handwritten note despite the existence of the typewriter - although the ballpoint pen came very quickly into use - and upon originals instead of photocopies or microfilm.
We propose to review here three aspects of the constraints which techniques bring to bear on the law. The first of these relates to the ownership of a technique, be it already established or new, and in the latter case we will see that the evolution is quickening in its pace. It then quickly became necessary to establish means of protection against the abuse of a given technique: this protection concerns the user of the technique, the consumer of the products of the technique or the consumer's neighbour and, finally, in the case of very modern tech niques, protection against the insidious assaults which such techniques could generate. The final aspect concerns international law and the inevitable adjustments and innovations which certain kinds of tech niques require. It is quite obvious that if technical progress sometimes profoundly disrupts the relationship between individuals, it does the same to the relations between nations.
It would therefore be more natural to study the evolution of the relationships between individuals and between nations in the light of technical progress, than simply to discuss the evolution of the law.
That would indeed have the merit of highlighting problems which present-day legislation or regulations have not yet resolved, since case-law is often incapable of remedying the deficiences in the law.
Nonetheless, it has seemed to us more interesting to observe this evolution of the law, based, of course, on the notion of technical progress, even at the price of underlining its deficiences. A technical system is one based on relationships or, more precisely, the two systems are intimately linked. We can do without neither technique nor law, and as with all the other systems, it is absolutely essential that some coherence should be achieved between these two systems. In fact, the difficulty we experience in coming to grips with technical progress and its implications is what is responsible for the disharmony and delays which we so often observe today. Here, again, research has been anything but adequate.
NATIONAL LAWS
The first effects of technical progress or of techniques, pure and simple, on the law, were naturally felt within national frontiers. It is


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
only after techniques had achieved a manifest scope that the problem was posed on the level of international law, which itself had for long remained in its early stages of development.
Ownership of Techniques The ownership of a technique can be looked at in two ways. The first, which was most quickly apparent, is in terms of the exclusiveness of the use of a familiar, even relatively old technique. The second, appearing very much later, is in fact in terms of a law of intellectual property, namely, the invention.
The ownership of the right to exploit a common technique is more economic than technical in nature. This right can be the object of private appropriation. This was the case, for instance, with the com munal facilities of the Middle Ages. To take but one example, the exclusive use of the wheat-mill of the Lord of the Manor, sometimes simultaneously accompanied by a prohibition on manual grinding, indeed constituted a monopoly of the technique. Was it a means of ensuring his power over a given population; or of ensuring the profitability of what was a significant investment? One would need to know whether the communal facility appeared at the same time as the vast expansion in the use of the water-driven mill, that is, some thirty years before the end of the 12th century.
State monopolies are similar in nature. They are often inspired, however, by both economic and political considerations. When a technique becomes saturated, but continues to be indispensable, it inevitably suffers a reduction in its profit-earning capabilities, particu larly in the face of new products. The State then replaces the private entities which hitherto exercised control over its management: this was the case with railways almost everywhere and with coal-mining in a certain number of countries. But there are also techniques which, on account of their development, have earned a pre-eminent place in the global technical system and in national life. In such instances, in order to preserve their independence vis-a-vis the power conferred by these techniques upon private interests, states have brought these tech niques into the public domain: so was it with electricity, atomic energy and certain branches of communications technology. One should observe, however, that these "nationalisation" measures are not, in any case, of generalised application, and that they may tend to be


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1053 confused with other similar instances in which the technical factor is not in any way a relevant consideration.
In the long run, technical progress was responsible for the appea rance of a particular breed of individual -the inventor. It is remarkable that of all intellectual creations, the technical invention was the first that it was thought to protect by the establishment of a monopoly, albeit temporary in nature, for the inventor's benefit, well in advance of similar protection for literary or artistic property.
There is no question that in its earliest and still uncertain manifes tations, the "patent" appeared simultaneously with the mercantilist policies and the great technical movement of the Renaissance. In the minds of those who conceived of this temporary monopoly, the idea was to nurture the invention as a source of national prosperity. The formulae used in the beginning were quite vague, varied from country to country and sometimes even within the same country from year to year, and from one technique to another.
It is to the Republic of Venice towards the close of the 15th century that we undoubtedly owe the first attempts to regulate this subject.
The year was 1474, to be precise. The prerogative granted to the inventor was expanded, moreover, to include a ban on the exportation of techniques, whether old or new, sometimes on pain of death.
Almost immediately, these prerogatives assumed a particular cha racter: they protected the invention as well as the importation of techniques unknown to the importing country. In the 16th century, this dual aspect of the patent was clearly in the ascendancy. In France, one of the earliest examples was the patent granted to Thesco Mutio in
1551. It conferred on this "Italian gentleman from Bologna" the right to make "exclusively for a period of six years all kinds of glassware in the Venetian style." This was clearly a patent for the importation of technique. The first patent was granted in 1581, but it concerned an invention. In 1594, Galileo was granted a patent in respect of "a structure to raise water to irrigate lands." In England, the first patents also dated from that period.
The most comprehensive legal formula was that promulgated in England in 1623, entitled "Statute of Monopolies." The title of the Act is significant. It resulted from the arbitrary decision by a Prince to grant a monopoly in the field of manufacturing. In the Act, certain cha racteristics of the modern patent appeared for the first time. In particular, it contained a section dealing with the novelty of the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
invention, but the section also permitted the importation of unknown techniques in the country, even though they were already old. The approach was more or less the same everywhere. The Venetian text of
1474, the French letters patent of 24 November or 23 January, 1602 and the English Act of 1623 were all predicated upon an innovation, but were not limited to such innovation.
In France, the system of "preliminary examination" of a new invention seemed to have been adopted towards the end of the 17th century. The charter of the Academie des Sciences of 1699 conferred on that body the role of examiner of the new techniques and the duty to determine their usefulness. We have preserved six volumes of the
Academie's work on "approved machines," the earliest known form of publication of inventions. The Royal declaration of 24 September,
1762 attempted to codify earlier practice and reduced the duration of a monopoly to fifteen years. But, surprisingly enough, the 1762 declar ation refused to recognise automatic patrimoniality of the patent, but reserved it for the "competent" heirs, but did not define the limits of the recognition of such competence.
There was another rule that was generally applied in Europe until the end of the 18th century: with the exception of the Academie des
Sciences in Paris, in the large majority of countries patents and prerogatives furnished a minimum amount of technical details.
After the English Act of 1623 and the French Declaration of 1762, it was the American law of 1790 which regulated the grant of patents a bit more closely still. It stipulated preliminary examination for brand-new inventions and provided for bureaus to conduct the preliminary stu dies. The American law therefore drew on the original French system of the Academie des Sciences, while at the same time making it compulsory, which was not the case in France.
There is no doubt that it was the French legislation of 7 January and 25 May, 1791, which gave the impetus to the law of industrial property.
These laws related to "the useful discoveries and the means by which ownership of them by their authors was to be assured." Preoccupation with industrial development would henceforth be complemented with a recognition of a new kind of individual property. The rights of the inventor were for the first time recognised in a general provision. Just as occurred in England in 1623 and in France in 1762, the goodwill of the Crown was replaced by an obligation on the state to guarantee the ownership of this new form of property - the invention. By contrast,


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1055 the French legislator rejected the idea of an absolute innovation and thereby implicitly endorsed the requirement of preliminary presenta tion to the authority responsible for issuing the patent. This, no doubt, was a consequence of the technical advantage which England was enjoying at that time, but also intended to facilitate the importation of foreign techniques. The "first importation" clause was to disappear with the law of 5 July, 1844, because it was seen as an obstacle to the transfer of techniques.
It has been remarked that the laws of 1791 lacked a doctrinal basis.
Invention was a mixture of both intellectual and commercial creation.
One could have considered the legal nature of industrial property: was it a right of ownership pure and simple, or a right to the intangible intellectual property, or a right to the clientele? Besides, a monopoly was being introduced into an individualistic and liberal system.
From then onwards, the patent was a well-defined means of protec ting a property right. But it was also an instrument for the propagation of new techniques whose ownership it had already guaranteed. In almost every country, the publication of patents became a necessary adjunct of the new right. Later on, the economically developed countries generally introduced the notion of industrial property into their legislation, sometimes modifying it, as occurred on several occasions: the United States did so in 1793, 1809 and 1836; Russia in
1812; Prussia in 1815; the Netherlands in 1817; Austria in 1821;
Sweden in 1834 and Portugal in 1837.
Two kinds of legislation prevailed around the world, corresponding to whether they stipulated preliminary examination or not. The "certi ficate of novelty" was, according to the case, either obligatory, optional or reduced to naught. In the beginning, France and England awarded patents upon request and left to experience and the courts the duty to assess the value of the invention; the United States and Prussia adopted preliminary examination and this led them to establish administrative bureaus specialising in considering applications for patents. American legislation was the strictest of all: it required the firms to submit their research and laboratory papers to notaries. In
1852, England adopted preliminary examination and was followed by a number of countries, leaving the French system somewhat isolated.
The evolution of techniques inevitably led to new concepts which were unfamiliar to the somewhat outdated legislation. Let us take a recent example. Since 1964, a computer programme in the United


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
States has been considered as falling within the law of patents. A French law of 2 January, 1968, allowed it to be patented. Such protection does not exist, however, in all countries.
"Know-how," which has recently come into the limelight, is a good example of this changing of concepts: technical progress and its increasing complexity has made the notion of the patent rather ambiguous. This new complexity falls substantially short of satisfying the criteria for patentability. While, as it seems, the term has been used since 1916, the formula has become familiar only during the last two decades. "Know-how" is linked to savoir-faire, competence, experi ence. The International Chamber declared in 1961 that industrial
"know-how" refers to applied knowledge - both methods and data - which are necessary for the effective utilisation and application of industrial techniques. "Know-how," which has an element of secrecy, is property which has an economic value and the law must protect it."
It is obvious that the jurist found himself confronted with concepts that were difficult to grasp: what exactly is the factory secret, so dear to legislators and economists at the beginning of the 19th century? What technical information exactly can be transmitted by rendering personal services? What is the notion of the state of technology, or improvement? What is a technical process? Legislation could only be based upon objective notions. G. Agnelli, President of Fiat, declared during an international conference held in Paris in October, 1968 that:
"It seems to me to be urgent and necessary that the very substance of "know-how" should be regulated on the legal plane . . . There is no doubt that "know-how" has since become something concrete, a form of industrial property that must be legally protected in view of the increased use that is being made of it."
In France, a decision of the Court of Douai mentioned it for the first time in 1967. Since then, a decree of 26 May, 1970, contained a definition of it which merits repetition: "All elements of a scientific and technical nature which accompany the acquisition or the grant of rights of industrial ownership, the technical studies, experiments and research and the scientific and technical data."
The absence of a law governing "know-how" has led to the conclu sion of contracts, important in the case of the transfer of technology from one country to another. Protection is therefore provided within the framework and on the occasion of these contracts. But this of course puts into question the validity of such contractual protection. In


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1057 this respect, there are conflicting legal decisions in many countries. An English proposal of 1968 is the only attempt to regulate what was being referred to as "industrial information."
Since a short time, "know-how" has turned to "show-how." From then onwards, it became necessary to engage in "show-how" which consisted in showing how to use equipment for a relatively long period, from six months to three years, depending on the particular plant involved. In this regard, Algeria has laid down very strict rules. For the construction of a iron and steel plant on the coast in accordance with contract stipulations, it was required that the foreign companies executing the project put the management in place, train Algerian technical personnel and work-force, set the production process in motion and conclude marketing agreements. Moreover, the contrac ting parties had to provide guarantees for a period of twenty years for the iron and steel plant against the risks of technical failure and against falling prices in the international markets. It was, therefore, a question of protection, both economic and properly technical.
Today, the question is being asked whether " the patent is an instrument of progress or just an archaic parchment." There are several reasons for this attitude.
The first reasons are purely internal in nature. While there is no doubt as to the usefulness of protecting industrial property, on the other hand, the practical measures that must be taken become more and more uncertain with the increasing complexity of the technical and industrial structures. The risks of disclosure from theft, movement of an individual from one society to another, sub-contracts, company mergers, services performed abroad, are all on the increase. But penal law protection is relatively circumscribed: it is dependent upon the identity of the wrong-doer, whether there was fraudulent intent or not, or communication to third parties, etc. In the Eastern-bloc countries where the inventions are evaluated, laws have been promulgated which protect even technical improvements. But, here again, many vague notions persist:
rationalisation, innovation, technical improvement. The end result is the grant of a certificate by the contractor who attests the authorship of the invention.
There are other aspects. Industrial property has largely passed from the realm of the individual to the collective. From protecting the individual, the patent has become a source of power for the large corporations which alone, or almost alone, are capable of providing


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
the necessary captial for research. There is scarcely but one example of a large public corporation which owns a large number of patents which does not use them to gain an advantage, even though it makes a selection among its applicants, namely, N .A.S.A. And this is espe cially so as its patents relate to a number of key industries.
The weight of the system has become, moreover, a source of considerable embarrassment. Every year, the world benefits from 4 to
500,000 patents out of 750,000 applications. The time required for study and the growing complexity of the inventions make the decision making slow (three-five years) and therefore costly. Besides, study periods are not compatible with the speed of the evolution of technolo gical progress. The boundaries between public domain and forbidden territory are becoming increasingly blurred. So much so that some believe that the system of patents is becoming a real obstacle to technical progress, while others are of the view that the benefits of a monopoly, however temporary, encourage research and prevent the waste of the research potential of an individual or an enterprise.
Situated at the cross-roads of law, technology, information and econo mics, the patent appears to be a meeting point whose social value ought not be ignored.
Nothing else is more clearly in issue here than the patent as a legal instrument and a promoter of technical progress. The guarantees often become very unstable even in those countries where the best protec tion exists, like Germany and the United States. Indeed, one notes that a high proportion of patents is annulled by the courts. By way of defence, the alleged counterfeiter counter-attacks and in most cases discovers an error which had escaped the examiners. The vast increase in the volume of documentation makes the task of researching the precedence increasingly difficult. The isolation of the national research bureau hardly facilitates the operations. And is the Third
World not entitled to protest this latest form of colonialism, coloni alism by technology which is the property of the rich countries?
Therefore, should the system be reformed? Should patents be created that are fashioned to suit their final destination? This is a problem that must be resolved on the national level and, as we shall see later, on the international level as well.
Radio and television are somewhat appropriate for this articulation of our observations, because they represent, in fact, at one and the same time the ownership of a technique with all its regulations and


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1059 protection of its users. It is useless to insist upon the importance of these two modes of communication: to give only one example, in 1949,
France had 297 television sets and almost 9 ,000,000 twenty years later.
Certain kinds of problems are created by the other modes of expression, like newspapers, and arise from the same policies. The question of the status of the transmitters is also political rather than technical: monopoly, pluralism among stations, and all the interme diate cases. But there arose a technical necessity to organise such plurality once it had been admitted. Let us remember that in the
United States at the beginning of 1964 there were 654 television stations and 5017 radio stations. "The unilateral and haphazard organisation among the air-waves cannot be tolerated." At the outset, the intervention of the state on its own territory has been and remains the rule. To the extent that state sovereignty over its air-space had been established in air law, it was possible for the state to prohibit the intrusion of radio waves into its air-space. But the state was also faced with the technical obligation to establish the frequencies of the dif ferent situations in that space so as to avoid interferences, including frequencies for amateur radio operators. Within the system of free operation, there was therefore an obligation to secure clearance from the administration for the frequency to be used. This problem soon presented itself on the international level.
There are certainly other equally important questions which these new techniques have raised and which the law has difficulties in keeping pace with: rights of authorship, piracy of programmes, diffi culties created by cable transmission. One expert in this law wondered recently whether the law of radio and television was anything but a pious hope.
The Means of Protection Whether directly or indirectly, technical progress sometimes brings a completely new slant to human relationships. These relationships themselves are widely diverse in nature. It is possible to distinguish several kinds which have in a way led to the creation of not one but several laws peculiar to each of the techniques involved.
The ordinary laws These are the current laws, those in which everyone takes their chances, as user or consumer, laws governed by contracts, expressed or implied.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
I - The first of these laws concerns the user of technology, to the extent that it involves dangers to him. And, quite obviously, this law, which is generally statutory in nature, must evolve in accordance with the changes in technology. The mining codes of the Middle Ages were already concerned with it, in a rather half-hearted way, it is true. The regulation of the lay-out and of the establishment of the levels were intended to protect both the miner and the other sub-soil owners, so intimately were the different kinds of explorations linked with one another.
Today, the statutory power is mainly exercised in relation to the difficult problem of security. Labour law, which primarily regulates economic and social relationships, is little concerned with this prob lem. Safety in the mines, we have just seen, has always been a preoccu pation with governments. It can be seen in both the 1744 Order relating to coal mines and in the great mining law of 1811 and even in the constitution of the mixed commisions at the end of the 19th century.
As techniques became more complex and the risks more numerous and substantial, regulation proceeded along two lines. First of all, certain rules had to be observed with respect to the machinery itself, both in their construction and with regard to maintaining a protective environment. There were also rules governing usage. We may cite the stamping of the steam engines submitted for inspection by a state department as one of the first rules.
Let us take, almost at the other end of the chronological spectrum, a complex machine, the car. The standards relate to the engine requiring it to be submitted to tests and upon certain accessories (headlights, brakes, etc.). Many measures are aimed at protecting the user as well as third parties to whom the car presents risks that are independent of his own free will: the highway code was created as a result of the invention of the car. One of the most recent regulations is by far the most surprising: the compulsory wearing of seat-belts. This regulation to some extent protects the user against possile imprudence on the part of a third party as well as on his own part. In this latter instance, the law protects the individual against himself. If compulsory vaccination is for the protection of the entire population, and if non-compliance can therefore expose third parties to risks, there is no legal obligation on one to undergo an operation when one has an attack of appendicitis, and the doctor has no right to operate on you by force. There is no law against suicide, except a moral one.


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1061 There are o.ther aspects, of course, of these forms of protection:
social, no doubt, but also financial. This fact was belatedly recognised because the first laws in France relating to accidents in the work-place were only made on 2 November, 1892. In France, in 1970, out of a total of 12,607 ,785 wage-earners, there were 1,110,173 accidents, 2,268 of which were fatal. To these must be added 170,328 road accidents,
1 ,558 of which were fatal. It is the least developed technologies, at least for the most part, that are responsible for the highest number of fatal accidents: these occur in construction (910) and metallurgy (318) .
Road accidents, it is well known, are infinitely more numerous. It is quite obvious that the cost to society is enormous: this financial aspect of protection ought not to be overlooked; protection is part of the public interest.
II - Improvements in techniques have also led to the development of certain forms of property, mainly intangible property. In these cases, it is not so much technique per se which is in issue but rather the products of certain techniques. It mainly related to reproductive work and it is well known how reproductive methods have developed. Chronologi cally, the time-lags in this area have been very great. This protection was first given to printed works. In fact, until the end of the 18th century, the monopoly was guaranteed by the prerogative granted by the King. Nevertheless, counterfeits were even more difficult to combat because they came from abroad, especially from the end of the
17th century onwards. Once again, the French Revolution created a new kind of property law from the moment that the Royal patent disappeared. The law of 1793 relating to artistic and literary creations gave recognition to an absolute right in the artist or the writer over his work.
This law was completely revised by the legislation of 17 March, 1957.
This revision had become absolutely necessary because of the increase in the methods of reproduction and diffusion: colour photography, phonograph records, radio, television, magnetic tape (the mini cassette) and photocopy are all means of diffusion which the legislation of 1793 naturally did not take into account. A 1954 copyright was worded as follows "all rights or reproduction, translation and adap tation are hereby reserved . . . . " In 1974, this was changed to: "all rights of reproduction, however minor, in any form whatsoever, including photographs, microfilm, magnetic tape, records, or any other form, are hereby reserved. "


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
But the new techniques are themselves the sources of particular products. These had to be protected in the same way as the literary production: a record can be copied, recorded on tape, and film or photographs can be reproduced. Let us enumerate as a recent manual has done, the questions which can be raised with regard to photogra phy: which kinds of photography are subject to the right of authorship?
Can one steal an idea from a photograph? Can one cite a photograph?
What is counterfeiting a photograph? Can there be unfair competition in connection with a photograph? What is the legal right with respect to a photograph? Does the person who orders a photograph become its owner? The matter is complicated by the existence of a method of diffusion which is easy and dissimulable prints. It has been described as the double game of "the single and the multiple." Since the end of
1960, two new markets have been developed: the market for photographic prints and the market for photo-albums. In the latter, the photographer markets his works just as the novelist his novels. But it was necessary that photography should be recognised as an art form:
for forty years, it has figured in the Museum of Modern Art in New York; since 1950 there have been photography exhibitions and public sales since 1971. From being the professional that he was, the photog rapher became an artist. How could his proprietary right be proved in relation to each and every print? Just as in the case of songs, the law on this subject is still in a state of flux as the technology evolves. The work of art can very quickly and completely become submerged in the public domain and the artist immediately loses his rights of control and often with it a significant part of the receipts that he would otherwise draw from it.
Consumer law Consumer law is one of those laws which at the present time have most sensitized populations, at least in the devel oped countries. One need only look at the number of committees, commissions and associations which have been set up almost everyw here to monitor the application of the rules already laid down and to formulate new ones. This consumer law has two aspects, one technical and the other economic: we shall deal here only with the former.
According to the products involved, the consumer used to deal directly with the producer who often handled all the phases involved in the making of a product. In our era of mass production, this is no longer the case: not only has the consumer lost all links with the


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1063 producer, but a single article can be the work of a large number of producers. It is well known, for instance, how the car has required manufacturers, sub-contractors, accessories, etc. The steel comes from some unknown producer, certain parts are made by sub contractors, the head-lamps, carburettor, horn/hooter and windshield wipers are all made by different manufacturers. The division of labour, a necessary price of technical progress, dilutes responsibility, increases the kinds of guarantees and certainly leaves the consumer unprotected.
During the Middle Ages, or more precisely since the Middle Ages, the regulations in the manufacturing industry were aimed at guaran teeing a certain quality merchandise. The first corporative regulations, the oldest of which date from the 13th century, contain, from a technical point of view, only prohibitions: prohibition on the use of certain equipment, as in the case of the spinning-wheel which it was claimed, left knots in thread, prohibition on carding considered very inferior to combing, prohibition on the use of certain products, especially for lubrication and dyeing. Quality was strictly controlled and was intended to protect the consumer, control being a kind of self-regulation on the part of different tradesmen. This control was matched by the rules of apprenticeship and access to the profession.
In the mercantilist era, until the end of the 18th century, the same policy was pursued with certain extensions and generalised appli cations. These regulations became more complete and continued to evolve as techniques were being perfected. However, control passed from the domain of the corporations to that of the state and was entrusted to officials outside the professions; in France, these were the inspectors of manufactured products who were also the most ardent supporters of technical progress. The appearance of manufactured products from sources outside the urban-based corporations had made this change of control necessary.
The French Revolution, in completely abolishing corporations and the inspection of manufactured products, left the consumer alone to confront his supplier.
It is only very recently that the control of certain products has made its reappearance, say, over the last thirty years and in a somewhat expanded form.
There is one early form of regulation that has not ceased to be important. It is necessary in any given technical system that there


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
should be a certain uniformity of measurement: what would one say of a system which makes it difficult to plug in a wall socket, or to find a screw to fit a bolt, or to set up an orchestra whose instruments are not tuned to a definite and universal vibration of the note A, in which ferrules were not placed on tubes, in which every make of vehicle had its own kind of spark-plug, etc.? This calls for a legislative process, dealing with measurements and regulations governing standards.
Measurement is an essential element. It has had to be gradually adapted, not so much, to be sure, to the requirements of technology, as to scientific imperatives. One need only look at the successive defi nitions of the metre in order to be convinced of this fact: first, it was a natural measurement, representing one ten-millionth part of one quarter of the earth's circumference, then it became an arbitrary measurement according to a standard that ignored the note played by thermic variations: platinum then special steels, such as invar; today, it is a scientific measurement. The fixing of measurements is a legislative procedure: weights and measures, but also the definition of the note A, are all within the province of the law. Is this not a first line of defence for the consumer who is thus able to rely upon a system of meas urements which is accepted by everyone because it was laid down by the legislator? Unification of measurements has been on the agenda almost everywhere since the end of the 17th century. Scientists were in as much need of it as were manufacturers and consumers. Is there any need to recall the basic accomplishment of the French Revolution in this regard?
The second element is that of the standard which came much later and affected quantities (dimensions) as much as qualities (the defi nition of the materials to be used). We do not have a history of standards, which would be interesting in more ways than one. At the outset, these were no more than technical practices which became customary without any legal constraints. Let us take the interesting example of the calibre of cannons which in France was reduced from the end of the 15th century to eight, then to six, in order to accommo date the supply of cannon-balls: this is perhaps the first instance of standardisation as dictated by mass production. The rules for drapers at the end of the 17th century prescribed the number of threads for the warp and the weft and the size of the material. The manufacture of spare parts for guns was begun in the early years of the 19th century in the United States by Whitney. But with the exception of cloth, the


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1065 particular objective was to standardise the manufacture of weaponry.
Since mass production was on the increase, it became necessary for lines of production which were inter-related to be harmonised and in all sectors, irrespective of who the producers were. It began with agreements between the professionals and through some kind of administrative rigidity, the mantle of regulation descended upon these agreements all in the interest of the smooth progress of the technical system. Mere acknowledgement by the administration, tacit or express, was gradually replaced by compulsion and corresponding penalisation. The fact is that progress towards universality moved more quickly here than in other fields. Then the bases of the problem were transformed and a new form of law somehow emerged. The emphasis very quickly changed from quantity to quality, and it was there that consumer protection became more obvious. It was no longer just a matter of technical facilities, justifiable though they might be, but rather pure consumption. The ban on certain harmful products and dangerous mixtures was really enforced. People have not forgotten the affair of the oils that were unfit for consumption or the adulterated wine. This movement primarily affects the preparation of foodstuffs but can also apply to the combustible materials used in the construc tion or the decoration of public buildings. As has been observed,
"buyers are increasingly demanding foodstuffs that simplify their household chores: ready-made products, ready-cooked dishes, preser ves, frozen foods . . . " Can these changes be effected without causing chemical pollution in foods? Now, we do know how rapid technologi cal progress in this field has been since the end of the Second World
War. The use of artificial products in animal husbandry, pesticides in agriculture, and chemical treatment of fruits and vegetables pose as many questions, some of which still remain without a satisfactory response.
It is only within recent times that this has become a source of concern in France: the law of 1 August, 1905, which sought to eradicate fraud in the sale of merchandise and the adulteration of foodstuffs represented the first step in consumer protection. Penal sanctions were provided for at the point of sale. The law went on to enumerate a long list of practices it sought to outlaw (fraud as to quantity, origin, nature or the qualities of substances). The difficulties are numerous: on one hand, the legislation must be constantly adapted to changes in techniques.
On the other hand, the central authority must of necessity take account


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
of the interests of certain producers whose powerful pressure groups are well known: this has been the case with the clearing of wine by using potassium ferrocyanide, a practice outlawed by the Court of
Appeal in March, 1960, but authorised later by a decree of Septem ber, 1962.
French standards are thus elaborated according to procedures which systematically involve the participation of producers, distributors,consumers, public bodies (Commis sioner of standardisation, scientific organisations and the French Association for
Standardization or A.F.N. O.R. ) . But the standard are accorded a legal status which reserves for them, in particular, an important role in the public markets, but without allowing their essentially contractual role, which is tied to the principle of consensus, to assimilate them to regulations.
Only one special brand, N.F. (the Norme Frangaise) has been created in accordance with the rules. In the food or the medical sector, the means of protection had to be infinitely more stringent. We will not deal here with pharmaceutical products whose commercialisation is subject to authorisation. We also know what the position is with respect to a certain number of other products which do not fall under the pharmaceutical legislation (tooth-paste, beauty products, etc.) .
Legislation was introduced in the food sector: the nature of products, procedures employed, proper manufacturing methods, are all regulated in this area and enforced. The legislation controlled the techniques.
The notion of contract, upon which French law rests, has today lost a great deal of its value. This notion presupposes, in effect, not only equality between contracting parties but also choice -free choice with regard to the responsibility being assumed. Automatic vending-machines, self-service and credit cards have all deprived the consumer of even the awareness that he is entering into a contract.
State regulation has had to remedy the defects. But the legislation was incomplete and today there is still an overwhelming imbalance of strength in the relationship between consumers, even when organised in groups, and the legal departments of the large public and private corporations.
Certain contracts are global in scope and are imposed not only without any possibility of modification, but also of substitution: tele phone, gas, electricity and even the maintenance contract for gas heating.


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1067 This has left no doubt in anyone's mind: mass production and distribution have had the effect of placing on the market products that are better priced but lower in quality. The continuous growth of companies over the last twenty-five years has occurred thanks only to the very rapid growth of a demand which is continually being renewed and encou raged by the availability of easy consumer credit. The "throw-away" economy, in which one discards rather than repair, thus became entrenched, voluntary obsolescence of products and the techniques of false innovation being two of the means employed by marketing in the interest of growth. For its part, technical progress has created gadgets which are capable of increasingly high performance, but which are also increasingly fragile - a logical consequence of the delicate nature of their mechanisms.
The consumer is increasingly incapable of judging the quality of merchandise and guarantees often prove to be illusory. In law, the
"guarantee against hidden defects" has for long been legally entren ched, the first laws dating from 1804, but the forms of action are such that despite the fact that the case-law systematically favours the consumer, it is very difficult for him to benefit from it. And the conventional guarantees often contain clauses that are totally illegal.
Even if mass production were to be strictly controlled, there would nevertheless be a certain waste that could not be eliminated without causing substantial financial losses to the manufacturer. In the view of some, in order to produce better, we must produce less: is such a thing possible in our present technical society?
From every quarter, people are clamouring for a law of consump tion, a practical law periodically reviewed in accordance with the evolution of techniques. The legal system in this area is profoundly out of touch with the technical system.
But it would be necessary to define the standards, define exactly the product and its nature, put an end to misleading advertisements and regulate in a precise manner all after-sale actions. Not only is the law out of touch, but the gap is growing increasingly wider.
The wild assaults Wild assaults are essentially those which affect an individual who does not participate in the technical activity whose consequences he suffers. One might think that this is a phenomenon of recent technology: in fact, it is much older. It has, however, become particularly developed with certain modern techniques.
The first aggressions are those perpetrated against the physical health of the "neighbours." We will look at only two instances in which the regulations, simple though they may appear at first glance, are nevertheless sometimes difficult to apply.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Our modern civilisation is, more and more, a civilisation of noise.
Boileau was already bemoaning this fact at the end of the 17th century, but today noise has assumed astonishing proportions. It has been estimated that a motor-cyclist riding a machine without a silencer and travelling from Neuilly to Vincennes at dawn can wake up approxi mately 300,000 persons. It is well known how noise can affect the physical and psychological health of individuals. There exist regu lations dealing with the kind of problems just mentioned which require certain engines to be equipped with silencers.
There are other regulations which impose upon individuals limits to the operation of their equipment. Such is the case, for example, with modern broadcasting equipment. Transistor radios have been out lawed in public places and on commuter transport. Rules have been made requiring the volume of radios and television sets to be lowered between the hours of 10.00p.m. and 7.00a.m. Even if the battle against excessive noise has been carried to its full limit and even if the regulations are strictly enforced, it is nevertheless true that modern technology produces continuous and often unbearable noise: the endless flow of cars on the great urban freeways, accelerating when the red lights change and the landing of aircraft are all difficult to control by means of strict regulations. If in the case of airports judicial action has been taken, the case-law has not, in the absence of a well established statute law, been able to put a brake upon this noise invasion. Will it ever be able to do so?
Among the other "harmful effects" (even the "harm potential" of a given technology has been studied), one of these which most preoc cupy public opinion is pollution. It is in fact as old or almost as old as noise. The furore it is creating today has made us forget that the problem of pollution was perceived a very long time ago. Ever since the 18th century, have we not sought to relocate abbatoirs away from the cities (by means of administrative measures, for example), or so locate hospitals that the winds would not carry the infectious ema nations to the people in the cities?
When, thanks to the techniques of the English, factories were concentrated on specified locations, rather than scattered here and there as before, it became necessary to consider the health of the people. Thus, there was enacted in France the great law against unhealthy establishments, the first general measure against what today we know as pollution. In truth and in fact, there was no obligation


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1069 beyond an enquiry into tlie level of discomfiture involved: if the populace was not opposed to industrial creation, all was well, whether the nuisance was obvious or concealed. And the cases of opposition were few and far between, so great were the potential advantages from the appearance of an industrial establishment.
Today, the legislative system is infinitely more developed and the consensus of the surrounding population is no longer relevant except to a very slight and limited extent, the construction of atomic facilities being the most obvious example. The technical departments are those which confirm or evaluate the potential hazards for a given envir onment, very extensive at times from a geographical point of view, since water and wind can spread these hazards over considerable distances. Prohibition on construction can be imposed, factories closed and fines imposed. In the last chapter of the historical section, we saw where that could lead. There are instances where the equip ment necessary to fight pollution imposes unbearable costs on a factory. In other instances, the final cost of the product will be such that the buyers will become fed up: this has been said to have happened in the case of the motor-car.
There are more insidious effects resulting from modern technology which can be equally damaging. These are more treacherous because they are less visible and less perceptible and more serious in the sense that they affect private life and public liberties. And we would go so far as to say that they are more serious still in that they can be the doing of the state itself and thus be perpetrated in a systematic fashion.
In the first group, one technique has been regulated, namely, photography. Here we see once again this new law evolving around a technique which, while not being completely new today, has nev ertheless developed considerably. Let us look at a few of the questions which a recent manual we have already cited has raised:
What are the rights of the subject over his photograph?
What is meant by "respect for private life"? Does this apply to street scenes or unauthorised picture-taking?
It is especially in this latter instance that case-law, and later statute law, has intervened, and particularly so because in the case of tele photo lenses it is sometimes impossible today to know when one is being photographed. And what about film-editing at which some have become so adept? It has therefore become necessary to protect the private life of individuals against the intrusions which the law at the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
end of th 19th century did not even anticipate. There now exist treaties in the law of photography.
Sound recordings are in exactly the same category, be it telephone wire-tapping or recordings on magnetic tape using microphones con cealed from the persons who are being taped. We will not dwell unduly on a problem which these last years has been the subject of a scandal which everyone remembers. There are regulations and case-law on the subject but these are both quite inadequate, especially as the tech niques in this sector are rapidly developing.
There are certain aspects of the new techniques which are even more formidable in that they affect both life and public liberties. And more so because they are in the hands of the authorities. So much so that today we are engaged in the elaboration of a "code of public liberties." We have already alluded to the fact that regulations governing wire-tapping were sometimes made in very vague terms, so vague that everything or almost everything was in fact still permissi ble. The security of the state would, if necessary, permit electronic surveillance of everyone, even on the basis of otherwise unjustified suspicions.
The other question has been raised for many years now. The centralisation of electronic filing without any control is truly a breach of confidence. The case of erroneous or false information thus stored, has been cited. What if, in the event of war some enemy or other got hold of these files? The danger is even greater because mechanical equipment is implacable and, being unconscious, is not compelled to withhold any information, and also because it is impossible for it to assess the value of and therefore screen the information it registers.
Our conclusion could be a very pessimistic one. There is certainly a shortage of law, a law which should be evolving together with techno logy. There is also a shortage of supervisory institutions with responsi bility to ensure the application of this law, or these laws. The law refuses to recognise the photocopy or the microfilm as proof (at least in most countries) but it has not succeeded in regulating telephone wire-tapping. What is in fact needed are magistrate-technicians entrusted with the task of applying a precise law and who will deliver executory judgements in every case in which circumstantial evidence is not relied upon. This should be the case in a large number of areas:
take, for instance, statistics or censuses. During the last census, it was readily admitted that people could refuse to respond to certain ques-


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1071 tions. A code is a good thing, but it amounts to nothing if one does not have the means to implement it.
INTERNATIONAL LAW
Technical progress very rapidly spilled over into the international arena. First, the problems of communication, then, more recently, new techniques raised questions that were completely different in scope to the extent that they affected the entire globe, then space.
From being relatively simple relations as they had formerly been, consisting only of international law and diplomatic confrontations, the relations between states became more and more complex.
In this way, international law underwent a substantial transfor mation. First, bilateral agreements were the most numerous. As the problems increased, international relations rapidly became gene ralised. It then became necessary to entrust the solution to inter national technical institutions; delegation of state powers to those institutions could not be avoided: (as, for example, the fixing of prices for international air transport).
The Origins of International Law With A Technical Basis The extraordinary technical advancement which began at the end of the 18th century, upsetting the relations between states, and its continuation into the 19th and early 20th Centuries, have progres sively led to the establishment of certain international rules that have been constantly revised in accordance with new developments. Inter national law had paid little attention to the developments in techno logy. One instance is the Geneva Convention after the dreadful slaughter of Solferino, which tried to inject a little humanity into a war which progress in the development of armaments was making more destructive of human life. Moreover, we are all aware of the difficulty that exists today to secure a reduction or prevention of the new forms of warfare (nuclear, chemical and biological).
Individual property of an intangible nature has also been the subject of agreements which have also been revised in accordance with


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
technical progress. This has been the case with literary property: the Berne Convention of 1886, which was periodically revised (the last revision taking place in Brussels in 1948), brought together a certain number of countries around a very rigid text. The universal Geneva
Convention of 1952, revised in 1971, brought together the parties to the Berne Convention and the other states around a more liberal agreement.
The problem of "industrial property" is much more closely related to the evolution of technology. Agreement was all the more urgent and difficult because the various legislations were clearly at variance with one another. The first international convention on patents was held in
1879. On March 20, 1883, a certain number of countries signed in Paris an international convention for a union for the protection of industrial property. The Union was given a permanent office in
Geneva with a purely administrative role (the office of artistic and literary property was integrated with it). This Convention was amen ded at The Hague in 1925, in London in 1934, in Lisbon in 1958 and in
Stockholm in 1967. But the amendments were not ratified by all the states parties to the Geneva Convention: a rather confused situation therefore resulted. The fact is that the patent is a license granted by the public authority and consequently is strictly national in scope.
Simultaneously, legislation was beginning to emerge in Europe: the result was the convention of 27 September, 1963, which brought about a unification of certain elements of the law of patents in the countries of the European Community.
A treaty of international co-operation in the field of patents was drawn up by the integrated offices in Geneva, constituting since 1970 the World Organisation of Industrial Property (W.l.P.O.). This draft was adopted on 19 June, 1970 at a diplomatic conference in Washing ton. It seems that the trend is towards a certificate of authorship of invention, as obtains in the Eastern-bloc countries.
We have observed how vague the concept of invention and, conse quently, also that of the patent were increasingly becoming: there were research results, but if the research did not yet lead to an invention;
there was "know-how," and now there is "show-how." From then onwards, one saw how difficult it was not only to create adequate legislation but also to harmonise - to the extent that this was possible - the different national legislations. There are indeed concepts that are not very easy to grasp in a concrete manner, that is to say, in the only


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1073 way that can make it the subject of a law. Technical skill, manual expertise, technical experience, technical knowledge, the state of technology and, correspondingly, research and development and technical improvement, the application of the technical process, are concepts that are almost impossible to define. On the international level, forms of protection can only be contractual in nature. But these can be subject to dispute in the context of national or international antitrust laws. From a political standpoint, these contracts may appear to certain countries of the Third World to be a new form of colonialism.
The international conventions that were inspired by the new communications technology were significant and indispensable. There were already precedents in existence, more limited in their objectives, dealing with certain great communication routes which political devel opments had internationalised. Such was the case with certain rivers. It was in the wake of the Congress of Vienna that the first decisions were taken to organise traffic both on the level of international law and on the technical level: the International Commission of the Rhine was founded in 1815, and of the Danube in 1856.
As soon as technology demanded continuity in the means of communications, of whatever kind they may have been, the need for an international law quickly appeared: so was it to be in the case of the postal service which existed for a long time in this respect on the basis of generally accepted customs, the telegraph, the telephone, railroads and air travel. It required a certain simultaneous harmonisation of state legislation and the adoption of common technical rules for such continuity to be achieved: it is well known, in this regard, that two countries, Spain and Russia, did not adopt the English railway gauge which had been accepted by all other countries.
The first international conventions were concluded in Brussels in 1863, opening to general application certain bilateral agreements dealing especially with telegraphy. In 1947, the Universal Postal
Union, both an economic and a technical organisation. passed under the control of the United Nations.
From the early days of the railway, it was quite evident that international agreements would be indispensable for the purpose of organising transport beyond national frontiers: there were technical questions, questions of time-tables and economic problems requiring logical solutions, whose implementation then had to be imposed upon


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
the various states. The first bilateral agreements were concluded in 1847, that is to say, from the very beginning of the railway when the lines reached state frontiers and therefore had to be connected with one another. In 1872, a European conference on time-tables was convened. This marked the start of co-operation to develop a common technology and to take common action. The oldest of the great international organisations for railway co-operation was the Inter national Association for the Congress of Railroads established in 1855 for the purpose of the mutual exchange of information. It was soon discovered that it was necessary to move one step higher. But it was only in 1922 that the International Union of Railways was created in order to promote "the unification and the improvement of the condi tions for the establishment and operation of railroads in light of the international traffic." The U .I. C. grouped together the majority of the
