European networks. A similar Association (the O.S.J.D.) brought together the railroads of the socialist countries and certain countries of central Europe which are members of both Associations.
More was achieved in the way of international co-operation; certain specialised institutions were thus established. Take,for instance, the
European pool for the common operation of some 250,000 carriages;
the Eurofina enterprise for the financing of orders of rolling stock and Trans-Europe Express groups (T.E.E. for passengers and T.E.E.M.
for merchandise).
International agreements were no less obligatory in the case of air transport. Here, the agreements had to be absolutely generalised and not limited only to the continent as in the case of the railroads. But they also had to include very broad concepts extending beyond the scope of mere air transport. It is well known that custom rather than regulations had led to the establishment of the so-called "territorial" waters extending to the distance of cannon-shot, namely 5 kilometres, to which we shall return a little later. Should the same thing apply to airplanes? The first international convention on air traffic was signed on 13 October, 1919 in Paris, that is to say, at the very moment that commercial lines were being first established. It affirmed that each contracting state enjoys "complete and exclusive sovereignty over the air-space over its territory," including therefore also the maritime territorial zone.
The Warsaw Convention of 1929 and the Rome Convention of 1952 were only private law conventions, that is to say, they regulated


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1075 transport conditions between companies and private individuals, and the respective obligations of the parties.
Public law conventions have attempted to resolve the problems of organisation and administration of aeronautical activities both from a technical point of view (navigation) and as regards their usage. In fact, on the eve of the Second World War, air traffic was sufficiently limited in scope not to give rise to very serious problems.
We alluded earlier to the fact that the problem of radio and television communications had quickly become international. There have therefore been efforts to arrive at as broad agreements as possible at least on the essential points. It must be admitted that the results have been very disappointing.
The first two conventions which, incidentally, did not enjoy unanim ity, were both timid and restrained. The Washington Convention of
1925 wanted the frequencies allocated by the states to be communi cated to the International Bureau of Frequencies. It was hoped that partial agreements could thereby be achieved. Similarly, the Madrid
Convention of 1932 established the principle according to which a state ought to limit the power of its stations to the level required to assure on its territory a normal service of high quality. The intention was, somewhat fruitlessly, to avoid conflicts: the development of techno logy, ideological differences and the Cold War transformed that
Convention into a more or less dead letter. Equally fruitless was the Geneva Convention of 1936 which required each country to undertake to ensure that the transmission heard in a country outside of which it originated should not disturb the public order, should respect the beliefs of others, whatever they might be and should not incite war.
At the end of World War II, during which the radio had played an important propaganda role, the situation was confused: most of the problems had not achieved a viable solution. It quickly appeared that the divergences of interests would inevitably cause every effort to reach an understanding to end in failure. Of course, the General
Conference of U.N.E.S.C.O. held in 1948 expressed the hope that radio or television reception would everywhere be free, taking into account the recommendations of the 1936 Geneva Conference which had been signed by only twenty states, not including the United States or the Soviet Union. The dispute between Russia and the Western countries prevented in reality any global accord. The only agreements in existence deal in fact with the division of the frequencies over


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
3950 kcs. (Geneva, 1951) and with a seasonal plan for high frequency time-tables (Geneva, 1959) .
The situation was therefore reduced to regional agreements which were more successful. Most of the Conventions were among Western
European countries. The Copenhagen Conference of 1948 attributed frequencies corresponding to 136 channels of 9000 kcs. for more than
300 stations. The Stockholm Conference of 1952, revised in 1961 , divided up the metric waves and the high and ultra-high frequencies. It was a modest beginning: still, these agreements were not rigorously observed. In this area, despite its great importance, obligatory inter national law is still far from making its appearance.
We have already stated, and we repeat, that the problems of pollution are at centre-stage. If they have assumed present dimen sions, they have nevertheless not been absent from the preoccupations of preceding generations. The first international convention dealing with pollution was held as a result of the Trail Smelter affair between the United States and Canada. It had to do with protecting a country against maritime-based pollution and a mixed commission was estab lished by the two countries on 23 February, 1931, in order to affirm the
"international obligation to prevent the acts of other subjects oper ating within their sphere of territorial competence from causing damage." This was an isolated event, no doubt, but one which was a forerunner of important developments.
Conventions relating to standardisation are indeed still at a forma tive stage. The increasingly generalised adoption of the metric system is one way of providing indispensable support. We have seen that within a majority of the countries themselves, the law of standards was still in its formative stages. This probably meant that it would take a long time to transfer it onto the international plane. In a certain group of countries: the United States, Canada and the European Commu nity, some initiative should be noted.
The evolution of techniques has therefore progressively led to the development of an international law encompassing an increasingly expanding number of activities of all kinds. Until the eve of World War
II, the processes by which this law was elaborated changed in accord ance with technical advancement: bilateral agreements, mulitlateral agreements, then international conventions were concluded in a grow ing number of cases. More often than not, it was the initiatives of a few states, generally the most economically and technologically advanced,


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1077 which gave the impetus to this legislation. However, in many cases, the conceptions were different and national legislation was often contra dictory. Sometimes it is the form of things, and we have seen this to be the case with everything relating to the problems of technology or techniques, in the elaboration of this international law which have long been considered indispensable. The work of the jurists could not help but be supported by the advice and the direction of the technicians.
Recent Developments in International Law The technical explosion of the post-war period brought into question not only some of the earlier agreements, but also raised a considerable number of new problems. New concepts finally emerged. In order to provide the solutions that were indicated, the international commis sions increased in number and one observes the particular importance of the great international institutions, the United Nations and
U.N.E.S.C.O.
Today, the notions of sovereignty are beset with difficulties caused by the appearance of the new techniques, difficulties resulting from techniques whose development has placed them on the scale of the planet and the universe. Real limited wars have been waged here and there because of a failure to find comprehensive solutions. Since we cannot deal with all these questions in a few pages, we shall limit ourselves to a presentation of a few pages, we shall limit ourselves to a presentation of a few of the problems which required rapid solutions when the difficulties became acute.
The advancement of certain techniques has rendered obsolete certain laws which had not been brought into question for a very long time. On the international level, the law of the sea had been limited to only two points: the breadth of territorial waters and the freedom of the high seas. The disappearance of the steam-ship had, however, made it imperative that a certain number of rules of navigation should be clearly stated and that responsibilities should be organised on the private level. Succeeding other conventions, the Geneva Convention of 1958 confirmed these principles and guaranteed access to straits and
"the right of innocent passage" (that is, passage which was not prejudicial to the peace, good order and security of coastal states).
Several events have occurred which have overturned the bases of the problem and brought into question the domain of sovereignty of the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
coastal state. The problem of fishing assumed new proportions with the appearance and increasing numbers of factory ships. The discovery of submarine deposits of petroleum (off-shore) and the possibility of exploiting these as well as the nodules containing certain metals, the ability of atomic submarines to remain submerged for long periods of time, all these technical facts have once again brought everything or almost everything into question. There was a wide range of reactions:
some countries took unilateral decisions. There were "herring wars" and "petroleum wars." It was therefore decided that the time was ripe for the preparation of a new convention to extend the breadth of the territorial waters to twelve miles, If this were done, 1 16 straits would no longer be part of international waters, but would belong entirely to states. These states would therefore be able to prevent the passage of certain types of ships, super tankers, for example, which create enormous risks in the case of accidents, or nuclear-powered ships.
Similarly, it was envisaged, especially for reasons involving petroleum, that the territorial waters could be extended over the entire continen tal shelf up to a depth of 200 metres, thus imitating the unilateral declaration of President Truman in 1945.
The final solution has not yet emerged from the discussions in international conferences. The fact is that the developments in techno logy are not all yet known and it may be that one might be encouraged to undertake periodic revisions of the distances. We shall see that the subterranean depths cannot be certain of escaping the constraints of international regulations, which have not yet been agreed upon. The often divergent interests emerge gradually and this encourages states to refrain from arriving at decisions too rapidly. They prefer, when possible, to stick to bilateral agreements like the ones concluded between Great Britain and Norway on petroleum exploration in the
North Sea, and with France in the Celtic Sea. But in these cases the facts were clearer and so created less difficulties. But the possibility that these agreements may have to be revised has not been ruled out.
As regards fishing rights, it seems that the difficulties have been practically resolved because the technology in this field is more stable.
Even though they may have been different, the problems of air traffic have assumed similar dimensions. It was on the eve of the First
World War that air traffic began to take on a definite importance, and rapidly became one of the essential means of transport after the
Second World War: today, some journeys can only be made by plane,


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1079 as we have seen. It therefore became necessary once more to revise air law, this time in a comprehensive manner. Like the law of the sea, air law is composite in nature. It is in fact partly administrative, financial, commercial, penal, etc. But, as has been said, air law is partly composed of rules which organise and control the operation of highly mechanised equipment which in turn is operated by people who are subject to very strict discipline. In this way, there can almost be said to exist a law of machines, of equipment, which are intimately dependent upon technology.
The Chicago Convention which was initialled on 7 December, 1944, even before the end of the Second World War, replaced the Paris
Convention. The principle of state sovereignty over its air space remained the first and most fundamental principle of this law. Later a certain number of rules were formulated in order to facilitate inter national air traffic, rules of non-discrimination, the principle of unifor mity and simplicity of national rules of navigation, the legal status of the aircraft, technical rules of usage and rules to control such usage (it is by virtue of these rules that certificates of airworthiness are issued which can even forbid the operation of aircraft which make too much noise), the capacity of aircraft operators, ground installations and their operation, rules of air traffic and the monitoring of such traffic.
The International Civil Aviation Organisation (I.C.A.O.), which was founded in 1947, was to be the organisation to introduce the reforms often necessitated by technical progress, to harmonise national rules and prepare bilateral or multilateral agreements. In order to demonstrate the scope of this international legislation, we can refer to some of the agreements in force which are annexed to the
Chicago Convention.
Licensing of personnel; rules of the air; meteorology; aeronautical charts; agreements on measurements in ground-to-air communi cations; technical operation of aircraft; nationality markings and registration of aircraft; certificates of aircraft airworthiness; facili tations; telecommunications; air navigation services; search and res cue; accident investigation; airports; aeronautical information services.
All the technical aspects of air traffic therefore had to be addressed. Moreover, their implementation is monitored by a pow erful association of navigation personnel who operate on the inter national level.


1ECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
On the level of private law, (relations between the transporters and the transported), the Warsaw Convention of 1929 had to be modified in the light of changes in air traffic: this was achieved by the Hague
Protocol of 22 September, 1955. The United States concluded sepa rately the Montreal Agreement of 4 May, 1966. The unification of these regulations, which relate only incidentally to technical problems, is now in progress.
The relations between companies (traffic, routes, prices, etc.) are regulated by the International Air Transport Association which succeeded in April, 1945 to a previous association. It is an inter national organisation, but non-governmental in nature.
International legislation is much more difficult to co-ordinate when one is dealing with individual problems. In such cases, chauvinism is the general rule. We shall take only two examples.
We have already pointed to the difficulties associated with an international regime of patents because the national rules were so contradictory among themselves. Despite an increasing number of conferences, the final result in this sphere has been a division which will be very difficult to overcome. In effect, what seems to prevail at the moment are multilateral agreements, often of a regional character.
The first proposals for an international patent, which originated with the Scandinavian countries, appeared in 1950. An agreement was signed among four countries: a patent awarded in one country was valid in the other countries without the need to satisfy any additional administrative requirements.
Within the context of the Council of Europe, partial results have been achieved, but with a particular reference to purely administrative matters. A European Convention harmonising administrative proce dures and documents was signed in 1953. In 1954, an international classification of patents was adopted.
We have already stated that the European Community was con sidering a patent that would be valid in all the member countries, and take precedence over all national patents. The formula is not without its shortcomings: relatively high costs, the need for the inventor to be constantly on the look-out for potential counterfeiters. In view of the objections that were expressed, several conferences were held in the
Community to try to resolve the difficulties: Munich in 1973 and Luxembourg in May, 1974. The formulae have therefore been refined:
agreement has in fact been reached on the German system and France


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1081 has renouncd its system of award of patents at will. The European Bureau of patents, which is indispensable to conduct the preliminary examinations, was subsequently fused with the German Institute in
Munich. The agreement has not yet been ratified.
The Organisation for Africa and Madagascar (and now also for Mauritius) has created a common office for industrial property, located in Yaounde in the Cameroon which began to operate in 1964.
At the start, there were twelve signatory countries to the agreement.
The domain of standards borders on that of patents. At any rate, it shares certain aspects with it and in particular that chauvinistic aspect which disguises obvious commercial interests. The standard often becomes in effect an instrument of commercial policy. It was enough to set compulsory standards in order to prevent the importation of foreign products. A striking example of this is provided by the case of refrigerators. At the end of the Second World War, the refrigerator industry was almost unknown in France. In order to develop the industry, standards were introduced, taking into account safety and energy consumption with the alleged aim of protecting the consumer.
The effect was almost immediate and within fifteen years the industry had been established without the need to resort to a restrictive customs policy. This continued so long as the standard was maintained. The
Treaty of Rome opened French borders, especially to Italian products, whose standards were considerably less strict and prices much lower.
France made the standard compulsory in order to protect the market of its own industry. The noise of the Concorde aircraft is another example in a certain number of countries. Beyond these special cases, more widespread no doubt than some would have it believed, the problem of international standardisation soon arose. "Trade in goods or tools, the circulation of scientific data and information of all kinds depend upon the universality of the characteristics attributed to the objects and the data which are exchanged." The first definition was the measurement which is the language of the standard: we know that today the universalisation of measurements is making good headway;
numerous international commissions are dealing with it. Soon, stand ardisation properly so called was to be quickly adopted. A great effort has been made in this direction and since then progress has appeared to be very rapid. But in many cases this can only have been a pious hope:
every make of lorry or airplane has its own engine and he who purchases one of them is thereafter tied to a supply of different


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
spare-parts for repairs. Assembly plants, which enjoy great popularity today, are only a palliative to the problem because here the problem of research and development in a given industry comes into sharper focus. The pure national standard, an accomplishment of yesteryear, now plays only a minor role in the majority of countries, at least in certain fields. "The whole art of standardisation lies today in building into each standard enough of what is national to enable it to take root in the country and enough of what is international to permit it to open the country to foreign trade."
As one would easily imagine, an international organisation was created: the International Standards Organisation (I.S.O.). And con ferences have been devoted to the application of standardisation to economic development. Similarly, a European standardisation commission has been established, comprising the Western European countries, the objective being that the "directives" aimed at eliminat ing obstacles to free trade should be based on standards enjoying a large degree of prior agreement.
This method of regulation, described as "with reference to stand ards" is enjoying growing support in principle. Distinguishing the role of "stating the technology" from the power to "state the law," and subjecting the former to mechanisms of consensus, and the latter to mechanisms of authority is certainly within the purest traditions of
Montesquieu, and the French practice in this respect, comparatively very advanced, has been emulated in Europe and the United States.
Born out of the confrontation between interests and needs, nurtured by the collective wisdom of the experts and finally borne onto the national and international plane by the achievement of significant, if not unanimous agreement, standards are acquiring the dimensions of a set of tools. From this point on, they can contribute to coherent policies: productivity, quality promotion, application of technical research, foreign trade, co-operation with the developing countries and the establishment of European unity. This desire, expressed by a
Le Monde journalist is particulary promising. This law, which is definitively discretionary, is certainly a generator of technical, econo mic and social progress.
Exchange in the field of ideas is extremely important. This was well understood with respect to certain measures taken immediately after the Second World War. The Marshall Plan attached great importance to the spread of technology by encouraging missions of experts to the


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1083 United States and leading to the establishment of the O.E.C.D . , always mindful of the transfer of technology, then to the European
Agency for productivity. The subsequent organisation with the devel oping countries poses exactly similar problems. Almost all the agreements are contractual in nature and often leave the less fortunate partners helpless before powerful economic institutions. N.A.S.A.
has decided to allow all who wish to do so to benefit from its "inventions" but it selects those to whom it grants free licences. This also constitutes a form of pressure.
In all the fields we have just described, only international agreements would be able to remedy what are often difficult situations.
It would be necessary to ensure that legislation recognises the income which the purchase of patents represents for certain countries, patents possessing all their economic and technical advantages, and the adop tion of certain standards which encourage the existence of more than one supplier. There is no doubt that technical colonisation is at its highest level at the present moment. Political decisions, and they can only be political, must lead to sufficiently profound modifications in the systems that are presently in place. Here, again, imagination must play its full role.
Some Broader Dimensions The new, so-called leading, techniques, have suddenly and enormou sly increased the problems of international relations: they have natu rally led to new and compelling concepts: space conquest, the exploi tation of the seas, satellite communiations and world-scale pollution are all completely overturning previously adopted solutions. Prof.
Ambrosini, the Italian delegate to the General Assembly of the United Nations, declared on 12 November, 1958, before the Political
Committee that: "Every new human activity that creates interests and can therefore lead to disputes must find an equitable and rational legal solution or otherwise lead to confusion and anarchy."
One might have thought that the law of the sea would be one of the easiest to preserve, while, at the same time, helping it to evolve in response to certain types of technical progress. Today, however, political conflicts and economic differences constitute obstacles to essential agreements. The exploitation of the seas - and, as we have seen, there had been some success in regulating certain problems and


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
certain types of fishing whose technology had been modified by the arrival of factory-ships- and particularly petroleum deposits and the treatment of the metallic nodules, could not but inevitably render obsolete long-established rules. Unilateral decisions were taken: we have cited examples of these, like the fishermen's war off the coasts of
Iceland which had vastly extended its territorial waters. In October, 1974, the Norwegian authorities protested against the activities of an
American petroleum exploration ship at a distance of 700 kilometres to the west of the Lofoten Islands: the ship was in fact found on "an extension of the country's continental shelf. "
A conference was convened in Caracas in June, 1974, in order to possibly revise the law of the sea. At the conference, the sea was declared to be a "common heritage of mankind." It was necessary to prevent the strongest and most technically advanced states from appropriating the submarine depths and all the riches of the sea hitherto considered free, and the militarisation of the oceans. It was therefore necessary to permit their exploration for the common benefit. These warnings had been given by the representative of Malta, Mr. Arvid
Pardo, to the General Assembly of the United Nations in 1967. It was necessary to find some kind of international regime that was appro priate to the oceans beyond the 200 mile zone in which coastal states exercise their own rights. Serious differences appeared from the outset between industrialised countries and countries of the Third World.
The United States and Japan which, together with Germany, are the most technically advanced countries with regard to marine explor ation, clearly showed that the "common heritage of mankind" did not in their view exclude capitalist-type solutions, even at depths of 500 metres. The nations of the Third World organised themselves into a bloc in order to demand the collectivisation of the international portion of the oceans. The positions were diametrically opposed.
The problem was a complex one. It was in fact less a question of sharing the new-found riches than of protecting the existing riches of certain countries of the Third World. The exploitation of sea minerals, which were subject to natural regeneration and which are presently known to exist over only 3% of the depths, affects countries which possess those minerals among their meagre resources. It has been calculated that by 1985, 18% of the world demand for nickel and half of the demand for cobalt could be satisfied. At the present time, 15% of all petroleum is extracted from off-shore deposits: 33% is forecasted


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1085 for 1980. Marine mineral deposits are 150 times more than those existing on land. The representative of Zaire articulated the concerns of the African countries. The production of metals from nodules would be less expensive than land production and if not controlled could cause a real "catastrophe" for the developing countries. It was therefore proposed that the exploitation of the seas should be carried out by an International Authority or through concessions by the grant of service contracts to private enterprises. The Authority would select the enterprises so as to give a preference to the developing countries. It would establish the rules for exploitation, guarantee a continuous transfer of technology for the benefit of the developing countries and have the authority to "modify, suspend or cancel contract in accord ance with changing circumstances."
There could certainly be other solutions: compensatory mechan isms, deductions from the profits of the exploiting companies for the benefit of the entire international community. But can one envisage such massive investments- estimated at between 2 to 300,000,000 U.S.
dollars- without exclusive rights over the zones awarded? Was this not also a form of pressure on the small number of metal-producing countries in order to prevent "artificial price increases?"
Everyone wants a solution, namely, an international convention which declares a new law protecting all interests, one which would not only be a maritime law but also a law of the sea. The extent of these problems and the divergences of interests are such that this solution seems difficult, if not impossible to elaborate. While disputes are going on in certain corners of the globe, discussion goes on in interminable international conferences. Progressively, one came back to the 12 mile problem, a distance which a majority of states seem to have accepted as the limit of their territorial waters, and to the creation of a 200 mile composite zone described as an exclusive economic zone. Three quarters of all the fish is caught in that zone and, at the present stage of technology, the majority of off-shore petroleum exploration also takes place in that zone. In this economic zone, each state would enjoy an exclusive right which would put an end to every or almost every preferential right.
The law is ill-adapted to the constraints of a developing technique and the hazards of geography: what if the continental shelf is very broad and relatively shallow in depth, as in the cases of Australia,
Argentina and Canada, to name a few examples? The dispute


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
described earlier between Norway and an American company related to petroleum exploration at a distance of 400 miles to the west of
Lofoten Islands and at a depth of 1 100 metres; this is a good illustration of the fact that any agreement would only be appropriate to a given state of technology.
After two unsuccessful meetings, a third international conference was held in Geneva in March, 1975. Despite countless private meet ings held over an eight-week period, agreement has not come any closer. Three reports formed the basis of the discussions. A 12-mile limit of the territorial sea and a 200-mile exclusive economic zone were accepted. Beyond that, the sea's riches would constitute the common heritage of mankind which no nation can appropriate for itself. The benefits of these riches would accrue to the world community, in particular to the developing countries and to the land-locked countries
(22 out of 148 countries). The superjacent waters would be inter national waters subject to freedom of passage, fishing, scientific research and overflight, provided that such freedom does no harm to any state nor affect the quality of the marine environment. In order to manage the common heritage, it would be necessary to create an international organisation, "The Authority," whose mechanisms and powers were diagramatically described in one of the reports.
At that meeting, 3000 delegates represented 150 countries. The Geneva Conferences of 1958 and 1960 had only resulted in the signature of separate conventions. The Conferences of New York in
1973 and Caracas in 1974 had isolated the differences. This time, a general convention was desired. The same antagonism would inevita bly pit the industrialised capitalist countries or the socialist countries, on the one hand, against the countries of the Third World on the other.
First, the dispute turned around the exclusive economic zone. Should such a notion be adopted, leading to the inability to fish by those countries which did not possess the necessary equipment? One could end up using some kinds of fishing licences, like those the American tuna operators, then the American government, had bought, the former from Ecuador, the latter from Brazil. If this exclusive zone were to be adopted, as seems likely, the fishing industry would be devasted by it. With respect to the common heritage of mankind, to use the term adopted in all the documents, it is quite obvious that the countries of the Third World would prefer to have it managed by an international organisation in which they would constitute a majority.


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1087 The other group accepted that a new law of the sea ought to aid in the development of the developing countries, but tried to preserve as much of the traditional freedom of action as possible.
The only tangible result of the conference was a new international conference which was to be held in New York at a later date.
We have referred to all the difficulties associated with the appea rance of an international law of radio and television. The struggles which pitted certain groups of states against each other during the period following the Second World War were only a reflection of the technical advancements which increased the reach of transmissions, and the accompanying international disputes over certain key princi ples. Judging from the great options defined by international conferen ces, for which, it is true, there is less than universal consensus, everything or almost everything could have been resolved: the limita tion of transmissions to the national territory, failing which, freedom of reception with simultaneous respect for domestic situations. The separation of conceptions in matters of ideas and hence of telecommu nications into two, or even several blocs, renders practically impos sible any prospect of a general and universal convention.
This is not to say that partial agreements have not been concluded despite all kinds of difficulties. The European Union for rediffusion
(1950) was entrusted with the task of resolving legal problems and gathering technical documentation. It was to lead to the Eurovision agreements which tried to remove obstacles of a technical, legal and financial nature. The system is now relatively efficient. The Inter national Organisation for Rediffusion and Television is making laud ale efforts to broaden the spirit of co-operation. The conferences held in
Atlantic City in 1953 and Geneva in 1959 resulted in the establishment of an International Commission for the registration of frequencies. It can, where possible, cancel fictitious or inactive assignations, and combat, also where possible, the monopolisation of frequencies which are not put into immediate use or are abandoned after a while. But it has no power to prohibit transmissions in an unregistered frequency.
There is no better example of these difficulties than the case of pirate stations. These stations were most often situated on the high seas and therefore beyond the reach of the national authorities. They can therefore transmit either on unassigned frequencies or even on fre quencies reserved to other states. One could invoke the responsibility of the flag-state, but registration was often in a state that was not a


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
signatory to the international conventions. The European agreement of 1964 made provision for indirect action. Such was the case with the station's supplies and researching the sources of their publicity material. It was therefore possible to suppress these stations. But certain countries did not ratify the convention. The failure to do so on the part of certain countries on the continent (Switzerland and
Austria) was not a serious matter, but the United States also did not become a party and one can well imagine Europe being flooded with
American advertising broadcasts since these stations are largely moti vated by commercial profit. The Scandinavian states have taken measures which even contravene certain rules of international law:
they have declared their restrictive law applicable even on the high seas.
The difficulty in establishing a law in this field extends beyond purely national problems, which have generally been rather easily resolved.
Here, too, the misunderstandings are deep-seated.
The passage from the layers of the atmosphere to outer space presented similar problems. The law of outer space, which is beginning to emerge, and which can only be constituted on the international level, is certainly the kin of air law, but it appears more and more similar to the law of the sea in its dimensions. Its origin dates, in fact, so far as discussions are concerned, back to the launching of the first sputnik on 4 October, 1957. On December 11, 1959, Mr. Kouznetsov,
Soviet delegate to the United Nations, declared that "the exploration of space is international by its very nature." Today, it is obvious that with all the extensions in the activities of satellites the questions have multiplied.
Present air law only took account of the atmosphere, and, for obvious reasons, did not concern itself with the area beyond. The first problem to be settled therefore was whether one should apply verti cally the principles of the law of the sea: absolute sovereignty over the atmospheric layers and total freedom of space beyond. In such a case, a satellite having its perigee in the earth's atmosphere and is apogee beyond, it would be subject at one and the same time to both air law and the law of outer space.
A United Nations Resolution of 14 November, 1957, laid down certain principles within the context of the agreement on dis armament. "The study of a system of control is aimed at ensuring that the launching of devices into outer space will serve only peaceful and


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1089 scientific ends." The International Astronautical Federation, a private law organisation, established a committee in October, 1957, with the task of "defining the spheres of competence of air law and the law of space." The difficulty involved in defining the limits between air space and space proper appeared rather rapidly to be of secondary importance or at the very least not to be an immediate necessity.
Gradually, ideas became clearer. The first was the freedom of space.
Just like the old freedom of the seas, it appeared that the freedom of space was a primordial solution. It was in fact paradoxical to attribute absolute sovereignty over an unlimited space which, on account of the celestial movements, is never the same from one moment to another.
Beyond this, the problems were minor from a certain point of view, at least that of the circulation of space devices. The situation was the same with the legal status of these space devices, these being for some time the province of only a few nations. For a long time, the appro priation of the moon or other planets was a relatively remote pos sibility.
This is why the Space Treaty of 1967 contained only few precise details. It established, however, the acceptance of the principle of the freedom of space and of free scientific research in space. It went on to state, but really in a very vague manner, that the activities in space are
"the prerogatives of all mankind." It was clearly stated, however, that the uses of space are for entirely peaceful purposes.
It was becoming obvious that what was important was not the mere fact of the entry of the devices into space, but rather the exact nature of the activities in space. These gradually became more clearly defined as the technology developed. One fact needs to be emphasised:
namely, that all possible military activities in space are of necessity beyond legal regulation because they can only be the subject of disarmament agreements which are primarily political, rather than technical or legal agreements.
The problem of telecommunications assumes various forms which must be separately considered. The first is satellite telecommuni cations such as those that are now developing with great speed. An international organisation for satellite telecommunications (shortened to INTELSAT) was created in 1964 with a membership of 86 countries. Between 1964 and 1974, there was considerable technologi cal advancement. In 1971, a convention was drawn up which soon revealed difficulties in its implementation.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
The role of INTELSAT is to transmit information by telephone and television on an international scale. The first telecommunications satellite was launched in 1965 (Early Bird). By the end of 1974, the organisation was operating four large satellites, INTELST AT IV and three INTELSTAT III, which thereafter were used only to fill the inadequacies of their successors. Once a new generation of satellites is produced, their predecessors become completely obsolete. A problem of amortisation therefore arises.
At the present moment, the Americans are the leaders in the organisation: all the satellites and all the launchers are American owned. A recent test-launch made jointly by France and Germany was possible only with the aid of an American launcher, and even so it involved only an experimental device. Until 1971, Washington con trolled everything and was the foremost operator. In 1971, the system was somewhat relaxed. The United States still owns two thirds of the interests in the organisation, but the American company which runs it is now only a temporary concessionary. Military networks apart, there are also private networks for exclusively American domestic transmis sions as well as a considerable number of projects. It was also found necessary to increase the number of receiving stations, like the one in
Plemeur-Bodou in France.
It is still true to say that the control of a technology in both its material and financial aspects is an obstacle to any logical construction, if it amounts to a monopoly. There was a law governing telegraph cables and one for radio transmissions, but there does not yet really exist a law governing satellite transmissions. And this largely explains the maintenance of the old technology.
With regards to these satellite telecommunications, there is another quite disturbing problem. These satellites are indeed capable of transmitting radio and television broadcasts and so are able to break the monopolies and the controls which the large majority of gov ernments exercise over these broadcasting media. Can one therefore speak of "infringing the public opinion" of a state? Opinions on this subject are quite confused and very divergent.
A 1948 U.N.E.S.C.O. Declaration recognised the right of every individual to listen freely to radio broadcasts from other countries
(there was as yet no question of television). On the other hand, the Space Treaty of 1967 recognised that space devices could only be used for the well-being and in the interest of all countries. It was necessary


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1091 to conform to international law whose aim was the maintenance of international peace and security. All came to nought, even the defi nition of the frequency bands and the position of the satellites. The major international authorities were all rocked by political debate, particularly in October, 1972. The U.S.S.R. submitted a draft treaty which proposed that countries invaded by transmissions would be able to jam the frequencies and even destroy the device; and provided, consequently, that broadcasting satellites should be purely government-owned. The United States argued for the free exchange of ideas and information. Some developed countries such as Canada,
France and Sweden, took intermediate positions. No progress has been made since then.
The last sensitive point is electronic long distance detection, one aspect of which is aerial photography. One can take aerial photographs without breaching the rules of air law. One can therefore photograph not only geographical spaces but also industrial or military installations and detect the presence of mineral resources.
Two American devices are used for this purpose: ERTS (Earth Resources Technology Satellite) and a compartment of Skylab, EREP
(Earth Resources Experimental Package) whose films and magnetic tapes will be brought back to earth by astronauts. In reality, only the
United States and the Soviet Union are capable of putting into orbit such voluminous devices for this type of work. Canada and Brazil have built stations for receiving information transmitted by ERTS, but only on their own territory, in return for information furnished to N. A. S. A.
All the information is stored in a data bank in Sioux Falls (South Dakota).
The United Nations has been considering this matter and would be disposed to set up an international organisation which would guaran tee free access to all information from that source. But the United
Nations cannot nor should not launch satellites. One would therefore have to entrust this matter either to the United States or to an ad hoc institution. It would be necessary to discuss the question of program mes, financial aspects, operation, in short, all aspects of the organi sation including legal solutions.
The last great problem is that of pollution. Population growth and industrial development have transformed pollution into an inter national problem: it affects the atmosphere as well as the sea and the rivers. Recent examples are still fresh in everyone's memories: the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Rhine, dead from Basie to the sea, the Torrey Canyon disaster, in which the ship sank on the high seas spreading its black cargo onto the
French and English coastlines, and the red mud of Corsica. Not only the coasts are threatened, but also the ecology of the seas and, and with the pesticides, the ecological balance of the entire earth.
From the international point of view, the problem of pollution has two main aspects: (A) protection and therefore prevention, even in zones under international jurisdiction; and (B) the measures to be taken in common in order to restore the necessary ecological balance.
The first steps in this direction have been very modest ones. A 1954 Convention signed by a certain number of countries prohibited the discharge of hydrocarbons into the North Atlantic. If the discharge took place within territorial waters, the offence would be the responsi bility of the country that exercises sovereignty in that zone. Otherwise, the country of registration of the ship would bear the responsibility. In fact, supervision only takes place within territorial waters with the result that pollution on the high seas is ignored.
In February, 1972, an international conference dealt with pollution from the dumping of ships' waste. Some interesting information was gathered but no decisions were taken. In September, 1973, an inter national convention was convened to discuss maritime pollution from land-based sources, that is , pollution caused by toxic substances discarded directly from the land or carried to the sea by water currents.
Eighteen European countries were either represented or sent obser vers, the same countries that did so in Oslo in 1954, namely, the riparian States of the North Sea, the English Channel and the North
West Atlantic. In reality, the aim was to draw up precise documen tation, although a future convention was also drafted. The various countries were in fact left free to adopt effective legislation. Too many special interests were involved to permit the establishment of a system binding upon everyone.
A trend was developing towards regional regulations co-ordinated as much as possible among one another. In Rome, in 1972, the countries of the Western Mediterranean met to conclude an agreement similar to that of Oslo. The discharge of hydrocarbons was prohibited. Follow-up action was supposed to be taken, but at the moment is not forthcoming. In the case of the red mud, for example, the difficulties involved in securing a strict regulation and enforcing it rigidly are manifest.


TECHNIQUES AND LAW
1093 The destruction of harmful waste and the control of certain kinds of pollution often require substantial financial expenditure. Certain fac tories have been forced to close their doors: one can imagine the kind of social effects that such a thing can have on the local populations.
Many works have been written on pollution but hardly any of them deals positively with the fight against it.
As far as rivers are concerned, it is necessary to bring together not only all the riparian countries but also all those situated within the river-basin in question. Only then can co-ordinated measures be taken, the only ones that are truly effective. Certain discussions were held within the European Community in 1974 with respect to the
Rhine: agreement was far from being complete (besides which, certain countries involved, like Switzerland, were not represented).
The final kind of pollution is that resulting from the use of pesticides, a problem compounded by winds and animals which can spread them very far afield. It has therefore also been necessary to consider regulations which one could only hope would be internationalised.
The first of these was taken against DDT after observations made since 1966: this product is now being outlawed just about everywhere. In
1973, measures were taken by the member countries of the 0.E.C.D.
to restrict the use of diphenyl-polychloride, its use now being permit ted only within closed systems.
It is not easy to establish laws when applying them on a worldwide scale, let alone generate respect for them even if they only affect individuals and individual actions. When law relates to new tech niques, certain of which are subject to frequent change and rapid evolution, the difficulties are all the greater because they often possess political and military implications: the opposition to the extension of territorial waters and the creation of economic zones, electronic long distance surveillance or satellite telecommunications, maritime pol lution (which will assume greater significance with atomic waste) are all problems whose solutions still lie in the distant future.
Law is demanding, and rightly so. In any dispute, there must be at least two parties involved, the object of the dispute must be clearly defined by precise laws, there must be proof, a procedure, a court, a decision and enforcement measures. In the present-day life of individ uals and of nations, in private as well as in public law, as in inter national law, technical changes and the techniques themselves are more and more of such a nature that to some degree they escape the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
legal process. And it so often happens that by the time the law takes cognisance of the facts, it has already become obsolete in relation to the changes.
New facts emerge that were not previously anticipated. And even new notions which are difficult to define: there is no better example of this than "the common heritage of mankind," the subject of so much discussion both in the law of the sea and the law of space, two aspects of the law which are themselves in a state of constant change.


TECHNIQUES
AND POLITICS
Everyone will agree today, that technical progress has become the state's business. A look at the newspaper is enough to bring home the fact that technology has spilled over into the realm of politics.
Strangely enough, no in-depth study has been made of this aspect of political life. This could indicate that, as yet there exists no truly coherent and systematic technological policy.
In fact, it is not new territory, even though contemporaries and often modern historians have not been fully aware of it - ample evidence of which will be found throughout the historical part of this work. There are several areas in which the political importance of techniques is self-evident. Such is the case, for example, of all military techniques.
Cities and princes have long been in the habit of snatching the best engineers from each other and there has never been a dearth of technical mercenaries. Doubtless, for a while, all were content to take advances in military techniques as a matter of course. For some time, there were more capable personnel available than there were research projects into new techniques. It was only at the end of the 19th century that there surfaced some sort of policy on technical research. At first, this was a public interest-cum-self-serving activity carried on by a handful of private firms until, in relatively recent times, it led to the creation of state research units and laboratories.
This brings us to certain fundamental distinctions. In fact, there are two main types of technological policy: first, the so-called transfer of technology type which we could describe as a policy of borrowed technology and second, the developmental policy. Then there are the research policies, which prove increasingly awkward, insofar as
-research is difficult to plan and especially today, is intricately linked with basic scientific research.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
THE HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
In any event, this attraction exerted on the authorities by technical advances in any shape or form, went beyond the strictly military. Over the centuries, attempts have been made at drawing up "listings," with a view, not so much of innovating, but rather of disseminating the most advanced techniques. The Ptolemies, with the School of Alexandria and the 10th century Byzantine emperors were, to a certain extent, the forerunners of Colbert. He foresaw an important technical role for the
Academie des Sciences which, more than fifty years later, would take shape in the form of the Description et Perfection des Arts et Metiers
(Description and Improvement of the Industrial Arts and Applied Sciences). It is not inconceivable that these "listings" may have been seen as a sort of starting point on the road to improved or new techniques.
Another example was the progressive takeover, by the state of the drafting of corporate regulations, once handled by the professional groupings concerned. However, these regulations dealt more with the definition of a product than with the techniques themselves.
If a certain ingredient or process was to be banned, it was because, rightly or wrongly, it was felt to be detrimental to a particular level of quality in the production, quality, notwithstanding the lifting of a ban here and there for economic reasons. When economics, with the advent of mercantilism, became a political matter of the state, corpo rate regulations followed suit as a matter of course. Economic and technical stability went hand in hand, resulting in the transition from professional autonomy, to state controls, to actual product inspection.
There is ample demonstration of the constant attention given to technical progress, as a whole, by those in charge of increasing production or reinforcing economic or military strength. However, the principles of an overall policy in this field took time to evolve. The attempts by the Ptolemies or the Byzantine emperors were not policies for progress. It was a case of the dissemination of the best-rated techniques with a few improvements. The same applied to Colbert with his Description des Arts.
What mercantilism tried to achieve was the broadest possible trans fer of technology. This could be seen as early as during the reign of
Louis XI in France at both the economic and military levels. In the


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1097 whole of late 15th and early 16th century Europe, similar, almost systematic attempts could be found in Muscovy, under Ivan III, where a call went out for architects, metal casters and miners, in Hungary, where Matthias Corvinus tried to collate all the technical treaties of his era and in early 16th century England. At the same time, we find Saxon miners and Italian engineers going out to peddle their services across the length and breadth of Europe.
For there to have been an organised search for progress, with direction and definition, there would have to have been a concept of progress, that is, a definite idea of and potential for achieving that progress. Co-ordination - an absolute necessity if one acknowledges a technical system - is one of the cornerstones of a "technological policy." Let us closely trace, with the help of the historical material at hand, the evolution of this slow process leading to the choice of the best techniques, to improved techniques and, where necessary, to new inventions.
Stasis can occur in the field of techniques. A static condition is that wherein all quantities, whatever their nature (be they industrial or agricultural production, money in circulation, population, etc.) are stationary worldwide, or determined to be so. It is obvious that, in this hypothesis, technology must also be conceived of as stable. Any inequalities would relate to distribution among the people within one state, from one region to another, from one individual to the next. The idea therefore, would be to acquire most of the wealth: to export, increase population, hoard precious metals. This was the basis of the technological policies which caused certain economic policies to succeed.
The first step was to hone the techniques in use to perfection, or in other words to bring them to saturation point and codify the rules. In order to circumvent the need for imports which require local currency outflow, the idea was to introduce into the country whatever manufac tured goods it lacked and consequently, foreign techniques had to be imported. There is no better example of a systematic application of this policy than that carried out by Colbert. The role of the Academic des
Sciences was to be important in this respect, as was also the Description des Arts et Metiers project. Indeed, when the first volume of this collection was published in 1761 , the collection was already largely outdated since it took no account of the Industrial Revolution in
England.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Louis XIV's Minister had ordered to be drawn up what was, to all intents and purposes, a catalogue of the branches of technology which it would be desirable to introduce into France. In the light of modern day experience of the transfer of technology, it would be appropriate to take a second look at Colbert's policy, noting its good points and understanding its foibles. For years, all French diplomacy was occu pied in that task. Entrepreneurs were brought in, like Van Robais, who set up a cambric manufacturing plant in Abbeville, Saxon workers specialised in the manufacture of tinplate, glassmakers from Murano and many others. This policy was supported by a whole infrastructure, from customs duties to product concessions, from tax exemptions to the beginnings of technical education.
Of course, this transfer had its repercussions. Certain countries had to define their technological status. Restrictions on the free movement of workers or machines were frequently imposed, with infringements carrying penalties as high as the death penalty, as was the case in
Venice. In 19th century England, a ban on the export of machinery was in force until 1825.
The crossover from the idea of technical improvement to that of the new invention, could only be achieved easily in the context of a techno logical policy. Here again, Colbert carried out some in-depth studies, wherein the methods used were really rather questionable. With a view to outfitting the Royal Navy with the best equipment possible, the
Minister had them amalgamated into one model ship, the best features from the various existing naval vessels. It was quickly plain to see that the supposed marvel would turn out to be nothing but a hybrid mon ster. The fact is that it was no easy thing to have a policy geared to invention or innovation. The notion of promoting the inventive spirit among technicians went against the grain of an economic policy much more attuned to efficiency by the utilising of known processes than to flirting with new techniques. Patents had made their first appearance in the 16th century and acquired more character and definition in the course of the 17th century. Import patents on foreign techniques were being developed. In France, the Academie des Sciences once again acted in a consultative role, advising the Government from a technical point of view, on the granting of concessions which replaced patents and on any grants and incentives which might be awarded to inventors.
This was the origin of the famous schedule of Machines approuvees (Approved Machines) eligible for certain state concessions.


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1099 It would be interesting to delve deeper than the studies done so far, into the reactions of continental governments to the English technical revolution of the 18th century. In England itself, the government remained almost totally detached from efforts to transform then existing techniques. Any intervention on its part was indirect, through the updating of certain institutions and the technical specifications of orders placed. Proper commercial and financial policies were more than adequate to generate technical research.
Generally speaking, the mercantilist notions of stability prevailed on the Continent as shown by the strict and broad-spectrum regu lations governing most manufactured products. For the governments, the first task at hand was to seek information. We note the almost complete lack of enthusiasm of the industrialists of these countries for the idea of crossing the Channel to Britain and finding out for themselves the facts about the technical transformations. In most cases, therefore, it was left to official missions to bring back the British novelties. These missions boasted more than one competent persona lity, the level of competence and degree of interest in technical progress increasing with every passing year. In France, as in Prussia, the manufacture or mine inspectors entrusted with the task of sup ervising the implementation of the traditional regulations were the very ones who followed, most closely, developments in technical across the Channel. For the most part, they were the ones who introduced the new techniques onto the Continent and who pressured their governments and national business sectors to follow suit.
Naturally, there were other forms of state intervention. Just as Colbert had done, foreign business and labour were invited into continental Europe in order to import British techniques. In most cases plants were set up with the help of state funding. We can name
Holker or Waddington in France, and Cockerill in Liege, as examples of persons who set up prosperous businesses in this way.
These interventions were in no way consistent. In other words, no overall policy existed with regard to the transfer of technology.
Meanwhile, the British technical revolution was drawing to its close in the late 18th century. On the eve of the French Revolution it was too soon perhaps, to conceive of wide ranging effective action in this regard. This action, therefore, was brought to bear mainly in specific technical fields deemed essential. Thus the Court of Turin quickly sought to modernise its mining industry. The engineer, Robilant was


1 100
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
sent on a mission to Saxony, then the most technically advanced country in this field and more specifically to Freiberg, where a school of mining was established. This mission culminated in the setting up, at the Academie, of a laboratory and a school of mining in 1754. Another engineer, Napione, made his way to England in 1786, bringing back with him elements of metallurgical techniques. On the run from the
French invastion, he migrated to Lisbon where he worked for a long time in the military arsenal.
The establishment of state technical schools - some open to private business as was the case of the School of Mining set up in France in
1783 - gave definite impetus to the technical modernisation process.
Neither should we overlook the military schools, almost the only schools at the time dispensing scientific and technical education of any recognised standard.
THE LIBERAL PERIOD
It would seem that considerable change took place during the first half of the 19th century. State assistance had been dwindling but, on the other hand, the technical curiosity of the business sector was on the increase. To a certain extent the previous trend had been reversed, probably as a result of the spread of liberal economic ideas. This general statement must be qualified according to the state in question and the type of intervention involved. In technically backward countries, state action was generally extensive. Is it any surprise that, in spite of recent symposia, the research that is vital for a good grasp of the problem is - we claim - lacking.
State assistance remains considerable though only indirect and in clearly defined forms. Through it, great power was wielded in the economic sector. In many a European country protectionist, even prohibitionist customs regimes were set up from 1821-1822, thus facilitating investment in "key" areas where technical change was most desirable. We must point out also that customs rebates and even tax relief were applied to imported equipment.
Technical education was another channel through which the state demonstrated its attentiveness to these problems. In France, in the


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1101 second half of the 18th century, technical schools were established, mainly for the training of state technical personnel - Ponts et Chaussees
(Public Works), Mines (Mining) , Genie Maritime (Marine Engineering). A higher body was created by the Revolution, covering these schools as well as the Military Engineering and Artillery schools.
It was the Ecole Polytechnique, a general scientific institution meant to provide advanced scientific knowledge before technical specialisation.
While intended for state engineers, these schools, nevertheless, would turn out a large proportion of management personnel to private industry. We have taken note of all the imitations which sprung up across Europe: in 1819, The Vienna Polytechnic, in 1816, the
Academic Mining School in Kieke, Poland, and in 1825 the Warsaw Polytechnic Institute, to name a few.
In France there were two original institutions worthy of note. The first, born of the Revolution, was the Conservatoire National des Arts et Metiers. Born, not only of the Revolution but also of the new teaching methods which originated in the 18th century: teaching through actual and concrete demonstration. This had been achieved for some of the natural sciences through the Jardin du Roi, for physics through all the physics laboratories. It was the Abbe Gregoire who launched this for the technical arts and sciences with the creation of the
Conservatoire where through the use of models, theory and practice were closely linked. In a short space of time, this establishment was to seem to be a school for executives.
It was on a slightly lower level that the schools of Arts and Trades were born. Outlined, under the Old Regime by the Duke of Rian court, they were taken up under the Empire which insisted on the training of "petty officers of industry." Then the schools flourished under the Restoration. As techniques became more complex, they became the nurseries for middle management executives.
The Ecole Centrale des Arts et Manufactures (Central College of Arts and Manufacturing), represented in 1829 the concerted effort of the private sector to disseminate technical education of a high standard.
There was another way in which a government could promote technical developments, which was by a wider dissemination of infor mation. Official missions were dispatched to England naturally, but these soon ceased. Certain governments, bowing under pressure from their academic circles, introduced state issued technical bulletins


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
which served as a useful vehicle for new ideas in technology as, for example in France, the Anna/es des Ponts et Chaussees or the Anna/es des Mines. Did this connote governmental policy or represent the initiative of civil servants with an interest in these problems?
The promotion of technical advances was also the objective of the industrial exhibitions which made their appearances with the advent of the Empire. The confrontation of products inevitably meant an interfacing of techniques. The first exhibition of the products of national industry dates back to 1798, the brȨinchild of Francois de
Neufchateau. They became a regular occurrence thereafter. It was also in this spirit, but on a much larger scale, that the world exhibitions came into existence, the first being held in London in 1851, followed by
Paris in 1855.
Cases of direct intervention became increasingly rare, especially in the more industrialised countries. In France, the state-financed sett ling of the steel-founder Jackson in the Loire region in 1816, consti tuted a unique case. In more technically backward countries state enterprises set the pace as was the case in Silesia, in particular. Direct state intervention to implant new or improved techniques can also be found in Austria.
Henceforth, all the problems were taken up by the entrepreneurs.
They were the ones who set up the schools from elementary to advanced levels, from factory schools and associations for worker training to the institutions for higher education. In this context, we recall two French examples, the foundation of an Ecole de Commerce
(Business School) in 1819 and the Ecole Centrale in 1829. Associ ations, often called industrial societies, sprung up everywhere, bring ing together businessmen for the exchange of ideas. Soon thereafter they began to publish journals or bulletins. Again we can quote examples for France, the Societe d' encouragement a l'industrie Nation ale (National Society for the Promotion of Industry), founded under the Empire, which awarded prizes for inventions, the industrial soci eties of Mulhouse and Rauen and the Societe de l'industrie Minerale
(Mining Industry Society) which, along with the Societe des Ingenieurs Civils (Society of Civil Engineers), was founded during the Second
Empire.
Thereafter industrialists crossed the Channel with a view to retur ning with ideas, designs and even workers. The state did not remain completely passive in all these activities but gave support, subsidies


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1103 and encouragement. The only difference was that the initiative now came from the other side.
In the second half of the 19th century, this state action which could hardly be designated a "technological policy," gradually waned. The slow development of technical education bore witness to this. Not until the very end of the 19th century did there materialise, in France, state professional education nor any obligation on the part of entrepreneurs to train their employees. The difficulties now being experienced in this respect are rooted in that painful awakening. From that time onward exhibitions were held but, increasingly the business community was responsible for them. State publications were buried under an avalan che of private publications. In some areas previously restricted to the state, private enterprise took over manufacture and technical research. This was the case for example, for armaments and related fields - all research on new substances was carried out in factory laboratories. If the very official department of weights and measures required a new non-expansible substance it would approach a private firm. It was the zenith of the liberal economic system - governments no longer had even the faintest policy on technology.
There were, of course, some exceptions. State intervention was observed in the form of aid, in the United States, France and Ger many, even more so in those countries where industrial development was slow, as in Poland, or late as in Russia or Japan. Generally speaking, however, any such action had a very specific target.
Japan was almost alone in appearing to have a technological policy, a coherent policy that is, on the transfer of technology. It has been pointed out that the first blast-furnace was introduced in 1858, ten years before the Meiji Revolution, by a samurai after he translated from Flemish, a Belgian treaty on iron and steel. After 1868, however, a complete policy of importation of foreign technology emerged with all the attendant machinery such as the highly developed technical programme started in 1872. In 1872 the Japanese administration availed itself of the services of 369 foreigners, culminating in a figure of
527 expatriates in 1875. A Ministry of Industry was incorporated into the Japanese government from 1870.
In reality, while the state did not ignore technolgical problems, they did not yet constitute a major concern. There were several reasons for this. First and perhaps foremost among them was the fact that the level of technological advance did not give cause for anxiety. In one way or


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1 104 another, the more industrialised nations had reached the same stage.
There were then no "technological gaps" which today are matters of grave concern. The second and equally important reason was that the cost or research was, as yet, reasonable and thus affordable for private firms.
The area under study here seems not to have been unduly affected by the First World War. Although it could be very useful, no technolo gical assessment was ever made of the 1914-1918 War. Earlier on, we noted that even in a field where techniques could have made rapid advances, progress was relatively slow. Such was the case for aviation.
Note also that we were dealing with techniques which were bordering on saturation point. World War II, however, was to change all that.
THE BEGINNINGS OF A
TECHNOLOGICAL POLICY
World War II certainly marked a turning point, vital in the conception of technical progress. World War I had highlighted the numbers of the combatants and their resilience, as well as a high level of a certain type of industrial production, from this point of view, running more along the lines of Taylor's theories on the organisation of industry than theories on the organisation of technical progress. Technical progress regarded from a global, as opposed to a fragmented approach, has only recently again become a political issue. Recalling something stated earlier, we must also note that the cost of research had become such that it was inevitable that it be shouldered by the state. The nationali sations carried out at the end of the last world war and the attempts at long term planning here and there doubtless contributed to the emergence of technological policies.
It was the juxtaposition of certain facts which changed the situation completely. We have attempted to sum up those facts as follows:
economic development became more and more linked to technical progress; the concept of national independence became increasingly a function of the acquisition of certain techniques the cost of research suddenly became exhorbitant; the existence of an "establishment" acquired greater definition (nationalisations, planning, etc.).


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1105 All of these could have led to types of state intervention which would vary according to the political and economic structure involved. Let us make no mistake, in systems which seem very liberal, state interven tion is no less manifest, as is the case in the United States.
It might be felt that the first step should have been to endow the state with the means and the infrastructure necessary for the development and implementation of a technological policy. It would seem here, however, that we were still at the very early stages. In some countries, ministries of technology or of science and technology were born, often to die soon after. In others, these matters were placed among the terms of reference of national agencies as in the United States or of general delegations such as in France, Italy or Germany. What emerged indistinctly was the fact that the organisational charts were yet to be streamlined. The old institutions still held sway in several fields, to list a few examples: military research, agronomical or medical research and scholarly research. It was difficult for them to conceive of drawing this multiplicty of responsibilities under one body - the only logical way if one would speak of technical systems and ensuring the links with fundamental research. As a result, institutions mushroomed, resulting in overlapping or shifting policies and at any rate a dangerous lack of co-ordination in the definition of a global policy as well as a waste of energy and money.
Not long ago Michel Debre, a former prime minister of General de Gaulle was arguing in favour of a Ministry of Science, the term science being taken in a general sense and thus covering all matters of techniques. "Science is a necessity of social living. To increase Man's power is to reduce his toil and give him leisure. Because of science and its offspring technology, the existence of workers could be changed and the lot of women improved." He went on to add the inevitable piece on medical research. A little further on, the author raised an important issue:
One last remark is vital. At the level reached or soon to be reached by certain scientific disciplines and not only the atomic field, job-related problems arise, some of a political and others of an ethical nature. The dialogue between experts and research personnel on the one hand and the society or nations's authorities on the other, goes beyond the traditional domain of the uses to which advances in knowledge and technology are to be put. It becomes a dialogue of great importance to mankind and addresses the conscience of Man. The leaders of a nation such as France must understand the scope of this internal dialogue, while being able take part in another dialogue with the leaders of other nations.


1 106
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
We will return to this important problem.
It is in this context that a very important question arises concerning the relationship between a nation's technical level and its indepen dence. From this dilemma stems the plethora of parallel research where a lot of money is wasted, bans on transfers of technology and all the other related conflicts which could develop.
What does a technological policy entail? First, it calls for a situation analysis of the present and necessarily, a forecast. Elements of this forecast are already there, the most important of which being existing technology which must be introduced into a given country. Of course, that is not as simple a matter as it may seem at first glance. Apart from the financial obligations, with special reference to the cost of licences, but including also the obligation to a more or less heavy outlay in investment, there is the matter of a certain technical colonialism.
Many other obstacles must be faced: coherence with existing tech niques, compatibility with the existing social order; adaptation of all parts of the infrastructure. This will be dealt with further on.
In the more developed countries, the forecast is not so much a scenario of transfers of technology as one of creation, of novelty.
Again we must make distinctions. Indeed there are lines of research which are inevitable, those we have termed development of non saturated fields of techniques. An example mentioned before is very pertinent here: the breeder-reactor for atomic plants. There are also new lines to be investigated. In the first instance, a technological policy must provide the necessary means to pursue types of research in which the direction and even the final outcome are already known, in principle.
