In the second instance, the difficulties are infinitely greater. The research which we have in this field has come out of the United States, at least in the early stages. Let us list the issues. The first is the scientific policy which must, in the long run, control the technological policy.
The United States Academy of Sciences has published an interesting work entitled Fundamental Science and National Objectives. The publication squarely sets forth the problem of the selection criteria used in science, from several methodological and institutional points of view, in response to questions put to the Academy by Congress.
A) What level of federal aid would be needed to ensure, through fundamental research, that the country maintain its position in the


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1107 vanguard of scientific and technological progress in their economic and military applications?
B) How would the Academy gauge the balance between the support already being given to the different areas of the scientific effort by the federal government and the adjustments which may be called for?
In fact, the issue could be summed up in one point: is it possible to programme scientific research, in the interest of its own development on the one hand and, on the other hand, its technical effectiveness?
To date, we have only reached the stage of studies in principle and the lay-person would smile to know that it is possible to know in advance, not so much the exact nature of the scientific discovery itself, but the area in which it will occur and its main effects. Projects dealing with the planning of scientific works, in accordance with the current methods of economic calculus, were quickly abandoned. However, this only gave way to some confusion. Naturally, research carried out during the Second World War and aimed, basically, at solving military problems was the first stage in scientific planning. Around the sixties, it became increasingly apparent that science was a key factor in the industrial race between developed countries, which prompted a second spate of important studies. There was no difference obviously, in the formulation of the question but it had greater scope since it had to cover many more sectors: what should be used as a criterion to select research with the objective of achieving technical progress and, through it, economic progress or growth? First and foremost, it must be noted that this is not an independent decision and that it depends on a prior political choice as to which technical sectors should be devel oped. In other words, as F. Perroux remarked, there is a contrast between politics for science and science for politics. "When scientific problems became political, they became national problems." In 1960, of all state funds for research and development, defence accounted for
61 % in the U.S.A. , 63.9% in Great Britain and 37.5% in France.
Defence, strictly speaking, added to atomic and space research received 89% of research funding in the U.S.A. , 78.5% in Great
Britain and 64% in France.
When the rules of economic calculus were used, in terms of concern with economic competitiveness, studies made represented the exchanges between research and production in the form of Leontiev type, double entry tables. It was quickly recognised, however, that


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
statistical data made such models impracticable. Methods had to be modified. A Research table was coupled with a Research Production table. Indeed, it was abundantly clear that scientific works leading to industrial application brought into play a complex range of disciplines, one of which in particular - mathematics - was much used by all or almost all research workers and scarcely used at the production level.
There were still many points which had to be clarified in a number of areas.
This theoretical research, which has not as yet yielded all the data it could effectively give, was the basis for certain scientific policies in the
United States as well as in the European countries. Many doubts and uncertainties persist, however. The effectiveness of British research and the growing scepticism in leading American circles indicate the limits that were being reached. We quote here from one of the latest studies appearing on the subject:
It must be noted today, in no uncertain terms, that not one country really has a scientific policy. In fact, as is amply borne out by the report on American scientific policy, what there is comprises a whole series of micropolicies implemented by a number of government bodies or ministries as dictated by their own individual needs.
Would this not also apply to purer technical research? To a large extent in countries of the capitalist economic persuasion, we have not gone beyond what Ricardo termed "trade secrets." Company labora tories carry out practical research in order to come up with inventions over which the companies hope to maintain a monopoly as long as possible. In many cases, the role of the state in these matters has been restricted on the one hand, to the development of information and on the other, to the preservation of those monopolies. That was the real purpose of the patent, to establish property rights and to serve as an instrument for dissemination. It is easy to understand that once they were dealing with fundamental research, in most sectors, it was left to the state to bear the brunt of the most pressing expenses. Where it was a question of industrial applications, firms would tend to take the initiative once again. The moot point therefore persists: if it is the vocation of th state to undertake fundamental research then the state is overstepping its role whenever it tries to programme or direct technical research.
The first fact is, undeniably, the cost of technical research. Ths cost keeps rising because an ever increasing amount of material and human


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1109 resources is required. In other words, at a certain level, only very large companies and very large states can afford such research. This mini mum required size no doubt guarantees the United States the conti nuance of some economic liberalism. Elsewhere the state is forced to substitute for firms which are too weak or groupings of states have to be formed to that end.
In his renowned work on The Stages of Economic Growth, W.W.
Rostow, listed a number of required propensities necessary for the take-off of growth. In particular he quoted the propensity to innovate.
To be more precise, we cannot speak of this propensity to innovate without mentioning the propensity to invent. A threshold could be set:
e.g. X percent of the gross national product would be earmarked for research. In that case, only the very, very rich countries would be capable of amassing the fortune without which no worthwhile research could be undertaken. In the United States, this percentage rose as high as 3% , then dropped in 1972 to 2.6% . In 1963-64 in the United States, total investment in research totalled 21 billion U.S. dollars, compared to 3.4 billion U.S. dollars for the E.E.C. and 6.2 billion U.S. dollars for the whole of Western Europe. In 1966, percentage of the gross national product were as follows:
U.S.A. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
3.4°/o England . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
2.3°/o Holland . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
2.0°/o France . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The second fact derives from the first. In the field of technology the gaps between the different world powers have grown wider and wider.
Older industrialised countries, not to mention the under-developed countries, are far behind the United States. The link between the two phenomena is obvious where technology is concerned and also as regards economic growth.
Over the period 1960-1966 it was estimated that 50% of the growth in the gross national product of the United States was attributable to technical progress. From 1945-1965, average annual increase of the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
United States' gross national product was 2.5% ; average annual increase in net sales of the five biggest advanced technology companies was 16.8% .
These facts were to shape government policy and different sets of circumstances would inevitably lead to distinct policies. However, there were some common factors. Indeed, in every single country, and notwithstanding the economic regimes in vigour, research was being funded to some extent by the state. The table below (Fig.1) shows that, in the United States, this state funding varies in accordance with the economic sector involved, but never drops to absolute zero. Never-

TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1111 theless, there are certain limits to this assistance which are not, strictly speaking, financial in nature. A slight drop in the percentage of the gross national product allocated to research was noted in the United
States. In fact the research effort seemed to have reached saturation point. The United States were stymied by a surfeit of research findings and by a realisation of the sterility of new projects since the store of basic knowledge which fuels applied research would not be renewed or would be renewed only slowly and more and more disproportionate to the practical results expected. Furthermore, research in turn had created needs, had put products on the market. It was socially and economically inconceivable to put the 1 ,250,000 consumers of artifi cially created top of the line products out of work in order to support the space programmes.
American policy tended to be flexible where others were more or less regid. The objective of technical policies was to prepare for decisions in the field of technology. Therefore, they must be coherent, on the one hand and on the other they must relate to all relevant questions which might arise.
I - The human problems must take priority. The number and quality of research personnel are vital factors in research. This is an indisputa ble fact. The number need not be an absolute, although it may be a high one in the field of research. It is preferable to relate the figures provided to the total population. The figures for 1962 were as follows:
Number of researchers per 10,000 inhabitants France . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5.8
Germany. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 6. 7
Belgium . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 7 . 1
Netherlands . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 9.7
U.S.S.R. .. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .. . . . . . . . . .
16.2 Great Britain . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
20.4 U.S.A. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
23.3 The second factor is the quality of research personnel. Training of research personnel is of prime importance, which raises the issue of technical education. In some places like the U.S.A. private foun dations undertake to provide such education and in other places, the state does it. A technical education policy's objectives would be organisation, financing and programmes. Naturally, the means to the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
end vary from country to country, more as a result of different methods and attitudes than the existence of differing needs. In one country emphasis has been placed on colleges - selective, elitist, rather more scientific than technical, slightly sclerotic. In another, preference has been given to the university - more open but less involved with concrete issues. Some vocations emerge during primary or secondary schooling - often too abstract - and then there is the training that puts too much emphasis on theory. It is significant that in so many countries research on this subject has not gone far enough to provide the decision-makers with the relevant facts needed to make those deci sions.
II - The financial aspect covers all the material problems, in fact.
Moreover, it forces choices to be made since it is impossible to do everything at one time. These choices occur at different levels.
The first and undoubtedly the most delicate is the choice between fundamental science, applied science and technical research (including what is referred to as development).
The second is the choice of the sectors into which the research effort will be channelled.
The third is the nature of funds to be used (private or public) and, at the same time, the choice of the beneficiaries of the research findings.
The fourth is the organisation of the data and the means of trans mitting same.
III. - The institutional aspect, to some extent, links the first two. It comprises the implementation of a predetermined technological policy which would affect, not only the institutions directly involved with technology (e.g. laboratories, patents, etc.) but all institutions, includ ing the legislature in so far as it must enable these to adapt to any technological changes.
A technological policy could never be an end in itself. It must respond to social, economic, material and political imperatives in a broad sense. It can be neither isolated, which is inconceivable in any case, nor should it be aimed exclusively in any one direction. Balance must be kept between all the possible end products of technical progress either through the interplay of specific forces or as a result or more general planning. There is no doubt, however, that some of these imperatives must take priority over others. We have noted in particu lar that, at least from certain periods, military demands brought a great deal of pressure to bear on technical research, especially at the time of


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1113 the Second World War. A dearth of raw materials can also lead to important inventions, from coke for blast furnaces to synthetic rubber and all the various product substitutes. Any policy on technology is inevitably subject to these imperatives is not a freely created construc tion.
We have come to the crux of the problem. There are huge gaps in what may be termed "national" technologies. On the other hand, theoretical studies aimed at instituting a scientific or technological policy are inadequate with regard to deciding upon effective and beneficial action. Strictly speaking, we are still within the confines of an empiricism which, while not generalised perhaps, is certainly very widespread. Once again we must reiterate that there exists no uni versal solution. In this field, the dimensions of states offer a choice of varying solutions, of liberal capitalism or collectivism. At a lower level, we must determine how freely choices can be made. The variety of reactions and the types of reaction from country to country must be emphasised. In the United States, universities often work in associ ation with private industry or even with the military authorities. A look at what pertains in France would reveal the strained relations existing between universities and production management, the preoccupation with pure science, the fear that could be generated by a search for military applications of scientific research. In the final analysis, the elements of the problem are ill-defined for the most part. This has been noted:
The growing complexity of the science system, of its interdependence with society and the institutions which complement and assist research work, complicates the task of the central scientific policy bodies responsible for the co-ordination of these various activities, regardless of the size of the country or its political system.
The way is never clear for a technological policy. In the end, circumstances dictate. There are policies for rich and dominating countries, there are policies for long industrialised countries of a size unable to support extensive research and then there are those so-called under-developed countries which we can describe as technically dependent.
American policy on technical research is based on liberal principles.
Indeed, bar a few sectors, it would seem that the state in no way tries to substitute private initiative, whether originating from business or from the universities. Quite to the contrary, a not insubstantial share of state


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
grants is allocated to the private sector. For the year 1966, the state provided 60% of research grants, industry 33% and universities 4% , while industry utilised 69% of total grants and universities 13% .
In this respect, the most striking example is that of N.A.S.A. This state agency organised, in fact, a massive diffusion of its technology which, as we know, affects a large number of sectors. From inception to the end of 1972, expenditure in this area rose to 35,000,000 U.S.
dollars. Not only was research undertaken to determine the practical applications of certain discoveries, but also six information centres were created in the United States in order to publicise these discover ies and their applications. Close to 2300 patents are thus available for production under licence. No fees are payale to N.A.S.A. but they do choose their licencees and, as a rule, never grant exclusive rights. This definitely denotes a concern for efficiency. This technical treasure is available to the rest of the world but under much stricter terms and conditions.
It was quickly noted, as we emphasised earlier on, that the program ming of research was difficult and even impossible at times. Action taken was necessarily very wide in scope and therefore, rather vague.
Between 1956 and 1967 the share allotted to fundamental research out of total research grants rose from 6.3% to 12.6% , applied research grants went from 19 .8% to 20. 7% , grants for development- conceived of as the field of invention and industrial application - fell from 65.4% to 62.4% and equipment grants fell from 8.5% to 4.3% . Later on it would seem that this division was revised. There was some saturation in the field of fundamental research and the trend was reversed, which brings us back to where we started, demonstrating that the program ming of research is almost impossible. Would it not have been better, therefore, to start with a complete analysis of available technology, to determine which fields were saturated and which were not, then, on that basis, to look for avenues for development and to entrust science with the task of filling the technical gaps?
Circumstances had long been different from one industrialised country to another. It is an undeniable fact that substantial technical progress was achieved due to World War II and that such progress occurred chiefly in the U.S.A. However, that country's allies (and here we refer specifically to Great Britain) were kept abreast, practically on a day to day basis, of the novelties being created across the Atlantic and some of those allies even contributed to a certain extent. In


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1115 addition, the practical nature of the British explains the peculiar head-start which this country had vis a vis the others. What was meant to happen, happened - once working together had become a habit, when the war was over, for obvious reasons British technicians had but one wish which was to get into the better equipped, better financed
American laboratories. This is the only logical explanation for the "brain drain" which undoubtedly played a part in America's post-war success.
An interview in July 1973 of the French government minister under whose portfolio these matters fall reflects almost perfectly the diffi culties that have to be faced. "The development of a research policy is a lengthy task." It became painfully obvious that there was no objective yardstick against which needs could be measured. It was announced that work had been concentrated on three main areas, called priority projects: the planned development of an autonmous
French computer industry, the uranium enrichment plant, the L3S rocket. This in itself confirms the veracity of the statements made by F.
Perroux. Indeed, uranium is the raw material for the atom bomb, the launcher is as vital as the computers for the calculation of trajectories.
There is no doubt that there did exist the will for independence since both launcher and computer are vital to the transmitting mechanisms of tomorrow. However, another fact is clearly shown: there are two ways to finance research, widespread allocations or a concentrated injection into large scale objectives. While the former is certainly rather ineffective, the latter forces draconian choices which are inevitably subject to certain fixed perspectives.
Structural problems always follow in the wake of technical progress and occur at different levels. The consequences of these problems are such that the government can ignore them only at the risk of total chaos. Not to ignore them entails a positive devising of solutions or taking of decisions.
The evolution of techniques has taken place in such a way that only the ever larger firms may reap its benefits. In a liberal economic system, it can be assumed that, at worst, only one firm will be able to acquire and exploit the most advanced techniques. Such monopoly situations already exist in fact, in certain sectors with all the inherent market effects. There are serious instances where the market is inevitably torn between two possible directions. This can occur at the international level and then the actors are the multinational companies


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
partially or totally independent of national governments. In the same way that some countries have thought of joining together to bridge the technical gap that separates them from the large nations, companies have tried to band together in order to reach the level of the large multinational corporations which control advanced technology.
Here are some concrete examples: the American company IBM is in the vanguard of the computer companies, with a good lead. In the same way, as we have seen, there are two nuclear lines which are at present economically viable. These two lines are controlled by two other American companies - Westinghouse and General Electric. We have said that where computers are concerned two large national companies were created with state financial assistance in Britain and in
France. This is an instance of government intervention in the solution of a structural problem. French and British policies differed, however, with respect to the construction of nuclear plants. Great Britain induced the merging of all potential manufacturers while French incited two rival groups to form, each of which, incidentally, was holding a licence from one of the two American companies. This division has since been reversed and the Creusot-Loire company now has a virtual monopoly. The decision was based on whether or not an export market existed. If it was estimated that the two rival groups were too much for the French domestic market, there was still the possibility of export. Great Britain chose not to incur this risk, deciding that one company could supply both local and export demand. Similarly, it was determined that two large rolling mills at full capacity would be sufficient to supply France with sheet metal and the whole of the heavy iron and steel industry was consolidated around those two mills. In this instance as with the big chemical industry or mechanical construction, these mergers creating large consolidated companies were promoted by the government on the grounds that technical progress demanded them. This route was not taken by the governments without some measure of trepidation, however. Any democracy by its very nature has a healthy fear of economic feudality, more so of monopolies. Where the latter was concerned, the only solution was to opt for a state monopoly (e.g. railways, energy, etc.).
Geographic distortions are important, perhaps more acutely so because they cannot be changed. They will be dealt with more fully in another chapter. They raise certain problems which are unquestiona bly political. We saw them at the international level and they also exist


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1117 at the national level. Certain techniques, the combined use of certain raw materials impose a given location on one part of the economic acitivity. This could cause the appearance of what the British called depressed zones. This fact does not apply to industrial activity. Simi larly, developments in agricultural techniques have changed exten sively the geography of production. Ricardo understood it quite clearly. This also means that the phenomenon to which we refer is not a recent one. Ever since the end of the 17th century and, in particular since the 18th century, it has been evident especially in the most economically advanced countries.
Two possible stances may be taken to deal with this phenomenon.
The process may be regarded as inevitable and efforts to stem it useless. Based on this stance, we can project a single scenario as an example: that of France in the year 2000, where we would find a French administration which has not built its political system with this sole task in mind. Though the process is an old one, it seems to have gained considerable momentum. Not one country it seems, among the rich, generally long-developed countries involved, has resigned itself to this concentration of all activities in a few privileged zones.
The means at hand to redress the situation undoubtedly would determine what policies will apply. Of course, there are technical means or in more accurate terms, the appearance of certain techniques have made deconcentation possible as for instance means of transport
- from the railway in the 19th century through electricity and pipelines to the gasoducts. Ore enrichment plants enable long-distance trans port of same as is the case for the liquefaction of natural gases.
Furthermore, insofar as technical means allow, voluntary or planned action may be undertaken to combat excessive concentration. The follow-up of one acitivity by another, the need for rapid transmission, long-standing concentrations which seem immutable today, all create frequent obstacles in the path of those efforts. It is felt most strongly in countries where centralisation is of long standing, being political in origin. The cost of rapid decentralisation would be so high as to make it impossible. To a certain extent, France and Britain have found themselves in this position. Decentralised countries such as Germany,
Italy and even the United States are in a much more favourable position in this respect. The large and even the largest American universities are located neither in the Federal capital nor even in the largest cities.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
What is today termed "national development policy" is much needed and very important. It will never be omnipotent because there are certain situations which it cannot control. Still, it is difficult to foretell whether steps taken will be stabilising actions - since retracig steps is more difficult - or whether they will be dilatory actions. No doubt we are still lacking some of the necessary methods of analysis to do so. Only the future can tell but it was necessary to stress the importance of the problem.
TECHNICAL CO-OPERATION POLICIES
Having seen how complicated it was to formulate a technological policy at national level, imagining the difficulties involved at the international level should be easy. In fact, the problem is a recent one.
The methods once used by Colbert or Peter the Great to equip their countries with the most advanced technology of their era can no longer work for us. The complex nature of technology, the cost of research and the wide technical gaps now represent so many obstacles to a worldwide penetration of modem technology.
There were only two possible solutions. First, countries of relatively limited means could join forces in the field of technical research to approximate the might of the very large nations. Western Europe chose this solution. The alternative was transfer of technology which would raise very different problems according to whether the recipient was an industrialised or a developing country. The former already possess an infinitely more sophisticated structural framework to receive new techniques.
When the Common Market was formed it was reasonable to assume that the joining of forces would extend into technical research. It was convenient for the privileged nations of Wes tern Europe to get together with a view of gaining access to vital human and financial resources. Surprisingly, the technical problem called for political solutions to some extent, or at least, so it was believed by some.
Euratom - the first large scale project of the European Community - found itself as someone wrote, "at the focal point of the two great revolutions of the second half of the 20th century viz the scientific


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1119 revolution, harbinger of the nuclear age and the political revolution destined to end in the creation of the United States of Europe."
Equally curious is the fact that none of the succession of treaties establishing the E.E.C. referred to the problem of technical research.
Events, situations and politics slowly brought Euratom to an end after a promising start. The heavy and growing energy deficit forced
Europe to move faster and to develop high yield reactors but each country had its own techniques. The British were attached to their own lines, the French preferred their own before switching to American technology. When it reached the point of considering a uranium enrichment facility, the split was no longer politically or economically based but rather technical on the one hand the English, Dutch and
Germans with their centrifugal process, and the French, Belgians, Italians with the Swedes and Spaniards too (not yet members of the
Community) on the other hand with their gas diffusion process. The technical choice overcame the political aspirations.
When it came to viewing the issues from a general perspective dissensions became even more acute. In 1966, the Italian government announced its grave uncertainty with respect to the gap that might put
Europe into a position of scientific, technological and economic dependence vis a vis the United States. The Italians therefore submit ted a project of principle to the different European authorities. The basis was an agreement on science and technology, a veritable ten-year plan which would enable Europe to catch up. It specified priority sectors (computers, space, satellites, aeronautics, the nuclear sector and desalination). It described a flexible method of international co-operation in conjunction with a large-scale diffusion of knowledge and information. It was all integrated into a European administrative and financial framework promoting mobility among experts and technicians. In fact, it was its link with a political cum military organisation - N.A.T.O. - which caused the project to fail.
Again, in 1966, the British proposed a European technical commu nity aimed at pooling the scientific and technological potential of
European states and at developing and implementing a common policy in this area. This project was dropped in the face of French opposition in particular. In 1967 the British tried again with the less ambitious suggestion of the creation of a European Technological Institute but met with failure again. A proposal along the same lines by the Benelux countries in 1968 met with similar fate.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Positions held are therefore clear cut. Some, including in particular the French government of the time obviously feared that technical and scientific integration sooner or later might lead, more inevitably even than economic integration, to a shrinking of national independence insofar as technical progress interlocks with military might and with economic growth. Moreover, it goes hand in hand with a certain spirit of domination which can be seen in the field of space, nuclear development, and computing. While such a policy may be partly justified by the existence of several possible lines of technical devel opment in a large number of sectors, there is an underlying notion that technology, which controls almost everything else, should remain far removed from European integration.
In spite of this, at the 1972 European Summit, it was decided that the European Commission would prepare a development plan for science and technology. The plan was submitted to the Council of Ministers of the Community in January 1974. It called for the formation of a committee on science and technology to examine national program mes and budgets, available research potential and objectives of the member states and to identify common objectives. The committee would report to the Council of Ministers and to the Commission to enable them to co-ordinate the scientific policies. In addition it was suggested that a European science foundation be set up with a view of promoting fundamental research. Classified industrial or military research would not fall under the committee's jurisdiction. That is as far as we have progressed to date.
That was the start of an "a la carte" Europe, as Louis Armand put it, with each project bringing together a different set of partners. Such was the case for the uranium enrichment plant project and for the
European nuclear research centre (CERN). In the wake of the war, European physicists were trying to catch up with their American colleagues. This task called for considerable means. International co-operation also seemed even more desirable in that it fit in with a desire for European unification. Nuclear technology provided the opportunity for the encounter. In the words of L. Kowarski, one of the founders of CERN:
The area of joint action must be clearly defined so as not to encroach upon the forbidden ground of nuclear fission (for military reasons) but it must be sufficiently close so that any success achieved through international efforts in the authorised fields could have some positive repercussion within the reserves.


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1121 The meson or high energy physics seemed a perfectly acceptable and valid area of research. The whole affair was run at an individual rather than at a state level. Eleven European states created CERN in 1952 and set it up near to Geneva on neutral ground, so to speak. Thanks to that body, the European states had a cyclotron at their disposal as early as 1959, before the United States. In the final analysis, however,
CERN not hope to escape the crisis which occurred in 1967 for both financial and nationalist reasons.
Nevertheless, CERN managed to overcome some its difficulties by restricting itself to very specific fields of nuclear research.
The same has applied in the field of space from the time of the creation of EUROSPACE by 300 industrial firms until 1961 when the international agreement was signed, allowing for the establishment of
E.L.D.O. in 1962 for the manufacture of missile launchers. Satellites came under E.S.R.O. , also set up in 1962. This was a European organisation, not connected to the Community since Spain, Sweden,
Switzerland and Austria participated. E.L.D.O. quickly succumbed to multiple acute crises. In early 1974, one year after its demise, it was described as follows: "A non-existant industrial organisation, based on a sketchy nationalism but more so on the rejection of management or even control by the Community of the regular and substantial credit overruns and of the repeated technical failures which culmi nated in a spectacular in-flight explosion of the rocket Europa-2 in
1971 ." E.S.R.O. , a better conceived, better managed organisation obtained results which were by no means negligible. Through it countries which had no indigenous space programme have been able to reach a certain technical level in the area of space equipment. Within that organisation decisions are taken by an absolute or two-thirds majority vote, industrial contracts are awarded after tender on the basis of the rule of "just returns" whereby each member is ensured a volume of contracts proportionate to its contribution. A technical control centre was set up in the Netherlands. From 1964 to 1972
E.S.R.O. built scientific satellites exclusively. Six were produced during that period. While at first diversification was shied away from, it later prevailed as a result of pressure from France and the controlling body. A move was made therefore, to applied use satellites: a meteorological satellite was to be launched at the end of 1976 and an experimental communications satellite at the end of 1977.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Rocket production once more seemed worthy of consideration and France and Germany agitated in favour of it, though for quite different reasons. In July 1973, France succeeded in enlisting its partners in the construction of the Ariane rocket. E.S.R.O. therefore officially became the European Space Agency in April 1974 despite a few personnel problems. It brought together ten countries, namely
Belgium, Denmark, Spain, France, Italy, the Netherlands, the Fed eral Republic of Germany, United Kingdom, Sweden and Switzer land. It inherited a heavy programme but, in order to avoid unproduc tive investment, production was rationalised and co-ordinated, each member having to keep the Agency informed on individual plans and to adapt them if necessary.
The difficulties so often experienced in setting up these organi sations caused the states to opt for bilateral co-operation on many occasions: Anglo-French on the Concorde project, German-French for Airbus. That is not to say that this type of co-operation has been exempt from conflict.
Political decisions have stymied technical co-operation on numerous occasions. The computer field is a good example of this. Almost everyone of the countries set up its own international company in the hope that the other countries would associate with it: International
Computers Ltd. (I. C. L.) in England, Compagnie Internationale d'Informatique (C.1.1.) in France. In both cases the states were large stockholders and also financed the studies and determined the direc tion of the research. There was a European attempt: Unidata, founded by the French company C.1.1. , the Dutch company Phillips and the
German company Siemens. The alliance fell apart in May 1975 and C.1.1. was absorbed by the American firm, Honeywell. The impression given was that everyone was willing to work together on condition that partners could vary from one sector to another. This was meant to ensure some sort of guarantee of freedom and independence.
Another more pressing problem is the gap that exists not between rich and super-rich countries, but between those countries and an under-developed Third World. What is referred to as "transfer of technology" has been studied at length. It needs to be studied in much greater depth, however. It is based on the assumption that there is harmony between the policies of the donors and the recipient countries.
According to the estimates of one international body, for the financial year ending 1973, under-developed countries spent approxi-


TECHNIQUES AND POLmcs 1123 mately 1.5 billion U.S. dollars annually in the acquisition of patents and licences along with the necessary technical back-up services. That expenditure was expected to soar to 9 billion U.S. dollars by 1980 if the
8% growth objective for the second United Nations Development Decade was reached. Another point to be considered is that the contracts covering those acquisitions often entail multiple obligations such as the compulsory purchase of certain products, export restric tions and clauses restricting competition on the domestic market. The international conference (U.N.C.T.A.D.) had no qualms in stating that developing countries are "subject to a type of colonialism which keeps them almost totally dependent on the developed countries" and that, from the political point of view, they remain "incapable of controlling and directing their own production processes." It is a fact that in some of the richer countries, the transfer of technology belongs to firms which own techniques which are in demand, with some exceptions, notably of a military nature. For them the problem is purely commercial. Obviously the sale of a licence eliminates a relatively large number of outlets. The fees represent only a partial compensation for this loss of potential revenue. This fact often prompts an attempt to boost income with related profits such as from the sale of technical assistance and of integrated products. At the same time efforts are made to limit the repercussions by means of bans on exports and restrictions on domestic competition. In a liberal economy it is difficult for the state to modify such a situation.
A situation even arose in the United States where trade unions fought against transfers of technology on the grounds that eventually, a drop in sales as a result of the start of production in other countries would mean the elimination of jobs. The dilemma has existed unchanged since the 19th century: to sell products or to sell factories.
The statements of one scientific policy-maker are characteristic of how far this idea can lead. In his words, "It is not in the interest of the developed countries to promote research and development in less industrialised countries because that would result in less revenue from licence fees as well as from the sale of technical expertise." There was even justification for the notion that "foreign companies have created almost monopolistic conditions preventing the progress of research and development in those fields which they control."
However, this is not the policy of all technology supplying countries.
There are other instances, other types of intervention though not all


1 124
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
completely unselfish. These fell under the general heading of technical co-operation which almost of necessity operates in areas where the question of patents does not arise such as technical education, econo mic infrastructure (public works in the broad sense), agriculture, medicine etc. Here also, the orientation given and the financing offered are often tied to material sold by the consultant country which is how the term "tied aid" originated and why it is so frequently used.
The influx, the transfer of technology is almost always a determining factor in commercial operations though the degree of impact varies. It is also acknowledged that this constitutes another form of colonialism from several angles. For instance the construction of new roads leads to the purchase of trucks and, for a long time afterward, the purchase of spare parts. Other examples could be put forward in this respect.
Technical co-operation, therefore, seemed to many to be a type of trade policy.
From the point of view of the recipient countries, problems abound and political options always determine the solutions. We find ourselves today faced with a situation which differs completely from that which prevailed in the latter half of the 19th century: the technical gap has become such and the financial requirements have reached a stage where new solutions are called for. In the 19th century, there existed embryonic acceptance structures, mostly capitalist in nature which could be developed without too much difficulty. Today there is a real issue as to whether these acceptance structures would be capitalist or socialist in nature. The various media report on this topic almost every day. There is also the question of sociological and thus institutional compatibility with traditional structures or the possible modification of those same traditional structures. It has been noted that the price of industrialisation, in terms of human suffering, has often been higher than it was believed to be in early 19th century England as well as in long colonised countries. Everything is linked to industrialisation, from the status of the workers - a social type who cannot exist in some societies - to that of women which is harder to change insofar as it represents a status of dependence.
The 18th century had witnessed in France as in Spain, very strong reservations with respect to the introduction of the spinning-wheel.
The same applies to the installation of a pump in a fellaheen village as we have already emphasised in the chapter "Technical Progress and
Society." The transition to a money-based economy and the introduc-


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1 125 tion of new tools, even in techniques as widespread as in agriculture, raise problems which are one hundred percent political.
Historical research yields no solution here but does help to better grasp the problems and their general implications. In the past and, as we have pointed out, even today to some extent, customs protec tionism was a commonly used tactic to promote transfers of techno logy. Europe had launched it in 1820-1825 in order to facilitate the adoption of the British techniques. Italy followed suit after 1880, as did
Russia. The fact is that industrialisation had to be achieved through technological upheaval. The main outcome was that the developed countries stopped exporting products in favour of factories and of new techniques. The difficult problem remained of sourcing management personnel and a labour force with the necessary skills. The export of management personnel often went hand in hand with the export of technology. For example, the European countries imported English labour between 1815 and 1848 until indigenous labour could take over.
Japan followed suit from 1868.
Conditions have changed since then but the problem remains as acute as it was in the past. From a purely technical point of view, a country cannot import techniques on its own since they are sold as part of an increasingly complex package. It is not simply a matter of deciding to import a particular technique - the country must be able to use it or in other words, it must have at hand the competent human element. Natural conditions must be favourable - although geographic factors do tend to become attenuated with time - and it is necessary that the techniques be compatible both with all the elements of a coherent technical package, attractive to investors and that their products be marketable.
The first reaction occurs in response to the source of the techniques or rather the vendor. As we mentioned earlier, there is an immediate problem of domination or of rejection of domination. One of several positions may be taken. First, there could be a rejection of practices deemed over-sophisticated and ill-adapted to both the means and the needs of the recipient. History provides us with examples such as the
Kingdom of the Two Scicilies which long rejected the railway on the grounds that it would provoke social unrest. The policy of the post1959 Chinese Revolution was motivated on the one hand by the fear of heavy dependence on things foreign and on the other by a search for original technical shortcuts to a solution of what were considered


1 126
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
unique problems. That policy was based on existing expertise in some sectors at least, fed by recent input from Soviet technicians and revitalised a very old tradition of practical ingeniousness. Mao Tse
Tung's words on the subject were very explicit:
There are two ways of learning: the dogmatic way consists of borrowing everything, whether or not it applies to conditions in our country. This is not the right way. The other way consists of using our brains and learning only what would be relevant to the conditions of our country, that is assmilating experience which can be made useful to us.
When we study what is positive abroad, we must not copy it blindly but use it to create and rely on our own strengths.
Naturally, this stance is incomplete. It is true that certain techniques impose conditions for their use, in which case there can be no question of adapting the foreign techniques to local conditions. It is these local conditions which must be modified in order to make it possible to integrate a new technique. In other words, there are conditional techniques and the more sophisticated the technique, the greater the constraints. Furthermore, the Chinese cultural revolution experienced failure in many technical areas.
Recipient countries have tried to defend themselves against this technical domination, all the more dangerous in its subtlety. A good example of self-defence is Mexico. A law was promulgated to regulate the transfer of technology. A national register of transfers of techno logy was opened where all transfer contracts had to be recorded.
Registration would be refused if the price was considered dispropor tionate to the acquired technique if the transfer entailed direct or indirect interference in the management of the buyer's business, if restrictions were placed on further research or on technical improvements or if the terms of transfer included an obligation by the buyer to acquire necessary material from a stipulated source. Mexico remains an isolated case of comprehensive legislation in this respect.
In mid-1973, Brazil chose a different course. The Minister of Planning stated: "The best way to keep a country under-developed is to feed it ready-made technology." Large sums were released to obviate this situation. Brazil's intention was to rationalise the use of its resources both human and natural, from that point onward. It wanted to give its industrial sector the means of making educated choices with regard to foreign techniques adapting it to Brazilian market condi tions. Brazil's way was not to import brains nor to force the multi nationals to set up research laboratories locally but rather to furnish


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1 127 Brazilian industry with the necessary technical background to enable it to absorb foreign manufacturing processes and be able to develop a few of its own. Stress was to be placed on the development of new techniques in the nuclear, space and oceanographic fields as well as on the expansion of technology-intensive fields such as electronics, phar maceuticals and aeronautics. Lastly, fundamental research was to receive a decisive boost in the form of an allocation of one-fifth of the plan's budget.
In keeping with their mission, the international bodies attempted a definition of the terms and conditions governing transfer of techno logy. It emerged that a transfer, intended to alleviate basic deficien cies, could be beneficial up to a point, but past that point could become a hindrance and even a danger. In a report on Innovation and the structure of the Canadian Economy, the deputy secretary of the
Canadian State Minister for Science and Technology remarked that, "contrary to popular belief, the practice of importing packaged tech niques characteristic of subsidiaries of the multinational companies can weaken the technological infrastructure of a country instead of reinforcing it." It was concluded also that there is need for decisions at state level which would mean the existence of appropriate agencies.
One international commission thus invited "the developing countries to consider urgently the creation of national governmental institutions
( . . . ) to handle transfers of techniques on an integrated basis." We come to the procedures which operate at national level, or at least in many nations. When there is a problem requiring a solution, the first step is to create an institution based on analyses often of debatable value. France presents a perfect example: as soon as a problem arises, a general representative is appointed who handles a series of lateral services notwithstanding the vertical organisation of the administration. As a result we have had a Commissioner for
Scientific and Technical Research, super-imposed upon the National scientific research centre (CERN) and all the research divisions of the various ministries. There was also a Commissioner representative for
Road Security, one for Energy and lastly, the Commisioner for Data Processing. The first and last mentioned had important technical roles to play. In fact, surely this was all in response to a logical need for cross-hatching of decisions. No time was wasted in trying to apply this policy at international level. Thus institutions were set up through which would transit new ideas in techniques. In this manner the United


1 128
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
Nations Conference on Trade and Development created an intergovernmental group on transfer of technology. Its aim was to foster co-operation by allowing the under-developed countries access to any sources of technical information which they might need. This would allow for exchange programmes to be set up between national institutions. We will mention the Agence Nationale pour la Valori sation de la Recherche or ANVAR (National Agency for Research
Presentation and Implementation) in France which operates on both sides of the borders, the Mexican National Council for Science and
Technology which organised an industrial information network to determine the needs of the Mexican economy for new technical knowledge and to spot national and/or foreign innovations with potential for fulfilling those needs.
Any coherent policy on transfer of technology must take into account a number of factors many of which are non-technical: finan cing, technical education and general academic levels, institutional and economic acceptance structures, development of social systems, correction of possible distortions etc. No given technique may be arbitrarily introduced - preparations must be made to arrange for its acceptance.
Opposition and misunderstandings punctuate the conferences which are regularly held. Rich countries do not easily surrender their technical arsenal. The under-developed countries want to have it all, all at once. This was the cry of the Group of 77 (which numbers more in fact) in the early months of 1975, going to the point of demanding that a stop be put to research on possible substitutes for the raw materials produced in the Third World countries. Some compromises must be arrived at after lengthy study of and patient research on the effects of transfer of technology in particular.
THE IDEOLOGICAL PROBLEM
For most nations the problems seem difficult and for many insurmount able. Solutions are few because no-one has studied the problem in a systematic manner. The question at issue is only mentioned with reluctance, it seems. It is on this subject that we will conclude.


lECHNIQUES AND POLITICS 1129 Management of existing techniques in all fields, policy making and the policy decisions invovled seem to demand extensive knowledge. It is no longer possible to know a little of everything. Today's world calls for much knowledge in a specific field. This leads to a contradictory situation where the politician knows practically nothing of techniques while the technician is closeted in his field. Decision making is thus rendered difficult in a world at once highly systemised and highly compartmentalised. Lloyd George defined the parliamentary system as a group of experts run by amateurs. In the eyes of A. Siegfried, politics was becoming more and more technical. He saw a contradic tion: the powerfulness of the state and the fragility of its power. We recall here the famous words of Saint-Simon:
Let us assume that France suddently lost its 50 top physicists, 50 top physiologists, 50 top chemists, 50 top bankers etc. , the country would instantly become a body without a soul
( . . . ) Let us concede that France kept all its great minds in the sciences, the fine arts and in the industrial arts and applied sciences but that it suffered the loss of His Grace the Duke of Angouleme ( . . . ) along with all the great officers of the Crown, all State Ministers with or without portfolio, all State Advisers etc. , this would certainly be tragic in the eyes of the French for they are kind souls. However the loss of these 30,000 individuals, counted among e most important of the nations's statesmen, would cause only emotional trauma since the State would suffer no political damage.
From an idealised concept of power we have moved to a concept which is diametrically opposed. From Saint-Simon to Burnham through to Marx and Comte and more recently Djilas or Galbraith, all foretell the abdication of political power into the hands of the techni cians. During the first half of the 20th century, the conflict between politics and technique ceased being mere concept to become a reality.
We are no longer dealing with Saint-Simon's simple opposition bet ween the industrious bees and the drone politicians.
Taylor in America and Fayol in France doubtless were the sources from which sprung the bases for a rational organisation independent of political power. In France, the rationalists and the synarchistes undertook the propagation of these ideas supported by American philosophers such as Veblen, Berle and Means, and Howard Scott.
For Burnham, the transition from feudalism to capitalism was marked by the localisation of sovereignty more and more completely in the "Parliaments." This sovereignty began to slip away from the
Parliaments from the time of World War I. It inched toward what Burnham calls the "managerial society." In this managerial society,


1 130
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
sovereignty is located within the administrative offices. The pattern would be same at all levels. Within the business enterprises, power would slip from their parliaments - boards of directors and general stockholders meetings - into the hands of technical management.
Burnham believed that one of the possible avenues for this trend was dictatorship.
In a symposium held in Nice in 1956 on "Politics and Technology,'' the analyses made by the jurists, while not quite so positive came close to all these ideas. Let us list at random the retreat of the law before the onslaught of regulations, the application and broadening of the tradi tional technical role of the state. The same applies to the institutions with the creation of economic councils, the growing role played by well-equipped and well-manned specialised commissions at par liamentary level, the appearance of the technical ministries and the proliferation of para-state agencies (such as the Gosplan in the
U.S.S.R. which controls all major economic decisions). In several instances Parliaments have lost almost all decision-making powers in a number of areas such as planning and the atomic field. The discount rate is fixed by the large central banks. To put it briefly, there has been a parallel weakening of the state's power and reinforcement of the authority of the specialists. The same process was identified in the tionalised companies by M. Chenot. In the final analysis the only possible problem would be if there were major rifts within the ranks of the technicians themselves.
One of the most recent authors on this trend would certainly be J. K.
Galbraith (1967) in whose words: "it is the imperatives of technology and organisation and not the dictates of ideology which determine the shape of the economic society." Parallel to this process was th formation of what Galbraith termed a "technostructure" which was to be more comprehensively technical than Burnham's managerial society and would approximate the Power elite of C. Wright Mills. For him, the veritable brain of the business was the group of persons who supplied specialised knowledge to the decision-makers. "There is no name for the group of those who participate in the decision-making process of the group, nor for the organisation which they constitute. I propose to call that organisation the technostructure." In this way the transition is deemed to have been made from the traditional power base to the power of capital and then to the power of the technician who would be impervious to external influences. This power substi-


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1131 tution from all the sundry previous power sources, the lawyer, big money, the politician, to the technostructure is increasingly effective.
The very nature, the very concept of the individual changed. Raymond Aron spoke of a "techno-bureaucratic structure."
This theme was picked up in literature from Aldous Huxley's Brave new World to G. Orwell's terrifying 1984, where we are presented with a view of what it would be like to live in a society whose powers had at their disposal all the resources of technology.
The rise of this new and present power in France has been studied.
The synarchy of Jean Coutrot, founder of the group X-Crise in 1930, has been noted. Recruitment for his group was among the large engineering colleges and it became a source for many of the ideas of the Popular Front. In many ways the Vichy regime constituted a state technocracy with multiple centres of economic decision-making (pri ces, economic control, equipment). There were even certain
Resistance movements which contributed to giving to the first post liberation governments full directorial power, nationalist structures and planning. A specific example was the Organisation Civile et
Militaire (O.C.M.) inspired by Keynes and the New Deal. While the movement may have lost some momentum in the late fifties, it gained even greater ground between 1960 and 1970 with the classified fields being strictly controlled by the experts and with the osmosis which operated between the political power and the administration. A powerful illustration of this is the shift of French senior civil servants out of administration and into the arena of political power. It has reached a point where the politicians are losing important ministerial posts to the technicians.
The situation is identical at the international level. An internatinal technocracy has developed which peoples all those innumerable inter national bodies entrusted with the background work invovled in decision-making, some of which have even been delegated decision making powers.
A good illustration of the above can be found in the May 1975 parliamentary discussions on nuclear energy where we witnessed a non-sanctioned debate, authoritative decisions taken by technicians, insufficient precise information, insufficient knowledge to a point where good and evil became indistinguishable no longer being clearly definable concepts but rather nebulous and multi-faceted. Fur thermore, the experts never even reached concensus.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
The issue of the relationship between politics and techniques is basic and vital. In order to attempt to resolve it we must study the slow process of decision making, the solutions rejected and the reasons for rejection. In fact we are forced to judge on options alone, some fortuitous, others disappointing for at the highest level, the final decision is always a political one and cannot be a compromise between groups of experts who do not always speak the same language.
In his three articles published in Le Monde in November, 1974, Nicholas Vichney tried to take apart the mechanism of the large advanced technical projects embarked upon by France, projects which he has nick-named "the new cathedrals." There is much to be learnt from the conditions under which these operations - which others term technical programmes - were launched and carried out.
In order that a scientific or technological project of any sort take on the appearance of a grand project, it must earn public acclaim at a time when science and technology hold a place of honour in the public eye. It so happens that during the SO's and 60's science and technology could do no wrong with such successes to their credit as television, the jet engine, the transistor, the genetic code . . .
Chronologically speaking all the big technical programmes were launched by the Fourth Republic, i.e. the plans for the tidal power station on the river Rance were finalised in 1951 based on studies started in 1920; the huge solar furnace in the Pyrenees at Odeillo, was approved in 1952 and inaugurated in 1955; work began on the graphite-gasline in 1952, the SECAM colour television process was patented in 1956, the decision to build the vessel "France" was taken in
1956, the idea of a commercial supersonic plane was put forward in 1957 as was the idea of the breeder-reactor. It was in that same year that the plans for the Pierrelatte nuclear plant were completed. Only the hovertrain dates back to 1962.
Generally, the projects originated in scientific and technical gov ernment agencies. Outstanding among them were the Research and
Resource Office of the Ministry of the armed forces and the national centre for scientific research (C.N.R. S.). In most cases nothing or almost nothing was actually invented and all the technology was either borrowed or rediscovered.
The chief motivating forces were a concern for independence - to have one's own technology and bridge often considerable gaps - or prestige. Often decisions were taken based on one of these consider-


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1133 ations. The political administration did not base its decisions so much on the intrinsic value of the techniques developed as on their ideologi cal connotations and in response to pressures, more often than not from military circles (nuclear technology, military applications for computing, space technology). In any case "the administration had to go along."
Results were very inconsistent. The Rance plant, which may have been a concession to Brittany, was an unsuccessful venture. The
ELDO project had to be abandoned. The electronic microscope's status as the most important in the world was only short-lived. The
"France" was, without a doubt a useless relic of a past era. The SECAM, the grid-tube and undoubtedly Concorde as well today have a very limited market.
Other projects which had to be undertaken in collaboration with other partners were the uranium enrichment plant, the Ariane rocket and the Super-Phoenix power station. Hovertrain derailed in the vicinity of the Defence building. Events seemed to point to these projects being born of love for particular techniques rather than out of a concern for the development of a truly new technical system.
Moreover, in certain fields the very influence from whose domination they wished to escape was the one to whom they were forced to turn, thus the graphite-gasline was dropped in favour of American lines and the geopositioning project was assimilated by an American company.
All in all it was a policy characterised by pinpoint efforts which were eventually abandoned after a considerable outlay. It was not a comprehensive policy - the only logical sort - but rather a series of isolated operations.
The ascendancy of the technocracy, whether real, assumed or potential, tends to bring to mind certain thoughts. The idea which stands out concerns the means of channelling it. At first glance it appears difficult to put up any compensatory forces to counterbalance victorious techniques since, naturally, those forces would not be technical in nature. The problem has only been touched upon to date.
However, it is symptomatic to note the present concern of the gov ernment and the political parties for a new "statute of liberties." This implies that said freedoms are threatened as a result of the use of certain new techniques.
We will not rehash all that has been said with respect to the dangers of computer records on private citizens. The compilation of a file, the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
contents of which are unknown to the person involved, place the fundamental human freedoms of the individual in jeopardy. In this domain, bar reaction from the personnel of the "Safari" programme, there is very little opportunity for individual reaction to invasion of privacy, at least under stable political conditions. The issue has been put forward here and we will stress it no further.
We will examine another area - television. No-one will deny that television or shall we say radio-television is an extremely potent instrument. In the so-called civilised nations where reading of the printed word - including newsprint - is in a steady decline, where telecommunications are fully state controlled, the potency of these new media is practically unrivalled since the cinema remains closely regulated at several different levels.
In fact it took technology to devise what some have called the anti-poison, counter-television. With the advances in portable equip ment (videos, super eight films), with cable television it has become possible to prevent television monopoly. Already groups are being formed with either political affiliations or expertise in the television field.
The same is true of certain decisions which entail often considerable expenditure and concern the environment or social structures. "Sci entific policy does not only mean a policy on science ( ... ) but also one which analyses the extent to which science affects politics, the ways in whic!t scientific and technical considerations influence important deci sions and choices made by the politicians in fields which are not necessarily scientific in themselves such as foreign affairs or town planning." Scientific and technical policy finds itself at the crossroads of political science, economics, philosophy and sociology. We quote the following from a report by the National Academy of Public
Administration in the United States: "the theoreticians of the political sciences in the 19th and 20th Centuries came up against the problem of the ability of democratic systems to harness technology and put its potential to use in the service of social needs based on a concern for the public good."
In 1972, after studies started in 1967 and research conducted by the United States' National Academy of Science, the Office of Technology
Assessment was created within the Congressional framework. This was the third body set up in the American Congress to enlighten the legislature by providing necessary information. It came after the


TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
1135 establishment of the Library of Congress and the General Accounting Office. Its job was to evaluate and assess technological programmes developed by all public services or private entities.
In the words of Messrs. Derian and Staropoli:
Technology assessment is first and foremost a response of the existing institutions to a loss of public confidence - a mechanism which should make it possible to prevent the excesses which would follow in the wake of a growth in technology. It is a means of by-passing the contradiction between those two mythical figures of science and techno logy, Pasteur and Frankenstein.
Viewed from another angle Technology Assessment would appear to be the taming of a certain type of technology- extremist and dangerous because of its lack of concern for the varied impact of its programmes on the society. This time the memory of the sorceror's apprentice may have led the administrators and politicians to the conclusion of a new pact and to the establishment of a new type of control.
Lastly, a third raison d'etre could be found for Technology Assessment. In a conflict where power is at stake, between those very forces which seem to share that power, science and technulogy have played a key role. Since technology has spilled over from the military into everyday life, its social and economic impact has become even more strongly felt. In this context it seems no longer possible for the Administration to leave complete control of technological programmes in the hands of the scientists. In the political arena, in his capacity as representative of the people and in keeping with his mission of control, the legislator has decided to intervene in the process.
The experiment is an interesting one. We will have to wait several years or even decades, in order to know whether the assessments made by this body were level-headed and valid, whether they were too optimistic or, on the contrary overly conservative. The fact remains that this could probably be the direction which should be taken in order to achieve a balance between political power and scientific or technical power.


ESSAY ON
TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
As a conclusion to this volume, we have written an essay, to be understood in the true sense of the term, with all its inadequacies and scruples. This subject has rarely been dealt with, obscured by a haze of received ideas, accepted without discussion. In fact, the problem has never been posed. The frequently evoked expression "applied sci ence" already indicates a link of dependence, but a one-way depen dence. The title of one of Bachelard's chapters, "knowledge and techniques," manifestly proves this. As soon as techniques are not
"scientific,'' they are refused all logic. Bachelard writes: "In tech niques, the goal is really integrated into the being who realizes it, he is the principal element, and this time the that must be does not have the resonance of a logical presumption, but of an order." The philosopher becomes rather entangled in order to oppose techniques and technical knowledge, of a different order and form.
The work of Guillerme and Sebastik on Les commencements de la technologie gives an analogous impression. For them, technology is a discourse on techniques "and their attempted history is that of a scientific discipline, or at the very least of a project of scientific treatment, with technical operations as its object." And a little further on, they continue: the title "thus aims at the constitution of the discourse on technical operations as a scientific type of discourse." Until the middle of the 19th century, there was a formal distinction between "arts" and "sciences." Still in our day there are schools whose names include "arts and crafts" or "arts and manufac ture." But technical knowledge is increasingly assimilated with scientific knowledge, or, if this assimilation proves difficult, with a sub-product of science. "The maturation of an empirical discourse


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1137 and its mutation in conformance with the demands of scientific discourse" is talked of.
In any case, the different stances taken must be exorcised. Technical knowledge is willingly cast aside as empiricism, which is another way of saying what those already quoted have written. For empiricism here means an absence of logic, and all knowledge, as such, is necessarily logical. It is, therefore, this logic which must be reconstituted and affirmed, in whatever form it may take. Are these modern authors aware of what a technique might be which is purely empirical, that is, aleatory, when an object is to be constructed which is perfectly defined in every aspect?
And how better to start this investigation, once again provisional and subject to revision, than by asking how technical knowledge has been seen within the ensemble of knowledge? The expression "classifi cation of sciences" is ambiguous in so far as the term "science" is as well, unless it is admitted, implicitly, that science means organized knowledge possessing to varying degrees its own logic; or, in other words, that each technique, at whatever level it is situated, from the humblest to the most sophisticated, possesses its own system. The successful creation of a clog is of the same order as the solution of an equation: the series of procedures, the successive use of all the tools, the creation of the forms - from the trial runs to the definitive form -, and the gestures which are their inalterable necessity, all this cannot be denied. A wrong gesture is analogous with an error of calculation.
This is the extent of our proposition: the situation of technical knowledge in relation to other types of knowledge is a first objective. It is only after this that we can attempt, with all the difficulties this supposes, to make an inventory of the different forms of technical knowledge.
TECHNIQUES AND THE CLASSIFICATION
OF KNOWLEDGE
This investigation, despite its erudite appearance, seems necessary. It is always worthwhile and useful to know how people have attempted to integrate technical knowledge into a global system of knowledge, to


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
situate it in relation to other types of knowledge. This is not an easy task, for not all the relevant texts have been brought together.
There were certainly ancient models of which we find traces from the Middle Ages on. In the early 10th century, Avicenna follows tradition when he makes most of the techniques practised by the mechanicians of the Alexandrian school dependant on geometry: the science of measuring surfaces (geodesy), the science of moving machines
(automata), the science of traction of heavy weights (baroulkos), the science of weights and scales, the science of instruments of division
(metrology), the science of glasses and mirrors (optics), the science of controlling water. If the other techniques are not represented, it is because they are not considered to be sciences, and thus cannot enter into a classification of sciences: indeed, to what can weaving or pottery be linked?
In the Christian west, one of the first authors to have considered this problem was Hugues de Saint-Victor. In his Didascalicon, he makes mechanics, that is, the study of techniques, one of the four fundamen tal branches of philosophy. It has been shown that this author was in fact inspired by Saint Augustine's De civitate Dei, which drew from
Cicero's De natura deorum, himself influenced by Poseidonius and Panaetius. For Hugues de Saint-Victor, the work of the artisans was an attempt to imitate nature; the ratio of the mechanical arts is from this point a means of understanding creation, and therefore a way of access to God. Furthermore, his Practica geometriae makes a clear distiction between theory and practice: here we find that the arts of the mechani cian and the engineer are made dependant on geometry, an idea which will survive, as we shall see, until the 17th century.
Al-Farabi's classification of sciences departs a little from these first conceptions. It is no longer a question of the mechanical arts imitating nature in some way. Here techniques are seen as applying theoretical sciences with a view to a certain efficiency. Thus we arrive at a subdivision of each branch of learning into speculation and action.
At the time of Hugues de Saint-Victor's death, Domenico Gundi salvo (or Domingo Gonzales) translated Al-Farabi's Statistique des
Sciences into Latin; it was also an adaptation, and a very broad one, adding personal contributions and memories of Hugues de Saint
Victor. Science, or what we today call science, is scientia doctrinalis: it embraces arithmetics, geometry, optics, the science of weights and, finally, of engines (scientia de ingeniis). There is still a distinction


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1139 between speculative science and active or practical science. Arithme tic can be commercial, geometry can be active as well as speculative.
The geometrical skill of the architect, as for Hugues de Saint-Victor or Vincent de Beauvais, finds its application above all in the military domaine. The incorporation of the "science of engines" is, here, forcefully attested.
