"The science of engines teaches us how to imagine and invent the way of adjusting natural bodies by an ad hoc artifice, in conformance with a numerical calculation, so that we can use them in the way we desire." This science is thus applicable to masonry, the construction of lifting apparatus, musical instruments, the making of bows for archery, weapons, burning mirrors, etc.
In his Speculum docrinale, Vincent de Beauvais put forward themes which are completely analogous and emphasizes the necessary connec tions between the different sciences.
In 1296, Raymond Lulle also reviewed the different sciences in his Arbor scientiae. As well as the different sciences of the trivium and quadrivium, he enumerates other sciences which constitute tech niques, and proves to be more complete than his predecessors:
de arte fabrilis de arte carpentoria seu aedificatoria de arte sartoriae de arte agricoliae de arte mercimoniae de arte nautarum de arte militiae metallurgy building dress agriculture commerce navigation war
The classification here is less by technique as such than by activity.
As we can see, this is enumeration more than classification, with all the epistemological problems which the latter entails. But we feel that it was interesting to show that the Middle Ages understood that technical knowledge existed, that it had to be related to other types of knowledge, without being able to find a logical link between them.
After this, we have to go a long way forward before finding preoccupations of a similar nature. Christian Wolff takes up the problem in the chapter De partibus philosophiae of the preliminary discourse to his Philosophia rationalis sive logica of 1728. As the authors of the article on the origins of technology already quoted explain, it is an attempt to unite into a coherent doctrine the learning implicit in the operations of art and put it on the intellectual map of the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
university system, that is, the global system of knowledge. Each art, like law or medecine, refers to the reasons for its operations which reveal its possibilities. Wolff calls this philosophy of art, technology,
"science of the arts and of works of art, or, if you prefer, science of the objects produced by man through the work of his bodily organs, principally his hands." So he is refering to traditional techniques, adv"nced techniques being integrated into the sciences themselves.
The 1763 edition of the preliminary discourse to the Encyclopedie includes, in fine, a figured system of types of human knowledge. These are divided into three major groups: those based on memory; those based on reason; those based on imagination.
In its detail, it deals with intellectual activities, in the widest sense of the term, rather than knowledge as such. For d'Alembert, traditional techniques are based on memory. Others are presented as sub-products of the sciences and are therefore based on reason. Military architecture and tactics, once again, derive from geometry. Mechanics leads to ballistics, hydraulics, navigation and naval architecture. Pyrotechnics and dyeing naturally originate from chemistry. Let us finally note that civil architecture is situated in the group based on imagination.
In fact the first real classifications of the sciences appear in the 19th century. Most of their authors are hard put to place techniques within them. In Auguste Comte's linear classification, according to the two principles of decreasing generality and increasing complexity, techni cal knowledge was difficult to place.
In his essay on La philosophie des sciences (1834), Ampere, himself so close to techniques, proclaims a natural classification of the sciences in the style of Jussieu or Cuvier. "Technology" is defined as a science of the first order, within the branching of the physical sciences and the reign of the cosmological sciences. Furthermore, the pertinent distinc tion which seperates technology from the natural sciences is that the latter pursue their object "independantly from the use drawn from them." Technology covers four sectors: (a) technography or the knowledge of the procedures through which we appropriate "the bodies for the different uses to which they are destined"; ( b) industrial cerdoristics or "calculation of the necessary capital investment";
(c) industrial economics, comparison of the results obtained;
(d) industrial physics or "knowledge of the causes by which known procedures can be perfected and new ones invented" and "prediction in both cases of the success that can be hoped for."


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1141 We can see how vague the terms remain. Techniques as such concern the first and fourth sectors and the differences between these two are very imprecise.
In his Essai sur [es fondements de nos connaissances (1851) A.A.
Cournot, in his turn, takes up the problem. Knowledge is classed under a double entry. The first is Comte's linear series, with some corrections. The second entry includes a theoretical series, a cosmolo gical or historical series and a technical or practical series. The latter consists of:
for the mathematical sciences: calculation, metrology, geodesy;
for the physical sciences: chronometry, the arts of the engineer;
for the biological sciences: agronomy, medicine, pedagogy;
for the noetic sciences: grammar, natural law;
for the political sciences: juridicial sciences, finance.
"Cournot's great merit, it has been written, was to be the first, in his classification of the sciences, perhaps not to have made the question of structures and geneses generally explicit, but to have distinguished, in all lucidity, between the question of the laws of structure and that of history in all its forms." We must note, in any case, that this is not a global system of knowledge, even if some rather general terms, for example, "the arts of the engineer," seem to cover a large territory.
But where can we place the clog-maker?
Present discussions on the serial or cyclical order of the classification of the sciences and the relationships of succession which arise do not contribute much to the insertion of technical knowledge. It has been said, and quite rightly, that there are several levels of scientific thought for each discipline of any worth and scope, and we can expect this relationship of succession to cover quite different forms of depen dance, particularly as for some domains this order appears to be cyclical and for others, linear. We shall see that technical knowledge involves several forms. Certain of these have been subjected to attempts at integration into these classifications, and precisely those which seemed most "scientific," while the others were abandoned.
AN ASCIENTIFIC VIEW OF TECHNIQUES
Attitudes are curious, sometimes contradictory, occasionally incomprehensible. In fact, it is because our concepts and vocabulary


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
are not adapted to the problem which interests us here. What does knowledge mean, exactly, and what does science mean? And the most varied of interpretations shift quite naturally into the haze of notions.
To push it almost to its most absurd extreme, can there be a type of knowledge which is not scientific? We are right to ask this question in that every discipline today has scientific aspirations, that is, where possible it is expressed in mathematical terms: the expansion of statistics and the calculations which follow prove this manifestly.
So what becomes of the clog-maker, to use an example to which we shall often return, or the plumber, faced with a problem to do with taps or drainage? There is a hint of mathematization: essentially, meas urement. And yet, in many of our traditional crafts, there is no measurement, the number is absent. When a knitter works, she follows a design, which we call a pattern, then she counts her stitches, decreases or increases before assembling the pieces to make a whole.
This is a technical process which we shall discuss later. But the potter creating an object does not always follow a pattern, a model, a template: he uses his own judgement and taste. Does pottery then not constitute knowledge?
If there were no knowledge, there would be no craft. And there is nothing more typical in this respect than what we call the handyman.
Lacking knowledge, he has been given increasingly perfected tools and methods which, thanks to these tools, are increasingly simple. In fact, do-it-yourself is less the negation of knowledge than a psychological derivative, or the indefinite wait for a specialist, or his bill. "Is he particularly gifted? Does he possess a workshoop which a specialist would envy? Not at all! With simple but good quality eqipment, he has learnt the thousand-and-one ways to maintain, repair and improve his home himself." On the one hand, he has nonetheless learnt something, and on the other, the tool has become an object of current sale, and is increasingly "universal." And, finally, the things of which the handyman is capable confine a very limited circle of operations.
Let us take the garage-mechanic who repairs your car. He has no knowledge of Beau de Rochas' work on the four-stroke cycle, nor of the metals he uses. He has been given only guidelines, taught how to use his tools, given a few tips, warned against what he does not know;
his knowledge hqs, in a way, been limited, but nevertheless he has knowledge, and or a type which is difficult to define. The fact that there are both good and bad garage-mechanics proves that, as in any science,


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1143 there are different degrees of knowledge. There are things which can be learnt, things which are experienced through the senses - like the sound of the motor or the colour of the flame produced by a Thomas furnace, the rational and the irrational. Is it for this reason that he should lack knowledge?
We have now come to a word which, after knowledge and science, reaches the heights of ambiguity. What is, definitively, and strictly in the domaine which concerns us here, empiricism? In our modern speech, the term has taken on an incontestably pejorative meaning, the absolute negation of all rationality. Serious work on empiricism is lacking: we cannot possibly undertake it here. And Bachelard's expression, approximatative knowledge, even if it reduces the prob lem to a few interesting factors, cannot completely erase it.
Let us take Alquie's defintition in a recent encyclopaedia. "As currently used, the word empiricism denotes the exclusive use of experience, without theory and even without reasoning." This literally falls into another notion which is no less ambiguous, that of experi ence. In fact, we are no more advanced than before.
Undoubtedly, we must return to the most ancient texts. The meaning of the Greek word, t:µndpux, is, at least in our opinion, sufficiently loose to allow many interpretations. If Aristotle's Meta physics is to be believed, t:µndpux represents a certain quantity of accumulated and concordant observations, which permits the deduc tion of a certain form of truth, without however seei':ng their causes and, consequently, without logical reasoning. Here, logic is purely statistical, and certainly lacking theory, and this is more or less true for techniques until the 18th century. But the absence of theory does not necessarily entail the absence of reasoning, and it certainly seems that a technique cannot exist without reasoning. From the conception of the tool to the realization of the object to be made, all is reasoning.
According to a recent study, progress in the blueprint, construction and working of hydraulic turbines in the United States was achieved by
"purely empirical methods." "The scientific teaching of Boyden and Francis was thrown to the wind and the slogan mill it and see completely took over. If a turbine did not give the expected response, its buckets were moved back, raised or lowered and its vanes milled until it gave something better." Improvements in the turbine were thus, it seems, engendered by workers feeling their way, for few construction workers claim to know the principles of their art and there


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
would have been disagreement between any two of them about the basic theory behind their practice. As an author of that time puts it, these construction workers were guided by their instincts. Of one of the happier workers, it was said: "His theories were wrong, but his ignorance did not hinder his success"; and of another: "He has neither plan nor working method and he has only followed the mill it and see system." A mill constructor made this remark around 1850: "The theoretical research of scholars has contributed little to the improvement of the mechanical arts in this country ... ; in practical mechanics, progress has not been due to scientists."
We need a more precise study of the conditions under which these turbines were elaborated. The quotations are contradictory: some suggest empiricism - but of which type? - others mention theories, albeit incorrect, which imply reasoning. We feel that this study should be pursued.
Gesture and the Spoken Word We shall be brief about this, taking up the title of a work by A.
Leroi-Gourhan: for gesture and the spoken word are precisely things which cannot be written down. Coming from a prehistorian, it could be believed that these terms have a chronological value. They go beyond this in that they are timeless and that many people, even today, think and are convinced that techniques cannot be read about and that they are learnt "in the workshop,'' by apprenticeship, that is, precisely through gesture and the spoken word. And this extends further than so-called primitive or traditional techniques.
And we shall see the dissociation of the various elements of technical knowledge, some of which are able to take on different forms. The primary acts of traditional techniques, which could be called technemes belong to a determined process, even if a complete operation, that is, the making of an object, demands a succession of technemes. Three categories of types can be envisaged:
I - The choice of material or materials to be worked on is essential.
This type of knowledge is close to the natural or chemical sciences in certain aspects. We shall return to this later.
II- Knowledge of the act and of its corollary, that is, the tool, or the necessary set of tools. And here we are in the domaine of gesture and the spoken word. The tool, which is the instrument of the technical act,


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1145 is perfectly specific. Of course, it is adapted to the material to be worked on, the operation to be executed, but it is also, in a certain sense, particular to the worker who uses it, that is, to the way in which it is used. It is unique, transmittable with difficulty beyond the stereotyped gesture and the spoken word which is supposed to explain it. It is the tributary of the hand which handles it, of the idea which the user has of its use. It is made of particular material, has an appropriate form; its weight. balance and handle are the necessary links between it and the hand, and all this to such a point that, in former times, the worker personalized his tools, perfected them, adapted them to his way of working.
III - Finally, there is the object to be made, also personal, like a work of art, and executed to more or less precise norms but which leave the artisan, beside imaginative touches and decoration, a quite astonishing margin of freedom. Under these conditions, technical skill above a certain level is difficult to transmit. Some succeed, some fail or fall into banalities. Success is the finished object, but here the object possesses dimensions other than its sheer utility, its correct use. Craft is here situated on the same level as art. And what can we say about artistic knowledge?
"Besides, wrote d'Alembert in the Discours preliminaire, it is the labour which makes the artist and labouring cannot be learnt from books." D'Alembert's development of this idea coincides perfectly with ours.
Everything made us determined to resort to the workers. We addressed the most skillful in Paris and the kingdom: we went to the trouble of going to their workshops, questioning them, writing from their dictation, developing their ideas, extracting the terms peculiar to their professions, making tables of the terms and defining them, conversing with those workers from whom we had obtained dissertations and (an almost indispensable precaution) rectifying, during long and frequent exchanges with some of them, the things that others had imperfectly, obscurely and sometimes inaccurately explained. Most of those who exercise the mechanical arts embrace them only out of necessity, and work by instinct alone. Out of a thousand workers, barely a dozen can be found who are capable of expressing themselves with any clarity on the instruments they use and the pieces that they make. We saw men who had worked for forty years without knowing anything about their machines. ( . . . ) But there are crafts which are so singular, labour which is so nimble, that unless you work yourself, operate the machines with your own hands and see the finished article forming under your own eyes, it is difficult to talk of them with precision.
D'Alembert, and Diderot in other texts, thus perfectly define this form of knowledge.


11'16
TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
What the Encyclopedie tried to achieve, with the difficulties insisted upon by its promoters, can now be done with the cinema, which gives us an exact restitution of gesture and the spoken word.
At this stage, the acquisition of knowledge is imitation. And the rules of certain guilds which limited the numbers of apprentices can be understood. This does not only show a sort of artisanal Malthusianism, but also the difficulty of explaining to too many people and rectifying the necessary gestures in the handling of a tool and the manufacture of an object.
The limits of this acquisition of knowledge are very distinct: it is not a question of heredity, and heritage is only partial. The report drawn up by 0. Greard in 1872 on the foundation of a school for apprentices for the city of Paris is symptomatic in this respect. And yet it was written at a time when the so-called traditional crafts had largely lost their particular characters. Apprenticeship cannot be achieved outside the workshop, and there are many crafts which do not lend themselves to apprenticeship. Thus it was not one school but an infinite number of schools that was needed. This was not the project of the councillor of
Paris, which was situated on two levels. Let us quickly pass over the first, which was to give the apprentice generally useful knowledge, but which lay outside the craft itself: scientific notions, moral aptitude destined to facilitate the practice of a craft. Beside this, the apprentice was to learn how to execute a few fundamental operations, "such as dressing or planing, joining, turning, etc." From there he went on to the tools adapted to these fundamental operations.
The Recipe "Recipe" was often the title for technical works - and still is for cookery. One of these, and one of the best known, is by Bernard
Palissy. The recipe is an affirmation, coded or uncoded, which permits the achievement of the desired result.
Its mechanism appears to be relatively simple. In fact, it consists of an accumulation of concordant observations in a given domain, without however seeking the causes of the observed facts. It is a skill of the memory. When a doctor determines that in precise cases a certain medicine possesses a curative value, when an angler knows where to find the fish he wants, this is the stuff of recipes. Thus the recipe goes far beyond the domain of techniques. We do not need to add that the


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1147 domain of the recipe has singularly narrowed. For evidence of this we need look no further than the definition of our most modern dictiona ries: the recipe is a procedure used in domestic economics.
Aristotle expresses this very clearly in his Metaphysics: "rigorously speaking, experience thus cannot attain the necessary basis of skill to which the term "principle" is most appropriately applied; it is expressed in propositions which, born outside intuitive evidence and syllogism, are in any case undemonstrated affirmations and opinions."
This constitutes an original form of human knowledge which nothing can replace and the interest of which, Aristotle affirms, is not less than that of demonstration. This type of knowledge comes out of inquiry, not pure reflection.
It undeniably holds interest, but also danger. The desire to accumu late the greatest possible number of observations explains why they are often superficial and incomplete. Erroneous links may be made which lead to heterogeneous series. There is definitively antimony, and a fundamental one, between the desire to know a lot and the desire to know well. Aristotle, in his Metaphysics, addresses some admirable sentences to this subject. "It is memory which forms experience in man's mind. For the remembrance of the occurences of a single thing constitutes, being multiplied for each case, experience in all its energy;
and experience is almost equal in value to science and art, which it greatly resembles." Here, experience could be see as the equivalent of
Bachelard's first observation.
The fundamental and essential difference between it and gesture and the spoken word, is that the recipe can be transmitted in writing.
Thus it allows the birth of a technical literature which covers certain domains of material life.
One of the essential domaine of the recipe is nature, raw nature and not its transformation. It is found in Vitruvius as well as Pliny the
Elder. Agriculture is one of the sectors most faithful to it. It can be said, with little mistake, that until the dawn of the 20th century, agriculture was very largely a technique of recipes. There is no need to quote examples, everyone knows these. And from this moment, a distinction can be made between recipes of time, season, and recipes of aspect. They can be translated into a simple formulation: a certain technical act must be executed at a determined time; if the material to be worked has a certain aspect, it is good or bad. For the first case, let us quote Vitruvius: the trees destined to provide building timber must


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
be cut in the autumn and bled beforehand to extract the sap; soft stones must be quarried in the summer.
The choice of materials to be worked is one of the essential domains of the recipe and this applies to a great number of techniques: wood, of course, but also ore for steel-making, earth for pottery, stone in construction work. At the beginning of the 17th century, Mathurin
Jousse, in his treatise on locksmithing, uses the same reasoning even for the choice of the metal for its different uses. And, from this time, iron was judged according to how it broke. This began a long tradition which, through Reaumur, was to culminate in metallography.
Duhamel du Monceau, in his Art du serrurier (1762), takes up exactly the same precepts and gives more detail. "The quality of iron can be better known by examining its grain: in order to do this it must be broken" with a chisel. In this way the various sorts of iron can be distinguished and assigned to their most suitable uses.
If the break is shiny and formed of big flakes like pieces of talc, you can be certain that the iron is very brittle, will be hard to file and difficult to hammer hot or cold; it will be soft in the furnace and burn easily; sometimes, instead of softening under the hammer it will become even more brittle; the iron is therefore of bad quality for all types of work;
except, because of its hardness it could be used in blocks where it is exposed to friction.
The most numerous recipes are those concerning time and quality.
But there is also a considerable number for mixtures. They are used to provide information concerning the proportions of different elements which go to make up a determined product. The recipe can be complex when the quality of these different elements also comes into play.
Let us take some simple examples. The proportions of tin used to make bronze vary according to the use to be made of it. In his
Pyrotechnie (1540), Biringuccio tells us that 23 to 26% is needed to make bronze for bells and 8 to 12% for artillery. He does not doubt that these proportions have evolved through the ages. Recipes are not entirely impervious to fashion. The proportions of the elements for gunpowder have evolved thus:
1380 . . .
1410 . . .
1480 . . .
Saltpetre 1 3 8 Sulphur 1 2 3 Charcoal 1 2 3


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1149 It is quite possible that this evolution reveals a parallel evolution of the products, particularly the preparation of saltpetre. In the same spirit,
Vitruvius gives us the recipe for making mortars: 1 part of lime to 3 of quarried sand; 1 part of lime to 2 of sea or river sand.
These recipes were certainly not arrived at through empiricism alone. Not only simple experience, in Aristotle's sense, was needed, but also real experimentation: all the treatises on fire of the second half of the Middle Ages bear witness to this.
In Leonardo da Vinci we can clearly perceive the nature of the recipe: the accumulation of observations, to avoid the ambiguous term of experience, but also a series of experiments, conducted in a logical manner. The Florentine undoubtedly and quite manifestly sought explanations of a scientific nature and it is because he did not find them that he gives recipes as a second-best. We shall take two examples.
The first is certainly founded on accumulated observations: "When the crack in a wall is wider at the top than at the bottom, this is manifest proof that the cause of the damage lies outside the perpendicular of the crack." This is the same as the doctor's knowledge according to
Aristotle.
The second example is certainly more complex and must be compared with other texts:
My house is on the bank of a river; the water is carrying away the earth of its foundations and is about to make it collapse; now, I want the river to fill up the cavity it has hollowed out and consolidate my house. In this case, we are governed by the (fourth) proposition of the second (book) where it is demonstrated that: the impulse of any mobile force pursues its initial line; this is why we shall construct a barrier at the oblique point nm, but it is better to make it higher up at op, so that all the material on your side of the mound can be moved into the concavity where your house is situated; and the material of the mound kwill do the same, which will complete the desired task for this winter. But if the river is great and powerful, we shall construct the said barrier in three or four parts; the first, in the direction of the source of the water, must jut out just beyond the bank, covering a quarter of the river's breadth; then, below this one, you will construct another at a distance equal to the summit of the cascade made by the water falling from the first dam, for at the summit of this cascade, the water leaves the top of the hillock formed by the gravel hollowed out by its first impact, when it falls into the river-bed from the dam.
This second barrier reaches out half-way across the breadth of the river. The third must be down-river on the same bank at the same distance determined from the second as the second was from the first; it will be longer, covering three quarters of the river's breadth.
You will procede in this way for the fourth dam, which will close the river transversally.
And these four dams or barriers will result in a far greater power than if all the material formed a single barrier of uniform thickness blocking the entire breadth of the river. This conforms with the fifth (proposition) of the second (book) where it is demonstrated that the material of a single support, if its length is quadrupled, will bear a quarter of what it bore before, but much less.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
This is a long quotation and the explanations are rather confused. In his great treatise on hydraulics, which was never published, Leonardo had established a certain number of propositions of a general nature.
We must not be mistaken: no demonstration was possible. These propositions had come out of observations made by Leonardo, thanks to little models made of wood carrying sections in glass in order to better follow what happens in a stream of water carrying particles of sand or encountering an obstacle. These propositions are used as recipes.
Description and Illustration To describe a tool or a machine is already difficult; to describe a technical operation is even more so. This is why description was very soon accompanied by its necessary corollary, illustration. Let us again listen to the Discours preliminaire de l' Encyclopedie:
But we are so little accustomed to both writing and reading about the arts that it is difficult to explain things in an intelligible manner. The need for illustrations is born out of this. It could be demonstrated through a thousand examples that a Dictionary which purely and simply gave definitions, no matter how well executed it may be, cannot exclude illustrations without falling into obscure or vague descriptions; this aid was, then, most necessary to us. A glance at the object or its representation tells us more about it than a page of discourse.
On this level, description becomes a mere commentary on the illustration which provides the essential knowledge.
Description is discourse. The problem is, then, knowing how to order the discourse. D'Alembert explains the method:
This is the method followed for each art. The following were treated: (1): The material, where it can be found, its preparation, good and bad qualities, different types, the operations to which it is subjected, either before or during its use. (2): The principle pieces made with the material, and the way of making them. (3): The name, description and illustration of the tools and machines were given, with parts detached and assem bled; the cross-section of moulds and other instruments of which the interior should be known, their outline, etc. ( 4): The labour and principal operations were explained and represented in one or two plates, where either the artist's hands or his whole body could be seen in action, working on the most important piece of his art. (5): The terms peculiar to the art were collected and defined as exactly as possible.
The technique of the discourse on techniques is not made any more precise. In fact, the Encyclopaedists attempted to translate gesture,


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1151 which was not easy, and the spoken word, which was simpler, and to take up recipes on a large scale.
The first descriptions we have are of the Alexandrian technicians' war engines. They are generally very brief and are always accom panied by illustrations, to which we shall return. In all the works of the
Middle Ages, from Villard de Honnecourt and Guido da Vigevano to the "Renaissance engineers" and Leonardo da Vinci, description is more than summary and illustration has become essential in technical knowledge. It is the same for the "theatres of machines " which flourished from the 16th to the 18th century.
Of course, from the 16th century on, a great number of technical treatises, covering almost the entire technical world, were published.
They also brought description and image together and all insisted on the difficulty of their project.
We know that it was at the end of the 17th century, at Colbert's behest, that the Academie des Sciences was to draw up a complete review of the "mechanical arts." "But, wrote Cl. Salomon-Bayet, the writing of a dissertation on a certain hydraulic machine is one thing, the unifying aim to enclose all the arts in a single treatise intending to present them not according to juxtaposition but according to deduc tion, is quite another." A methodical concern which, to the distinction made by Buot in 1675 between a general treatise on mechanics and the individual mechanical arts, adds the classification into chapters each of which would refer to a given, dominant subject and, within each of these chapters, a hierarchy, one craft being conditioned by another;
this relationship of dependence was coupled, without justification, with a cartesian series of links from the simplest to the most compli cated. In short, des Billettes presents us with a treatise with a double entry, logical and classificatory. On the one hand, in an order of sequence according to "crafts exerted with tools only," the first art described would be that which provides for all the other arts and for itself the means of "making anything desired": iron ore, furnaces, smelting, the production of iron and steel; on the other hand, a distribution under four titles (animal, metals, stone, plants) would easily cover 100 or 120 crafts. Thus it was not an accumulation of descriptions. If an Academie des Arts had been born out of the
Academie des Sciences we would perhaps have had a "reasoned" dictionary of techniques, beside those of Thomas Corneille and Jac ques Savary des Brulons. In fact, the Description des arts et metiers


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
started by the Academie des Sciences around 1693 and of which the first volume, due to the efforts of Reaumur and Duhamel du Monceau, appeared in 1761, was merely a juxtaposition of descriptions.
Diderot and d' Alembert had had similar ideas. Apart from the Discours preliminaire, it is in the article ARTS, certainly written by
Diderot, that we can find revealing sentences. It says that the arts must be viewed in the context of their history and in relation to the natural sciences: "The obstacles encountered and surmounted in their devel opment would be presented in an entirely natural order and the synthetic explanation of the successive procedures in Art would facili tate its understanding by the most ordinary of minds and would put the artist on the path to be followed in order to come nearer to perfection."
And he adds: "As for the order to be followed in such a treatise, I believe that the most advantageous would be to relate the Arts to the products of nature." His conclusion goes without saying considering the amplitude of the undertaking: "A treatise of the Arts, as I conceive of it, is therefore not the work of an ordinary man."
The two attempts at a technical discourse of a descriptive nature, but also relating all technical operations to each other, trying to give through description a syntheitic view of techniques, finally failed. The technical part of the Encyclopedie is also a juxtaposition of descrip tions: could it be otherwise in a dictionary, even if it be reasoned?
Abbe Jaubert's Dictionnaire raisonne universe/ des arts et metiers, first published in 1773, puts across the same sentiments. And in the first half of the 19th century, the same juxtaposition of techniques appears: the best example is that of the Roret "guides" which were immensely successful, being reedited throughout the century.
In this domain, despite what some may say, influenced by the present situation, technical discourse cannot be a scientific type of discourse, and we would still have to agree on the term "scientific."
There is no relation between these two types of knowledge except insofar as techniques have become scientific, and these days they are only scientific in certain domains and often in marginal aspects.
Otherwise, description alone, for all its vagueness, is the only form of knowledge. In the assembly of a television set, there is on the one hand scientific theory, without which it could not be achieved, but on the other hand, without knowing exactly what a diode or a triode is, the assembler has descriptions and diagrams. There is no need to know the four-stroke cycle in order to be able to drive a car. Therefore today


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1153 there is a series of levels in techniques: the conception, which demands scientific knowledge, sometimes thorough; the manufacture, which is mostly rid of it; and the use, also a technical gesture, which is unconcerned with it.
And now we can better understand the importance of illustration which, almost from the beginning, the technician uses without any other kind of explanation. The illustration is also, and above all, description with its dual finality, the drawing of the tool or machine and that of the object to be made. We do not need to stress that nothing has been written on technical drawing, on the modes of representation precisely of technical knowledge, for illustration certainly constitutes a type of knowledge in this domain.
The first technical treatises we possess are those of the Greek mechanicians of the Hellenistic period, until Vitruvius. We know that they were illustrated. But the manuscripts we possess go no further back than the 10th century, and we do not know whether or not the illustrations they carry are faithful reproductions of the originals.
Fortunately, for the Middle Ages, we have the notebooks of engineers and architects: Villard de Honnecourt (c. 1270), Guido da Vigevano
(c. 1320), plus a few isolated drawings such as that of Dondi's clock (mid-14th century).
There was of course nothing in these drawings which could make them real aids to technical knowledge: no perspective, no a fortiori figuration in two planes. Furthermore, the drawing is always total, never divided into parts. Each element, wherever it is situated, is always represented in the position where it can be best distinguished: in other words, a wheel is always represented as a circle. This is practised to such an extent that interpretation is often difficult. However it must be noted that the skill of the illustrators varies considerably.
Drawing techniques developed from the early 15th century on.
Certain basic principles were maintained, particularly the total drawing. However, Leonardo da Vinci, even if he continues in this tradition, has left us from his interest in primary mechanisms some drawings of details, for example of gear mechanisms. But on the other hand, perspective which precisely helps the total drawing to become intelligible becomes a widespread practice. The transition can already be detected in the notebooks of German engineers in the first half of the 15th century; it is complete in the notebooks of Italian engineers in the second half of the century.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
From Francesco di Giorgio di Martini onward, we find near perfection. There is a distinct evolution from the automobile vehicle of this Siennese technician to the anonymous reproduction of a machine for cutting precious stones of the same period, and it ends magnificen tly. We even begin to find drawings which are no longer in perspective but in a given plane, generally elevations: from those of cathedral fa<;ades, for example Rheims and Clermont, to that of a crane in the collection of an anonymous German engineer.
Leonardo da Vinci's drawings are perfect examples of this stage.
Beside the quick sketches from observation as much as of new ideas, beside drawings where the artist seems to override the technician, beside muddled drawings where he visibly seeks a graphic solution to a problem he cannot solve, like the automatic weaving loom, rather incomprehensible despite certain speculative interpretations, we find drawings which are exactly in line with those of Francesco di Giorgio.
We remain within the same tradition with the theatres de machines, collections of plates accompanied by brief and generally imprecise texts. They appeared around the second half of the 16th century and were increasingly fashionable until the mid-18th century. Here, technical knowledge is reduced to an engraved image in perspective.
Sometimes difficult to reconstitute. This will also be the case for the technical treatises of the period.
However there were some new trends. One of the most interesting and elusive is to be found in the work of Leonardo da Vinci. His studies of men at work attempt to reconstitute the best positions for a given task: the digger, men passing material to each other on a building site put across a representation of gestures quite remarkable for the period, but once again isolated.
With Agricola and his De re metallica, we encounter on the contrary a type of drawing which will extend from the mid-16th to the end of the
18th century. In fact, the picturs representing machines do not only include the total machine, as it had been represented since the mid-15th century, but also the principal parts taken individually, generally spread out on the ground. This method was to be employed in both the Description of the Academie des Sciences and Diderot and d'Alembert's Encyclopedie. Let us once again quote the Discours p re/iminaire:
Artists were sent into the workshops. They sketched the machines and tools: nothing was omitted which could show them distinctly to the eye. If a machine merited detail


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1155 because of the importance of its use and the number of its parts, they passed from the simple to the composed. They started by assembling as many elements as could be seen without confusion in the first figure. In the second, the same elements were seen with others. In this way, the most complicated machine was successively formed without hindrance to the eye or the mind. Sometimes we must go back from the knowledge of the finished article to that of the machine, and sometimes we must go forward from the knowledge of the machine to that of the finished article.
It was the same for technical operations. But here, restrictions had to be imposed.
If you wished to say and represent everything concerning a single art, this would provide volumes of discourse and plates. If you proposed to represent in illustrations all the states through which a lump of iron passes before being transformed into a needle, you would never see the end of it. That the discourse should follow the artist's procedures in the finest detail is all very well. But we have had to restrict the illustrations to the movements of the operation which are very easy to draw and very difficult to explain. We remained with the essential circumstances, those whose representation, when well executed, necessarily results in the knowledge of those which are not seen.
The intentions are very clear. However we shall observe that the results were not always as laudable as the intentions. Often, the sight of the images - those of the article FORGE spring to mind as an example - gives in reality only a very superficial knowledge of the various technical operations. The refining of pig-iron, for example, could not be represented. We have reached the limit of the system of the exposure of knowledge.
This technique, the exposition of techniques, is from this point on, saturated, even though it extended almost until the end of the 19th century. We need look no further than the images of L. Figuier under the Second Empire, one of the most remarkable popularisers of techniques which were then modern. The illustration of the works of
Jules Verne came out of them.
So-called "industrial" drawing was born of descriptive geometry, that is, in the second half of the 18th century. But let us not be mistaken. The industrial drawing is used in execution, not for comprehension or knowledge in the broadest sense of the term. In technical knowledge we always find that the skill of execution takes prority over the skill of explanation. There remains an indescribable zone of gestures and spoken words between the drawing of the tool and the machine on one side, and the drawing of the objectto be made on the other.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
What we need to know, for this is where true technical knowledge lies, is how the drawing and its dimensions have been obtained. The fundamental difference between scientific knowledge and technical knowledge is that the former is indivisible, whereas the latter is divisible. A drawing is an aid used in knowledge, but is not knowledge itself. When the first associations for adult vocational training were created in France, and in this they succeeded the institution of the 18th century, the two fundamental subjects taught were calculation and drawing, the former barely used, in fact, except for the understanding of the latter.
The Scale Model And we pass on quite naturally to the scale model. Even if it is possible to situate it in this slow progression of technical knowledge, it is not without ambiguity. In fact, in what we previously said, the model replaced the illustration, and the explanation with which it is fundamentally associated, the writing of treatises. We return not to the gesture, insofar as the model is linked with machine, but to the spoken word. And we are at the opposite pole to the technical drawing, the model seeking to aid comprehension and not, as we shall see, reali zation.
The notion of the model as a type of demonstration is undoubtedly ancient. It seems that certain Greek mechanicians used it both for direct information and as an aid to a difficult description.
The model is antithetical to a technique with scientific aspirations. It was used to show how things "worked," limited to considerations of a purely material order. The illustration only gave "demonstrations" in the purely visual sense, which were static. Here, it is a question of dynamic demonstrations. An unlimited multitude of plates which would have to be engraved was replaced de visu by a "living" mechan ism whose functioning could be indefinitely repeated until it was understood. Moreover, we know that links were constantly made between the animated object and moving anatomy, the automaton and the artificial man.
We are obviously reduced to suppositions concerning the origin of the model. On the one hand, there is the automaton, with its myster ies, its ability to astonish, attract attention, excite curiosity. On the other hand, there is the "mock-up" which must have existed for a very


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1157 long time, to make architectural projects visible, and they multiply from the 16th century on as Palladio shows. The model is precisely the passage to the third dimension without which things may remain beyond comprehension.
Demonstration through models by technicians certainly preceded the use of models by physicists: think of all those physics cabinets thanks to which phenomena could be perceived for which a scientific explanation could often not be given. The technician's demonstration cabinet had exactly the same significance. Here again, there is a history to be written the origins of which would certainly go back a long way, even if they are still unknown to us. A few texts stand out in the history of the idea of the model, which we call the scale model.
It is true and seems undeniable that the Greek mechanicians of Alexandria used models. When Hero of Alexandria describes the cheiroballistra, a weapon of projection, he obviously has a model beneath his gaze. Certain remarks to which we shall return, offer manifest proof of the use of models for the transmission of knowledge.
We must go forward a long way in time before finding other indications. Let us look at a project of Descartes which undoubtedly does not conform with the notion of the model and we shall see why:
His advice was to have built at the royal school and in other places devoted to the general public various halls for artisans: to destine each hall to each craft's guild; to adjoin to each hall a cabinet filled with all the mechanical instruments necessary or useful to the arts which were to be taught there; to create sufficient funds not only to pay the expenses incurred for the experiments, but also to pay the keep of the Masters and Teachers whose number was to equal that of the Arts to be taught there.
There are two remarks to be made concerning this text. The first is about the sheer convenience of the plan. It seems that what Descartes had in mind was a collection of full-size machines. The advantage of the model is that it reduces the amount of space used. The second brings us back again to the question of gesture and the spoken word.
Gesture is surely limited in that only machines are involved. But Descartes' text is elliptical: it does not seem to exclude crafts where only tools are used.
Let us go forward a few decades. A very curious document has been preserved on this subject. It is a catalogue entitled: Explication des mode/es des machines et forces mouvantes que l' on expose a Paris, dans la rue de la Harpe, vis-a-vis de Saint-Cosme, dated 1683 and illustrated with twelve plates. There was thus a link between the model and the


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
illustration as we defined them earlier. Let us note, but with reser vation, for the documents have not been traced, that a similar exhi bition was held at Niiremburg in 1569. The preface to our catalogue clearly expresses the method employed:
The explanation of machines and forces of movement must be considered as something serious, important and very useful to the public, through the knowledge and practice that anyone can acquire in order to become adept in a little time. It is a way of demonstrating that instructs by inspection alone and determines by a real and effective experience ( . . . ) Ancient and modem authors have proposed many fine machines in their writing; but some have left us their ideas in drawings without proportion and others always disguise something of the reality of their experiments, and so we have decided to interpret the former and examine the latter well so that the public may be satisfied in its inquiries without risking experiments and achieve reliable results in its projects. To this effect we are exhibiting models six feet high of all the machines and other inventions ( . . . ) which are constructed of wood, iron and copper in the correct proportions and have the same effect as if they were of usual size ( . . . ) The diversity of minds to be encountered in the public much troubled those who were in charge of choosing the machines of which models are exhibited. But as this exhibition has the progress and instruction of those who wish to perfect themselves as much in mind as the satisfaction and curiosity of those who have intelligence of and capacity in these matters, we thought it appropriate to mix those machines which are new and extraordinary, to attract their approbation, with those in common use, to act as demonstrative examples to the others!
This text surely deserves a long commentary. All the terms should be weighed and assessed: professional apprenticeship, the information of the general public, the demonstrative aspect of the model, the incitation to research and innovation. The programme is exactly that of the Theatres de machines: mills for grinding, sawing wood, polishing weapons, crushing powder and fulling wool.
"The conception of the promoters of the exhibition of 1683, notes M.Birembaut, presented the originality of generalising the practice of models of machines described since the Renaissance in numerous illustrated treatises, published under the name of machines. Their authors had given their imaginations a free rein and contrived, on paper, the assemblage of machine elements without worrying to check the functioning of the assemblages represented or to assess their interest."
There were twenty one models of machines. But we must not be deceived. Eleven were executed according to plates by Besson, B6ck ler, Ramelli, Salomon de Caus and Strada. Two were reproductions of
Roman catapults. Finally, eight were realisations of inventions due to two contemporaries.


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1159 The idea, which does not seem to have been a great success- nobody in the period talks or writes about it -, was of course not entirely new.
When Arnold de Ville presented his project for the conveyance of water to Versailles (the machine of Marly) he had to make a small scale model on the Seine at the Palfour mill, at the foot of the slope of
Saint-Germain. It was started in June 1679 and presented before Louis XIV at the end of 1680. There are other examples of these "half-size" models. One is that of a series of war ships presented to the king on the canals of Versailles, while other small models were executed with the same intention.
The creation of an exhibition of numerous models had already been proposed. There was perhaps also a proposal for a gallery of models at the Louvre. In his project of September 1675 for a total spectacle, combining fun fairs, gambling houses and theatrical representations,
Leibniz added a site for "inventions, mock-ups, models, etc." We should follow the slow progression of the model until the 18th century, in parallel with the physics cabinets. The distance between the automaton and the technical model is blurred. More or less the same people make both.
Duhamel du Monceau, the great 18th century technician, had a collection of models which was unfortunately broken up some years ago. Diderot had models constructed in order to understand certain particularly complex machines. While Gargantua's preceptor took his pupil to visit artisans to give him notions of the various crafts, Madame de Genlis had models made of certain workshops represented in the plates of the Encyclopedie for her royal pupils. We know that Vaucan son also assembled both the machines he had invented and a collection of small models. He represents the perfect final stage of this modelist trend which was, need we emphasize, as much for demonstration as for research, diffusion and technical progress. We do not need to be reminded that James Watt began his research on a scale model of
Newcomen's engine belonging to the model collection of the Uni versity of Glasgow. The great Swedish engineer Christofer Polhem also worked extensively with models, now conserved in the Technical
Museum of Stockholm.
Heir to Vaucanson's cabinet, the Conservatoire national des arts et metiers continued the tradition during part of the 19th century: the model remained for some time an instrument of demonstration for those who wished to enter industry: today it merely constitutes a


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
museum of technical souvenirs. When, in 1817, Franc;ois de Wendel wished to venture into mechanical construction, he asked for a loan of the model steam-engine from the Conservatoire des arts et meters.
When Bourdon, at Le Creusot around 1838, studied what was to become the power hammer, he worked on a model which he made himself.
The conception of models for studying natural phenomena useful to know for some techniques was more delicate. We shall only take two examples. The first goes back to Leonardo da Vinci. It was a simple piece of apparatus, certainly, but adequate for the reproduction of what is sometimes difficult to distinguish in nature. We have the drawing of little wooden channels, sometimes equipped with glass in the walls, designed to study the movement of running water, the displacement of alluvium, the consequences of an obstacle before knowing what is needed to regularize the flow of a river. Some industrial laboratories in Grenoble still do the same. The identical process can be seen in the realization of hulls in numerous "careening basins" which can be found in many places dating from the end of the
19th century.
It is worthy of note that it was soon understood that the passage from the model to the object itself presented difficulties. A single coefficient of enlargement has no value.
Even before our era, Callistratus suffered a failure concerning the conveyance of stone which he was taking to the temple of Ephesus. He had not realized, to begin with, that certain things seem good when seen on a small scale and that they cannot be executed on a large scale;
whereas, reciprocally, there are many others for which scale models cannot be made, but are constructed on the spot when they are needed. It seems that the Greeks used the model in technical research and understood that the model was not necessarily the reduction on the same scale of all the elements of a machine.
The principle was broadened. Galileo affirms that the total dimen sions of a mechanism cannot be increased in a uniform way without modifying the proportions. The problem of the passage from the mock-up to the actual machine, that is, from the calculation of the dimensions of the parts relative to the dimensions of the whole mechanical organism cannot be reduced in any way in its conditions of resistance. Friction must also be taken into consideration.


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1161 The author of the 1683 catalogue quoted above knew this perfectly.
The model of the machine for raising water had been executed, the beam engine constructed at the Paris Arsenal: the proportions were
1/6 for the buckets, 1/4 for the beam and 1/3 for the frame. As the proportions to be given to the dimensions of each model in passing to full-size indicate, only four models had been reduced using a homothe tic ratio. The promoters of the exhibition had not and could not have the ideas which are familiar to us on the geometrical similarity of machines.
A SCIENTIFIC VIEW OF TECHNIQUES
In a recent study which has already been already quoted, Les commencements de la technologie, the authors aimed at the consti tution of the discourse on technical operations as a scientific type of discourse. They talked of the maturation of an empirical discourse and its transformation in conformance with the demands of scientific discourse. It was in fact an attempt to establish a closer link between the arts and the sciences.
In the final analysis, this amounted to the confusion of technical knowledge with scientific knowledge which are however situated on fundamentally different planes. In discussing the problems of Greek techniques, J.P. Vernant certainly thought scientific theory possible, but declared that it was degraded in being applied. Of course, a theory is rational, whereas techniques, being natural, are irrational. We feel that the problem has been badly expressed. Levels must necessarily be established.
On the "lowest" level, science and techniques can only meet in very special cases. There has been a whole movement trying to give a scientific explanation to a certain number of elementary operations.
Many people, before and after Charles Fremont, have attempted this interesting investigation. The fact is striking for tools: the work of the hammer, saw, screw, etc. A theory of the making of a wooden clog is impossible, unthinkable.


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
To take the contrary view to J.P. Vernant, it can be said that theory, when there can be one, distorts reality. It reduces it, in eliminating a certain number of phenomena which, even if they are of a minor order, nevertheless exist and in limiting itself to x number of elements able to be thought of scientifically speaking: thus, for example, Clausius' thermodynamics. We shall return to this.
Finally, there are techniques which could not have been born without an established scientific sytem. This is the case, for example, for the chemical industry and the nuclear industry. But here again the path from theory to practical realization is often a difficult one, as we shall later see.
The use of a particular science for techniques should be considered and even the way in which this use has been thought of. In other words, we must determine how much pure science enters into a technique.
Theory brings to light a certain number of principles explaining technical phenomena: they do not govern them, at least, not totally.
This margin represents the difference between scientific knowledge and technical knowledge. This does not exclude the conjunction, in certain people with a dual curiosity, of the two demands. From Thales and Archytas to Hero of Alexandria, Leonardo da Vinci, Monge and many others in the course of time, men are to be found who have paid equal attention to each of these branches of knowledge. But we may suppose that the technician took precedence over the scientist. The inverse rarely occurred and we find very few scientists who really become technicians. In other words, possession of one of these two types of knowledge does not necessarily imply that of the other. If
Hero of Alexandria thought, like the founders of the Ecole poly techni que, that a minimum of scientific knowledge was desirable in the technician, the level would necessarily remain relatively low. And we can see many cases of technicians refusing to let science dominate techniques. Even economists are beginning to take notice today and fight against a mathematization which is judged to be definitively too far away from concrete realities.
The use of science by technicians has long remained ambiguous.
Using science does not formally mean from that moment that tech niques become scientific. In that way a few obstacles can be overcome, very few, as we shall see. It rather indicates the desire of the technician to seem scholarly: a sort of rehabilitation. In those who have undertaken this, there are two parallel processes with a few episodic confrontations and this because the articulation between these two


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1163 disciplines has always been difficult, right up into the 19th century.
Indeed, a certain conjunction is needed, that is, a type of science which can be used and, on the other side, a type of technique which is
"scientificable." Plato in his Republic gives Socrates a certain number of primary truths to say. Indeed, Socrates and Glaucon review a certain number of techniques and ask how science can be useful to them. They reach the conclusion that the level of science needed is extremely low, a little arithmetics and geometry.
Like his predecessors, Vitruvius distinguishes between practice and theory (ratiocinatio), that is, science. "Practice is experience of pro longed and accomplished use, obtained through the hands with the help of whatever type of material is needed, with a view to its fashioning. As for theory, it can demonstrate and explain, to the extent of the penetration of reason, the things which are executed." He too lists the sciences necessary to the architect. Of this it transpires that geometry alone "offers substantial help to the architect." Arithmetics is useful only for establishing estimates and calculating expenses.
It can be said that until the 19th century, geometry had been presented as the only science which could be used by technicians, and that it really was - and above all in the domain of construction - or it was supposed that it was.
Hero of Alexandria certainly represents the culmination of the conjunction between science and techniques, which Pappus will take up a few centuries later without much innovation. It is a conjunction, not an intimate link or interpenetration: apart from cog-wheels and the reduction ratio in relation to the theory of levers which, as we shall see further on, are indisputably a first attempt at the scientific theorization of a technique, the technician only calls upon science to count and trace diagrams. We are back with Vitruvius.
G. Beaujouan, in two complementary studies, brought to light the relationship between scientific knowledge and technical practices during the Middle Ages. We went along with his examples and conclusions. Of course the Middle Ages, through various traditions which enriched them, knew ancient science including Archimedes. All those who practised this science, from the Oxfordians to the Parisian
Nominalists, also sought to transform it progressively. Thus new links were established between the two types of knowledge. They are however of the same nature. In fact, as we shall see, people are more attached to the necessary formula than to abstract demonstrations. It is


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
interesting that, continuing the idea of Hero of Alexandria, techni cians or even scientists and technicians combined try to create a practical science. Let us take a concrete example: the 13th century practical geometry in Picardy dialect conserved at the Bibliotheque
Sainte-Genevieve in Paris follows a long Latin tradition. "These practi cal geometries in the popular tongue, notes G. Beaujouan, are gene rally linked with treatises on calculation, with the heritage of the agrimensors and often to the practice of the quadrant." They simplify by providing precisely the useful formulae without burdening them selves with the detail of demonstrations.
Towards the end of the 13th century, J ordanus de N em ore's Liber de ratione ponderis, soon abridged to De ponderibus, was perhaps the bedside book of the "engineers" of the period, so much were scientific preoccupations mixed with problems of practical techniques in it. The author was a great admirer of Euclid and applies himself to the demonstration of mechanical theorems by geometric proof deriving from a few explicit postulates of a physical character. Here we find the same procedure as in the Alexandrian era. He was the first in the west to construct the theory of inclined planes and gave a formula for gravitas secundum situ, introducing the notion of momentum. Finally he applied the principle of virtual displacement to the balance of an angle lever. Going from the static to the dynamic, he enunciates, in
Book IV, a series of propositions some of which are in fact inexact, in which an incontestable interest for the work of the engineer shows through.
People of the Middle Ages hardly assimilated numerical formulae and a Euclidean type of reasoning. The geometric knowledge with which the master masons were equipped excluded both demon strations and calculation; this type of recipe comes down to construc tions of figures retained better by the eye than the mind. From this came knowledge of the golden section and the implementation of characteristic angles, for example 36°, in the construction of the decagon and pentagon. Sene has shown this using the Medieval set-squares which have been conserved: "Roman set-squares and those of the first Gothic age seem to be bevel-squares, that is, they lack the side of the hypoteneuse, which is perfectly classical, but the most ancient amongst them possess a notable peculiarity: their arms of unequal width and, which is even stranger, very often the edges are not parallel in twos: they converge and diverge, creating an internal right


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1165 angle situated on a different axis to the external." Thus we find these interesting angles: often 60° and 30°, sometimes 54° and 26°. Through the non-parallelism of their internal sides, they can also give the golden angles, that is, the angle which form with the diagonal the sides of a rectangle obeying the divine proportion.
Even though the Elements do not teach us to construct a regular pentagon from a given side (in fact the pentagon is only treated in relation to the circle of inscription) it was not very difficult to imagine a
Euclidean solution to this problem. This is what Abu'l-Wafa did soon after 930 in his book on geometric constructions necessary to artisans:
he puts forward two constructions, one very simple, the other more sophisticated but using a single opening of compasses. This solution is taken up by Roriczer in his Geometrica deutsch (1487-1488), working from two equal circles each passing through the centre of the other which gives an apprximatative solution, satisfactory for the technician.
The affair of the construction of Milan cathedral shows us the limits of this utilitarian science. We are in 1391 and it is under discussion whether the nave will be constructed ad quadratum, with equal height and width, or ad triangulum, according to an isosceles triangle. In the second case, the height of the isosceles triangle was to be calculated with a base of 16 unitates each containing 8 quantitates, or a total of 96 of the latter (application of a modular rhythm). Today we of course know the formula:
v'3 h = 96 x 2 = 83,136 Gabriel Stornalco, an "expert in the art of geometry" was called in for this calculation. The text which we only know through a modern transcription, was certainly inaccurate as G. Beaujouan has shown. In fact Stornalco used a very widespread formula which indicates that the ratio between the height and the base is approximately 13/15 or 26/30.
The formula then becomes:
h = 96 x 26 = 83 2 < 84 30 ' If we look to new horizons, writes G. Beaujouan, and leave the sites of cathedrals to study the navigation which heralded the great maritime discoveries, we find the same problems of method: the same difficulty in harmonising science and techniques, the same uncertainties of the historian faced with secrecy, the same difficulty in following oral (or craft) traditions of which only hints appear in the written documentation, finally the same efforts of a few ingenious scholars in imagining, thanks to maps and instru-


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
ments, or with the help of later manuals, the procedures which have curiously remained absent from written medieval learning.
The use of nautical maps with rhumb lines drawn according to the compass allowed 14th century seamen to resort to certain scientific methods. But nautical astronomy only seems to have been born around 1480-1490 in Portugal. It was less the precision of astronomical charts or a sudden link between astronomy and sea-faring which led to the desired result than the vast effort in organisation and scientific policy of John II. A real nautical science was then in sight. We shall return to this.
We are already in the Renaissance, at the dawn of the new science.
From this period, the reflections of modern historians on the rela tionship between science and techniques almost completely disappear.
The birth and development of this new science are followed attentively without seeking the many links between the two types of knowledge.
Apart from a few significant works, for example those on hydraulics, ballistics and thermodynamics, this history remains to be written. It would seem however that we then witness the inversion of techniques so dear to Bachelard. The technician no longer turns to science for the little knowledge, the few formulae useful to him, he creates the science he needs. The bookish scholar is replaced by the practician trying to rationalize his technical knowledge. But let us not anticipate. Contrary to what certain people have thought, when technical knowledge cannot advance without scientific aid, science must advance if it is incapable of giving the answers. The two types of knowledge are different from each other but there must be a compatibility between the two systems of knowledge. And this is of course reciprocal, as science cannot advance either unless certain technical constraints are overcome.
Having been episodic, the links now become constant and this progression proves to be continuous from the Renaissance on. Techni cal knowledge is always approximatative, to such an extent that an incorrect scientific theory can often provide useful systems of expla nation: thus for phlogistic or caloric, in every way of the same nature as the physics of Hero of Alexandria or of Strato of Lampsacƴs concer ning gases or vacuums.
From the 16th to the 18th and even in the 19th century, most scientists, the majority of them, were technicians: Benedetti, Stevin and Galileo, Roberval and Varignan, Blondel and Duhamel du
Monceau. Science became independent only gradually, in its stride,


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1167 from Tartaglia to Euler, from Vi eta to Priestley. And the proposition can be extended to the natural sciences which took longer to be constituted into systems. The observation of nature, classifications which were like tables, were as much the work of technicians as of scholars.
And the relationship between techniques and science, that is the frontiers between scientific knowledge and technical knowledge are increasingly difficult to define. The vast technical apparatus of modern science, the scientific demands of technical knowledge - chemistry perhaps represents this indivisible link for the first time - make them difficult to dissociate. The contingencies of nature, the constraints of material, the irrationalities of nature mean that the two will always be separatd by two aspects of exactitude.
Calculation Although this is undoubtedly the most elementary mathematization of techniques, it supposes a radical transition in technical reasoning. It could be said that empiricism has become absolutely impossible.
Calculation finally takes on two essential forms, the module and what we shall call the formula, for want of a more adequate term. The formula and the module constitute general rules, constantly applica ble, which allow the solution of a technical problem. They essentially concern a number of determined techniques, few to begin with.
The first step: the table Faced with a difficulty which first observation, the translation of the Greek word EµJrt:tpux, does not allow to be surmounted, we must, according to Bachelard, totally change our method and in a way invert the problems.
In an area which is often complex we should first determine what Philo of Byzantium called the primary element. This primary element must be measurable: dimensions, weight, to take the measures known for the longest time. Then, a certain number of other elements, well chosen and also perfectly measurable, must be varied in relation to this primary element. Of course a certain number of elements will always remain inaccessible because they are not measurable: for example the friction in a machine.
These are no longer simple observations isolated from each other.
Varieties are abandoned for variations. Thanks to the ordered num ber, wrote Bachelard, "material is so to speak rid of its irrational character, for it is no longer necessary to advance knowledge of it


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
beyond the limits assigned by the goal pursued." No Greek technician has given us a very detailed explanation of the way in which he proceeded. Therefore it is only possible to make suppositions about methods employed.
Ordered knowledge, series of ordered numbers can be put into a table and Pythagoras showed this with arithmetics. He did the same in order to pass from a series of sounds to a series of numbers in creating the musical scale. And of course a table of numbers has value only if these numbers have a reason to be related to each other. There is perhaps a hierarchy of these tables. The first were tables of observation which were to lead to a universally admitted formula. Tables of execution had to be drawn up for the formula created, but this was merely for facility. This is exactly the difference between navigation charts and the first range tables for fire arms towards the end of the
Middle Ages.
For the technician, the scientific explanation of what he realizes in practice is of little importance, for from that moment he can put his realization into effect. And often scientists will be able to work from these tables. An inventory of these first tables would be needed in order to follow the steps taken by our first calculators in detail.
Unfortunately, we only have them from the late 15th century and in the 16th century at a time when the "new science"' as Tartaglia would say, will make them practically useless, at least the most elementary, precisely those which preceded the formula or the module.
In any case these tables were not perfectly "regular" and these irregularities correspond to the irrationality of nature. There is resistance. There is also resistance in the application tables insofar as there is also an irrationality in the numbers. These are the penalties of approximate knowledge.
The module Its definitions are perfectly clear: the measure adopted for the regulation of the different parts of a construction, the smallest common measure which the dimensions of the different elements entering into the composition of a building must possess so that these elements can be superposed and juxtaposed without alteration. The term machine can be substituted for the term building without changing anything else.
It is difficult to know how this notion, which is not easy to conceive of, was arrived at. The Greeks called the module r6vo{;, the tone, a


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1169 term which can be connected with other techniques, particularly music. And let us remember that harmony is also a polyvalent notion.
It is possible that achitecture was the first to benefit from this new notion. Unfortunately we do not possess the treatise by Philo of
Eleusis on the proportions of temples. He must have already been the heir to a long tradition of which Vitruvius was probably the culmi nation. It is clear to see from this moment that in construction, be it architectural or mechanical, a primary and necessarily a major element was chosen according to which all other parts were deter mined based on coefficients modified by the module.
According to Vitruvius, the modular rhythm included: the propor tiones, the ratio of size linking the elements in twos; the symetriae, the ratio linking each element to the fundamental unity, the module.
In the Doric order, the entablature is of 3.5 modules (symetria) and is in a ratio of 1 to 4 to the column (proportia).
The module is the diameter of columns measured at the base of the shaft. The interval between columns naturally depends on their height.
The stability and resistance to collapse suggest the relationship (the figures are given in relation to the module):
interval between the axes 2.5 3 4 4.5 height 10 9.5 8.5 8 The wider the spacing, the less the height. The last proposition expresses the minimum height.
The same applies to the capitals. Here the dimensions are expressed as 1118 of the module:
Thus the height of the capital is a third, or six times the module.
We shall not dwell on this as the use of the module in architecture is well known and had been abundantly treated.
The second example of the module concerns the construction of ballistic engines by the Alexandrian technicians, Philo of Byzantium and Hero of Alexandri. A primordial element had to be chosen in


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
order to deduce the others. It seemed, quite rightly, to Philo of Byzantium that this element should be the ratio between the weight of the missile and the energy needed to project it. If the first term of the ratio was easily measurable, the second was not. Choosing the diameter of the bundle of elastic fibres, in reality the aperture in the framework of the machine through which this bunble of fibres passed, was an easy way out: indeed the nature of the fibres constituted a difficult factor to evaluate and in addition these fibres often lost an important amount of elasticity in the course of their use. A formula was arrived at, mathematically concretized by Diels as:
d = 1,1 Vp (the diameter in digits and the weight in drachmas). The 1.1 aims to correct all other incertitudes, natural or in calculation.
Hero of Alexandria added the module for the machine for projec ting arrows. Here it is the length of the stretcher which is taken into consideration:
Using this, the working drawings for these machines have been realized. That of the euthytone was inscribed in a square with the sides measuring 16 modules, and that of the palintone, in an isosceles triangle with 19 modules as its base and height.
