A curve shows the translation into dimensioned drawing of the tables of application of the module to different parts of the machine, as they have been given to us by Hero of Alexandria.
Going further and perhaps too far, V. Prou has sought the rela tionship between the module, which we shall call m, and the diameter of the projectile, d, the density of the projectile equalling 2. 75 or 11/4
(flint marble, an qrdinary stone). Thus the diameter of the round missile of the palintone equals 3/4 of the module. In the euthytone, the diameter taken at 1136 of the length of the stretcher equals 1/4 of the module. It could be said that the diameters of the two sorts of are complementary (they total 1) and similarly the angle of orientation of their respective wings, in relation to the angle of the pivots, has a right angle as its total.


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1171 Vitruvius provides us with other applications of the technique of the module, for example the Archemedian screw. Here the module is the length of the screw. The diameter represents the 1116 of the module, the pitch of the helix, 1/8. The diameter of the enveloping cylinder is equal to the pitch of the helix. The inclination must be 3 high to 4 at the base, which represents a Pythagoras' triangle.
Vitruvius even.indicates the application of the module in the ship:
here, it is the interval between the trenails against which the oars lean.
To momentarily talk at a tangent, we know that these steps led to a universal module to which everybody was to conform. From Pythago ras to Euclid and Ptolemy, we can see the propagation of the divine proportion and the golden number which, in their turn, will give rise to a sometimes exaggerated symbolism. If the module con tined to play an important role in architecture, and all the architectural treatises from the 16th century on bear witness to this, its use in other techniques is almost completely unknown to us. For example, it seems that Matthew
Baker, the English ship-builder of the second half of the 16th century whose papers we possess, did not use it in planning his ships.
However, it can be said that as soon as a new machine was constructed, principally an active machine and not a passive one like the loom, and the constructor was not helped in his difficult task by theory, the module and its accompanying tables were called upon.
We shall only take one example, a famous one, that of the steam engine. Unlike the explanation of its functioning, the genesis of the successive inventions which led to a successful result and the way in which they were materially conceived have been neglected. Of course, we possess some elementary tables from the mid-18th century on for
Newcomen's engines. The determination of the modules was in fact due to a Frenchman working for Russia, Sebastien Maillard, whose volume entitled Theorie des machines mues par la force de la vapeur was first published in 1783, then reprinted in the following year. In fact it contains no theory. But we can see how Maillard and his successors, slow in acquiring measuring apparatus (for heat and pressure), tried to get round obstacles of a scientific nature using the module and formulae.
The Formula Despite the ambiguity of the word here we are of course refering to a numbered formula with which a determined technical problem can be solved. Let us immediately give a concrete


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
example: the bending strength of beams, one of the architect's numerous problems.
These formulae can barely be obtained, here again, without the elaboration of tables. And once the formula is established, a second series of tables is drawn up for its application without lengthy calcu lation. Vitruvius gives us an example of this second series of tables with the formula for the narrowing of columns or the height of archtraves.
Unfortunately we do not possess these tables but in this case they can be reconstituted perfectly. For the stress resistance of beams, Vitru vius like Leonardo da Vinci had to make a series of ordered experi ments in order to establish the laws of the elastic limit of beams, norms for the stress resistance of square and cylindrical beams and those which are free-standing or fixed at one or both extremitiesand for the resisance of composite beams - and Alberti also studied this - the builder's nightmare. Leonardo da Vinci even tackled the problem of the beam fixes at one end and loaded (a beam supporting a balcony for example), the so-called Galileo's problem.
Here again, we would have liked to have a repertory of these formulae which would be interesting for more than one reason: the link between certain formulae, the approximative formulae and incor rect formulae would appear more clearly. As there is no such repretory we shall quote a few examples which will, we think, help us to better understand the role of the formula.
Vitruvius, one of the first in this area, and he could be said to be a compilator, provides us with some. Vitruvius seems to have understood the problem of the resistance of beams, but does not appear to have given a formula. This is not the case for the calculations used in architecture. Choisy has brought to light the calculations and plans implied by the Vitruvian theory of the orders in architecture.
This comes into play in the bending of stylobates and entablatures and · in the entasis of columns: unfortunately the diagrams have been lost.
These tables could provide material for curves, thus for an algebraic formula, a parabola in the first case, two parabolic arcs with a common summit in the second. Tables of corrections of perspective also exist which provide us with the fractions relative to the narrowing of columns and the height of architraves. The diagrams were then converted into formulae, first degree equations. For the height of the architrave, A being the height of the architrave and H that of the column, we have:

= 0.065
+ 3000 H
1173 And for the narrowing of columns, for a column whose height is H, the relationship between the diameters d and D, we have the following equation:
These algebraic formulae are of course interpretations: neither the Greeks nor the Romans practised algebra. It could be said concerning the narrowing of columns: for a column of known height, the rela tionship between the architrave and the column carries a certain value;
then as the column becomes taller, this relationship is proportionally increased, 2/3000 of a foot per foot. Thus here we return to an elementary arithmetical formula.
One of the oldest formulae expressed in numbers was given to us by Frontinus. It concerned the measurement of the outflow of a pipe, an essential factor in combatting fraud. Frontinus implicitly admits that this outflow is in direct proportion with the section of the flux and that the discharge of the section of n units will equal the total dicharge of n pipes whose section is one unit. This is inexact. We know that the quantity of water discharged by a totally submerged pipe is given by the formula:
Q
= µA v;GC in which A is the section of the flux, C the load and µ a variable experimental coefficient. Consequently, the section is only one of the factors determining the discharge in a given case. It has been pointed out that of the two factors µ and C, neglected by Frontinu, one, the coefficient, could only entail a relatively insignificant mistake, whereas the second, the load or pressure of water in the pipe under consider ation, was constant. Indeed, the variations of µ according to the diameter of the pipes are minimal enough for the dimensions of the pipes used and could only entail a maximum error of 13 % in excess and more often 10%.
Let us go forward to the end of the 15th century. Leonardo da Vinci had found, for square horizontal beams fixed to their extremities, that


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
resistance varies in the same way as the square of the side and inversely to the length, which was not bad. Pursuing his research, he tried to give the formula for the bending of square beams loaded in the middle.
According to him, this bending was directly proportional to the weight, but he did not go so far as to establish relationships which we can take into account today, the direct relationship with the cube of the length and inversely proportional to the fourth power of the side of the square. He eliminated the elasticity module and the moment of inertia to which, however, de Nemore had alluded.
We can see Leonardo da Vinci making series of ordered experi ments to compare the resistance of prismatic or cylindrical beams, investigate the effects of the stress exerted by bending and tension, and these investigations were to be taken up again later by Hooke. His reasoning which we can see perfectly in such studies is incontestably intuitive to begin with and then he proceeds by analogy and approxi mation. He also lacks certain elements of the problem and certain types of development.
The role of the formula, applicable but indemonstrable, was con siderable. It is still used today, but perhaps in certain cases arises from scientific demonstration for which the technician has no time. We shall return to this. The formula is undoubtedly more refined than the module because it is more directly assimilable and of easier use. It is in fact a recipe transposed into mathematical language.
A-Posteriori Theorization Of course we should first define what theory is in technical matters. It is less simple than might be believed at first view. It is certainly the scientific explanation of a technical process. In order to do this, a number of conditions must be filled. First we must have an adaquate science at our disposal, that goes without saying: ballistics cannot exist without dynamics. On the other hand the various elements of which the technical activity concerned is composed must be capable of being submitted to scientific treatment. Thus we can oppose a bladed turbine wheel to clog making. We can already see zones being constituted, some chronological, others sectorial.
Why theorize? It is difficult to answer this question. Indeed there are various attitudes. There are technicians who give rise to the remark that this theorization in general intervenes when the technique concer-


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1175 ned has in a way reached perfection. In this case it would be for fun, a scientist'scuriosity without practical use. Let us quote Fran(5ois Blon de!, one of the creators of modern ballistics:
( . . . ) For most of those who professionally carry arms, even officers and particularly those who have not been cultivated in their youth by the study of the arts, boldly say that war is a purely practical skill which cannot be learnt in books or by rules and those who only know its theory are prevented from taking part in the action; they say that this equipment of mathematical study often only makes a man presumptuous.
This stance is extremely frequent even in our time. In a different order of ideas, we have recently seen a strong trend against the mathematization of political economics.
It is quite useless, Blonde) continues, to want them [the artillerymen] to weigh themselves down with this quantity of precepts and mathematical operations which are so difficult to understand and put into practice; because through good sense and practice alone they punctually execute what might be proposed to them, without being subject to rules of measurement or calculation.
Other things show that theory has always contributed to techniques in the way of perfection and precision. It could be said that theory's function is to reduce the margins which always exist in approximatative knowledge. For Blonde!, who was both a practician and a theoretician, his own position was necessarily the right one. A theoretician without practical knowledge is as valueless as a practician without theory.
This indisputably brings us back to the difference between theory and practice in a single subject. J.P. Vernant, in a very interesting article on Greek technical thought and interpreting texts which are often difficult to grasp , wishing to make a clear distinction between science and techniques, says that theory is "degraded" when it is applied, in the sense that the rationality of science cannot be exactly applied to the irrationality of nature. In a theory there are always parameters which cannot be taken into consideration because they are not measurable and often not conceptualized. This is why there are different degrees of theorization. Theory is a scientific projection of a technical phenomenon and will always be a partial projection as a part of concrete reality will always evade it. There is no degradation, just an absence of perfect correlation. It is in this flaw that attitudes, diffi culties and misunderstandings have been engulfed.
Almost an entire history has still to be written on this subject, a history which has as yet touched on only a few privileged sectors. This


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
history will be difficult to write because the texts are not easily assembled and are sometimes impossible to interpret. It is also difficult to know how to approach it: whether to study each sector, each period, limit it to a few notable personalities who, in a given period, if they have not made a systematic effort, have at least allowed their curiosity to wander over a large number of techniques. The lack of monographs, and it is incontestably with them that we must start, leaves us almost powerless.
The Greeks of the Hellenistic period only had, in truth, an embryo nic science at their disposal. They had received a system of "simple machines" from their predecessors. It is in fact with Archimedes that we find a first attempt at theorization, on the lever. After this, the entire effort of theoreticians was devoted to the reduction of all other simple machines to the lever, with mixed success. This was the case for cog-wheels and, by extension, the reduction ratio, from Hero to
Pappus. The division of force is then made possible thanks to the application of the theory of levers.
It is obvious that in this period it was hardly possible to go further.
As we have seen, only geometric artifices were added to solve such and such a problem. But his was a means of construction, not a theory. In fact it is not until "modern" science was unleashed in the Renaissance that we find a more successful effort. Let us make ourselves clear. The situation could not be reversed from one day to the next. Occupied as scientists were with the creation of this "new science" even if they were also technicians, the birth of the scientific system as a whole was indispensable if techniques were to hope to profit from it. The history of science has been much treated, unlike the history of what we call the
"applied sciences." Let us take two examples, one individual, the other serial. In our opinion, the efforts of Leonardo da Vinci to create rational tech niques, that is with a scientific base, have not been sufficiently stressed.
In the many notes he left us which are too partially grouped around a few major themes we feel a passionate desire to give the techniques which interested him a scientific aid which the science of his time could not yet contribute. He is not on the right path, but he guesses at it, even better, he glimpses it. He certainly has the notion of rational tech niques, but he feels incapable of formulating it. He is still in the stage of
"half-formed doctrines" of which we have just spoken. The serial problem, which is a monograph, has brought us many elements: the


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1177 history of ballistics proves this. Let us take ,in parallel, resistance to breaking or the simple bending of a beam. The research is of the same order. The Ancients, as Vitruvius attests, necessarily studied this.
Then we must go forward to Alberti and Leonardo da Vinci to understand that the solution was not possible, there was no possible scientific solution. Thus they were restricted to formulae. But the propblem is taken up again by Galileo in his second dialogue, which is only an approach, then by Hooke, neglected by modern historians,
Belidor, as a constructor rather than a scientist and finally and definitively by Coulomb and Navier, the latter also unjustly neglected.
If we admit, justly it would seem, that Vitruvius is only a reflection of previous research, it means that it took twenty one centuries to arrive at theory, that is the general solution to the problem.
What is asked of theory, from Hellenistic Antiquity to the Renais sance, is precisely that it should give formal answers applicable in all cases to the problems of the technician. We have not yet a perfect notion of the gap which seperates theory from practice, a gap and not degradation.
There is no better example than ballistics, undoubtedly because of its brilliant treatment of almost fifty years ago. And we shall not stress its history too much, as it is now very well known. In reality, this work presents us with original texts, and this is already an immense service, rather than explaining a reasoning which is difficult to show. In fact, the problem lies on two planes: on the one hand the concepts of force and inertia, and on the other, ballistic curves, of course without counting the oppositions between science and practice. The ruptures are interesting and instructive. And we can see, at the beginning of this new science, the constant passage from the concept to the table and inversely, from the results of each to a rational system.
Let us put impetus to one side, which could give nothing, with Buridan, Albert of Saxony, Nicole Oresme and Leonardo da Vinci, who is a perfect example, with the addition of the curve. But may we here ask a question? When the artillerymen of Charles VIII of France fired on the beaches near Naples across canvasses hung at regular intervals forming a first curve, was not the problem provisionally and partially solved? And the "experiment" will be repeated. Tartaglia deals clumsily with the problem of maximum range. The use of the right angle, the angle of 45° and a curve in three parts, one of which is a vertical fall, gravity acting on the whole trajectory, all, or almost all of


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
this comes from the theory of impetus. For Rivault de Flurens, the range increased in the same way as the cosine of the weapon's angle of elevation: therefore there was no maximum range.
It is with Galileo that we enter modern mechanics and he gives us a series of propositions concerning ballistics which marks the real beginning of the science. The half-parabola for the curve and the composition of movements put him on the right track. Torricelli and
Mersenne lead to FramƵois Blondel who writes a geometric theory of the parabolic trajectory and instruments for plotting: the force is lost with the resistance of the air and the movements composed are altered.
Maupertuis adds arithmetic ballistics, following the work of Newton, Huygens, Varignon and J. Bernouilli. With Robins, Euler and d' Alembert ballistics, as the friar Marsenne had christened it, really entered its scientific phase.
A complete history should be written of this science created to give a scientific explanation to techniques. It could feature, apart from the major names of modern physics and mechanics, characters like Simon
Stevin who went from business and finance to mathematics and then, made responsible for the problem of water and questions of fortifi cation, gave himself up to applied science. The great Euler has also been neglected apart from a few centenary studies. And yet it is to him that we owe important works on the theory of the ship, the archime dian screw, ballistics, impulse and reaction turbines and windmills.
Certain problems should be followed through as they have been for distillation and thermodynamics, from Watt to Clausius. We see scientists suddenly turning to certain questions in the 18th century when, thanks to the Academies? problems held the attention of all scientists at the same time, for example the case of the archimedian screw. Daniel Bernouilli's hydrodynamics, Pitot's dissertation adres sed to the Academie des Sciences in Paris in 1736, Euler's dissertation adressed to the Berlin Academy in 1754, the work of Belgrade in 1767 and finally that of Paucton in 1768 allow us to follow the slow elaboration of a general theory which is undoubtedly more of scientific interest than of technical scope.
The history of the theory of machines would also provide a fruitful subject to work on. It is not a question, as in other cases, of "scientific curiosities"' but a real elaboration of advanced technology. It started on individual machines: machines for raising water, water and windmills and what were called forces of movement: the evolution can


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1179 be clearly seen from the work of S. de Caus, Les raisons des forces mouvantes (1615) to the Traite des forces mouvantes (1722) by J.-F. de
Camus . And very quickly from the mid-18th century we pass on to the general principles of machines: La mechanique generate (1741) by
Abbe Deidier precedes the Essai sur /es machines en general (1786) of Lazare Carnot.
From the beginning of the 19th century, the theory of machines is practically constituted: Lanz and Betancourt in 1802, Hachette in 1811 and Borgnis in 1819 prepare the ground for Reuleaux's La cinematique
(kinematics).
So we can see that apart from a few brilliant exceptions, most cases are wishful. Only when we have more studies and works at our disposal shall we be able to take up the problem of technical knowledge again.
We always come up against vague terms: science, applied science, technology, techniques, the frontiers between them are ambiguous. It is not easy to distinguish where technical knowledge starts. It is clearly separate from scientific knowledge in that it takes possession of results without being concerned how they were obtained. Artillery men had experimental range tables from the end of the 15th century. Ballistics has permitted explanation and the drawing up of tables which are perhaps more exact. Technical knowledge lies in these tables, not in the scientific reasoning which allowed them to be established.
There is also the problem of the margins which separate, and will always separate, scientific knowledge from technical reality, sometimes called safety margins, appreciation margins or other analo gous terms. Rigourous reasoning and approximatative knowledge are the two essential modalities.
A-Priori Theory We shall be brief on this last point. indeed this case is hardly encoun tered before the periods closest to us. It nevertheless poses numerous important problems. Two concrete examples will help us to become aware of these difficulties.
The first is that of chemistry. There is no doubt that a chemical industry existed and that chemistry was used even before Lavoisier and
Priestley had founded modern chemistry. Saltpetre, spirit of salt and fatty acids were already the object of some industries, sometimes advanced. Here technical knowledge is far removed from scientific


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
knowledge. If things came out right, it was often through unreasoned intuition. The article STEEL in Diderot's Encyclopedie proves this abundantly. Old opinions are ridiculed: steel is a purer iron than ordinary iron, a metal which is full of metal parts which constitute its being within the same volume. And the author of the article arrives at a formula which we shall not repudiate: "Steel is a medium state between cast iron and wrought iron." On the other hand, we know that the industries which called upon this elementary chemistry were necessarily blocked at a certain level.
When a valid chemical system had been elaborated, the industry could take possession of it and in fact the industrial system "sticks" very closely to the scientific system. Science has defined the broad bases of the chemical industry, hydrochloric acid and sulphuric acid.
But the heritage was not immediate. The passage from the scientific system to the industrial system is subject to a certain number of contingencies of a material order which mean that technical knowl edge, here again, is not exactly scientific knowledge. Between Glau ber's empiricism and the realization of the Leblanc process there is both the discovery of modern chemistry and the technical knowledge which can apply it to a manufacture which, along with the steam engine, is considered to be one of the greatest inventions of the industrial revolution. It constitutes the difference between chemical science and "chemical engineering."
The conjunction is necessary and science precedes techniques.
There is no phenomenon more important to our discussion than the constitution of factory laboratories and the first of these were chemical laboratories. When Watt and Boulton worked together, they were two technicians combining their knowledge. When Saint-Gobain called upon Gay-Lussac, the famous Gay-Lussac tower was the fruit of the combined work of of a chemist and an engineer. Kuhlman, in the north of France, was a chemist who became a technician. It was in 1867 that the French metallurgical firm, Holtzer, set up the first real factory laboratory with a chemist, Boussingault, and an engineer, Brustlein, designed to perfect special types of steel. From then on, the major chemical or metallurgical companies had their own laboratories where scientists, scholars and technicians worked together. Here the fron tiers between scientific knowledge and technical knowledge disappear.
This history of this remains to be written.


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1181 We shall only evoke the problem of atomic energy. It can easily be understood that the atomic industry could not have been born without the corresponding theory. But it experienced the same discrepancies, the same complementary systems of knowledge which allowed the passage from laboratory to factory.
A Confused Situation Stitch by stitch, or nail by nail, to use technical imagery, the situation of technical knowledge continues to become increasingly complicated, and even this is an understatement.
It can be thought of as a parabola opening up. On the one hand we have techniques which could be called elementary, those where ges ture and the spoken word remain essential. It is true that they are tending to diminish and disappear. But they can be found in a few modern crafts which are however different both in their better adapted tools and increasingly perfected and useful products. Gesture and the spoken word are only accessories here: using given tools which have been perfected, an apparatus which has already been provided must be assembled. This can be clearly seen in the fact that today there are many crafts concerning installation and repair.
And there is a huge gap between this elementary stage and the following one, where the tools and the objects to be installed are elaborated. On the one hand there is the plumber and the garage mechanic, and on the other, the mixer tap, carburettor and system of tools. Here we have entered a vast complex where both technical knowledge and scientific demands come into play on different levels depending on the case, and also scientific knowledge and technical demands. Everything is mixed together and the relationships are difficult to distinguish. However we can sense that the relationships are multiple: the materialof which the tool is made, the calculated forms of the object to be produced and the general conception of the technical operation seem to give priority to the scientific aspect and this is uncontested, be it at the price of a certain presumption which Blonde) mentioned.
This path has been pursued very far. From then on there could be no techniques without science. Everything was mixed together with questions of financial interest because economic activites were concer-


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
ned. The scientific schema still remains different to the technical schema despite their close relationship. Unlike the scientific formula, the technical formula can be patented. The patent, description and if needed, illustration, is the basis of a type of technical knowledge. In any case it is the patent which largely transmits innovations. Thus it is in patents that we should seek out the frontiers between scientific knowledge and technical knowledge.
There is no isolated scientific knowledge because there is a scientific system: the classifications of sciences brought this to light. The case is of course the same for techniques: there are technical systems where everything is linked and technical knowledge is necessarily composite;
But there is one essential difference between the two types of knowl edge. Scientific knowledge is formal, whereas technical knowledge is often aleatory. The first difficulty concerns what used to be called perfecting and today is called development. In order to reach maturity, technical knowledge must be accompanied in its total environment by handiwork, tricks of the trade, rectifications: it would be interesting, in fact, to look at the detail of this process which can be long. Let us not take the delays between the "idea" and its "realization" into consider ation. They are often the result of completely incorrect assumptions, particularly about the notion of the idea. The processes which led to the Bessemer converter have been more or less defined although its history, unlike that of the steam engine, remains to be written: indeed in his autobiographical memoirs the inventor completely distorts reality. Thus we see Bessemer, self-taught and not a practician, working on previous patents and assimilating into his own principle a practice which was already well known, the refining of cast iron. His principal effort was directed towards the retort and blasting apparatus.
The English patent is dated 12th February 1856. But it took another six years before the first industrial casting could be effected, with contri butions from France and Sweden. And the definitive Bessemer process was formed only gradually until the Thomas process of 1879-1881. The detail of this story and in particular the complementary patents ought to be known.
The increasing complexity of modern techniques has led to new notions which singularly draw a haze over the concept of technical knowledge. We have already alluded to this with reference to patent rights. Let us take up a quotation from 1961: "Industrial know-how is understood to mean the applied knowledge - methods and data -


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1183 which is necessary for the effective utilisation and the putting into practice of industrial techniques." Thus technical knowledge is no longer enough, it must be accompanied by know-how: technical skills transmissable through the agency of a personnel. Let us also quote the terms of a decision of the court of Douai in May 1970: "All elements of a scientific or technical order which accompany the acquisition or transfer of patent rights, technical sudies, experiments and research, information of a scientific or technical order." Now things have become clearer. A technical process can be clearly defined: this was the role of the patent. But it might not be applicable, for all that, at least not in a short space of time. What counts then is the way in which the idea was born and all the stages of development, in short, the history of the invention. Then there is the history of its use, difficulties or incidents which may have arisen, in short, the practice of the procedure. And we begin to consider that these various elements are as important as the knowledge itself. And the wealth of the archives of industrial firms in this respect can be imagined.
Thus "know-how" was necessarily followed by "show-how"' that is the personal agency of which we have spoken and which in fact concerns compliments of knowledge some of which are difficult to put into writing - here gesture and the spoken word often won back a certain right -, but also the training of the personnel who will be in charge of the procedure and in some cases the first contracts of sale.
It is remarkable to note that Colbert, importing new techniques into France, was perfectly aware of all these problems: the contracts signed in this period with foreign entrepreneurs carried clauses identical in spirit to modern contracts of transfer of technology.
What will become of technical knowledge now? It seems to be integrated into an ensemble which contains a little of everything.
There is a singular lack of studies on this subject.
We shall conclude quite naturally with a question which has been thoroughly studied: how technical knowledge is transmitted, how technical education developed. We shall only touch lightly on this subject which has been much discussed. The evolution of the forms of technical knowledge has necessarily conditioned its content: here we find the same mechanism as for technical literature.
The first idea is founded on knowledge through gesture and the spoken word. Apprenticeship precisely excludes technical literature.
"It is a craft, declared Blondel to his artillerymen, which cannot be


TECHNIQUES AND SCIENCES
learnt in books or by rules." D'Alembert says: "it is not by consulting books that people learn their trade." Gargantua was also taken by his tutor into workshops in order to initiate him to technical life. Similarly,
Colbert, introducing foreign techniques into France, summoned quali fied workers who are to teach the crafts to the natives. It was the
Malthusianism of the guilds and their demise that led to the disappea rance of apprenticeships of which the vocational schools were to be born where, in fact, a colletive and multiple apprenticeship took place.
The first schools were undoubtedly born out of a charitable intention, as apprenticeship was not free for poor children. And we see, at the end of the 19th century, in a vocational school like the Ecole Diderot in
Paris, opposition against the development of theory and a defence of gesture and the spoken word.
In the domain of description and illustration it seemed inevitable that the school would become useless. On the one hand the model was a way of teaching by demonstration which could have been greatly extended but it came too late. We can see this already in a project of
Descartes, developed in 1648, which aimed to institute vocational schools "for perfecting the arts." "Have built at the royal school and in other places devoted to the general public various halls for artisans;
( ... ) adjoin to each hall a cabinet filled with all the mechanical instruments necessary or useful to the arts; ( ... ) create sufficient funds not only to pay the expenses incurred for the experiments, but also to pay the keep of the Masters and Teachers." We know in fact that one of the rare establishments corresponding to this form of education was the Conservatoire des Arts et Metiers, founded under the Revolution.
The third form of education concerns techniques which are closely related to sciences. It is symptomatic to note which techniques are involved. Let us put to one side the engineering school of the university of Leyde which, in 1600, entrusted Stevin with a mathematics course and also the Descartes's project of 1648 which demanded "masters skilled in mathematics and physics for answering the questions of the artisans, showing the reasons for all things and inciting them to make new discoveries in the Arts."
France, through the notion it created of schools to transmit a technical knowledge already impregnated with science, is a very good example after the German cameralists. The first schools were those of hydrography, for teaching the rules of maritime navigation from 1682 with a clearly defined political goal. Then towards the mid-18th


ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
1185 century each scientific technique had its school: the army with the artillery schools, the Ecole du Genie at Mezieres (1748), the Ecole
Militaire (1751). But we see appearing at the same moment the Ecole des Pants et Chaussees (1747), the Ecole des Constructeurs des Navires
(1763), the Ecole des Mines (1783). We can see quite clearly that from then on the promoters of this type of education are convinced that science is a fundamental element of technical knowledge. Thanks to the Convention, this achievement will be crowned by the creation of the Ecole Polytechnique, destined to give state technicians the scienti fic training, elementary in this period, indispensible to technical practice. The evolution of technical knowledge in certain domains is put across by the teaching of these techniques. It would be extremely interesting to know exactly what was really taught in these schools. Of course the technical treatises written by most of their teachers give us a good idea of the forms of technical knowledge which could be acquired there but we lack certain significant details.
As the reader will have gathered, this is only an essay. We hope that those interested will undertake new research rather than merely revising what already exists. There have been some isolated attempts to do this, but often without sufficient means to do it properly.
Techniques form an integral part of the history of ideas: they have been far too long neglected.



BIBLIOGRAPHY
PROLEGOMENA TO A BIBLIOGRAPHY
General Bibliography The reader should refer to this part of the bibliography for all or several of the periods discussed. We have divided it into several logical groups in order to facilitate consultation.
GENERAL REFERENCE
The following are very useful, particularly as they are selective and include assessments of the works cited. The only drawback is that these works quickly become out-dated.
E. S. FERGUSON, Bibliography of the History of Technology , Cambridge (Mass.), M.l.T. , 1968. This is the bibliography of the last few decades.
F. RUSSO, Elements de bibliographie de l'histoire des sciences et des techniques, 2nd edition, Paris, 1969. The subjects are divided into periods and large sections.
G. SARTON, Introduction to the History of Science, Baltimore, 1927-1948, 3 books in 5 volumes. This momumental work also covers technical authors, and traces the period right from the beginning to 1400 AD.
Current bibliographies of the major journals of history of science and technology should also be consulted, as well as those of general historical journals.
JO URNALS
Once again, we must specify the works to be consulted, as there are various aspects to the problem of journals.
Introduced at the beginning of the 19th century, specifically technical jour nals became an important source of material for the history of technology. It would be interesting to make a good critical list as we have already stated above.


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
General history journals occasionally deal with the problem of the history of techniques. This is also true of two other groups: j ournals on economic history, on the one hand, and journals on the history of science , on the other. The latter have been listed in the bibliography by Russo .
We have listed here only those j ournals which deal exclusively with the history of techniques.
Germany: Archiv fur Geschichte der Naturwissenschaften und der Technik, Leipzig, 1908-1931 . This j ournal was recently resumed by
Zeitschrift fur Geschichte der Naturwissenschaften, Technik und Medizin, Leipzig, from 1960.
Beitriige zur Geschichte der Technik und der Industrie, published in Berlin by Matschoss , from 1909 to 1928 , resumed by the following review.
Austria: Blatter fur Technikgeschichte, Vienna, from 1932.
France: Thales from 1934, an organ of the Institut d'histoire des sciences et des techniques of the university of Paris.
Technique et Civilisation, a j ournal published at Saint-Germain-en-Laye between 1950 and 1956.
Documents pour l'Histoire des Techniques, prepared by the Centre de documentation d'histoire des techniques du Conservatoire national des arts et metiers. Two issues published; from the 3rd onwards, forms one of the annual publications of the Revue d'Histoire des Sciences.
Revue de l'Histoire de la Siderurgie, Nancy, from 1960, became in 1969, Revue d'Histoire des Mines et de la Metallurgie.
United States of America: Contributions from the Museum of History and Technology, Smithsonian Institute, Washington , from 1945 . This consists of monographs on techniques or inventions, many of which are invaluable material.
Technology and Culture, published since 1959, an organ of the Society of the history of technology.
Great Britain: Transactions of the Newcomen Society , London, since 1922, the oldest periodical which specializes in the history of techniques. Excellent bibliographies are regularly published.
Hungary: Technikatortenet Szemle, published in Budapest from 1963.
Italy: Le Macchine, Milan, from 1968. An organ of the National Museum of the History of Science and Technology.
Czechoslovakia: Agricultura, published by the museum of Nitra, since 1962.
Sbornik Narodniho Tecknikeho Museo , published by the Museum of tech niques of Prague , from 1955 .
GENERAL HISTORY
Many works trace the general evolution of techniques from its origins to present day. We will merely list the most worthwhile reference works.


BIBLIOGRAPHY
1189 C. SINGER, E.J. HOLMYARD , A.R. HALL and collaborators, A History of Technology , Oxford, 5 volumes, 1954-1958. Within some of the large chronological periods, the subject matter is divided into technical sectors. The bibliographies are fairly comprehensive. This monumental work has been at the origin of the following summary:
T.K. DERRY and T.I. WILLIAMS, A Short History of Technology from the earliest Times to 1900, New York-Oxford, 196 1 .

Some works are more cursory but even so often provide interesting ideas.

J. NEF, The Conquest of the material World, Chicago , 1964. This is in fact a collection of studies on techniques from the Middle Ages to the end of the 18th century.
Important works on wider periods must also be included in this category:
F.M. FELDHAUS , Die Technik der Vorzeit, last edition, Munich, 1965 .
This work, presented in dictionary form, covers the period from Classical Antiquity to 1800.
A. UCELLI, Storia della tecnica dal medioevo ai giorni, Milan, 1943 .
More rare are works which concern the history of techniques in a particular country. Among the most interesting are the following works:
U. FORTI, Storia della tecnica italiana, Florence, 1940. This is fairly brief.
J. NEEDHAM and collaborators , Science and Civilization in China, Cam bridge , 7 volumes, 1954-1971. This is a brilliant work which covers many aspects of the Chinese civilization and in which techniques occupy a very important place .

JI. - Heavy Industry Certain aspects of this have been dealt with more thoroughly , but there is still much to be done. Refer first to a very general work :
T. RICKARD , A history of mining in relation to the development of civilization , London, 1932, in 2 volumes.
The iron industry is undoubtedly the best well covered subject. Three works may be pointed out, one of which is an old work but nevertheless important because of its mass of information , the second is strictly on technology, and the third is more recent and fairly general.

121-226.
Unfortunately other industries are only covered by frequently old and summary monographs, among which are the following:
A. BLANCHET, Essai sur l'histoire du papier, Paris , 1900.


BIBLIOGRAPHY
1191 W. ENDREI , L'Evolution des techniques du filage et du tissage du Mayen Age a la Revolution industrielle, Paris, 1968. This work contributes some very important elements to the history of textile techniques.
R.J. FORBES , Short History of the Art of Distillation , Leyde , 1948. A work which seems equally definitive.
C. PAGE, La Coutellerie depuis l'origine jusqu'a nos jours, Chatellerault, 1896-1904, 6 volumes.
III. - Tools and Mechanisation The important history of the tool still remains to be completed. Some attempts, however, have demonstrated the interest of such a history. We will point out nevertheless:
C. FREMONT, various monographs on tools and certain mechanisms, published by the Societed'Encouragement. They are extremely important written works.
P. FELLER and F. TOURRET, L'Outil, Brussels, 1970. A work intended for a large public, with remarkable illustrations, which contains both inter esting ideas and concepts.
V. G. CHILDE , The Story of Tools, London , 1944.
Occasionally, but all too rarely, studies concentrate on one particular subject:
W.L. GOODMAN , The History of Wood-Working Tools, London, 1964.
Mechanisation has for some time increasingly attracted the attention of researchers. Thus works which are not perfect but nevertheless very useful are avalaible on this subject.
T. BECK, Beitrage zur Geschichte des Maschinenbaues, 2nd edition, Berlin, 1899. This work, a true pioneering book in this field, deals more particularly with authors who wrote on machines , rather than on the results which were obtained.
A.P. USHER, History of Mechanical Inventions, 2nd edition, Harvard, 1954. This work created a great stir. It takes its sources both from the authors of technical manuals and from industrial reality. Usher tries to justify a thesis which is not totally convincing, but it raises vital problems.
In a more specific field:
L.T.C. ROLT, A Short History of Machine Tools , Cambridge (Mass . ) , M.l.T. , 1965 .
In still more specialized fields, there are numerous works which may be useful to the historian of techniques: they are more or less worthwhile . We will just mention two :
A. CHAPUIS and E. GELIS, Automata. A Historical and technological study , London, 1958. Essential for the genisis of certain mechanisms.
T.P. CUSS , The Story of Watches , London , 1952.


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
The reader will easily understand that it is impossible to give an extensive bibliography at this point, as this would fill volumes. Moreover, the research centres and general bibliographies which have been mentioned are able to provide far more comprehensive information. We have merely tried to provide some very broad directions. Similarly, we wished to indicate the measure and significance of the deficiency of some material in the history of techniques: we must repeat that only a collective and international effort could contribute elements to our knowledge of technical progress which the subject still lacks today.

THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
If one excludes the publication of certain texts, very little has been written on Greek techniques. Nearly all the works cover what we vaguely call Antiquity, which can mean Mesopotamia, or Egypt, or Greece and Rome. These have already been cited in most bibliographies, so we shall include here only recent or complementary works.
It is worth noting a few works on Greek science:
B. FARRINGTON, Greek Science, 2 vols. , London, 1944-9.
A. REY, La jeunesse de la science grecque, Paris, 1933.
A. REY, La Maturite de la pensee scientifique en Grece, Paris, 1939.
A. REY, L'Apogee de la science technique grecque, 2 vol. , Paris, 1946-1948.
L. ROBIN, La Pensee grecque et Les origines de I' esprit scientifique, Paris, 1923.
The following work is quite old but some of the texts included are useful:
A. de ROCHAS, La Science des philosophes et /'art des thaumaturges dans l'Antiquite, Paris, 1912.
This anthology of articles dealing with the relationships between techniques and science is worth reading:
JEAN-PIERRE VERNANT, Mythe et pensee che.z les Grecs, Paris, 1971 .
One of the main problems was bringing to light and editing all the many texts which constitute the most important source of the history of techniques:
J. BEAUJEU, "La Litterature technique des Grecs et des Romains," in Actes du Congres G. Bude, pp. 21-88, 1948.
Some works are still fundamentally important, despite their age:
Hugo BLUMNER, Technologie und Terminologie der Gewerbe und der Kunste bei den Griechen und den Romerne, 4 vols., Leipzig, 1975-1886.
K. MICHALOWSKI, Technika Grecka, (in Polish), Varsovia, 1959.
A. NEUBURGER, Die Technik des Altertums, Leipzig, 1921.

THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
As yet, there has been very little research done into Medieval techniques; this is mainly because the primary sources usually require specialist knowledge.


BIBLIOGRAPHY
1205 Apart from a few essays, there is only one work on this subject, which, despite its title, does actually contain specialist studies.
Marc BLOCH, "Les Inventions medievales," in Anna/es d'histoire econo mique et sociale 1935, Vol VII, pp. 634-643.
Bertrand GILLE, Esprit et civilisation technique du Mayen Age, Paris, 1952.
Bertrand GILLE, Les Developpements technologiques en Europe de 110 a 1400, in "Cahiers d'histoire mondiale" 1958, Vol III, pp 3-108.
Lynn THORNDIKE, "Technology and Invention in the Middle Ages," in Speculum, 1940, Vol XV, pp 141-159.
L. WHITE, Medieval Technology and Social Change, with comprehensive bibliographies, London, 1964.
Most of the works dealing with the problem of energy are mainly concerned with water mills:
A.M. BAUTIER, "Les plus anciennes mentions de moulins hydrauliques et de moulins a vent," in Bulletin de philologie et d'histoire, 1960.
Marc BLOCH, "Avenement et conquete du moulin a eau," in Anna/es d'histoire economique et sociale, 1935, Vol VII, pp. 538-563.
E.H. CARUS-WILSON, "An Industrial Revolution of the Thirteenth Century," in The Economic History Review, 1941 , Vol XI, pp. 39-60; this deals mainly with the appearance and expansion of the fulling mill.
Bertrand GILLE, "Le Moulin a eau, une revolution technique medievale," in Techniques et civilisations, 1954, Vol III, pp. 1-15.
C. PARAIN, "Rapports de production et developpement des forces pro ductives: l'exemple du moulin a eau," in La Pensee, February 1965.
R. TITLEY, "Note on old Windmills," in Transactions of the Newcomen Society, 1930, Vol XI, pp. 1-14.
The problem of harnessing was the subject of a major work:
Richard LEFEBVRE DES NOETTES, L'Attelage et le cheval de selle a travers /es ages, Paris, 1931. The second volume is an anthology of pictures.
Lefebvre des Noettes' thesis has been corrected on a number of points: in particular see J. Sion's note in the above-mentioned volume of Anna/es d'histoire economique et sociale and Haudricourt's research in the first and only volume of the Revue de Geographie humaine et d'Ethnographie.
Most of the history of the techniques of naval construction still remains to be done. A few works in particular are encouraging for their stimulus to this important area of research:
A.W. BROGGER and H. SHETELIG. The Vikings' Ship, Oslo, 1951.
P. HEINSIUS, Das Schiff der Hansischen Fruhzeit, Weimar, 1956.
The history of agricultural techniques has not attracted much interest, except for a few detailed points. This leaves large parts of this particular area untouched; it would be an extremely useful subject of research.
G. DUBY, "La Revolution agricole medievale," in Revue de geographie de Lyon, 1954 pp. 361-366.

The same things could be said about industry, or industries. Some inter esting works have been written about a few of them - these are often rich in information but restrained in their approach. ENDREI's work on the textile industry has already been mentioned in the general bibliography; as well as this there is the valuable study by:
Guy De POERCK, La Draperie medievale en France et en Artois, Bruges, 1951. This is one of the best works of its kind.
The iron and steel industry in Poland, Hungary and Czechoslovakia has been dealt with in several articles published in the Revue d'histoire de la siderurgie. Another important article should be added to these:
SPRANDEL, "La Production du fer au Moyen Age," in Annales d'histoire economique et sociale, 1969, pp. 31 1-312.
Also worth consulting:
Max PRINET, L'lndustrie du sel en Franche-Comte avant la conquete franr;aise, Paris, 1900.
Important research has been done on military techniques:
Charles CHADWICK, A History of the Art of War in the Middle Ages, London, 1929, 2nd edition.
F. LOT, L'Art militaire et les armees au Mayen Age en Europe et dans le Proche-Orient, 2 volumes, Paris, 1945-1947.
And on a particular point:
Maurice MERCIER, Le Feu gregeois, Paris, 1952.
On the subject of techniques concerning construction, this interesting anthology of pictorial documents is worth noting:
F. VAN TYGHEM, Op en Om de middelleeuwen Bouwerf, Brussels, 1966, two volumes.
Hardly anything has been written about the great public works executed during this period. One book is worth noting:
H. CLOUZOT, Les Marais de la Sevre niortaise et du Lay du xe a la fin du XVI' siecle, Paris, 1904.

CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
The history of the classical period has been badly or incompletely studied.
Although there has been some very thorough research in some sectors, there are others which have remained in the dark.
A few works have attempted to present the problem in its entirety:
A. MIELZI, la Eclosion del Rinacimento, Madrid, 1951.

THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
The bibliography for the industrial revolution is abundant but very unequal.
Obviously, a complete list is not possible. We will therefore limit ourselves to the most recent works. Most of these often include critical bibliographies.
Note finally that a considerable number of them go further than the period which has just been discussed and do not establish any kind of break between the first and the second industrial revolution: the dates between the two vary, a fact which illustrates that there is some confusion.
After the outline given by Toynbee, the notion of an industrial revolution has been proved in an old but nevertheless interesting work:


BIBLIOGRAPHY
1211 P. MANTOUX, The Industrial Revolution in the 18th century, London, 1961 , rpt. Chicago, 1983.
Since then, certain facts have prompted debate, and new global interpretations have been presented. Among other works note the following:
T.S. ASHTON, The Industrial Revolution, London, 1948.
P. DEAN, The First Industrial Revolution, Cambridge, 1967.
H.W. FLINN, Origins of the Industrial Revolution, London, 1966.
C.FOHLEN, Qu' est-ce que la revolution industrielle?, Paris, 1971.
D.S. LANDES, The unbound Prometheus. Technological Change and Industrial Development in Western Europe from 1750 to the Present, Cam bridge, 1969. Taken from his participation in HABBAKUK and POSTAN,
"The Industrial Revolution and after," in Cambridge Economic History, volume VI, book 2, Cambridge, 1965, with a very large bibliography.
J.-P. RIOUX, La Revolution industrielle, 1750-1880, Paris, 1971.
Agricultural problems are still integrated within what is limitedly termed by some as the "industrial" revolution. Nevertheless, some research has been undertaken in this direction:
P. BAIROCH, Revolution industrielle et sous-developpement, Paris, 1963.
P. BAIROCH, "Agriculture and the Industrial Revolution (1700-1914)," in The Fontana Economic History of Europe, book III, Number 8, London,
1969.
A.J. BOURDE, The Influence of England on the French Agronomes (1750-1789) , Cambridge, 1953.
A.J. BOURDE, Agronomie et Agronomes en France au XVIIJeme siecle, Paris, 1967.
F. CROUZET, "Agriculture et revolution industrielle," in Cahiers d'his toire, 1967, pages 67-86.
The industrial revolution has often been approached from a national angle, so that certain nuances may be brought to light, and to establish the variations in the diverse processes of this movement. We have chosen the following important works:
A.L. DUNHAM, The Industrial Revolution in France (1815-1848) , New York, 1955.
W.O. HENDERSON, The State and the Industrial Revolution in Prussia, Liverpool, 1958.
W.O. HENDERSON, The Industrial Revolution on the Continent, London, 1961.
R. MARX, La Revolution industrielle en Grande-Bretagne des origines a 1850, Paris, 1970.
"Studi sulla rivolĤzione industriale," special issue of the review Studi Storici, 1961. Studies have been done on Italy, France, Belgium, Hungary and
Germany.

The Fontana Economic History of Europe, book III, in volumes, each of which concentrates on one country. The following studies have already been published:
J.M. BIUCCHI for Switzerland, P. DEANE for England, J. DHONDT and M. BRUWIER for Belgium, C. FOHLEN for France.
As soon as the last volume is finished, all the major European countries will have been reviewed for the period from the middle of the 18th century to 1914.

THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
There is an abundance of literature available on this subject, but the quality is varied. In some sectors, there has been some very interesting, pertinent research, but in others we are limited to works which prefer to astound the reader with a few spectacular facts, rather than deal with more essential elements. There are many biographies of inventors, but again, these have various faults, not least of which is chauvinism. Furthermore, invention is usually portrayed as a fairly logical process, and this can distort the true picture.
A large part of the bibliography of the preceding chapter is useful for this period. We shall therefore limit this list to the most important works only. The notion of a second industrial revolution has been examined in only one work, and certain problems are still not fully dealt with.
H. P ASDERMADJIAN, La Deuxieme Revolution industrielle, Paris, 1959.
The following works are mainly assessments of this period, and would be a good starting point for further research:
A Century of Technology (1851-1951) , ed. by P. DUNSHEATH, London, 1951.
"The Development of Industrial techniques in the last fifty years (18801930)," in Civil Engineering, 1930, a special volume.
Cinquante ans de perfectionnement technique, 1900-1950, Paris, 1952.
There are some interesting studies on particular techniques. These were written some time ago, but some have been revised since. We shall mention just one of them here:
L.C. HUNTER, "The Origins of the Francis and Pelton turbines," in Science History Review, pp. 209-242, 1964.
Most vital perhaps are the works on new techniques, on engines and their application. Unfortunately, these studies are hardly satifactory, which once more means our choice is a limited one:
Y. LE GALLEC, "Les Origines du moteur a combustion interne," in Techniques et Civilisations, pp. 28-32 and 47-50, Vol II, 1952.
E. DIESEL AND G. GOLDBECK, From Engines to Autos: five Pioneers in Engine Development, Chicago, 1960.
F. SASS, Geschichte des deutschen Verbrennungs-motorenbaus von 1860 bis 1918, Berlin 1962.
There is not one single suitable history on the motor or aviation industry.
We have included a couple of works, and also added an exhibition catalogue, which has some vital information on this subject:


BIBLIOGRAPHY
1215 R.CHAMBE, Histoire de /'aviation, Paris, 1949.
J. ROUSSEAU, Histoire mondiale de /'automobile, Paris, 1958.
Le Siecle de l' automobile. Centenaire du moteur ii explosion. De Beau de Rochas ii nos jours, Paris, 1961.
On one particular technique:
HOWELL and SCHROEDER, History of Incandescent Lamp, Schenec tady, 1927.
Inventors' biographies are numerous. Here again, one can only offer a selection:
BEAU DE ROCHAS, Documents pour l'Histoire des Techniques, No 2, octobre 1962.
BELL, The Bell Telephone, Boston, 1908 H. BESSEMER, An Autobiography, London, 1905 J.-B. BOUSSINGAULT, Memoires, Paris, 1892-1900, 5 vol.

SCIENCE AND TECHNIQUES
We have considered it pointless to cite all the general histories of science, already well known by a wide public.
From the 16th century, the wealth of technical dissertations has enabled a better study to be made of the relationship between sciences and techniques.
Some of the following dissertations have been used:
A. AGRICOLA, De re Metallica, Basel, 1556.
B. BELIDOR, Architecture hydraulique, 2 volumes, Paris, 1737-1739.

P. BOUGER, Traite du navire, de sa construction et de son mouvement, Paris, 1746.

TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
The Bibliography on this subject is enough to fill whole libraries. We must therefore limit ourselves to a select few. This choice is guided by two criteria.
The first borders on chauvinism, in that we have concentrated on French publications. The latter is compensatory: in many cases, the works that we cite include considerable bibliographies in every language to guide the reader.

FOR THE CONTEMPORARY PERIOD:
For this period, from the 19th century to today, we are both overwhelmed by works and unable to draw anything valid from them. Everything, or almost, should be taken up again in a more precise way and the problem posed definitively.
Some biographies may be of use. It would be inconceivable to mention them all here. We have chosen a few exceptional ones:
Stevin:
R. DEPAU, S. Stevin, Brussels, 1942.
E.J. DIJKSTERHUIS, S. Stevin, The Hague, 1943.
A.J. VAN DE VELDE, S. Stevin, Brussels, 1948.
Roberval:
L. AUGER, Un savant meconnu, Gilles Personne de Roberval, Paris, 1962.

Table of Contemporaneous Events



The table of contemporaneous event presented here is only a last resort, material conditions requiring four columns. More space would be required to divide techniques which are often very removed from each other. A third column was needed for geographical areas in order to compare gaps and concomitances. A veritable atlas would therefore have been necessary.
The material was very rich so choices were difficult. We have excluded material which may be found in other volumes in the
Encyclopedie de la Pleiade: major events in history and scientific discoveries.
The four columns in this table represent the following subjects:
1. EXPLOITATION: energy, agriculture, forestry, hunting, fish ing, mining, and heavy metallurgy.
2. TRANSFORMATION: transformation of materials; thermal, chemical, physical or mechanical procedures, artificial materials and all the mechanisation which relates to it.
3. ARTS AND CRAFTS: the fabrication of consumer products and the mechanisation concerning it, and technical education.
4. GEOGRAPHY: building, the organisation of space, transports, and the military arts.

ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
PROLEGOMENA TO A HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
by Bertrand GILLE The difficult emergence of the history of techniques. The conditions of its integration with other, generally recognised disciplines: using different types of knowledge and using different methodologies. The place of the history of techniques in a group which already includes economics, demography, the history of sciences or ideas, and factual history. A brief look at the history of the history of techniques, which enables us to follow the development, and the wealth and the shortcomings of a discipline which is now established. From the fragmented history of inventions, having no links with other human activities, to a modem history of research which is gradually being integrated with other areas of research. The reasons why the history of techniques is somewhat isolated: the lack of organised research, its partial character, sometimes narrow outlook, and the mistrust of historians who are sometimes reluctant to venture into a field which is foreign to their traditional preoccupations. The future perspectives. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
3 CONCEPTS AND METHODOLOGY: The notions of system and structure.
The complementary techniques which are united in a single group under the heading "technics." The technical act, the link between the indispensable coupling of matter-energy. The various natures of technical combinations;
from the unitary combination to the technical ensemble; from the technical ensemble to the technical chain; the technical structure which services one or more chains; the conditions of use of a technical chain and the complicated relations which can exist between sub-groups themselves, and between a sub-group and a global ensemble. The technical system: the coherence bet ween techniques which are in fact quite independent of one another - this implies a level of progression which is common to virtually all of these. A history of techniques: the obvious need to confront technical systems with other systems of human activity with which it is clearly linked, and without which none of them would exist; technical and economic acts, and the close interdependence in which they act and react towards one another, and with one another, following a course of evolution which for the most part can only be a common one. A new dependence of the preceding double system, which links it directly to the social system, and therefore to the political one, and then through these to other systems; demographic rises, education, local resources,


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
etc. The dynamics of technical systems, one of the best ways of approaching the problems of static analysis. The questions posed by dynamic analysis:
determining the limits of a technical system which to an extent governs all progress; the notion of structural limits; the impossibility of either increasing quantities, or diversifying production, or of reducing the costs. A general formulation of the causes which explain the failure of a technical system as a whole, which needs to be completed by the research into the particular problems of individual techniques; the example of agriculture and the typical cases of a few industries revealing the blockage of an entire system when impassable technological limits are reached, and how this can provoke imba lances and generate crises. The exterior factors which can correct patent imbalances; customs tariffs and protectionism, the exportation of raw materi als, the creation of products to replace essential materials. The interest afforded by dynamic analysis which enables us to outline structures and systems, and reveal the structural limits beyond which a country is forced into invention and the transformation of past systems; the two essential poles of technical progress are thus established; technological lineages and blockages.
Four distinct notions relevant to the global explanation of the technical sector:
scientific progress, and its obvious links with technics; the conditions which generate "invention" and the relationships of the latter with the different existing structures; diffuse rationality, soft determinism, the research into the events and the causes which favour or inhibit one structural combination rather than another. The different factors which create favourable conditions for an invention, the application of which will itself create other conditions, bringing about another invention; indisputable rationality, evident determinism, the elements of one inevitable conclusion: the freedom of the inventor is closely circumscribed and limited by the requirements to which the invention must respond. Towards an attempt to define invention, and the group of subsidiary activities which must be put into practice if the invention is to become practically, politically, and economically operational - or from the departure point of a new idea, to the extreme limits of its application. Innovation, the complement to invention, although it is quite different, in that it marks the boundaries between technical progress and economic need; invention is primitively a gratuitous act, whilst innovation is an act which is subject to economic imperatives - these are opposing points of view which can often postpone the application of a technical invention. The production function, an ensemble of technical relations between the factors and the products of an industrial branch which determines the possibility or the impossibility of an innovation. The concerns which may be resolved by the use of a new method, and the conditions it must fulfil in order to be defined as progress; the variables representing the production factors. The inevitable internal modifications undergone by enterprises, changes due to the increasing role of technical bodies, changes in the management and finance methods, and the effect of the latter on the adoption or the rejection of possible innovations. The different levels at which innovation can be decided upon: the individual level, that of the enterprise, or the group level, which implies the role of banks or other enterprises, and the collective and national level, that of the state. The necessary convergences which permit the practical realisation of a technical innovation. What is technical progress? The factors of growth linked to technical progress, and the distortions engendered by these factors, firstly at the level of a single enterprise, secondly at the national level. The final convergence of technical with economic progress, resulting from points of


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1311 convergence which can end up in an ensemble of four factors generating technical progress: scientific progress, invention, innovation and economic growth. Successive or retrospective effects which can link each of these factors to the other, and or each group of factors. Towards the intimate fusion of each of the formerly independent factors, resulting from a raised level of knowl edge, which has replaced revolution with a succession of independent evolutions. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
9 DOCUMENTARY SOURCES: The research and critique of sources of a very diverse nature . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
66 THE TEXTS: The most important part of all historical documentation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
67 Technical literature: From unorganised empirical knowledge, which does not come from true experimentation, to the "technical treatise," which is possible as soon as a technique demands a reasoning process, even if it is only partial.
Greek treatises, the first works of technical literature, to which the Romans were to add treatises on architecture and agriculture, with noticeably changing the Greek conception of technical treatises. Medieval technical literature: the book of recipes. Theatres of machines and engineers notebooks. The exploitation during the Renaissance of medieval forms, but at the same time the important development of a specialist type of literature concerned with a given technique, and bordering on a scientific approach. The description and perfection of the 17th century: Diderot and D' Alembert's Encyclopedie. The technical treatises and journals of the 18th and 19th centuries. Towards the use of ancient technical literature, for the elaboration of a modern history of techniques. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
67 Direct sources: Two relatively recent sources: public administrative archives and company archives. Information, direction, and the protection of public rights, three functions of the state which require constant research in the domain of techniques, and which thus provide the occasion for setting up numerous important archives: patent and privilege, technical press and company records
73 Indirect sources: Varied sources which are quite different from each other, and which mainly concern the earlier epochs: standards historical and literary accounts; medieval diplomatic sources and acts of donation; company regu lations and mining codes; notary acts. The difficulties posed by the historical development of indirect sources: the number of texts, the vagueness of the descriptions, the amount of time needed to study them, the delicate operation of interpreting them, often due to the language. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
75 lcoNOGRAPHICAL SOURCES: The extent and diversity of questions posed by the study of iconographical documentation. Pictures of a general type which do not claim to be precise, and actual technical drawings. Recommendation for the efficient use of iconographical documentation: collection of documentation;
the critique, interpretation and use of these documents. From general icono graphy to technical drawing, or from the object considered as a decorative element, to the object described for its own sake . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
78 OBJECTS: The conservation of objects: a recent preoccupation. Two types of object: tools or mac.hines of production, or manufactured objects. The origin and dating of such objects, which are difficult problems requiring carefully thought out solutions. Two types of instrument of production are available:
ancient instruments which are still in existence today, and ancient techniques which are changed to varying degrees, but which are still in use. Scale models and the collections of these, and other possible sources for the study of objects.


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
Archaeology, the history of art, the history of techniques are all comple mentary disciplines. The development of the research and conservation of objects and future course to take. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

RESEARCH CENTRES: The principle centres in Europe of specialised research in the study of techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
89 MusEUMS: The museum, a centre for the privileged conservation of objects and pictures, two of the essential sources for the historian of techniques. Art museums in general and specialised museums . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

TECHNIQUES AND CIVILIZATIONS
THE ORIGINS OF TECHNIQUES
by Bertrand GILLE Outlines of an evolving logic of techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
99 SOLUTIONS BORN OF IGNORANCE: identifying the origins of techniques?
99 GIFTS FROM THE GODS: Abundance of the mythology of techniques, especially in polytheistic religions. Greek mythology and techniques; techne and metis.
Athena, a technical power. Hephaestos, inventor of the working of metal by fire, and a complementary power to Athena. The myth of Prometheus. An intermediary between the gods and men, a necessary link between the appea rance of man and the birth of techniques. Daedalus, a third stage leading to the secularisation and humanisation of techniques: invention rather than rev elation, and transfer of divine activities to men. Awareness on the part of
Greek writers of the role of technical development in man's cultural progress . . .
100 NATURE'S EXAMPLE: The very probable direct influence of environmental obser vations on the creation of tools. Various facts concerning types of observation that are very different, but which imply an easily definable effort of imagi nation. Three types of observations whose practical conclusions will be increasingly elaborated. The search for material destined by nature for a precise task; useful observations; necessarily preceeded by awareness of a problem to be resolved and always achieved by gradually established rules.
Obvious parallels between animal and human implements, similarities, analo gies or identities, which do not for all that prove a simple imitation by man.
Examples of animal achievements or behaviour that may have served as models for man, but association of activities has only been made a postiori. . . . . . .
108 THE INGENIOUSNESS OF MAN: An initial, thorny question: how to identify the first man among the various creature possessing similar characteristics?
Behaviour when faced with material regarded as an implement, perhaps the first evidence of humanity . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
118 Lucy OR THE FIRST LINEAMENTS: "Precise data" on the origins of man from recent explorations in East Africa and conclusions that may be derived from them. A provisional human genealogy drawn from discoveries made in East Africa:
fashioned pebbles . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
119 STAGES OF PROGRESS: Attempting the difficult task of chronological clarification .. .


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1313 Classifications: Classification necessary for determining the stages of progress with stone tools: characteristics of the object, characteristics of gestures essential to the fabrication of an object and its use. Problems to be resolved in order to establish the typology of an epoch. Relationships between the nature of the raw material and the process of fabricating tools and relationships between human intellectual processes and the stages of fabrication. The major types of prehistoric tools and their morphological varieties. Two conditions opposed to basing technical systems on nothing but technical description of groups of tools: tools which are inseparable from the objects produced or effects achieved by them, and the latter are unknown to us; tools constitute only a part of a broader technical system of which we are unaware because of a lack of solid evidence . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Paleanthropians; gradual evolution ending up with
