THE FIRST GREAT TECHNICAL CIVILIZATIONS
by Bertrand GILLE An evolution of human techniques suddenly accelerated for reasons which are not very clear other than two fairly vague general explanations: evolution of the human race, and climatic changes. Sufficient documentary material to understand the methods used by Neolithic man but which does not explain which paths he followed. Appearance of the first great technical civilizations . . .
146 THE NEOLITHIC REVOLUTION: The techno-economic conversion which marks the Neolithic period: sedentarisation, appearance of genuine agricul ture and animal husbandry, discovery of ceramics. Probable region originally interested by this "technical revolution," the reasons to explain this are numerous but not very convincing . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
148 SEDENTARIZATION : Settlements necessarily grouped around sources of food.
Dwellings sometimes in fortified groups; fortresses . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
150 AGRICULTURE: Complementary exploitation of the animal and vegetable world through agriculture and animal husbandry sometimes supplemented by hunting, and gathering wild plants. First cultivated plantsҪ first domesticated animals. Periods which saw probably the beginnings of agriculture and animal husbandry. Regions in which archaeological discoveries have made possible the dating of the transition from gathering to cultivation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
152 ANIMAL REARING: Preconditions necessary to pass from hunting to domestication.
First animals domesticated and first regions of domestication. Diversification of domestication. The perfect concordance and complementarity between agriculture and domestication . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
155 CERAMICS: Attempts to date its appearance; its rapid regional diversification precludes a single centre of origin; ceramics not necessarily linked to agriculture since it preceded or followed it, depending on the region. The use offire . . .


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
METALLURGY: The notion of useable native metal and the idea of working it adequately to produce a useful material: two facts creating a new problem. An inexplicable series of cultural, technical, geographical, and geological interactions. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

162 EGYPT: The enigma of the sudden appearance of advanced techniques in the Old Kingdom while the Egyptian evolution was previously evolving no differently from other regions of the Near East. An independent Egyptian technical system . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
163 The beginnings: Progressive settling of nomads. An agriculture and methods of animal husbandry suggesting a knowledge of advanced techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
165 Exploitation techniques: Cultivated plants. Light and heavy agricultural materi als; the light wooden ard, hoe, sickle and limited equipment to a few tools purely for the cultivation of grains, the essential plants. Vine growing. Meth ods of raising animals, and the chronology of animal species domesticated;
beekeeping, probably an Egyptian invention. Hunting and fishing. The pro duction of metals in ancient Egypt and the problems it poses, some of which are still not resolved. Chronology of metals, ores used, and the reduction of ores.
The massive use of stone made by the Egyptians, and the techniques, some still unknown, used by the quarriers. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
167 The artisan class: Great manual skill but with rudimentary tools that evolve very slowly. Joining. Leatherworking. Weaving. Ceramics. Jewellery. A domain where the history of techniques is still unknown. The change from stone to metal as the material for tools during the first dynasties . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
178 Construction: Pyramids, temples and obelisks as astonishing remains of an exceptional architecture, but we known nothing of domestic architecture built of raw bricks, too fragile to survive today. How the great stone monuments were assembled and erected. Evolution of monumental architecture, generally without very sophisticated techniques or important material problems . . . . . . . . . . .
183 Space: Peculiar Egyptian geographical conditions that made the Nile the natural and only means of communication. The boats used. Urbanism. The Nile and the problems posed by its floods that the Egyptians strived to measure and regulate; irrigation; improvement of the Nile for navigation and supplying the quarries and towns with water. Possible reasons for the blockage of the
Egyptian technical system . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
187 MESOPOTAMIA: A possible parallel between Egypt and Mesopotamia, even though the differences between them are as great as the similarities . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
192 A breaking off: A fairly advanced Neolithic civilization leaving proof of elaborate settlements and perhaps the first developed agriculture in the world. Jarmo
Hassuna; the Halafian civilization. The Sumerians of El Obeid; agriculture and the materials used for building and tools; ceramics of the Uruk type; exploi tation of the delta of the Tigris and the Euphrates; appearance of a social and technical organisation hitherto unattained; the temple, writing, urbanisation, agriculture and crafts as a coherent system of diversified and complementary activities. In spite of the proximity of the two regions, a quite surprising

1315 synchronism between Egypt and Sumer. The beginning of the historical period and the clear Egypto-Sumerian synchronism . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
193 Primary activities: Slow and progressive evolution of agricultural techniques;
cultivated plants and trees; tools used; animal husbandry; hunting and fishing.
Wood and metal industries; the appearance of iron and its mysterious origin . . . .
199 Secondary activities: The textile industry; working wool, linen, hemp, cotton;
methods of weaving and dyeing cloth. Leather. Woodworking. Fine stone engraving and inlaid work. Diverse techniques of the arts of fire using different sorts of furnaces. Metalworking, where the Mesopotamians excel despite a scarcity of ores. Palaces and temples as the only remains of Mesopotamian buildings, the ordinary, less solid dwellings having disappeared; the storied towers.
Urbanism. Irrigation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
207 THE EXPANSION OF THE NEW TECHNICAL SYSTEM: The undeniable but little understood diffusion of the technical systems developed on the banks of the Nile and between the Tigris and the Euphrates . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
217 THE NEAR REGIONS: Syria and Palestine, geographical regions naturally destined to benefit rapidly from the technical progress of the Egyptians and the
Mesopotamians. The lower valleys of the Indus and its tributaries, another region further away where the synchronism with Egyptian and Mesopotamian developments is difficult to explain. Relationship between technical systems and economic and social systems may explain a certain clustering of identical processes. The "horns of the Fertile Crescent." What the Hittites borrowed from their predecessors and their two contributions; spread of the use of iron and the domestication of the horse. Greece, the islands near the Continent,
Crete; the Cretan palaces, a technical revolution probably linked to the advent of monarchies; the Mycenean tombs; Greek palaces and acropolises.
The originality of early Greek civilizations: probably most evident in artistic expression rather than in techniques, most of which came from Asia Minor . . . . .
219 DISTANT AREAS: The change from bronze to iron, a usual step in the progress of the material world where it is difficult to establish even its approximate chronology. Possible causes to explain a more or less rapid rythmn of evolu tion. South-east Europe and the Mediterranean basin. North Africa. Two autonomous centres of civilization very removed from each other and very far from Egyptian and Mesopotamian influences: China and Central America.
The Near East as the certain origin of major innovations leading to modern techniques and as proof that closed civilizations are less innovative than open civilizations. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
THE GREEK TECHNICAL SYSTEM
by Bertrand GILLE The special conditions in which the Greek technical system was formed: small city-state economies and the necessity of massive imports . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
243 TRADITIONAL SECTORS AND PROGRESSIVE SECTORS: Uneven advances to be sure, but generalized to all sectors of material life by the beginning of the 6th century BC. Outside a few exceptional regions, a poor soil hardly able to encourage the development of agricultural techniques; culti-


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
vation of cereals; implements used; the olive tree, grape vine, fruit trees;
machines used to process agricultural produce; animal husbandry; hunting and fishing. Mineral resources, of which the lead mines of the Laurion were the most important; techniques and tools of mining. Extraction of clay and working of quarries. Textile techniques: Woodworking. Pottery. Metalwor king. Buildings at first made of wood and bricks; then the Greeks substituted these materials for stone once lifting machinery and transporation becomes adequate; construction processes. Transport of materials; land transport on rudimentary roads which remained so for a long time, reflecting the slight circulation of goods; portage and vehicles; maritime transport and the exami nation of three main components of sea transport: ship, port, and navigation.
Effects on technical progress of the "Greek miracle" which took place between the 6th and the end of the 4th centuries BC; stable technical sectors and progressive sectors; a new phenomenon: the union of science and techniques from the 6th century BC onwards: the rise of science, especially mathematics, and the organisation of scientific knowledge by scholars feeling that this is required, while in a parallel movement technicians draw up the first technical treatises; some important mutations shaping the evolution of many techniques.
Diversification of the use of energy. Mining techniques. The revolution in military techniques in the first half of the 4th century BC; the work of Archytas and his role in the appearance of a more rational applied mechanics; a considerable advance in Greek poliorcetics, but difficult to determine what contributions the Greeks made to the advance of military machinery; the work of Aeneas; engineers and architects; the Athenaeum; towards a tradition of authors who transmit from generation to generation, discoveries and advances in the art of war. Civil work where the desire to apply the sciences is evident;
urbanism, water supplies and river improvement . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
244 THE SCHOOL OF ALEXANDRIA AND ITS POTENTIAL: The era of the Ptolemies of Alexandria: a period during which we may observe a remarkable convergence between science and techniques, both striving to achieve perfec tion if not progress. The library and museum of Alexandria, more of an enormous conservatory than an instrument of progress. Towards the arrangement of the sciences into systems as well as the confrontation of the diverse kinds of knowledge. The great names of the school of Alexandria:
Euclid, Strato of Lampsacus, Hero. Aristarchus of Samos, Eratosthenes, Erasistratus. Their attempt to apply theory to practice limited by one of the favourite methods of the mechanicians: to take up no technical problem that could not be given a full scientific explanation. Archimedes' departure: to begin with techniques as the real inspiration and then move to theoretical studies. Limits of the school, which still represents a remarkable convergence of all sorts of intellectual endeavours. The work of Ctesibios, Philo of
Byzantium, organising experience in a first step towards scientific technology.
Apollonius of Perga, Hero of Alexandria, a mechanician whose important work recapitulates what has been acquired in the past and sometimes offers new solutions but which stands midway between science and techniques, always giving what has been learned from accumulated experience as much credit as science; science as an adjunct seeking to explain experimental results and not as a pre-existing theory from which a practical application is derived;
automata as an occasion o use an ensemble of scientifically described phe nomena; automata also designed to produce their effects over time and in an ordered fashion; the problem of moving model to reality. Disciplines, such as


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1317 architecture, that did not interest the Alexandrines and the possible reasons why. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
283 BLOCKAGE OR LIMITS: A clear blockage of technical thought despite the presence of all the elements necessary for significant advance. Two ways of explaining the reasons for this blockage: a restrictive attitude towards manual work and the material things in life; and the existence of a social system based on slavery. The concept of ideas as the noble activity of the mind; material implementation as a lower activity subordinating thought to manual skill.
Intellectual research as a sequence of reasoning whose logical results are immediately grasped by means of rational intuition; practical advance as a material result, the operations of which lead to a solution making the reasoning intellectually clear and evident. Slavery as an institutional consequence of the inadequate material means provided by the technical system, or as a basic institution blocking Jabour-saving techniques with cheap labour that made them uneconomic? Was there really opposition to technical advance? The scientific inadequacies and material difficulties substantiating the thesis of technical blockage by insurmountable intellectual and practical obstacles: the general conditions of a technical mutation are still very far in the future . . . . . . . . . .
TIE ROMANS AND TIEIR SUCCESSORS
by Bertrand GILLE The perpetuation of the technical system developed by the Greeks despite a certain number of significant inventions, and a special genius for the organisation of geographical space . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
CIRCUMSTANCES AND SURROUNDINGS:
GooD PUPILS AND CONSCIENTIOUS OBSERVERS: The Romans, originally a farming people, adopt agricultural techniques of the peoples they conquer. Assimilate the systems they discover; but they also improve upon them. Technical literature: less a true technology than the arrangement of technical rules and standards in a general organisation; writings on agronomy; various technical treatises showing the same tendency to collect, and condense the heritage, for the most part hellenistic; Columella, Pliny the Elder, Vitruvius, Vegetius . . . . . . .
321 GEOGRAPHY AND TECHNIQUES: The evident relationship between available resour ces and the evolution of techniques which concern them. Relative poverty of the Mediterranean basin and the direct consequences of this: the Romans having to depend heavily upon imports, which causes a certain technical stagnation. The expansion of the Empire gives the Romans resources, the exploitation of which will involve real technical progress . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
326 ORGANISATION: An immense geographical territory, the relative unity of which can only be maintained by strong governmental institutions and adequate technical means. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
328 THE EXPLOITA TION OF NA TURAL RICHES: Agriculture and mining, two areas in which the Romans concentrate . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
330 AGRICULTURE: Roman agriculture differs little from the agriculture of other Mediterranean peoples. The Roman desire to adapt plants to the different soils and climates of the Empire. Animal husbandry. Roman agricultural imple ments intelligently modified and adapted rather than new. Organized culti vation; considerable extension of the grapevine as a direct result of Roman policy; the agricultural calendar . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
THE MINING INDUSTRY: Exploitation rules and technical prescriptions. Our present ignorance of Roman mining methods owing to the lack of information concerning them. The production of metal and its uses . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
337 ENERGY: Almost exclusively limited to natural energy resources: animal, wind, and water energy . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
340 IMPLEMENTS: Simple and composite tools, or machines . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
343 SIMPLE TOOLS: Possible considerable development in Roman tools despite the scarcity of remaining evidence. Cutting tools. Wood working, metal working.
Towards greater specialisation of each tool: the different kinds of saws as an example . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
343 MACHINES: Little advance in mechanical knowledge, the Romans only exploiting what the Greeks left . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
349 SOME TRADITIONAL TECHNIQUES: Techniques taken from the Greeks or the Barbarians, but difficult to determine which; textiles, ceramics; glass making, cabinet making; chemical industry; techniques necessary for the development of writing . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
352 BUILDING: A domain where the Romans are masters, at least in building techniques; as construction methods also inherited from the Greek past . . . . . . . . .
354 TYPES OF BUILDINGS: Some types of building determined by political or economic considerations but also as a form of undeniable artistic expression . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
354 PROCESSES: Large cut stone joined with mortar; methods used to consolidate structures; decoration, finishing; walls; doors and locks. The Romans make the vault invented by the Greeks into a general innovation and perfect the cupola.
Advances in carpentry. The arched bridge, probably a Roman innovation; the enigma of how they laid the foundations under water. Development and improvement of heating systems . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
356 GEOGRAPHICAL SPACE: A NEW SUBJECT OF TECHNICAL ORGANISA TION: The diversity ofthe Empire and its unity of organisation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
363 MEANS OF TRANSPORT: Land vehicles and traction means. River and sea navigation and, ship building methods . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
363 THE INFRASTRUCTURE OF TRANSPORT: River improvements. The road, one of the major Roman innovations; the way in which the road was built and its layout.
Creation of artificial ports, the Western Mediterranean being poor in natural harbours . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
366 ROMAN WATER WORKS: Very large scale application of old ideas and proven and improved techniques. Urban water supply, and the proliferation of its civil engineering works, often made possible by the wide use of lead conduits. The steady Roman contribution to technical progress which seems to stabilize precisely with them . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
368 BYZANTIUM: Byzantium, a faithful reflection of earlier civilizations . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
371 TECHNICAL THOUGHT: A blockage of technical thought well illustrated by a technical literature which merely repeats old treatises. Almost total reproduction of works of school of Alexandria . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
371 THE PRINCIPAL TECHNIQUES OF BYZANTIUM: Review of Byzantine technical princi ples, the originality of which lies not so much in the methods used than in the perfection of finishing and decoration. Rich artistry masking a set of stable techniques inherited from preceding civilizations. Byzantium as the natural continuation of the Roman tradition of assimilation. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1319 GREEK FIRE AS AN EXAMPLE: An advanced application of the techniques of incendiary weapons known for centuries. One new idea: rocket launching tubes. Twelve centuries of great technical stability . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
BLOCKED TECHNICAL SYSTEMS
by Bertrand GILLE By comparison with the successive mutations of the West European technical system, other systems seem blocked at the end of the Middle Ages . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
380 CHINESE TECHNIQUES: Uncertain chronology of Chinese technical discoveries and the difficulty, at the moment insurmountable, of evaluating the take-off of Chinese technical progress and the impossibility of giving a comprehensive explanation of the technical blockage of China in the 15th and
16th centuries. A final problem: the Chinese contributions to European techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
380 TECHNIQUES AND TECHNOLOGY: The subjects covered by ancient treatises as evidence of mature techniques. Chinese technical literature . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
382 EXPLOITATION OF NATURAL RESOURCES: The stages of Chinese agriculture, visibly comparable to those of other centres of civilization, with local variations.
Mining techniques. Uses of energy, obviously natural energy at first, and then converted forms . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
385 PREPARATION OF RAW MATERIALS: Landmarks showing the appearance or the improvements of new techniques and demonstrating that in some of these, notably metallurgy, the Chinese could have been initiators. The arts of metal and fire: glass making, ceramics; lacquering; paper, clearly a Chinese discovery; gunpowder, a more dubious Chinese invention. Sugar refining and alcoholic fermentation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
388 CRAFTS: Chinese textile techniques: materials used; spinning. Stages which mark the evolution of printing; wood-cuts, typography . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
393 GEOGRAPHY: The immense territory of the Chinese Empire and the appropriate and coherent techniques it required: road networks, river networks, ship building. Urbanism and fortifications; the Great Wall . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
398 BLOCKAGES: The technical parallels between China and the West can be explained either as borrowing from each other or simply as convergent evolution. The measurement of time and distance and the compass are two examples of how difficult it is to prove the paternity of an invention. The blockage of the Chinese technical system at the end of the Middle Ages and the possible reasons for this. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
401 PRE-COLUMBIAN AMERICAN TECHNIQUES: A technical blockage at an early level of development and the difficulty of explaining it; the field is often left open for sometimes extreme suppositions. Some "missing" techniques that are essential for advanced technical systems; absence of writing; very few domesticated animals; native metal used, but no smelting of ores; the critical lack of iron; no use of the wheel. Agricultural techniques; soil conservation, cultivation methods, and plants cultivated. Peoples taking advantage of an abundant food supply but an unbalanced diet due to the lack of animal proteins and Inineral salts. Hunting and fishing. Necessarily primitive tools because of the lack of iron. The history of pre-Columbian metallurgy; ore smelting very late; only casting of metals with low melting points; alloys of malleable metals


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
better suited to artistic decoration than to tool making which remained very primitive; metal working. Construction, a technique in which the pre
Columbians excelled; stoneworking certainly extremely difficult owing to the primitive tools which probably served as an intermediary between the material and an abrasive rather than as actual cutting instruments; quarrying and fitting of building stones. The fire using techniques: pottery and its decoration.
Assembly techniques: wood working and wicker work. Textile techniques: the raw material used, colouring, spinning, weaving. Architecture: rural, urban, and monumental. Weapons. Urbanism. The problem of communications: only oral communication of knowledge, writing being unknown; very primitive sea and land transportation, probable causes of a technical confinement preventing progress. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
407 THE MUSLIM WORLD: The historical circumstances probably explaining why the Muslim world is more the heir to a technical system already blocked than the originator of the blockage, even though the Arabs had demonstrated a great capacity for adaptation on several past occasions. An accumulation of technical treatises that contrasts strangely with a lack of concern for technical innovation. Perpetuation of the Grieco-Roman Jheritage, at least/ in/ agricul ture. A scattered empire in which the people of one province do not know the technical advances of the other caliphates. Crafts: much more the crafts of conquered peoples than of the Arabs themselves. The art of war: the means of communication. Urbanism and building. Hydraulics as the main accom plishment of the Muslim world, heirs of techniques developed before the conquest; use of subterranean water; dams and irrigation; bucket-chain water lifters (norias). Persia, expecially Sassanid Persia, as the probable causes of such technical stagnation after a period of normal development . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
THE MEDIEVAL TECHNICAL SYSTEM
by Bertrand GILLE An epoch of intense technical activity. Chronology and rise of the medieval West.
An undeniable growth in which economic progress and major technical innovations are linked together. The beginning of a model for studying the evolution of the medieval system . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
440 THE FRAMEWORK OF TECHNICAL PROGRESS: The external conditions which explain why technical evolution was necessary. The medieval spirit and the notion of work; relationship between the spiritual and the material aspects of life. Various kinds of knowledge, and the world of techniques in which a technology begins to appear; the search for links between the active aspect and the speculative aspect of the sciences. The attempt of scholars to classify technology among the formal sciences. Techniques and sciences, two comple mentary factors of a single need felt by scholars. The different sectors of technical research, and the projects with the aim of achieving this. The elaboration of a remarkable and abundant technical literature with two aims:
to instruct the public, and to inform the technician of methods used by others in his field. The favourite subjects handed down from ancient technical literature;
proliferation of agricultural treatises in the 14th century: either systematic works or collections of recipes. The subject matter covered by purely technical recipe books that grow in number throughout the Middle Ages; the first examples of the books called "Theatres of Machines." The medieval intellec tual background of techniques more a driving force than a brake, despite the


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1321 argument of some scholars that techniques were scorned in the Middle Ages.
The joint movements which, linked to the physical conditions of life, had considerable influence on the development of the economy and therefore on the development of techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
442 THE INNOVA TIVE FIELDS: Appearance of a new technical system originally composed at one and the same time of old inventions, some technical novelties, and a certain conservatism. The use of energy as the essential factor of economic growth, and the major advantages it offered to the Middle Ages:
increase in the number of ways it could be used and the progressive diversifi cation of its uses and the techniques related to it. Animal energy. Hydraulic energy. Wind energy. Thermal energy, a new form little used in ancient times.
An expansion of mechanical knowledge linked to the development of energy uses. Gearing down mechanisms. Lathes. The main uses of the water mill. War machines as the expression of a new machinism. Transportations and the horse harness; the reasons why the modern horse harness was restricted to agricultu ral uses in the Middle Ages despite the appearance of the iron horseshoe;
origin and components of the modern horse harness which is more suitable to agricultural traction than for the actual transportation of goods. Sailing vessels;
Nordic and Mediterranean vessels using very different techniques, in hull as well as in rigging; evolution of the different construction techniques and the parallel evolution of the hull, rigging and mast design in the Nordic countries;
characteristics of Mediterranean vessels: many small sails and masts; the convergence of Nordic and Mediterranearntechniques., The two-fold evolution of medieval metallurgy and building following the ancient tradition: the
Catalane forge and the low furnace. The transition from primitive production to the beginning of reorganized production at the end of the 12th century. The evolution of medieval textile techniques; materials and traditional spinning methods; the improvement of the existing loom and an important innovation in carding with metal points; the spinning wheel; improvement or appearance of several new instruments that transform the textile crafts into an organized and mass production industry. Other minor changes, notably in glass making, cabinet making, and architecture, which confirm the medieval desire for a better material life . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
454 TRADITIONAL FIELDS: Adaptations of ancient techniques to a different natural environment. The reasons for a certain stagnation of agricultural techniques. The plants and methods of cultivation. Techniques inherited from
Antiquity, soil improvement and drainage. Agricultural implements: from the light ard to the heavy plough. Agricultural production and methods of animal husbandry. Medieval mine working; a survival from Antiquity despite the adoption of new types of organisation; mine shafts and tunnels; methods of extracting the coal and tools. Mining machinery and presses. Ceramics, glassmaking. Salt mining and methods of mining salt when dissolved in water.
Distillation. Chemical techniques, all inherited from Antiquity apart from the discovery of explosives, the origin of which remains a mystery. The tools of the medieval age. The medieval technical system and its internal tensions . . . . . . . . . . . .
CLASSICAL SYSTEMS
by Bertrand GILLE The technical domain: a rather shadowy area probably due to the brilliant achievements of the Renaissance in the fine arts, literature and sciences, but an


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
age in which a profound evolution probably took place. Repopulation during the 15th century following the epidemics and wars of the preceding century may partly explain the development of mechanisation. The economic renais sance and the major discoveries: a consequence of the appearance of a new technical system? Modification of social structures and behaviour: the growth of the towns and the birth of modern states. A rebirth of interest in all new technical ideas: intense activity of technologists throughout Europe and an effort of each centralised state to modernise economic structures. Italian princes of the 15th century exemplify the interest in technical effectiveness, humanism and science simultaneously. The reasons for such a deep transfor mation of the technical spirit: realism, utilitarianism, empiricism, and experi mental and mathematical reasoning. A first encounter between science and techniques and the appearance of an early form of technology. The first printed technical collections to attempt a synthesis of reflection and experimentation.
The engineer, now a multi-talented technician, first emerged in Italy from among artist-technicians; the great technical treatises, and the advance from
"recipes" to the search for "reasons;" Leonardo da Vinci; the generalisation of research seeking to bridge the gap between diverse techniques. Milestones in the 16th century development of the technical spirit . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
502 THE CLASSICAL SYSTEM: Spread of mechanisation, probably characteristic of the new technical system born with the Renaissance; the use of wood, and the limits it imposed upon innovation (e.g. the crank and connecting rod system). Renaissance technology blocked by inadequate development of energy exploitation. Mills with water-wheels; windmills. Exploitation tech niques, a domain where the best technical system was most evident; agricultu ral techniques modified and improved; the appearance of new and improved plants and the appearance of new edible varieties; areas where change was much slower: forestry, fishing and animal husbandry; resistance to new ideas in agriculture which was bound by tradition. Mining: example of profound change. The birth and development of new techniques in metallurgy: iron production; the advent of iron smelting; development of blast furnaces;
parallel development of machinery to produce iron, particularly in the water powered forge hammer, rolling, slitting and wire-drawing mills. Non-ferrous metalworking: precious metals, copper, bronze, and a newcomer, the combi nation of iron and tin. Other fire using techniques directly linked to the quality of the fuel used, wood or coals; diverse improvements and innovations in the glassmaking industry: Venetian or crystalline glass, coloured glass, plateglass.
Pottery, a very traditional technique where only the clay mixtures and the glaze evolved; from pottery to faience. Perfecting of mechanical techniques and their uses: the improvement of tools and the better use of energy. Research into the three fundamental elements of the tool: materials, forms, and varie ties. The conjunction of tool and machine and the diverse operations that made this association possible. The new and numerous applications of the crank and connecting rod, such as the diversification of lathes. Progress through experi mentation of the chemical industry where the most notable advance is made in explosive. The evolution of equipment in the textile industry, improvements in looms, provoking the first reaction of craftsmen and printers against new machinery. The major assembly techniques; woodworking and furniture making, carpentry and building construction; advent of printing, development of the paper industry and improvement of various machines, one dependent on the other. Military techniques, a field where a vast network of different


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1323 techniques came together to contribute spectacular advances, particularly in artillery; firearms; the adaptation of fortifications to the threat from artillery.
Centralisation of states, extension of the known world, imperative reasons for longer and more frequent journeys which required better and more comforta ble means of transportation; the invention of the pivoted front-axle unit facilitating the evolution of vehicles; modifications of suspension systems and wheels; road networks and organisation of riverways; the advance in maritime navigation: ships, navigation techniques, construction of ports. Soil drainage, in the Poitevin marshes, on the Venetian coast and in the Zuyderzee, and irrigation works, notably in the south of Spain. Birth of urban planning as an idea applied to the moderisation of existing cities. The Renaissance, a time of profound technical change, witnessing the major discoveries and economic take-off which characterised it . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
523 DEVELOPMENT AND LIMITA TIONS: A classical system which survived during the 17th century and into the first half of the 18th century, marked by a blockage of technical progress and of technical thought, the causes of which are difficult to discern. The great depression which followed the growth of the 16th century and the subsequent drop in the birth rate, two important facts which partly explain the 150 years of technical stagnation following the Renaissance.
The reversal in the relationship between science and techniques, techniques now disdained in favour of science, whereas techniques had formerly served as a spring-board of scientific discovery. Science, increasingly atonomous during the 17th century, becomes its own motive for action and discovers new principles, and the limits which prevent it from initiating real technical progress. Transition from scientific imagination to technical imagination, a natural link still impossible in the 17th century; rationalisation and perfecting of existing techniques. Scientific description, demonstration, and experi mentations as a complex opening the way for an aspiring technology; the technical literature of the 17th century. A quick review of different techniques that illustrate the slow changes of the 17th century to the lack of important mutations, apart from some partial inventions enabling old techniques to progress as they had not before. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
by Bertrand GILLE The chronological framework of the industrial revolution may be characterised by the appearance of new techniques, the earlier manufacturing processes having reached their saturation point. The economic conditions which stimulate the necessity for a new technical system . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
588 CONCORDANCES: A concordance of factors where each one revives the quest for new techniques. Demographic growth and the new population growth. The economic resurgence preceding the formation of the new technical system.
Accumulation of capital encouraged by currency stability and changes in local restraints and practices. The new drive for technical progress: the factor of a small group of men, either technicians, as in England, or politicians as in
France. Technical treatises as the reflection of the internal preoccupations of each state and as a means of achieving progress. Technical literature. The concomitances and reciprocal influences that could stimulate or slow down the development of science and techniques, and the points of severence where each one turned inwards, failing to interact with the other . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
GLOBAL PROGRESS: Successive technical revolutions having wider and wider effects owing to the increasing interdependence with the progress of mechanisation and economic forces. The various factors which lead to the greater development of mechanisation: the advent of metal replacing wood in machine building, the application of the steam engine as a new source of energy, using coal instead of wood as fuel. Stages in the history of the steam engine. The substitution of iron gears for gears made of wood. Fruitful application of an old idea: automatic machines. Birth of the machine-tool and the mechanisation of transportation. Considerable modification in the struc tures of certain exploitation techniques, the most profound change being in agriculture and ironmaking. Mining techniques profit directly from advances in parallel technical developments. Stages in the evolution of ironmaking, an industry completely transformed by the fuel change from wood to coal; coke smelting, then the puddling process and rolling mills; the role of the steam engine; crucible steel. New markets and openings for iron. The birth of the chemical industry. The take-off of textile techniques. The limits of the industrial revolution and the end of imbalances, the true significance of which is difficult to appreciate . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
602 D EVELO PM ENT: General application of the new technical system as a result of incremental progress and modifications. Growing awareness of technical and economic problems as the major consequence of the industrial revolution; the first attempts at integrating technical problems in a general explanation of economic movements and equilibria: the evolution of the relationship between techniques and science, the birth of a "scientific technology" and the creation of schools specialised in the sciences fundamental to crafts which are subse quently taught in schools of instruction; education for the working class.
Information as the natural complement to education. The appearance of a new type of profession, the engineer. The economic and financial transformations necessarily involved in the concentration of larger and larger units of produc tion. Two distinct kinds of development to be noted in the development of industry: diverse adaptations to new conditions of production quite different from those in countries initiating new techniques, and the continued devel opment of partial discoveries necessary to re-establish harmony within the system; the energy sector: a sector where water power continued to be developed concurrently with the invention and improvement of the steam engine. The slow and partial progress of exploitation techniques; agriculture, animal husbandry, mining, and ironmaking. The profound change observed in transport during the first half of the 19th century; steamboats; the railway stimulating major progress in various techniques; machine-tools; textile tech niques; the chemical industry; new techniques in food distribution. A world transformed from the middle of the 19th Century . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
630 CONSEQUENCES OF THE TECHNICAL REVOLUTION: To date, little precise information of the European industrial revolution. The qualitative result> of technical progress, often obscured by the significance of quantitative results: greatly improved quality of finished goods. Appearance of new products impossible to produce with previous techniques. The enormous growth in quantities produced and its consequences: growth in productivity, decreased costs of production, and under certain conditions, occasional lower consumer prices. Different reasons to explain why the drop in prices was not always proportional to the lower costs of production; monopolies, tariff protection, necessarily heavy reinvestment in new plants and equipment.


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAP1ERS
1325 Uniformisation, normalisation, and standardisation, all unavoidable conse quences of the growth in demand and production; from individual production to mass production; from the family enterprise to industrial concentration.
Financial and human consequences of the technical revolution: displacement and concentration of labour, the concentration of capital and the reorgani sation of financial markets. A new concentration of incomes involving the regrouping of urban manpower, the loss of rural industry, depopulation of the countryside, and the rise of an urban proletariat. The adaptation of the new techniques to human, geographical, and national conditions: a problem which is approached in different ways by the countries involved . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
THE MODERN TECHNICAL SYSTEM
by Bertrand GILLE The second half of the 19th century, a period of a second industrial revolution, the importance of which is often under-estimated, masked as it is by the first industrial revolution of the 18th century. The appearance and development of a new technical system, progressing at the same time as the advent of new discoveries; aviation and electricity for example. The possible reasons to explain the virtual necessity ofthis second industrial revolution . . . .. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
ENVIRONMENT AND CONDITIONS OF AN INDUSTRIAL RE:YOLU
TION: External and internal causes, the economic, social, and political factors that converge to require a technical mutation. A rising level of consumption which largely compensates for the past effects of demographic growth which ceases in the 19th century. The appearance of new markets, created by both colonisation and the internationalisation of commerce, for capital goods and consumer goods. Economic conditions indispensable for the take-off of new techniques: distribution and concentration of capital, growth in the size of businesses, the second condition being almost as consecutive as the first;
unrestricted interest rates; the formation of a dense banking network; concen tration of ·businesses. A modification of mental attitudes reflected in both educational reform and a general diffusion of scientific knowledge and its technical applications. The contribution of measuring instruments to the improvement of techniques. The difficult problem of the links between science and industry, between scientists and technicians; the inventors and intuition, scientists and reasoning: towards the birth of a true technology and the transformation of technical education. A major example of the coming together of science and techniques; scientific metallurgy. Techniques become a veritable extension of science at the same time as they become essential to the economy. Growth of demand and the subsequent increase in production: the important problem of the quantity of manufacturing products and the imperative reasons why old techniques must be superseded if the costs of production are not to rise. Evolution of energy production as a function of ever increasing needs. The problems created for the railway by the enormously increasing demand for transport. Maritime transport, an example of a limited area of existing techniques. Tensions and disequilibria caused by growth itself, necessary economic progress possible only through technical progress, and the vital change from a technical system having reached its limits to a new technical system with new potential. A consequence of the rise of the new technical system: a displacement of labour from the country to the towns and a transformation of the social system. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
THE GREA T MUTA TIO NS: The production of energy: use of special steels in the construction of the steam engine, more powerful and efficient but near its limits; hydraulic turbines, an area in which improvements will be important and constant. The advent and development of new energy converters; conver gence of a certain number of partial inventions and clearly defined requirements which results in the birth of the true motor; the different stages which mark the development of the four-stroke engine which will surpass its original purpose and become the essential means of a new mode of transport;
the steam turbine, the turbo-compressor; a slow development marked by associated scientific and technical advances resulting in an important new converter: the electric motor. The various contributions of the converters which provide modern technology with transportable and almost infinitely powerful forms of energy. For new techniques, new materials which will constitute the second major advance in the Industrial Revolution underway;
iron and its limits as a material; steel production and the modification which its advent necessitates in the iron and steel industry, both "upstream" and
"downstream" within the industry; from iron smelting to steel; from Bessemer steel to the Siemens-Martin process; the first alloys and the beginning of the era of special steels. The fundamental inventions which revolutionise traditional chemistry: towards a synthetic chemistry destined to replace old chemicals and then to move on quickly to the creation of artificial materials. The intervention of electricity in the production of materials; fabrication by electrolysis and arc furnaces. The exploitation of natural resources as a domain unable to escape the effects of the upheavals in industrial techniques; three sectors linked to agriculture and which profit in particular from technical and scientific advances: regeneration of soils with the use of fertilisers, protection of crops from their natural enemies, vegetable or animal, and the development of mechanisation; neighbouring techniques of the mining industry that bring important changes, the use of new materials and mechanisation; oil, a new comer among energy sources, its exploitation and utilisation. Another fundamental evolution, the transformation of transportation; modernisation of the railway: locomotives, steel rails, signalling; modern sea transport and its contradictory requirements: speed for warships and liners, maximum capacity for freighters; speed and tonnage, power and the encumbering weight of the engine; the role of the internal combustion engine in road transport; the beginnings of aviation and its problems. The innumerable technical accomo dations entailed by the extension of the means of transport and communi cation: traction methods; means of storing perishable goods and oil, new arrangement of railyards, track networks and necessary sheds and stopping places. The parallel progress in industrial equipment and tools: iron smelting equipment, rolling mills and machine tools. The main accomplishments of the
19th century industrial revolution: enormous diversification of materials, considerable increase in productivity and therefore in the speed of production.
How the main inventions are received, the resistance they sometimes encoun ter, the often difficult adaptation of a workforce having to submit to the new regime of industrial productivity and to integrate into the machine-worker system . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
693 DEVELOPMENTS: The balance sheet of the industrial revolution on the eve of the First World War, the promises it held, the role of the war in the development of new techniques. New perspectives discovered which will be explored by multiple improvements of the new techniques that will bring


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1327 modem industry to its present level. Improvements "upstream" and "downstream," the confluence of efforts made in different sectors, solutions found for minor but complementary problems: methods used to expand all the recently acquired techniques, well illustrated by transport methods; railways, navigation, automobiles, aviation. The slow transition from technical research to industrial techniques observable in the old sectors which require modifi cation and change rather than revolution. Productivity, the best measure of the global effect of several components of the system that would be difficult to evaluate individually. The problems of the acquisition of techniques by non-initiating countries: the possibility of adopting new techniques if the social and economic infrastructure is sufficiently developed for such an adaptation . . . .

by Bertrand GILLE The reasons which make the historical analysis of the contemporary evolution difficult. Three major phenomena which encourage the advent of new techniques: the great depres sion in the West of 1929-1931 , the Second World War, the increase in demand.
The rivalry and competition between the capitalist and the socialist worlds as another cause of research and change. Rapid mutations and slow mutations;
mutations that are exemplary and constraining for other techniques. Che mistry, an example of profound and rapid mutation, one which stimulated a great number of secondary mutations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The pro gramme; methods of calculation; the tradition of logic. Analogue computers and digital computers; the evolution of the second generation of computers;
micro-modules and the integrated circuits of the third generation; multi programming; large capacity auxiliary memories; tele-informatics; the interconnection of several computers. Pocket calculators. The technical future of computers and their probable limits . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
796 AuTOMISATION: What is automisation? The three stages that have marked the progress of automisation. From automatic machines in industrial production to automation, or the control of the various automatic machines in a production chain by means of automation; transfer machines. From the linear structure of yesterday's machines to the cyclical structure of today's steel making machinery. Some examples of advanced automisation and the advantages for certain industries. Automisation of measurement and control, and consecutive automisation of the correction of errors. Applications of automisation to transportation. Automisation and space exploration . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The renewal of marine techniques that are now nearly saturated in the merchant navy. The technical evolution and hopes of the railway which declined a short while ago. The exceptional technical transformation in military aviation and the repercussions in civil aviation. Urban transport as a failure of contemporary techniques.
Space exploration as a technical triumph. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

820 LANDSCAPES AND LIFESTYLES: A still incomplete, profound technical change, whose impact on the organisation of lifestyle and land development is still


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1329 difficult to determine. Techniques and everyday life; techniques and land occupation. Broken equilibria, to be restored . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
825 FEARS AND ANXIETY: Precise and imprecise causes of the malaise occa sioned by modern technical progress. Technical successes and human hap piness, two notions which can live together only if they both evolve at the same rate and only if the social system adapts to the technical system. Anti-scientific and anti-technical attitudes. From general fears that are hard to define to concrete fears that do not reject technical progress overall but fear the consequences; the atomic industry. The relationship between technical power and political power, that is to say, between an immediately appreciated power and an obscure and distant power. Informatics, the cause of a new feeling of vulnerability of the individual to government control, and the problem of individual liberty and technical progress. Material life, another sector where technical progress may be feared; the replacement of man by machines: the upheaval of occupational structures, the disappearance of some occupations and the appearance of new ones, the simplification of jobs, the increase in hierarchy, the extinction of intermediary occupations, and the bipolarisation of occupations which confines the worker in menial jobs while the technician is required to learn continuously. The pollution that is inherent to industrial growth and the quality of the material environment of life; the effects of pollution: immediate effects that are immediately measurable and long-term effects whose harm cannot be foreseen; the possible solutions for pollution and what they would cost. Other hazards and strains of industrial growth: illnesses of urban life; the automobile as a social problem, an economic problem, a sanitary problem, as well as a technical problem. The five fundamental points chosen by the Meadows research team at M.l.T. to represent the main directions of humanity's future: the population explosion, food production, industrialisation, the use of non-renewable resources, and pollution. The necessity of future technical progress that is very limited and rigorously directed . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .. . . .
830 H 0 PE: The naive faith in a better future that is determined by constant technical progress. An important question, "what is technical progress?", and the answers to this are a combination of objective and subjective arguments. The hope of an acceptable future and the two different attitudes it gives rise to. A critique of the method used by the researchers at M.I. T. : their dubious choice of the variables to be used in the model, their material conclusions called into question, doubts about their exponential projections of all the present trends which are the basis of M.l.T. demonstration; the reports of Kahn and Wiener of the Huson Institute; the sectors where major inventions might revolutionise present trends, and outdated sectors, such as transportation and traction, where improvements are still possible. The possible substitution of new techniques for outdated ones. Exploratory forecasting and how it is limited by being based upon extrapolations; the "envelope curve," from prior technical trends. Planning and technological forecasting. Two complementary kinds of research: exploratory forecasting which seeks to foresee possible solutions to known technical or scientific problems, and normative forecasting, which, taking into account the needs and tendencies that affect them, explores the means to solve them. Some spectacular results of the Delphi method; tables from Rand Corporation forecasting of scientific and technical breakthroughs, the conquest of space, the progress of automisation. The aspects and conclu-

THE EVOLUTION OF TECHNIQUES AND ECONOMIC ANALYSIS
by Jean PARENT The difficulty of including technological change in economic analysis and the relationships which link the evolution of techniques to growth models. The different meanings applied to the concept of "technique" in economic litera ture. The production function. The notion of technical change; the modifi cation of a product; the modification of a process: a distinction easily made in theory, but less discernible in concrete terms. Techniques and technology, the differences between them and the links which unite them; invention and innovation, the different principles derived from often common sources. The respective positions of the inventor and the innovative entrepreneur for
Schumpeter and Usher. Technical change, representing a sudden change within an enterprise, compelling the latter to adapt in order to re-establish the coherence threatened by the initial change . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .. . . . .
881 THE BIRTH OF TECHNICAL CHANGE: The relationships difficult to analyse, between technical progress and the functioning of the economy. The essential economic effects produced by technical progress, of which there are two different phases, distinguishable by their economic repurcussions, of introduction and generalisation. Profit, the essential, if not the only source of technical progress which is therefore closely linked to the capitalist system, which itself is served by the entrepreneur-innovator. The two means available to the entrepreneur to avoid competition: monopolisation or technical inno vation, whose effects, however, are only short-lived. The basic characteristics of innovation and the entrepreneur. Individual and group capitalism in the face of technical innovation and research, of which fundamental research is one of the more interesting aspects. Innovations, clusters of innovations and the dynamics of these . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Examination of the levels at which the diffusion of an innovation can be considered, and the principle conclusions which justify a study of this diffusion. The various types of reception to the propҧgation of a technical innovation in companies of different types. The obstacles to the diffusion of a new technique: the economic failure of tech niques owing to the lack of geographical or human adaptation, or the inflexibility of the market . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

by Andre FEL Mankind's technical contribution to the environments they inhabit, or the difficult relationships between the stable elements of geography and the constantly changing elements of techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The various cumulative processes in action in a large town and the paradoxical situations arising from urban concent rations. GEOGRAPHICAL ENVIRONMENTS AND INNOVATION: Ideas, the initial source of technical progress, and the factors which are independent of the area where they originate, but they can only develop in relation to their surroundings. The association of social and economic history with geography, which is the only way of explaining the creation of centres of innovation or improvement in technical knowledge. Stagnation or disappearance, upsurge or decline, the frequent consequences of collective action. 940 THE RESISTANCE OF "CRAFT" INDUSTRY. AGRICULTURE: The persis tance of semi-traditional forms on the fringes of certain modem industries, which find advantages in this unexpected association. The "workforce" industries, a sector which is relatively protected against the "economies of scale" of the industrial system. Massive production and specialised knowledge.
A few branches of traditional cottage industries in the face of modem industrialisation: glass-making, cutlery, glove-making, weaving, perfumery and fishing. Agriculture, the producer of Jive products linked to often unforeseeable environmental conditions, an activity which is ill-suited to industrial rationality. The very recent "agricultural revolution", due to the imposition of industrialisation which will not only create upheaval in tech niques but also in the traditions of the land. The resistance of traditional structures which makes it difficult to establish a general theory of techniques in the rural world . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
946 TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND POOR COUNTRIES: The diffusion of technical progress in developing countries and the obstacles it encountersҨ technical progress, which frequently represents foreign countries and colonial power, and which is therefore an object of resistance; the resistance of village societies to innovation; the slow advance of all types of modernisation which often progresses in small more insignificant details before radically altering the system of production. The indigenous populariser, the indispensable mediator between technicians or experts and those employing the techniques in the poor countries. The haste with which the poorer countries adopt the highest forms of industrial technology, resulting in modem industrial enclaves in countries which are not yet ready to accomodate them. The towns, which monopolise technical progress, islands in an uncoordinated geographical space. The research for harmonious development . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

effectiveness rather than knowledge, the latter representing a means rather than an end. The situations in which science and techniques are complementary, and those where they remain parallel . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Middle Ages.
