Some examples of the links between science and optical techniques . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
977 MECHANICS: Mechanics, a unified group of doctrines and principles which were once separate . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
979 Statics: The passage from empiricism to the science of the notions of force, moments and work. Scientific hydrostatics and hydraulic techniques. . . . . . . . . . . . . .
980 Machines: Complex devices permitting combinations of movements, a technique which does not use science directly . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
981 Dynamics: A branch of mechanics which owes very little to techniques, and whose practical application, however, remained very limited . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
The Mechanics of Fluids: A science whose technical application was also a relatively late development . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
982 HEAT: The physical behaviour of gases: a field where science and techniques helped each other, then diverged, then were definitively interlinked. Steam engines and the mechanical production of energy by using heat. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
983 ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM: A field where science and techniques appear to be most interdependent, and where a history separating science from techniques would be particularly difficult; machines for the continuous generation of electricity; measuring electric power; the long-distance transmission of energy;
the electric telegraph; the microphone; radioelectricity . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
984 CHEMISTRY: The original links between scientific and technical chemistry. The rise of scientific chemistry in the 18th and 19th centuries and its foundation, the capital of experience and creation which had been accumulated over the centuries. The application of a new science by industry; the particular case of metallurgy; the slowness of science in relation to technical creation, of which photography is a good example . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
TECHNICAL PROGRESS AND SOCIETY
by Bertrand GILLE The compatibilities and imcompatibilities between technical and socio-political systems. Is social evolution a cause or a consequence of technical evolution?
The difficult research into the links between man and machinery or imple ments, and the general reflections which the appearance of any new technique must provoke, - with a view to evaluating the consequences which it may have on social evolution . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
990 HISTORICAL BACKGROUND: A regrettable lack of interest on the part of historians in the history of techniques, which only pre historians have felt compelled to deal with, since material remains are the only information on prehistoric societies. The undeniable relationship between society and tech niques: the more complicated technical systems become, the more social systems differ. An interdependence, which makes either one system predomi nant over the other and vice versa, and which can create a blockage in the civilisation concerned when the social organisation does not evolve at the same pace as the technical organisation. The collective systems and a few examples of social constraints imposed by technical necessities, which themselves are subject to natural conditions: mistrust, a frequent social or corporate reaction, which is sometimes organised, in the face of possible technical change. The basic characteristics of social and technical reactions throughout history. The evolution of techniques which generates the growing specialisation on the part of those who employ them, and which makes their role in the functioning and organisation of society more and more important. The birth of the modern
Western societies and the role attributed to the industrial revolution of the 18th century which was one of the principle causes of the changes in the social structures. Demographic movements and job diversification, the conse quences of the rise in industrialisation. The training and evolution of the working class. The organisation of labour, another consequence of industrial mechanisation which itself was to have profound repurcussions on the transfor mation of the workers' condition; specialisation, recruitment, the training of


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1335 the industrial proletariat. A new movement, already apparent in the mid 19th century, and which is still rising: · the rise of the tertiary sector. The first steps towards a scientific organisation of labour; Taylorism, the almost caricatured example of the experimental method applied in the field of industry, which up until then had been governed largely by empiricism; the production line and the degredation of the working class. The existence of a certain discrepancy between social realities and social ideals . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
996 TODAY'S TECHNICAL SOCIETIES: the influence of the evolution of tech niques on social transformations in the developed countries. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1019
DISTRIBUTION: The characteristics of contemporary social structures. The explosion of the tertiary sector and its effects on the social and financial organisation of states. The evolution of the rural sector and the evolution of professional structures in industry. Transfers of various types engendered by automation which is bringing about an ever-increasing reduction in the size of the workforce. The increase of the tertiary sector generated by the evolution of administrative techniques and the subsequent renewal of the old "middle class", which is in fact a heterogeneous amalgam and one difficult to categorise.
White collar workers and employers, vague categories which are integrated vertically into the traditional pyramid of the classes. The maintenance of social conservatism in a stable technical system, or at least one which is evolving slowly, and the social transformation in a new or rapidly evolving technical system. The relationships between techniques and training personnel . . . . . . . . . . . .
1020 THE ORGANISATION OF LABOUR: Reorganisation of Jabour imposed on the United States by the difficulties of recruiting workers during the Second World War;
job enlargement and the contemporary reaction against Taylorism, or the end of the production line in favour of working in teams, within which each worker can see the whole production process: an increase in responsibility, and an increase in the possibilities of initiative. The social and psychological difficulties which any attempt at a new technique must undergo. Towards a reconciliation between workers and labour? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1030 TRANSFERS OF TECHNIQUES AND TRADITIONAL SOCIETIES: The two cases presented by the introduction of a new technique: an entirely new technique, or a technique improving an already existing one. The obstacles which present themselves before the transformation of a traditional type society and an industrial one. The countries of the Third World, the modern examples of an almost impossible compatibility between technical systems and social systems due to the obstacles imposed by traditions and attitudes. From traditional to modern agriculture, from the artisan to the industrial stage: an evolution which is all the more disruptive since it involves less well developed countries . . . . . . . . . . .
1039 TECHNICAL SOCIETY AND POWER: Technical progress and the rooted fear of unemployment which it incites in workers. Automation, a new technical revolution or simply the logical progression of an increasingly powerful system? A disqualification of tasks which leads to an increase in specialised workers, to a decreased standard of quality required, and to the double evolution of the workforce, which increases in the number of immigrants, and of women, and therefore in the reinforcement of managerial power. A more obvious tendency towards heterogenity in the working class, encouraged by new techniques and the new organisation of labour. Modern technology and the powers which it invests in the indispensable and increasingly omnipresent technocrat . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
TECHNIQlJES AND LAW by Bertrand GILLE The backwardness of law in relation to economic evolution, i.e. in relation to the evolution of techniques. New techniques, and the new relationships which they generate within society, which themselves then require new types of regu lation. Three aspects of the constraints that techniques bring to bear on the law. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1050
NATIONAL LAWS:
THE OWNERSHIP OF A TECHNIQUE: Appropriation of the right to exploit a technique: private or state monopolies. The patent, which protects the technical invention, and the privileges granted to the inventor. The privilege of exploiting the technique was often two-fold, in that it was forbidden to export the invention, or to import new techniques from the country concerned. From patents to the law of industrial property. Two different types of protection by patent, depending on whether the law stipulated preliminary examination or not. "Know-how", linked to savoir-faire, competence, experience; a new notion imposed by the growing complications of the process of applying modern techniques. "Show-how", a question of protection, both technical and economic. One question prompted by the increasing complexity of the latest techniques: does the patent still enhance progress?. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1052
THE MEANS OF PROTECTION: The protection required because of the establishment of new relationships in society caused by technical progress . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1059 Ordinary laws: The various laws which are designed to protect the users of techniques which are considered dangerous, or the consumers who must be safeguarded against danger. The social and financial aspects of protection. The increase in the methods of reproduction and diffusion which have brought about stricter laws in order to protect the artist, the photographer, etc . . . . . . . . . . . . 1059
Consumer Law: The division of labour, the inevitable price of technical progress, but which also means a dilution of responsibility leaving the consumer unprotected. The quality control of manufactured products, which cannot be undertaken by the consumers themselves, but by leglislation and official bodies; the leglislative process of measures, the control of standards, which is the essential means of defending consumer rights. Prevention of fraud, and quality control of commercial products. The different areas where legal controls are applicable. A contemporary statement: the discrepancy, ever greater, between the law and technical evolution . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1062 The Wild Assaults: Assaults which affect an individual who does not participate in the technical activity whose consequences they suffer. Assaults concerning the physical health of others, noise, and pollution of all kinds. The effects of modem technology which are more insidious, less obvious, and therefore more destructive, because they impose upon private life and public liberties; the instruments of such intrusion: photography, sound recording, telephone tapping, or the centralisation of information in electronic files . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1067
INTERNA TIONAL LAW: The increasing complexity of the relationships between different states. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1071
THE ORIGINS OF INTERNATIONAL LAW WITH A TECHNICAL BASIS: The different problems posed by various forms of protection due to the nationals of different nations. Individual or collective protection, literary and artistic property, industrial property, all these are domains where it is difficult to reach inter-


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1337 national understanding. The very rapid diversification of techniques of means of communication, which make imperative clearly determined international conventions: river, postal, railway, aerial, radio networks. The problems of pollution . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1071 RECENT DEVELOPMENTS IN INTERNATIONAL LAW: The technical explosion of the post-war period made most of the international agreements outdated, and necessitated the search for new solutions. The problem of the sovereignty of maritime countries. The justification for air traffic laws. Patents and standards, problems which are very difficult to coordinate on an international level . . . . . . . . .
1077 SOME BROADER DIMENSIONS: The rules of national and international protection to be found for new areas. Towards a law of the seas? A difficult adjustment to make to laws which have to be permanent, since the area they cover is evolving.
Another area of basic disagreement: radio and television programmes. The laws of space, similar to air traffic law, but different in that it must account for unforeseeable factors posed by space exploration. Satellites telecommuni cations, another example of the incompatibility of international law, and the mastering of a technique through a private or state monopoly; the violation of air space by radio and television communications, and even by photography in orbiting satellites, over which governments no longer have absolute control.
The fight against pollution on an international scale, in which each combatant seeks their own interests without reconciling the interests of others. The inevitable slowness in elaborating laws in the face of the increasing acceleration of technical transformation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1083
TECHNIQUES AND POLITICS
by Bertrand GILLE The entry of techniques into the political domain, the first of which to gain state attention was of a military nature . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1095 THE HISTORICAL BACKGROUND: The economy, an area which is rapidly becoming the affair of governments, and whose stability brings about a stability in state control. The first principles of a policy dealing with technical progress.
From the choice of the best techniques to perfected ones, and then possibly to a new invention. The stabilisation of existing techniques linked to internal economic stability; the importation of techniques from abroad by the instal lation of new factories, which dispenses with having to import the products.
The protection of national techniques. The search for new techniques often in opposition with an economy which is generally more concerned with recog nised efficiency, rather than risks which carry possible profit, but also possible expense. The English technical revolution of the 13th century and public powers. The reaction of the continental countries to the English industrial revolution: the interest of governments which try to generate the curiosity of private individuals, but the results of which are hardly apparent before the 19th century . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1096 THE LIBERAL ERA: Direct and indirect aid from the state: the creation of schools, training, import tax relief, reduction of tax burdens, subsidies. The slow substitution of private for state initiative. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1100
THE BEGINNINGS OF A TECHNOLOGICAL POLICY: The abandonment of a purely liberal policy following the First World War, the preparations for the Second, and the transformation of technical progress into an essentially


HISTORY OF TECHNIQUES
political problem. Nationalisation, general planning, increasing investment, and other factors which made the intervention of the state inevitable in the most varied of technical domains. Advanced technology and its economic consequences due to the costs of research and its increasing sophistication: the intervention of increasingly private groups or the creation of large domains reserved solely for the state. The increasing discrepancies between rich countries and advanced technology, and poor countries and less developed industrialisation; new techniques, new sources of inequality. The current role of political, therefore technical, economy, and the questions to which they must provide the answers: the number and scientific quality of researchers, the financial choices, and the choice of investment, the definition of a technological and scientific policy, the examination of the links between any technological policy and the social and economic needs of the nation, the analysis of the present situation, but also a forecast of the future needs and therefore the planning of scientific enterprise. Problems posed by fundamental research on the one hand, and by technical research on the other; scientific and technical politics. Two methods of financing research: capital invested in all areas, or capital concentrated on precise and determined objectives . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1104
TECHNICAL COOPERA TION POLICIES: Towards the inevitable technical union of small industrialised nations which cannot cope individually with the investment imposed by advanced technology. Countries of the Third World: a greater problem for concern than that posed by the small industrialised nations: the countries of the Third World; technological transfers and vol untary or involuntary development of the poorer by the richer countries; the different policies of countries supplying the technology; the export of tech niques and the imperatives which accompany it, for both exporter and importer; the need to elaborate a coherent policy for the transfer of techniques. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1118
AN IDEOLOGICAL PROBLEM: Two possible options for resolving the problem of the economic structure posed by the evolution of techniques: the grouping of enterprises and multinational enterprises, or state industries;
private or state monopoly; major principles ofa technological policy . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1128
ESSAY ON TECHNICAL KNOWLEDGE
by Bertrand GILLE For a definition of the relationships between technical and scientific knowledge . . . 1136
TECHNIQUES AND THE CLASSIFICA TION OF KNOWLEDGE: The various ways of integrating .technical knowledge in the global system of knowledge. Hugues de Saint-Victor, Al-Farabi, Raymond Lulle, the enu merations of science rather than actual classifications. The first real classifi cations of the sciences in the 19th century; Ampere, Cournot . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1137 AN ASCIENTIFIC VIEW OF TECHNIQUES: What is knowledge? What is science? What is craft, and how can one define it in terms of knowledge and science? What is empiricism? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . , . . . 1141
GESTURE AND THE SPOKEN WORD: The transmissable: experience through gesture and the spoken word; the intransmissable: the complete success in the artistic creation of the object . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1144 THE RECIPE: A type of knowledge which stems more from enquiry than from pure reflection. Based on experience and primary observation, this is an important


ANALYTICAL SUMMARY OF CHAPTERS
1339 source of a lot of technical literature because it is transmissable through writing. The principle areas served by the recipe: recipes of time, quality and mixtures. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1146
DESCRIPTION AND ILLUSTRATION: Description, the simple commentary on the illustration which provides the essential knowledge. The "mechanical arts" and the encylopedists' conception of this. Techniques, a group of levels which are linked but which do not necessarily derive from the same epistemological path.
The tool, or the machine, and the object to be produced, the dual purpose of the technical drawing; the evolution of illustration, the technical demon stration of techniques which results in the modern "industrial" design, another expression of savoir-faire, which is not the same as knowing why. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1150
THE SCALE MODEL: The model, the "living" mechanism, which can be used constantly until it is understood. The origins of the model and the evolution of the notion of the model: demonstration, research, diffusion and technical progress . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1156 A SCIENTIFIC VIEW OF TECHNIQUES: Scientific theory and technical reality, or, from the development of a scientific system to its practical real isation. The difficulty in determining the role of pure science in techniques, and of the margin which separates scientific and technical knowledge. Geometry, the only science recognised as useful by technicians until the 19th century.
Towards what is an increasingly difficult definition to make between scientific and technical knowledge . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1159 CALCULATION: A radical transformation of technical reasoning which excludes all notion of empiricism. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1167
The first step: The Table: A collection of all the measurable elements, and the establishment of an ordered knowledge for which the factors and the figures can be put into tables . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1167 The Module: Definition of the module. The first application of the module in architecture. The modular rhythm. Ballistic engines, another application of the module technique. The module and its use with tables, used by the builders of a new machine whose construction is not yet based on theory . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1168
The Formula: Numerical formula for resolving a specific technical problem.
Formulae and relevant tables initially applied to the strength of materials in architecture . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1171 A-POSTERIOR/THEORISATION: Essential criteria that a technique must fulfil in order to be reduced to theories. The various attitudes of technicians vis a vis theory.
The pitfalls that make both a theorisation of techniques and the history of the theorisation of a given technique difficult. The example of ballistics. The theory of machines. Strict reasoning and approximate knowledge . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1174 A-PRIORI THEORY: Blocked areas that cannot escape empiricism except through the impetus of a new science. Chemistry, example of the movement from a scientific to a technical system . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
1179 A CONFUSED SITUATION: A vast contemporary complex that brings into play technical knowledge and scientific requirements. Essential differences between the two types of knowledge. Brief history of an invention and practice of a process, complementary elements of knowledge proper. Methods of trans mitting technical knowledge: gesture and the spoken word, vocational training centres, science-based technical education . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 1181

THE HISTORY
OF TECHNIQUES
Edited by Bertrand Gille Translated from the French Volume 1: Techniques and Civilizations
Volume 2: Techniques and Sciences Bringing together a wealth of previously scattered information, this classic work traces the history of techniques and analyzes its relationship with other areas of knowledge.-Parts 1 and 2 provide historical coverage, from prehistory to the twentieth century, of man's attempts to master his environment. Part 3 examines this endeavour from the point of view of other major disciplines: economics, geography, science, sociology, law and politics. Together they provide a unique reference tool that puts forward a stimulating new perspective on this increasingly important and interesting field.
About the editor At one time in charge of the private economic archives section of the French National Archives, Bertrand Gille went on to teach history at the
University of Clermont-Ferrand. He was later elected to the Ecole Pratique des Hautes Etudes (/Verne Section) and remained a major contributor to the literature on the history of banking, technology and industry until his death.
Related publications of interest HISTORY AND TECHNOLOGY An International Journal, Executive Editor,
Pietro Redondi.
THE SCIENCE OF HISTORY A Cybernetic Approach, by W illiamJ. Chandler.
THE SHAPING OF SOCIO-ECONOMIC SYSTEMS The Application of the Theory of Actor-System Dynamics to Conflict, Social Power, and
Institutional Innovation in Economic Life, by Thomas Baumgartner, Tom R. Burns and Philippe DeVille.
STATE AND STATISTICS IN FR ANCE 1789-1815, by Jean-Claude Perrot and Stuart J. Woolf.
ISBN (2 volume set): 2-88124-155-7
GORDON AND BREACH SCIENCE PUBLISHERS
New York London Paris Montreux Tokyo ISBN Volume 2: 2-88124-146-8
